Posted: November 23
Dark Souls III is FromSoftware at the absolute peak of its craft—a stunning, emotional, and relentlessly thrilling conclusion to one of the most influential trilogies in gaming history. It blends the haunting atmosphere of Dark Souls 1 with the refined, aggressive combat of Bloodborne to create a game that feels both familiar and boldly evolved.

From the moment you rise as the Ashen One, Dark Souls III grips you with its breathtaking world. Lothric is a place on the edge of collapse, where time is distorted and kingdoms bleed into one another. The result is a world that feels mythic and dreamlike—ruined cathedrals, smoldering battlefields, twisted forests, and cities swallowed by ash. Every region is crafted with such detail and intention that exploring it feels like uncovering the final chapters of a long, tragic saga.

Combat is where Dark Souls III truly shines. Faster, smoother, and more refined than any Souls game before it, the battles feel fluid and exhilarating without losing the weight and strategy the series is known for. Every weapon has personality, and the Weapon Arts system adds layers of depth, letting you fight with style and precision. Whether you prefer dex katanas, colossal ultras, pyro builds, or pure faith miracles, the game welcomes all playstyles.

The bosses in Dark Souls III are nothing short of spectacular. Each encounter feels like a masterpiece of design and spectacle—from the elegant brutality of the Abyss Watchers to the tragic duel of the Twin Princes. And then there’s the Soul of Cinder, a fight that serves as a love letter to the entire series. These aren’t just battles—they’re emotional moments, tests of skill, and unforgettable set pieces.

The soundtrack deserves its own praise. Emotionally charged, haunting, and epic, it elevates every major encounter. Yuka Kitamura’s compositions give each boss a soul of its own, turning every victory into a moment of catharsis.

And beneath all the combat and exploration lies a story about cycles—of fire, of struggle, of heroes rising and fading. Dark Souls III carries a surprising emotional weight, especially for players who’ve journeyed through Lordran and Drangleic. It feels like witnessing the final breaths of a fading world, and yet, there’s beauty in its melancholy.

Dark Souls III is a masterpiece that honors its legacy while pushing its formula to its highest potential. It is grand, tragic, thrilling, and unforgettable—a perfect ending to an iconic series and one of the finest action RPGs ever made.

A brilliant finale. A powerful experience. A worthy legend.

Overall, a solid RPG experience of exploring, finding quest givers, and clearing dungeon areas. It's age is beginning to show, especially in muted brown levels, but still, a sight to see. The level design is still breathtaking and the art shines through the aging graphics. The combat is smooth, but still a bit clunky since its still an older game.
Yet, the story and enjoyment seem to be aging very nicely. I'd say this is a fun game to recommend to anybody, not just rpg gamers.
The level design is my favorite, just wish they added a bit more ambience in osts to match. The cons: its still a souls game. Fromsoftware makes excellent action games, but even they are still guilty of inserting dirty gimmicks and 'gotcha' ambushes. Just because its souls like, doesnt mean its actually a fair game in difficulty to the player. Example: draining stamina on swings, running, blocking, dodging. I dont like punishing blocking or dodging, both have to be fair if players need it to counter damage. Otherwise, guess they must get hit? Level design, story, fun are good. Combat and systems are fun but subpar.

Posted: November 22
Dark Souls III is the secret lovechild of Bloodborne and Dark Souls [1]. The story proves to be a direct sequel to the first title of the trilogy, whole amping up the gameplay and pacing to something more akin to but still not quite Bloodborne. This proves to be both a boon and a hinderance, leaving the game in a state that may turn off fans of the aforementioned titles--or at least hinder the experience.

Firstly, the gameplay is of a faster pace than Dark Souls or Dark Souls 2. While heavier armor and weaponry can still bring about slower pacing, and the shield is still very effective throughout the game, the enemy and boss movements and speed require greater speed than the Hollowed or Bearer of the Curse are capable of. Perhaps this is not a direct result of From Software previously working on Bloodborne, and may well be a result of modern innovation. The fact of the matter is, the enemy and especially boss experience feels more familiar to a Bloodborne player than a Dark Souls player. Even some of the NPC designs and the human trees look like something out of Yharnam or the Nightmare realm. While this game does still require a fair level of patience, it is far more forgiving and even rewarding than Dark Souls for rushing. Even the bonfires are plentiful, removing the challenge of boss runs or bonfire runs. With the absence of a Lord Vessel, and the game immediately allowing for fast travel as soon as you've found the Shrine Bonfire, it makes the overabundance of bonfires redundant--in one instance, you acquire a bonfire after defeating a boss, then immediately find one within staring distance a safe walk away. Perhaps this was in order to make the game more welcoming to gamers unfamiliar with the Soul's Like genre. Even Bloodborne didn't have lanterns so congested together in the game, and it's supposed to be fast paced.

Despite these similarities and gripes, the world building is just as masterful as is to be expected from From Software. The story is far more connected to the first title than Dark Souls 2 in its entirety, and acts as a direct sequel to Dark Souls. Even one of the bosses is an allusion to every player character ever created for, and who has completed the first game. The Knights of Katarina make a return, and we have references to the NPCs and even bosses from the first game and its DLC. Not to spoil where, but an area from Dark Souls is revisited in this game, altered ever so slightly to keep the familiarity while playing up the passage of time and events on the wear and tear of the setting. The story is truly compelling, and interacting with the various NPCs or reading the item descriptions really immerses one into the story.

Now second, the game has some unfair hitboxes, and enemies that the first game would have made parry-able cannot be. This experience really takes out the point of even learning how to parry--outside of dealing with mobs--since the game does not bother testing all that it has taught you the way the first does. The final boss in particular, while being a parallel to Lord Gwyn, cannot be parried despite being design like the type of bosses that usually can. For a final boss, you would expect the game's mechanics to be put to the test. You can parry Lord Gwyn, and are even given Black Knights outside of his fog door to practice on. You can parry Gehrman, The First Hunter, for he is a hunter just like you. The final boss in Dark Souls 3, despite being humanoid, cannot be parried. Pile that together with certain mechanics to the boss fight, unfair hitboxes--check out YouTube videos that show the hitboxes--and several combos that can one shot you without wearing specific rings or using a miracle or min-maxing your vigor, and you're left with a final boss that can leave a sour taste in your mouth after you conclude the game. The final optional boss would have served as a better final boss, despite being much more difficult. Despite these complaints, its lore almost makes up entirely for it. Almost. Thankfully, you can enlist a summon if you follow a specific questline, or even summon other players. The boss run is also the most direct and peaceful in the game, but just keep the challenge ahead in mind when plotting your attributes.

In conclusion, Dark Souls III is a game that I would recommend only to fans of Dark Souls or Bloodborne--who have already completed either of both games, and are looking for more akin to it--however I would not recommend it as anyone's first From Software game. If you can only choose one from the Dark Souls trilogy to ever play, I find the first to be a greater experience all the way through to completion, however I do recommend playing Dark Souls III afterward if you do decide to try another game. For a faster pace, I recommend getting a PS4 and playing Bloodborne instead of starting with Dark Souls III, and I wouldn't recommend going back unless you are ready to adjust to a slower pace.

Posted: November 20
When I decided to buy Dark Souls 3 I pretty much walked into it expecting it to be less entertaining than Elden Ring. Oh man.. It surpassed my expectations in literally every possible way.

Since I'm not really an open-world games enjoyer, the way this game is constructed was perfect for me and I can't think of a single location that I would change when it comes to that topic.

Combat was comfy, took me a second to get the hang of since I had a small break from souls games, but after that it felt perfect. Pretty much most of the bosses were amazing, except for some exceptions, but overall there were plenty of them who were enjoyable, mainly because of their sick choreography.

I'm not really into lore, but the overall story was amazing and witnessing all of the possible endings convinced me to actually get interested in it for once.

As of now, after finishing the game 3 times. Dark Souls 3 is close to being the best game I've ever played and I intend on playing DS1 & DS2 in the future.

Don't even get me started on the platinum trophy, that was pure hell and pain.

Posted: November 17
I was a little disappointed with the base game. It was my first souls game. People who review dark souls are inclined to say that it was very difficult for them. However, it was generally not an experience for me. I had pure strength/tank build with heavy rolls and majority of bosses were beaten by me in up to 10 tries. Some of them were completed literally on the first try... I managed to beat final boss on the first try and was very disappointed. The only S-tier boss in the base game for me were Abyss Watchers. I would put majority of bosses (yes, even Nameless King, I beat him on the 5th try, I have no idea why people find him difficult) into the B tier. They bosses were not bad, but they were not particularly memorable either.

I have no idea what the lore is, I'll have to watch explanations on Youtube. There are also some absurd moments that are not explained yet you somehow are supposed to figure them out. I don't think that's possible to reach Archdragon Peak by yourself without watching a tutorial beforehand. I understand that philosophy of Dark Souls games is "this world is cruel, no clues, figure it out", but this was a little bit too absurd I think.

There are 2 DLCs. I would say if you wanna buy this game, buy it with the DLCs, otherwise you will miss out on a lot of good experience. Ringed City DLC is absolute cinema and has the best bosses in the game. Gael and Midir are both S-tier bosses. Painted world of Ariandel DLC is mediocre in terms of location and NPCs. However, it has the most difficult boss in the entire game (Friede). I spent 5 days trying to beat her, I had to take a shield to do that, I had never done that before. I would put Friede in the A tier. Yes, the fight is great, but I think it's a little unfair.

Locations have great design and are memorable. I liked their skyboxes, but I wish they had higher resolution. I like how the game allows you to create any build you want, it's interesting to experiment with different weapons and spells.

It was a good experience overall. I expected more difficulty, but it's the problem of my expectations, not necessarily of the game. Maybe I should try Sekiro for more challenge.

Posted: November 17
Creative Bankruptcy: The Game
They fixed and unfixed many of the issues with Dark Souls II.
They kept power stancing, because it obviously makes sense to include that. They also added weapons which were made specifically to be used with both hands, like the Sellsword Twinblades and the Drang Hammers. But obviously, the coolest one is the Farron Greatsword. Its name doesn't make much sense considering that it's a dagger and an actual sword, but hey, cool, regardless.
Which is why it's so dumb that they made the player character mainly right handed, just like in the first Dark Souls. For example, holding an axe in your left hand makes you block, and you can only do a heavy attack with it. Meanwhile, if you have a shield in your right hand, you start punching with it. In Dark Souls II, your character is ambidextrous, however, sometimes you deal less damage with the left hand.
As for the story, areas, and story, the game tries its hardest to emulate Dark Souls 1, from the main quest to the ending, the boss designs, and the levels.
Crystal Sage is just Pinwheel that's not too easy. Old Demon King is just Demon Firesage from DS1. Wolnir is basically Gravelord Nito, again, from DS1. Yhorm the Giant has to be defeated with the Stormruler, a weapon from Demon's Souls, used specifically to defeat the Storm King, a boss fight, that is actually really fun with a cool setting. Dragonslayer Armour is basically the Penetrator from Demon's Souls. Champion Gundyr could be a different version of Iudex Gundyr that the developers thought would be cool, but couldn't decide on which one to put in the game. Soul of Cinder is literally the the Burnt Ivory King from Dark Souls II's Crown of the Ivory King DLC. Same moveset, almost the same attacks. As for the DLCs, Halflight is, again, literally just the Old Monk from Demon's Souls with the same invasion gimmick (which is quite cool). Then Slave Knight Gael, who is a red Artorias.
Areas: Firelink Shrine, same name and function as DS1, but a different setting. I like this version more. Farron Keep => Blighttown. Catacombs of Carthus => Catacombs from DS1. Irithyll + Anor Londo => Anor Londo. Grand Archives => The Duke's Archives. Untended Graves is funny because there's an extremely difficult out of bounds glitch where you can climb up backwards from Cemetery of Ash to reach the illusory wall to reach Untended Graves, but I think you'd get stuck as you probably can't enter the fog wall from the other side (at least in DS2 you couldn't do that), but if you drop back down you'd arrive in Untended Graves. Archdragon Peak is great, especially the Nameless King, I'd say he's my favourite boss. Kiln of the First Flame, same name as in DS1. Painted World of Ariandel => Painted World from DS1. So the pattern here is to evoke an emotion from the better designed game, DS1, by copying everything they can, but making them much smaller, and making them look just as terrible as they were in DS1, because of technical limitations.
More about the bosses (and the enemies), oh dear. It seems like they just tried to make them difficult for the sake of it. They all aggressively ambush you in hordes, as if this was Left 4 Dead or something. Two perfect examples of creative bankruptcy are the Deacons of the Deep and High Lord Wolnir. The deacons are just enemies spawning infinitely without giving you any room for movement at all, and the first stage of the fight is just some collective soul that you can only deal an exact amount of damage to. Then the second stage which is just a crowd crush simulator. Then there's Wolnir, a hard to reach boss with incredibly high defence, whose weakness are his bracelets. He may or may not spawn skeletons just to make things harder for you. Then there's the mist around him, which can make reaching the bracelet on his right hand challenging, because it pretty much instantly kills you. If he starts moving upwards, you're basically dead. Dragonslayer Armour can be pretty difficult by itself, especially because you're fighting on a bridge, and then for some reason the level designers also decided to put some monster in the sky to shoot at you during the second phase. Why? The rest are fine, I guess. It'd be silly to complain about attacks with weird timing. They're annoying, but they're fine.
Magic works the same way as it did in Demon's Souls, meaning you have a mana bar, that you can refill with items (here, mainly the Ashen Estus Flask). I like this one more than the system in DS1 and 2.
As for armour, this is the only game in the series where you can't upgrade armour. Which is sad for cosplay builds, as now you have to choose between high defence or fashion. Meh.
Weapon infusion also works the same way as it does in DS2, which I'm quite fond of.
As for PvP, there aren't really any OP builds like in DS1, but it's still just as unforgiving. Parrying is more difficult (with regular shields?) and rolling i-frames are longer. Parrying would be fine with latency, but dodging with server delay just makes fighting a nightmare. At least you can FINALLY invade without being embered/in human form. Thank GOD.
Overall, this game is a bit of a disappointment for me.

Posted: November 14
The finale Lordran deserves — flawless, brutal, beautiful 🔥🗡️

Coming from Dark Souls Remastered and Scholar of the First Sin, jumping into Dark Souls III feels like coming home — but to a version of home rebuilt, refined, and perfected. DS3 takes everything learned from the first two entries and polishes it into a complete, and satisfying conclusion to the trilogy.

From a story standpoint, this game feels whole. The way it connects threads from DS1, respects its legacy, and even to DS2. The world design is tighter, the pacing cleaner, and the atmosphere thick with themes of fading fire, forgotten heroes, and cycles long overdue to end. It’s melancholy, epic, and nostalgic in all the right ways.

From a gameplay perspective, DS3 is easily the smoothest experience in the series.
No bizarre hitboxes.
No strange input delays.
No awkward stat like Adaptability to fix your roll.
Just pure, fast, responsive Souls combat at its peak.

Boss fights are cinematic without being unfair, weapons feel weighty yet precise, and builds flow naturally thanks to excellent stat scaling and weapon arts. If DS1 had the charm and DS2 had the freedom, DS3 strikes the perfect balance between the two.

My only complaint is the Covenant item farming. Some of the covenant ranks require dozens of items that enemies barely drop. It’s a grind—classic FromSoft pain. I highly recommend listening to music, a podcast, or watching something on the side while doing it, because otherwise it will test your sanity more than any boss in the game.

Also, a small but helpful tip: pressing “C” in keyboard (R3) while in the inventory toggles between detailed and simplified view, hiding part of the menu so you can see your character model. Super useful when you’re checking for armor details.

Still, even with the farm, Dark Souls III is an incredible sendoff to the series.
A world worth suffering for.
A finale worth remembering.

9/10 — Cinder restored, patience destroyed. 🔥

An absolute masterclass of the series. I know I’m doing myself and the lore a bit of a disservice, but I’ve been playing all the Soulslike games in reverse. Elden Ring was my first, and from the moment I began, I was hooked. Its exploration was immaculate—every discovery felt rewarding and purposeful. Then came Sekiro, with its flawless, no-nonsense combat system. Sword against sword, no cheese, just pure skill and reflexes—those fights pushed me to my limits. After that, Bloodborne swept me away with its haunting, mid-century Lovecraftian horror aesthetic. Absolutely breathtaking. I could gush about all of them endlessly.

Now enters Dark Souls III—a game that takes my favorite elements from the previous three and fuses them into one brilliantly executed experience. It’ll kick you in the teeth, but you’ll love every moment of it. I still have two games left in the series—Dark Souls II, notorious for its unconventional environments, and Dark Souls I, the beloved pioneer that started it all. I’m looking forward to exploring both, learning the story in reverse, but I doubt anything will top DS3.
If you’re a fan of Elden Ring, you owe it to yourself to play this. The cinematics are grand, the soundtracks swelling and emotional, and every area feels meticulously crafted to reward curiosity. The bosses will break you down—but each victory feels earned, a triumph of willpower over despair. Both the base game and its DLCs are unforgettable. Sister Friede, in particular, was the ultimate test of everything I’d learned up to that point—a perfect culmination of skill and patience.
Dark Souls III is the peak Soulslike experience, a masterpiece that has carved its place in my heart forever.

As a long term player of the Fromsoft franchise, from console to PC, I give this game a masterpiece due to the past mistakes that Fromsoft has went through. They have made the perfect linear game that is very challenging. Elden Ring is also another masterpiece of it's own but the game is easy for me. But this game was the most difficult. Not more difficult than Sekiro. This tells a lot about it's own story, lore, combat, and dialogue. Even the aesthetics too. This game made me realize that the more I die the more I keep pushing to actually learn the game. It's like you have to tell yourself to slow down and pay attention. This is a game full of rage and fun. And understanding the 5 stages of your own grief. It teaches a lot about life when it comes to pushing yourself to do the most uncomfortable thing. The franchise makes me not want to touch any other game. That is why the game is emotional for us. We rage, cry, feel grief. Feel like you can't make it through, when in reality, anything is possible. Any build is possible to beat any boss. And the bosses gets harder every time you progress. So I thank for Fromsoft to build a perfect game from past mistakes. And that's what it is about to make a good game and to perfect your own art and craft without the need of external validation from anyone.

my honest review about Dark Souls III:
I jumped into it after completing Dark Souls Remastered, and I can confidently say it’s excellent in its level design and the world feels interconnected like in dark souls 1 but doesn't come to the level of dark souls 1's interconnected world, the combat is fast and amazing, bosses are tough and sometimes can be frustrating, but once you learn their patterns, the victories feel incredibly rewarding.

I recommend Dark Souls III to anyone who has completed Dark Souls Remastered. The game feels like a true conclusion to the series, with faster combat, unforgettable boss encounters, and a hauntingly beautiful world that constantly pushes you to improve how you approach.

That said, the game isn’t without flaws. On PC, it can freeze temporarily or permanently unless you force close the game and even if the freeze is temporary my character's walking after the freeze is like fat walking even tho my character wears light armor! and the framerate sometimes drops from a stable 60 FPS down to the mid‑20s. These issues are especially frustrating during boss fights, where every frame matters!

FromSoftware: Fix DSIII stuttering

Frame drops were not a problem during my first playthrough in 2018. I have since upgraded to a 9800X3D. After numerous patches, performance declined. How? That makes no sense

I’ve tried to:
-Enable VSYNC/Shader Cache - unlimited in Nvidea control panel
-Set affinity in Task Manager (uncheck CPU 0; I also followed a suggestion to disable all even-numbered CPU cores, to no avail).
-Disable “Device Association Root Emulator” under “Devices By Connection” in Device Manager
-Disconnect bluetooth devices, plug in directly via USB
-Set graphics settings to low/medium/high
-Update drivers
-Set Screen Mode to Windowed
-Run DarkSoulsIII.exe in Windows 8 Compatibility Mode
-Rollback on older patch
-Disable/stop Windows Management Instrumentation
-Disc usage under 50% (storage capacity at ~30% usage)
-Install Proper PC Experience from Nexus

FPS drops to 27 or 57 - usually in double intervals, seemingly at random (even while leveling up character, in menus or mid battle)

CPU never exceeds 50°c or 20% utilization
This is the title i experience this sort of issue w/. How FromSoftware managed to make DSIII perform over time is beyond me. No stuttering in Sekiro or Elden Ring so they clearly have idea how to program. This specific performance bug wasn’t a problem 6 years ago.. Now, there are numerous crash reports & similar performance related posts sited online 2023+

I found wired peripherals, no bluetooth devices connected to reduce stutters; However, i just had a newborn so headphones are a necessity. It’s either play the game in complete silence or deal w/ constant stutters using wireless headphones

Unpopular opinion TLDR: This game is really good, and has its moments, but compared to DS1-DS2 those moments are few and far between. This game is far more annoying to play than it is to play DS2 by kilometers.

I still love this game, and like it for what it is, but at the end of the day its the 3rd installment, yet it sheds a lot of the identity that the first two games had cultivated.

Bosses are ehh most of the time, you'll have good fights like sister freid, demon prince, pontiff, soul of cinder etc.

but in between all of that is narrow, bottle necking corridors that open to areas of mostly nothing of interest. And inside those corridors are annoying enemy placements, enemies with overloaded movesets, linear map design, bosses that feel extremely arbitrary and randomly placed as if they had almost no connection with the worlds lore, sorry dancer. Lack of "set ups" and exposition as to why i should care about anything. it just feels randomly thrown together.

Like for example, in the beginning of the game, you go and kill vordt to proceed, but you need a banner. Well do you need to back track or do something interesting to get the banner? NOPE. just turn around, go straight, and theres conveniently a lady sitting on a chair that just gives it to you, she also happens to be THE high priestess of lothric, how quaint! This game is FULL of sht thats just random and not very thorough.

Then most the weapons suck, and feel bad to use which only leaves a few weapons that are really worth using.

Ringed city is one of the worst DLC's of ALL time. almost made me quit the game all together, and again, MUCH worse to experience than DS2 by a long shot.

10/10 must play.

Posted: October 30
Most impactful tip I have for this game:
Get the crystal rapier ASAP so you can farm for gear, and sell armor pieces for souls.

Farming is alive and well.
There's no Tseldora Set like DS2 to boost your soul rate at the start of the game, but you can get to the Silver Serpent ring if you're okay with doing a platforming challenge.

Dark Souls 3 is the prettiest of the series by a longshot. It is the most polished and feels refined compared to Dark Souls standards, I don't need to grab the flip ring from the DS1 forest and smoke Lautrec for his ring to use a light roll build. Cloranthy ring still feels good and Grass Crest Shield is a warm comfort.

I played Str/Pyro and it felt really good due to my friend ahead of me being able to drop a +6 Chaos Lothric Greatsword once i was in Irythyll. You get enough Large Titanite once you're there to have most of your gear at +6. The Fire Lothric Sword carried me when Titanite was a struggle. Pyromancy takes a lot of juice to level up compared to like a dark ds2 build but feels fun and rewarding.

I miss lifegems a LOT but there's enough bonfires around to pop back up and fight whatever you were dealing with, which also feels really good. Runbacks have advanced in technology to feel good about dumping time into one specific boss.

DS3 is a big jump in its series, a love letter to DS1, and overall fun. There's still an awful poison biome, and dogs, but that's to be expected. The quality I like most about this game is that I don't need to binge it to have fun. Swag souls are better than ever, just make sure you're under 70% capacity so you can medium roll. Go do the Alt+F4 thing on Rosaria to cheat a free respec, bc its worth testing out all the cool weapons in the game. Make sure to Toast Emote with both your friends and adversaries.

Our Souls are Dark, welcome aboard.

I've beaten the vanilla game, including dlc, maybe 3-4 times. My most recent playthrough was with the convergence mod. This game is alright but not Fromsoft's best work. They really don't do sequels well. The game is very linear with only I think one optional area, three if you consider the dlc optional. I would never recommend buying this game for full price because it's just not worth it considering Elden Ring with its dlc is actually $5 cheaper than this game plus dlc. I honestly consider the games that came before it to be significantly better games. Bloodborne does the fast paced combat well. Dark Souls 2 has slow paced deliberate combat which this game is lacking in favor of light attack spam. Dark Souls 1 has incredible level design. Demon's Souls, the OG not the remake, has a better overall art style and gameplay feel. Overall I would recommend this game if you can get it on sale, though I've never seen it go for less than $40 from official store fronts. That's about the right price for this game I would say.

You learned that losing is fun in Dark Souls, that pain is part of the process and dying is how you GIT GUD.

You learned to level ADP or eat ♥♥♥♥ in Dark Souls II, where every dodge felt like a coin toss and every win came with a bit of spite. Don't Give Up, Skeleton!

And now comes Dark Souls III, the grand finale before Elden Ring took the crown. It’s the most complete and polished of the trilogy, the one I always come back to. Dark Souls is the classic, Dark Souls II is the oddball experiment, and Dark Souls III is the sharp, mean little sibling that ties it all together.

It’s a brutal, beautiful crawl through a world on its last breath. Everything’s dying, everything’s burning, and the game asks you one final time: do you keep the fire going a little longer, or do you snuff it out and let the darkness take over?

Either way, it’s the perfect goodbye, grim, relentless, and unforgettable. Don't give up, you CAN win.

🟪 Some Info:

[📝] — I kinda don't want to review this game as a "Souls Game." I don't think I have anything to add that hasn't already been said. However, maybe some more casual player is looking at Dark Souls III thinking, "Oh, this game looks so good, and everyone recommends it, but it's hard and I'm afraid to play it." So, from one casual player to another, let me share how DS3 felt for me. :-)

🟩 The Good:

[✅] This game is such a grand adventure—exploring locations, doing odd things here and there, gathering items, collecting souls, progressing through the game—it was all great.

[✅] Aside from being a Souls-like, this is also an action RPG. That means full character development with stats, equipment, magic, and more. There was always something I wanted to invest my souls (experience) into, which made leveling super fun!

[✅] "Fashion Souls" 😄 — Every piece of equipment (armor and weapons) is fully rendered in-game, so you can customize your character’s look however you want. Since armor stats don’t matter much, you’re free to prioritize style!

[✅] So many builds work! You can go with almost anything and it’ll be viable. I went for a very heavy build (big 2H weapon, heavy armor), and it worked like a charm. There wasn’t a single part of the game that forced me to respec or change anything. I was really satisfied with how my character turned out.

[✅] I didn’t encounter any technical problems or bugs that impacted my gameplay.

🟦 The Neutral:

[➖] The bosses—like I said, I’m more on the casual side of gaming, so I don’t have any strong opinions about them.

🟨 The Bad:

[⚠️] I already mentioned this, but the game doesn’t really allow for "face tanking." Even with a heavier, defense-oriented build, I didn’t feel like I could absorb much more damage. You still need to stay mobile in fights.

[⚠️] Some particular parts of the game were clearly designed to be annoying. You’ll learn those moments, you’ll overcome them—but they’re still annoying.

[⚠️] Some locations could use an extra bonfire (checkpoint). It’s not too bad if you know the layout, but for first-time players, it can be frustrating. Unfortunately, this also applies to some boss paths, which can be quite far from the bonfire, forcing you to dodge enemies and potentially lose focus before the fight.

🟥 The Ugly:

[💥] The camera during fights with large opponents—sometimes I couldn’t see what was happening at all. The enemy would take up the whole screen, and I had no idea what my character was doing or what my status was.

🟫 DLC Content:

[📦] DLC 1 – The snowy one: I have mixed feelings about this. The snowy mountain areas were enemy-heavy and not especially interesting. But the village and cathedral sections? Oh wow—they were super atmospheric, reminding me of the best places from Bloodborne (another Souls-like, sadly only on PlayStation 😢). This DLC also introduced a boss with three phases (three health bars). While it didn’t give me too much trouble, it’s not a design I enjoy. I think this DLC was meant to be played mid-game. I did it after finishing the main game, and I barely earned any souls during exploration.

[📦] DLC 2 – The end-of-time one: I didn’t enjoy this one much. While there were some interesting areas, most of them had something that made exploration miserable. For example, the flying "Butterfly" that constantly shoots at you until you find a specific summoner hidden somewhere. Or the long staircase packed with very tough enemies. The bosses here were on another level—huge health pools that forced me to spend real time learning the fights. And while I should’ve been frustrated… I actually felt good about overcoming them.

👍 Recommended
🕒 Hours Played: Over 43h
🎯 Completion Status: Last full playtrought (Base game + DLC - around of 90% of game content. All optional areas and NPC fights). Heavy 2H build (Dark Knight set + Fume Sword)

Just for the record. I have asked AI to help with typos corrections and grammar. If some sentences end up strange, that may be the reason. Please let me know.

Nice closure for the franchise, I still need to play the DLCs, I´ll buy them when I know I have time again to make a new playthrough from start. Good lvl design, good enemies en general, few times I thought the area was difficult 'cause bs, sad that Anor Londo was so brief, but I guess that's in line with the areas of the others Lords, except for the twins, and maybe you could argue Farron Keep, but being honest, now that I know where the altars are, you can speed through it rather quickly, maybe a detour for ash demon and other things you want.

Regarding the bosses, not that many, at least only in the base game. But they are in general good bosses, there are only like 3 I don´t like, 2 being meh and the other one being annoying as frick (the tree). I would have liked that they were a little bit more difficult, I mean, only one boss I felt was difficult, the Nameless King, but because he hits like a truck, he 2 tapped me and some times directly one shoot me (I was not using embers, 20 vit and 20 vigor slvl 65), so any mistake I had to paid it dearly, but the boss is not that difficult in a vacuum, the first phase turned a little bit like cinematic boss fight, the storm king only has like two attacks that are dangerous and can be difficult to dodge, the camara is the real enemy, never lock on that phase. The Nameless king is rather easy to dodge, except for that one lighting attack the falls upon you (that i needed full health to survive), I guess if I wasn't wielding an ugs 2h (like in the rest of my playthrough) the fight would be way more simple because I risk way less when going for more than one attack, besides he would always break my poise, so I couldn't trade hits like I did with other bosses, without taking into account that after a hit I had to heal or die if I took damage again. But yeah, the only boss fight I had to practise, took me like an hour to finally beat him, lost count, but like 30 tries more or less, especially sad when killed by a oneshot.

But again, the fights were easy or nicely balanced, my fav being probably the soul of cinder, dancer and nameless king (I liked the fight but Jesus that damage). Nice lore, nice sceneries, needed more vibrant color palette, your weapons and equipment upgrade pace felt nice, had like 5 max weapons at the end, the rings (as always I guess?) felt that had little flavor, they didn't add much thought to the build, in the sense that after acquiring the one that felt like a nice bonus never took it off again, I only changed one of my rings depending if I'm fighting a bosses or exploring. Felt nice that I never had to farm souls, never felt under level, I finished probably at slvl seventy...ish, I "maxed" my strength (66) for the final fight , my build was really simple, have enough str at the beginning to use my weapon, then level vit/vigor to 20, 15 end, 13 dex and start increasing str and never look back.

This is already way longer than needed, feels like a reddit post at this point, but I'll finish saying that this game is great, feels like the formula has been refined over the years, not gonna lie, DS1 still has a unique charm, I cannot say that DS3 the better game or that DS1 is, DS2 is a story for another time, I don´t hate it, I actually enjoyed sotfs, but that game had a ton of weird gameplay decisions and BS in it, but hey, I played that ♥♥♥♥ way more than I actually like to admit, in conjunction with Bloodborne, the 2 PS4 games I played the most.

One last time, great game, still a good one to pick even nowdays, I would suggest to anyone play the souls saga including Demon Souls, and try the rest of from games like Sekiro and Bloodborne, 'cause damn they are good.

[h5]Pros[/h5]
- Compared to Dark Souls 2 (my first Souls-borne series game) you can control your character SOOOO much better.
- new and interesting weapons to play around with and master

[h5]Cons[/h5]
- trying to 100% this game is ABYSMAL! You have to get all spells/pyromaniacs/miracles in this game and some are only gotten from the covenants, factions where you either PvP other players that are typically only found in a certain range of level (SL120-150) or you farm the drops off particular enemies... the issue is that the rates are HORRIBLE... 1-2% and you'll need up to 30 of a single one of those items for just ONE of the covenants.
- the story of the game like other souls-borne games is pretty convoluted, you just know to push on wards to a boss and slay them.

[h5]Conclusion[/h5]
I recommend Dark Souls 3 to 100% only because it is an enjoyable experience if you like previous dark souls entries. The grind for the covenants took me around 6+ hours total which is the worst but it isn't ideal. The bosses are fun, the weapons are fun and multiple endings to experience!

DARK SOULS III – recenzja

PLUSY:

+ najlepsza grafika z serii, świetne oświetlenie i lokacje, przy których chce się po prostu stać i podziwiać
+ muzyka jak zawsze perfekcyjna – podbija klimat i emocje podczas starć z bossami
+ bossowie to majstersztyk – szybcy, agresywni i różnorodni; wielu z nich to absolutna czołówka serii
+ ogromny wybór broni, czarów i zbroje które są najładniejsze z serii
+ dużo broni można ulepszyć na maxa
+ długość gry idealna – zachęca do ponownego przejścia
+ świetny system walki – dopracowane hitboxy i i-frames, tylko fast roll przesadzony XD
+ dwa znakomite DLC – nowe lokacje, bossowie, finały; płatne ale super
+ finalny boss to emocjonalny majstersztyk – sceneria, muzyka, walka z samym sobą
+ klimat inspirowany Bloodborne – szybsze tempo, mrok i styl

MINUSY:
- kamera lubi płatać figle regularnie
- gra jest zbyt liniowa
- mgła w firelink to jest jakiś absurd, po co tam wogóle jest?
- mało broni z naprawdę dobrym skalowaniem
- w PvP trafiają się cziterzy, choć mniej niż w DS1
- głupie, żmudne, monotonne farmienie do wbicia platyny, najgorsze z całej serii

Dark Souls 3 to perfekcyjne zakończenie trylogii – piękne, trudne i satysfakcjonujące. Choć DS1 wciąż króluje w moim sercu, to DS3 jest tuż obok – dopieszczone arcydzieło FromSoftware. Pełna radości, satysfakcji, krwi, potu i łez przygoda. Bardzo polecam grę jak i całą serię Dark Souls – CHWALMY SŁOŃCE!!!

After playing more than 100 hours, it became clear to me why this game has rightfully EARNED its Negative review. First of all, The "difficulty" is Artificial and in no way shape or form skill-based. All it did was slap Higher HP bars on enemies and programmed them to do higher damage values to the PC.

That's it! I'm serious! Want proof? Look up the mod on nexus "Easy Souls" and by just understanding how it works, you'll get what I mean.

Secondly, this game is HIGHLY Telegraphed. By which I mean that there's no way for you to "fix" any prompt or input you make unless the action resolves; You press to attack> You lift your arm to complete the animation and that's it. Same as the enemy!

You can't stop it
You can't dodge midway
You're FORCED to bait out the enemy's animation to finish as well

Some even string together a 9-attack combo and they can't really let you do anything. Not a single cast or a move. And they call THAT difficulty...

It's almost as if FROM doesn't know some of us are nimble minded enough to actually dodge danger by the millisecond. OR they claim this game is difficult by rigging it with stringent coding.

Anyway, Latter is what turned the game Garbage and thus earns its bad review.

If you still insist on playing this, I urge you to download either Easy Souls or Honest Merchant. Thank God that if not for the latter one, I'd have wasted my money on this game.

Peak gaming experience.

Pros:
- Great atmosphere and vibes
- Beautiful art style and graphics
- Best level design in the series
- Detailed linear levels with lots of exploration that is worth your time
- Clever shortcuts that prevent/shorten run-backs
- Fast paced Boss fights with quality that hold ups to ER's best
- Easily has the best soundtrack in the series and those Boss OSTs are something else...
- Interesting lore and entertaining NPC quests
- Satisfying callbacks to previous games
- Some of the coolest weapons to use

Cons:
- Camera issues as per usual in these games
- Lock-in can't keep up with some fast attacks, which can turn you around (very few instances)

Easily the best Dark Souls game. Took all the good ideas from the past 2 games and scrapped the artificial difficulty. Probably not as polished as ER, but I had just as much fun if not more.

Dark Souls III stands tall without the light.

Story
Set in a mythical land of swords, shields, and dragons; DSIII takes place at the end of time itself. To make a long story short, a fire known as the First Flame is responsible for the existence of the world's very metaphysics. From this fire, all the opposites that create life were born. Heat vs. cold. Light vs. dark. Life vs. death. But as all fires do, this one has been fading as it burns through its fuel, and it has been fading for a very long time. To stave off the end of the world as they know it, the people of the world have created a tradition of sorts to choose Lords of Cinder; would be self-immolating heroes of immense power who are expected to sacrifice themselves to the Flame to extend its life. But the world has only so much fuel to burn. After untold generations of this process, the fire is so desperately in need of kindling that the very lands of the world itself are being pulled towards it; conjoining them into a messy mishmash of high castles, stinking bogs, subterranean ruins, and everything in between. Meanwhile, there's no one left alive with the sole power to sate the fire's hunger, and so the dead are risen. Past Lords of Cinder are woken from the sleep of death with a duty to to make their sacrifice again. Only this time, they don't show. The thrones of the ritual site sit empty and so even more dead brought back to life to save the fire. You are one such Ashen Undead. A prior would be Lord that proved too weak to feed the fire upon your burning; reducing you to ash. Now you walk the earth again, cold and fragile, with both a duty and a physiological need to seek the life giving heat of embers. Your job is to seek out the Lords of Cinder who have failed to uphold their duty and force them to their thrones, one way or another. You may even discover a few other secrets of the world along the way and help paint a future worth saving.
The story itself isn't delivered up front. Most of it will require attentiveness from the player; reading item descriptions, speaking with NPC's, and paying attention to the environments and the things found within them. Dark Souls III portrays a hopeless, dreary picture of a world; yet some small hopes remain for you to find.

DSIII is a third person action RPG that emphasizes skill and timing as much as it does character build and gear selection. Not so much a difficult game as it is rigidly punishing to failure (though some of the later bosses certainly demand mechanical mastery from the player), the game expects a player's full attention as they carefully make their from battle to battle. Some baddies are certainly more challenging than others, but at the end of the day, there are myriad of ways a player can improve their own odds if they come up against a wall.
Players can progress their characters in many classic directions: The strong man wielding an axe larger than he is. The dexterous fencer flowing around his enemies and prodding them to death. The powerful mage obliterating opponents from afar. And that's to say nothing of archery, clerics with their miracles, flame wielding pyromancers, or even the many in between classes. There are a multitude of weapons throughout the game with a massive variety of different move sets, special combat arts, or even hidden effects. Armor ranges from delicate and dainty silks to stone plate several inches thick. A weight system helps mediate how quickly your character can dodge around enemy attacks while carrying all this gear; encouraging players to either build themselves into slow but sturdy tanks or light and nimble fighters whose entire goal is to remain untouched in combat. Or you can even be a combination of the two somewhere in between. Attack animations are often committed acts. Once you start swinging your sword, your character is going to follow through, so you want to make sure you pick your windows of attack wisely and avoid being greedy, lest you suffer an enemy's reprisal. Certain enemies can be stabbed in the back if you managed to get around them or have their attacks swatted aside for you to then hit them with brutal, high damage riposte attacks. A plethora of damage types helps keep players on their feet both in regard to incoming damage, and how resistant some enemies might be to the damage you're throwing out at them. The world of Dark Souls III is like a long and meandering roller coaster as you push through from one locale to the next. Exploration is a key means to finding new gear, upgrade materials, or even entire areas hidden from the main path with their own surprise and rewards. And that world is populated with a huge cadre of enemies too numerous to describe. They come in every shape, size, and color imaginable, each with their own attacks, patterns, and AI tendencies. And that's not even mentioning the great many bosses through the game who will test your build and skills the most. Though some are a hairier fight than others, almost all of them are a joy to outwit or out skill; the bosses most especially. While blocking enemy attacks is a viable way to play the game, you'll get the most value from learning to dodge their attacks entirely. There are some sincerely spectacular fights that will push your character too their limits and the sense of satisfaction when you come out the other side is simply wonderful.
The game also features unique online elements that helps build a sense of community. The ghosts of other players on their own journey will briefly fade into and out of existence. Bloodstains on the ground can be touched to witness a player's final seconds of life. Players can write messages both helpful and misleading using predefined words and phrases that will then appear spontaneously in the worlds of others. Summon signs can be set on the floor, allowing others to bring you into their world so that you may assist them in toppling a challenge that's giving them trouble. You can even be invaded by ne'er-do-well players seeking to kill you for your souls and embers, but likewise, benevolent players can volunteer their own time to come to your rescue; being summoned automatically to invaded worlds to help drive off the villain. You can even engage in Undead Matches; match made duels or outright brawls between solo players or teams in custom built arenas without the fear of consequence to your character's progress or inventory.

An imperfect masterpiece of visual story telling, world design, music, and sound. Dark Souls III strongest foot forward right out the gate is the world it takes place in. Everything is a stark great broken up by splashes of fading color an the glow of dwindling fires. From the most opulent sky scraping castles to the deepest and dingiest underground hellscapes; the entire visual construction of DSIII truly evokes the feeling of a world facing its mythological heat death, as if everything that could be burned to keep it going already has been. Even the very earth itself. The art design behind individual assets is simply top notch, from armor to weapons to demons and statues. It all looks incredible just in concept alone. Animation is fluidly weighty and just fantastic. Sound design can be easy to take for granted but here it elevates itself in unexpected ways at certain points through your journey. Meanwhile the use of music is just sublime. Most of your journey will go without a soundtrack which is most often reserved for boss fights, but when it kicks it; it just amplifies the excellence of the scene or battle it accompanies. The weakest link in the game by far is its lighting, which will vacillate wildly between being utterly god tier and looking like it belongs to a game ten DSIII's senior. Thankfully, the latter moments don't make up the bulk of the experience.

Posted: October 19
This is an amazing game and the perfect conclusion for Dark Souls trilogy. For many people, this will easily be the best Dark Souls game. If you are a fan of other Souls games, this one here is a must play.

The game can be played either as your first souls games, right after Dark Souls 1, or after you have completed both Dark Souls 1 and 2. You will still enjoy the gameplay, which is the main draw to these type of games. Personally, I definitely recommend playing at least Dark Souls 1 first so you can understand better the story and all the references. You can save Dark Souls 2 for later if you want since it is not that connected story-wise to the other 2 games (though it is still reference a little in this game), and it also has quite a bit of differences in the gameplay.

1) Visuals and performance:
- Very beautiful looking, a huge improvement over the previous games in terms of textures and lighting quality. The game has a lot of grey-ish colors. Some people dislike this, but personally I think it fits with the theme of "Ash" in this game
- Art design is incredible, locations looks stunning and enemies look incredibly interesting. Prepare for all the breathtaking, screenshot-worthy scenery!
- The game is still locked at 60fps but otherswise runs very well even on low-end machines. I played with a GTX 1650 laptop at Max settings and still get 60fps consistently. Some stuttering may happen but it is honestly quite rare.

2) Sounds:
- There is almost no background music in this game except at the main hub.
- All of the boss soundtracks are bangers, orchestral and bombastic, even the main menu theme goes extremely hard.
- Sound effects are quite satisfying.

3) Difficulty:
- The overall design of each level is a lot less dangerous than in Dark Souls 1. However each enemy encounter is a lot harder due to their speed and aggression.
- There are more enemy ganks in this game than in Dark Souls 1, but still less than in Dark Souls 2. Here they are more manageable since your character is so much faster and enemies tend to stagger easily due to their low poise.
- Bosses are a lot harder than in Dark Souls 1 and 2, so prepared to die.
- Bonfire are plenty and are quite close to each other if you know where to look.
- Overall, while the levels are easier than those in Dark Souls 1, the bosses made this one the hardest in the trilogy. Dark Souls 3 is also a lot more accessible than 1.

4) Combat:
- Fast and a lot more action-oriented than the other 2, both for the player and the enemies.
- The dodge roll is mandatory and should be your priority option to avoid damage.
- Enemies attack quickly and relentlessly and shields are nerfed in this game. Relying on them to block too much and you will run out of stamina easily.
- Stamina management is quite easy in this game, unless you spam the dodge roll too much.
- There are many weapon types with powerful options, 4 ring slots with a lot of useful and unique rings that you can choose from. Most weapons have special skills too.
- This game introduce the FP or mana system. Spells and weapon skills spend FP and it can be restored using the Ashen Estus Flask. You can choose to allocate between healing (normal Estus Flask) and restoring FP (using Ashen Estus Flask).

5) Bosses:
- The bosses are also fast and relentless. Except for some gimmick bosses, this game has the highest boss quality in the trilogy.
- Many bosses are brutally difficult, so expect to die at least a few times.
- Every attack feels fair (no bs attacks) and well telegraphed, with very tight hitboxes. Along with the amazing soundtrack, fighting and learning every boss is truly an exhilarating experience, full of adrenaline and goosebumps.

6) Level and World Design:
- The world progression is mostly linear. There is usually 1 or 2 new areas you can explore at any point in the game. However, each individual zone is very big. They still loop around with many shortcuts and secrets. I think this linear-ness helps them balance each area more carefully.
- The world and zones are very cohesive, meaning you can still visually see the next or previous areas while traversing the current one.
- While the best levels in Dark Souls 1 are still unmatched here, I believe Dark Souls 3 levels to have the most consistent level of high quality. All levels feel very well designed and none of them feels terrible to play through.

7) Story:
- The opening cutscene does very well to set the stage for the story and get the players interested, introducing the world and the key figures.
- Some people think this game has too many references to Dark Souls 1 and call it fanservice. I personally disagree and think that it is a reasonable amount since the games are set in the same universe and are directly connected.
- This is the perfect ending to the trilogy. It has 3 endings and 1 of the endings has an extra variation. All of which feels fitting to the theme of the story. Aside from the most basic ending, 1 require some careful exploration and the other involves some questlines that are very easy to miss.

8) DLCs:
- The DLCs for this game are phenomenal, having the best bosses in the game.
- The story of the DLCs reinforce the theme of the main game.
- Depending on how you look at it, the ending to the DLCs can be seen as the True Ending or Epilogue to main game.
- It is definitely a must buy if you already enjoyed the main game.

My first taste of FromSoft came with Elden Ring, and I was obsessed from the start. I didn’t think the older games could compare — I figured the smaller, more linear world wouldn’t hit the same… but I was completely wrong. Dark Souls 3 might actually be better.

The boss fights are incredible — visually stunning, perfectly designed, and absolutely punishing. I thought I was a veteran after 500+ hours of Elden Ring, but DS3 humbled me fast. Sometimes just getting to the boss was harder than the fight itself, which made every victory feel even more satisfying.

Even for a game that’s nearly a decade old, it still looks and plays amazing. The world feels handcrafted and full of secrets — hidden walls, secret levers, and sneaky platforming. There’s a constant feeling of curiosity and dread around every corner, making even familiar areas feel alive and unpredictable.

Everything I loved about Elden Ring is here — the community messages, the sense of discovery, and that perfect mix of pain and reward. If you were on the fence before, do yourself a favor and play this game — Dark Souls 3 is FromSoft at its absolute best.

10/10 — Brutal, Beautiful, and Totally Unforgettable

John DARK SOULS™ III

Posted: October 17
Dark Souls III offers a challenging yet rewarding experience that stands out as a strong entry in the Souls series. For players coming straight from Dark Souls Remastered (DSR), the combat mechanics will feel slightly different—faster, more fluid, and with a noticeable reduction in backstab-centric strategies, especially in PvP. It takes some adjustment, but the evolution is welcome and adds a new layer of depth to both PvE and PvP encounters.

Visually, the game is stunning. From decaying castles to eerie swamps and ash-covered ruins, the environmental design is consistently breathtaking and full of atmospheric detail. The story, while still cryptic in classic Souls fashion, is deeply intertwined with the lore of Dark Souls I, offering a satisfying sense of continuity for returning players.

Completing all the bosses and uncovering every reward is an immensely satisfying journey. The game strikes a great balance between challenge and reward, with boss fights that are memorable both mechanically and thematically.

For the best narrative and lore experience, I highly recommend playing the series in the order Dark Souls I → Dark Souls III. This path offers much more clarity and emotional impact, especially when recognizing callbacks and connections between the two games.

Verdict:
An excellent and worthy finale to the Dark Souls trilogy. Visually impressive, mechanically refined, and narratively resonant—Dark Souls III is a must-play for fans of the series.

Dark Souls 3 – Brutal Beauty at the End of the Fire

I absolutely loved Dark Souls 3. It’s one of those games that manages to feel both scary and rewarding — a brutal challenge and a work of art all at once. The level design is absolutely stunning: dark, ruined cities, haunting cathedrals, and twisted landscapes that feel alive with history (and enemies waiting to kill you). Every area is a maze worth exploring, and every shortcut you unlock feels like a small victory.

The combat system is really good — fast, fluid, and incredibly satisfying. Whether you’re swinging a greatsword the size of a horse or precisely dodging spells with a nimble dagger, every fight feels intense and rewarding. Add to that a ridiculous amount of weapons, armor sets, and builds, and no two playthroughs ever have to feel the same.

I will say, the story can be confusing — sometimes it feels like reading a fantasy novel backwards in a language you don’t speak. But that’s part of the charm. The lore is there for you to uncover piece by piece if you want to dig deeper, and if not, you can still enjoy the mysterious, apocalyptic atmosphere that makes the game so unique.

In the end, Dark Souls 3 is a masterpiece of contrasts — brutal yet fair, beautiful yet oppressive, and overflowing with secrets that keep dragging you back for more. And with its co-op option letting you bring a friend (or a total stranger) into the chaos, the whole experience becomes even more unforgettable.

Recipe for Potato Dumplings (Varenyky)
Ingredients:
For the dough:
Wheat flour – 500 g
Water – 200 ml
Egg – 1
Salt – 1 tsp
For the filling:
Potatoes – 1 kg
Onions – 2
Lard or butter – 50 g
Salt – to taste
Ground black pepper – to taste
Preparation steps:
Making the dough:
Sifting the flour: Sift the flour into a large bowl.
Making the dough: Add salt and the egg to the flour. Gradually pour in the water, constantly stirring. Knead the dough until it becomes smooth and elastic.
Resting the dough: Wrap the dough in plastic wrap or cover it with a towel and let it rest for 30 minutes.
Preparing the filling:
Boiling the potatoes: Peel the potatoes, cut them into large pieces, and boil until tender in salted water.
Preparing the onions: While the potatoes are boiling, finely chop the onions. Sauté the onions in lard or butter until golden brown.
Making the potato puree: Drain the potatoes and mash them. Add the sautéed onions, salt, and pepper to taste, and mix well.
Forming the dumplings:
Rolling out the dough: Roll out the dough into a thin sheet about 2-3 mm thick. Use a glass or cutter to cut out circles approximately 7-8 cm in diameter.
Forming the dumplings: Place a spoonful of potato filling on each circle of dough. Fold the dough in half and pinch the edges tightly to seal the dumplings.
Cooking the dumplings:
Boiling water: Bring a large pot of water to a boil, add salt.
Cooking the dumplings: Gently drop the dumplings into the boiling water, stirring carefully to prevent sticking. Cook until done (approximately 5-7 minutes after they float to the surface).
Serving:
Removing the dumplings: Remove the dumplings with a slotted spoon and place them on a plate.
Serving: Serve the dumplings hot, optionally drizzling with melted butter, sour cream, or sautéed onions.
Enjoy your meal!

English version (french below) :

Dark Souls doesn’t really need an introduction anymore.
For those who are afraid of the game’s reputation (I know I definitely was), let me tell you one thing :
Yes, the game is hard, but it’s far from impossible and there are ways to make it easier. You can summon NPCs or even other players to help you (though the system can be a bit confusing at first).

It would be a real shame to miss out on the atmosphere, the music, and the beauty of the game just because of its difficulty, it’s absolutely worth it ! And the same goes for the DLC, they probably have some of the best bosses in the series, so if you finished the main game but haven’t played the DLC yet, you definitely should !

For completionists, getting 100% isn’t too bad except for a few things: first, you’ll need to farm some resources either in multiplayer or from certain enemies (I did it the "farm on ennemies" way and trust me, if you do it by playing online, do it, because it is a very long farm).
You’ll also need several playthroughs, not only for the different endings but also for some rare items, so don’t worry if you miss something on your first run.

So don’t be scared, give it a try, and who knows, you might just become a Souls-like enthusiast ! :-)



French version

Dark Souls, ça se présente même plus.
Pour ceux qui ont peur de la réputation du jeu (c'était mon cas), laissez moi vous dire une chose :
Oui, le jeu est dur, mais il est loin d'être impossible, et il existe des façons de vous aider, vous pouvez demander à des PNJ ou même d'autres joueurs de vous aider (même si le système n'est pas le plus facile à comprendre).
Ce serait dommage de passer à côté de l'ambiance, des musiques et de la beauté du jeu simplement à cause de sa difficulté, parce qu'il vaut très clairement le coup ! Et le DLC est tout autant concerné que le jeu de base, les DLC ont probablement les meilleurs boss donc si vous ne les avez pas pris et que vous avez fini le jeu, franchement allez-y !

Pour les complétistes, le 100% n'est pas très dur à finir SAUF pour certains points : premièrement, il faudra farm des ressources soit en mode multijoueur, soit sur certains mobs (pour avoir choisi la deuxième option, je vous conseille fortement de jouer en multijoueur si vous en avez l'occasion parce que le farm peut être très très long). Il faudra également faire plusieurs parties, non seulement pour les différentes fins, mais aussi pour certaines ressources, donc ne vous en faites pas si vous avez loupé quelque chose sur votre première partie.

Bref, n'ayez pas peur, essayez, et qui sait, vous deviendrez peut-être des pros des Souls like ! :-)

Biggest complain by far is the stuttering/freezing. Holy moly it is bad in some places getting near the end after the Dancer. I have a beast rig too. Running on a 4080 at 2k, and there are some places it freezes a solid 10 seconds. Only title I've ever had stuttering on since building this.

Really dislike some of the hitboxing and registry of attacks. For one, enemy ranged clips through enemies. Fireball, arrows, whatever, goes straight through. Enemy melee attacks clip through terrain so they will hit you through pillars and trees, and your attacks bounce off and give you a mini stagger. Combined with annoying camera angle against a wall, this can be really frustrating.

Few other minor ones.

But the combat is fun. Bosses are challenging. Level design is great, Builds are meaningfully different in style and substance. It will push your buttons if you have never played a Souls game.

Souls games and especially this one feel needlessly difficult. Some enemies spam attacks with almost no opening and other times there is just to many enemies present to find a opening. This makes it less of a skill hurdle to over come and more of a "how do I best cheese this fight so it is actually fair?" I like the Souls series because of the open world exploration, combat mechanics, discovering the story, large variety of weapons, meeting weird characters, and the RPG elements.

If you have the patience to deal with difficult encounters and straight up unfair match ups then you might enjoy this. When it first came out i played online coop to assist others with clearing bosses and getting help to clear difficult areas. I'm not a fan of the pvp because it just feels like people make the most cheesy stun-lock builds possible. Just a fyi the covenants in the game can be progressed offline but it takes a lot of grinding for the items needed to progress them according to the online guides.

I would say my only actual negative is how most weapons I wanted to use scaled off of intelligence but I was a strength build trying to dip into faith (I was going for a paladin theme). I ended up completing the game just on strength because it got me better results because it was just to much of a time investment to level up a new weapon or meet the requirement for faith spells. The amount of work it takes to defeat some bosses, enemies, and search for treasure only to get something not for your build sucks and it happened a little too much. There is a way to respec your character in game but I didn't find it.

Posted: October 11
The Fire Fades, But The Flames Of Combat Burn Brighter Than Ever
Verdict: Recommended

TL;DR: The Definitive Soulslike Action Experience

DARK SOULS III is the exhilarating conclusion to the landmark trilogy, offering the most refined, aggressive, and rewarding action-RPG combat FromSoftware has ever created. It successfully blends the signature labyrinthine world design of the first game with the faster, more fluid fighting mechanics popularized by Bloodborne. The core experience is a masterclass in boss design, build variety, and Gothic atmosphere. If you love methodical, high-stakes combat and world-building shrouded in deep, sorrowful lore, this is the genre's pinnacle.

The primary caution for new players is the series' infamous difficulty curve, and for veterans, the price. It rarely sees deep discounts, a point of contention for a game of this age. However, the sheer quality and value of the total package (especially with the two incredible DLCs) justifies the cost. If you are prepared for the challenge and can accept the price tag, you will get an action-RPG experience that sets the standard for the industry.




The great strength of lies in its pacing and combat fidelity. The movement is significantly faster than its predecessors, allowing for quick, responsive rolls and attacks, requiring players to transition from the shield-centric defense of to an aggression that rewards timing and positioning. This is perfectly exemplified by bosses like Pontiff Sulyvahn, who demands immediate reaction and aggressive parry windows, shifting the focus from slow attrition to a dance of death. The new 'Weapon Arts' mechanic adds another layer of combat depth, giving every weapon a unique, often powerful, ability that consumes FP (focus points), making magic and skill builds more viable and exciting than ever before.

While the interconnected world design isn't quite the contiguous marvel of Lordran, the individual zones are visually stunning and masterfully structured, culminating in the breathtaking vista of Irithyll of the Boreal Valley and the sprawling, apocalyptic ruins of The Ringed City DLC. The indirect, environmental storytelling is at its peak; you don't just read about the world's despair, you feel it as you cross the corpses of failed heroes and battle the horrifying remnants of corrupted gods.


As a , I must address the price. maintains a high value proposition years after release, which can be frustrating for budget-conscious players. However, the recommended purchase is the or (base game + Season Pass). The two DLCs, and especially , contain some of the best bosses and most intricate level design in the entire FromSoftware catalogue. Slave Knight Gael and Darkeater Midir are not just bosses; they are monumental trials that serve as the true final examination of a player's skill. This content nearly doubles the game's longevity and adds essential lore closure. Wait for a sale on the full package, but do not skip the DLC.


On PC, the game is well-optimized and runs smoothly on a wide range of hardware, offering solid 60FPS performance without the major technical flaws seen in some other series entries at launch. The active multiplayer community, years later, is a testament to the game's quality, allowing for constant, fun engagement in cooperative 'Jolly Cooperation' or competitive PvP duels at hot spots like the Pontiff Sulyvahn area. Server stability is generally good, though the occasional connectivity issue is a given for a peer-to-peer system.
















is not merely a good Souls-like; it is one of the genre's greatest achievements. While the high, sustained price and the existence of one slightly weaker DLC may give a Consumer Advocate pause, the unparalleled quality of the core combat, the epic scope of the narrative, and the sheer challenge of its final bosses () create a monumental gaming experience. The mechanical improvements make it the ideal entry point for players looking for a faster pace than the first two games, yet it still delivers the classic, rewarding difficulty the series is famous for. Prepare to die, prepare to master, and prepare to be fully absorbed.

Dark Souls III isn’t just a great sequel — it’s the evolution of everything FromSoftware had been building since Demon’s Souls. It takes what worked in the previous entries and cranks it up to eleven.

Combat is faster, tighter, and more fluid than ever. It keeps the methodical weight of Souls combat but borrows just enough speed and flash from Bloodborne to feel fresh and responsive. Every swing, dodge, and parry feels deliberate — and deadly.

The atmosphere is unmatched. The world is crumbling, beautiful, and haunting in a way that only Dark Souls can deliver. From the ashes of Lothric Castle to the frozen desolation of Irithyll, each area is dripping with mood and lore. The level design is masterful, weaving verticality, shortcuts, and secrets into every zone.

Enemy variety is excellent, and the game never lets you settle into comfort. You’ll face grotesque horrors, armored knights, cursed beasts — no two areas feel the same. And the bosses? Almost cinematic. Dark Souls III puts massive emphasis on its boss battles, with stunning visuals, unforgettable music, and second phases that change the tempo and tone completely. Some of these encounters are among the best in the entire genre.

This is the most polished, refined, and focused entry in the series — a love letter to longtime fans and a high point for Souls design. If you’ve played the earlier games, Dark Souls III is a step up in nearly every category.

DARK SOULS™ III is brutal, beautiful, and absolutely unforgettable. It’s a masterclass in level design, atmosphere, and challenging gameplay that rewards patience, observation, and skill.

The world of Lothric is haunting and majestic, full of interconnected paths, hidden secrets, and cryptic lore. The environments are as dangerous as they are stunning, and every enemy encounter feels meaningful. From the crumbling castles to the poison swamps, every area tells a story—without the need for lengthy exposition.

Combat is faster and more refined than in previous Souls titles, striking a perfect balance between weight and responsiveness. Boss fights are intense, often grotesque and theatrical, and some of the best in the entire series.

Dark Souls III doesn’t hold your hand, but it never feels unfair. When you die—and you will—it’s a lesson. The game pushes you to grow, adapt, and overcome. And when you finally beat that boss that seemed impossible… it’s pure satisfaction.

Whether you're a longtime fan or new to the series, Dark Souls III is an essential experience for anyone who appreciates deep, challenging, and meaningful gameplay.

Now lets talk about the world... i have now replayed the souls series after playing elden ring... and personally i think the souls games are 100x better than elden ring. i prefer this linear style intertwined world, its magnificent. this is what i want to see more of. i could replay this untill the end of time. however, because elden ring is so massive... i just cant see myself ever trying to replay it! i dont want to go through dungeons that all look the same and alot of them have practically the same bosses. the world is far to big and just annoyingly complicated. it was cool to figure things out, and the bosses were literally amazing. but im sorry, Dark souls/ bloodborne/Sekiro/Demon souls- these are better games, i have played and ng+ the hell out of all of them. I want to see more, i just do not want an open world to explore, its been done time and time again. thats what makes dark souls so refreshing.
Yes dark souls 3 is a great game.

Posted: October 2
Dark Souls 3 is a fantastic ending for the Dark Souls Trilogy. The combat/boss fight in this game are by far the best out of the trilogy thanks to the faster/more fluid gameplay and tighter hitbox.

The music is great as always with multiple iconic Ost such as Vordt and Soul of Cinder theme. The navigation in the game is also more streamline which make traversing the map less confusing and in combination with the generous amount of flask the game is a joy to play.

I have completed the game 3 times on the PS4 back in the day and still found the game great enough to do an additional 3 extra full run on Steam. There are also huge amount of cool armors and weapons/spells in the game so no matter what style of combat you prefer there will be a build to suit your need.
The game also have 2 quality DLC for you to further explore and test your skills. While they are a bit short it is a quality over quantity type of approach that will leave you wanting more.

I cannot praise this game enough and it stand along side Dark Souls 1, Elden Ring and Sekiro at the top of my most enjoyed Fromsoftware game.
I highly recommend this game and would give it a 9 out of 10 score.

Posted: October 1
Dark Souls III – A Masterpiece of Challenge and Atmosphere

Dark Souls III stands as one of the finest entries in FromSoftware’s legendary series, blending everything that made the franchise iconic with refinements that make it feel modern and unforgettable.

The first thing that hits you is the atmosphere. Every location drips with haunting beauty, from the decaying castles to the ash-covered landscapes. The world design feels interconnected yet expansive, encouraging exploration and rewarding curiosity with hidden items, shortcuts, and lore.

Combat is where Dark Souls III truly shines. Faster and more fluid than its predecessors, it strikes a perfect balance between punishing difficulty and fair challenge. Every enemy encounter demands focus, every boss battle feels like an event, and victory never stops being satisfying. The game constantly pushes you to improve—turning frustration into triumph.

The storytelling is subtle but powerful. Instead of cutscenes and exposition, the narrative unfolds through cryptic dialogue, item descriptions, and environmental clues. This approach rewards players who pay attention, creating a sense of mystery and depth that few games can match.

Visually and sonically, Dark Souls III is breathtaking. The gothic architecture, grotesque enemy designs, and epic boss soundtracks create a mood that’s both oppressive and awe-inspiring. Combined with flawless level design, it feels like a true dark fantasy epic.

Overall, Dark Souls III is more than just a game—it’s an experience. It challenges your patience, your skill, and your perseverance, but it also delivers unmatched satisfaction when you overcome its obstacles. For many, it remains one of the best action RPGs ever created, and a fitting conclusion to the Souls trilogy.

Score: 10/10 – A brutal, beautiful, unforgettable masterpiece.

Dark Souls III is a masterpiece of challenge and design. Every boss fight feels like a battle against your own limits punishing but fair. The world is beautifully crafted, dense with secrets that reward exploration without holding your hand. Combat is tight and rewarding, with each weapon feeling unique and impactful. The lore is subtle, woven into the environment and NPCs, making discovery feel earned. Sure, the online can be spotty sometimes, but summoning allies or dueling adds an extra layer of intensity. It’s brutal, it’s punishing, but every death is a lesson. If you want a game that respects your skill and patience, this is it. One of the best in the series, if not the best.
