Что ж, наконец Гаэль добит и время подвести итоги. Я бы очень хотел полюбить эту игру, в особенности после второй части, которую я считал очень плохой игрой, но тройка переплюнула её почти по всем фронтам.
Для начала незначительные плюсы - общий визуал и некоторые боссы. Несмотря на то, что в целом игра серое нечто, картинка стала побогаче и некоторые виды действительно очень красивые. Касательно боссов, хотя я и распишу дальше почему считаю эту игру говном и главным образом из-за её геймплея, а так же немалую часть там будут занимать как раз боссы, ребята по типу Фриды, Гаэля и Мидира в целом мне понравились. Они всё ещё полны всех тех проблем из-за чего я игру невзлюбил(в особенности Фрида), но относительно всего остального пойдёт.
Теперь к претензиям. Одна из главных претензий - игра стала быстрее, враги и боссы стали куда быстрее, их атаки богаты на комбухи, на каждой локации, НА КАЖДОЙ, построение врагов сделано таким образом что ты всегда дерёшься против нескольких видов врагов одновременно. Кто-то дебафает, кто-то шмаляет, параллельно в тебя несётся какой-нибудь громила, выдавая свою комбуху из 10 ударов с сальто, разворотами и прочим, а под ногами носится с десяток мелочевки. Круто. Игра стала комплекснее. Чем на всё это может ответить игрок? Ээээ.. Кувырок? Всё? То есть игра апгрейднулась в плане механик врагов а мы всё так же играем за утюг из первой части который еле машит оружием(буквально любой враг быстрее вас, как пример какой-то рыцарь с 3 метровым молотом из башни на болоте второго длс, который ВСЕГДА лупит быстрее вас), еле кувыркается(ваш кувырок почти ничего не значит, у врагов комба + атаки на 720 градусов, которые всегда тебя заденут, как бы ты не уворачивался), еле пьёт фласку(как часто я огребал просто из-за того что анимация Эстуса длится вечность, многие враги успевают закончить свою атаку вдалеке, подлететь к тебе, замахнуться и ударить пока персонаж всё ещё пьёт) и всё это выливается просто в люто фрустрирующий опыт. До этой игры я проходил бладборн и секиро, лучшие игры фромов, и хотя с секиро сравнивать нечестно т.к. он вышел позднее, я всё-таки распишу что думаю касательно персонажа, тем более опираясь на опыт бб. В бладборне вся игра гораздо быстрее душ, НО, там и персонаж шустрее. Вместо кувырков у нас сайдстеп, который куда реще и короче, вместо эстуса у нас пузырьки, которые лопаются почти мгновенно и ты можешь продолжать бой. Вместо условного щита есть пистолет, который позволяет парировать. Есть раллинг хп, когда ты получил урон и можешь тут же отбить часть хп, просто нанося удары, что способствует агрессивному стилю игры, ты постоянно в атаке, а не кувыркаешься как колобок от врага. Вообще последнее наверно самое важное, я даже пробовал такой мод на дс3 и с ним игра ощущается куда приятнее, но всех проблем не решает(по итогу проходил её без модов). Про геймплей секиро можно вообще отдельную стену текста расписать, как часто я ловил себя на мысли что будь в дс3 персонаж уровня секиро, каждая битва была бы литералли синема. В той формуле геймплея ты всегда в бою, всегда атака, ведь даже парированния работают на атаку, превращая бой в танец. Опять таки в дс3 весь танец это спам кувырка на спам врагов, чтоб потом дать 1 тычку в единственное окно босса и дальше кувыркаться. Весело? Если у вас беды с головой, то да. И теперь самое главное. Эта игра была бы лучше даже с идеями двойки, за которые я её хейтил. Самоцветы для отхила, адаптивность, чтоб улучшить кувырки и скорость эстуса - вот настолько эта игра сделана хреново, что даже плохие идеи прошлой части, за которую ту все хейтили, хочется перенести сюда чтоб она не ощущалась настолько ужасно.
Дальше построение уровней. Мало того что от метроидвании типа дс1 и бб тут вообще не осталось и следа, все уровни просто прямые кишки, не считая открытых локаций типа БОЛОТ(все же их любят да?), замки не ощущаются замками, в игре мне не запомнилась ни одна локация, нет ни одного места которое я хотел бы снова посетить. И кстати во многом из-за уже упомянутых комбинаций врагов. Встретил элитку, которая вешает дебафф на то что у тебя хп срезается до 1? Сложный враг да, геморрный? Не переживай, другалёк, в конце локации будет комната с десятком таких бичей, гуляющих по кругу, которых надо будет мансить по полчаса. Весело да? Или вот демон есть который вешает фаерболл у тебя за спиной пока ты с ним дерёшься, сложный и бесячий враг, но не переживай, дальше будет комната где их будет пять сразу. Повеселишься. А мост с лучниками во втором длс? Сказка. Волки из первого длс, которых больше чем численность китайской армии, вообще песня, в особенности если ты помер и переигрываешь локу, чистый кайф. Но самая вишенка это город перед Мидиром, толпа мелких магов, каких-то рыцарей с атаками на километр и самый сок - громилы, которым надо прыгать на голову, чтоб убить. Их там минимум штук 6, почему бы и нет? Конечно можно с ними драться на мужика один на один, что я и делал изначально, но даже с моим оружием они ощущались как губки для урона. То что им надо прыгать на голову это интересно, если бы их было ну парочку на локацию и тебе надо было бы их разводить на это, но их просто куча, как и всего в этой игре. Ты дерёшься против миллиона бомжей всегда. И я не понимаю почему дс2 за это хейтили а тройку нет. Она куда быстрее, врагов больше, они более мерзкие, дс3 гораздо хуже ощущается чем вторая часть. И тут можно добавлять ещё примеров врагов, но и такого хватит..
Про боссов я мог бы расписать много, но тут и так уже вышло никому ненужное полотно. В целом это просто спам фест. Вся сложность боссов третьей части что они нечестные, хотя этому сложно дать определение, но когда босс просто не дает тебе дышать на арене как чемпион Гундир или не даёт дать урон после её атаки, как Фрида(я буквально не успевал взмахнуть мечом, она отскакивала раньше) и сразу начинают тебя лупить просто вихрем атак, это неинтересно. Это не хардкор. Это не хочется изучать и становиться лучше, это просто фрустрирующий опыт. Условного безымянного короля я убил без урона во второй фазе, я даже не почувствовал облегчение, мне пришлось настолько задрочить его атаки, что победа над ним уже не вызывала никаких эмоций. Ну не должно быть так.
Про лор я даже писать ничего не буду. Вторичность на вторичности, трижды переваренный кал, что подтверждает второе длс, когда уже стало понятно, что эти бесконечные циклы возжигания огня привели к тому что всё понамешалось. Все там дети Гвина, пятиюродные сестры братья, сватья, та вот бочка на самом деле раньше была Солером, а девка за дверью якобы дочь Сита и дочери Гвина. Ага, шиза + дурка.
Миядзаки как-то говорил, что не хотел бы делать сиквелы, надо было придерживаться этого правила, в конечном итоге мы получили вот это нечто, которое берёт картинкой, пафосом, боссами с 50 фазами, но по факту является даже хуже двойки. Я прошёл это просто чтобы закрыть гештальт по серии и скорее всего вымучаю на все ачивки(остальные платины на консолях), но это худшая игра фромов и один из худших игровых опытов, что был у меня в жизни. Каждый геймплейный аспект игры не оставляет ничего кроме разочарования и фрустрации. Не должно быть так.. Я возможно когда-нибудь ещё перепройду первую игру, которую проходил десятки раз и она стала для меня личной, может даже вторую часть переиграю, но это, я постараюсь забыть эту игру сразу как выбью подсолнух.
Спасибо тем, кто потратил время на прочтение этой шизы на игру 10 летней давности. У меня всё.

Posted: September 28
Dark Souls III is the culmination of FromSoftware’s legendary series—an unrelenting, elegant, and haunting farewell to one of the most influential trilogies in gaming. It is both a refinement of everything that came before and a bold statement in its own right, weaving together the brutal combat of Dark Souls, the speed and fluidity of Bloodborne, and the apocalyptic grandeur that defines the series.

From the opening moments, Dark Souls III sets the tone with its dying world. The Kingdom of Lothric is a place where ash, fire, and ruin blend together into breathtaking decay. Every environment feels like a painting steeped in melancholy—towering castles, twisted forests, sunless swamps, and cathedrals of impossible scale. The world is hostile yet mesmerizing, begging to be explored despite its constant threats.

Combat is where Dark Souls III shines brightest. Faster and more responsive than its predecessors, it demands both precision and aggression. Every swing of a sword carries weight; every dodge, parry, and counterattack feels vital. Boss fights are unforgettable—towering monstrosities and tragic knights alike, each battle a dance of patience, timing, and nerve. These encounters test not just reflexes but also determination, leaving players with equal measures of frustration and triumph.

The RPG systems provide immense flexibility. Whether you build a heavy knight, a nimble dexterity fighter, or a sorcerer wielding destructive magic, the game offers countless approaches. Experimenting with weapons, spells, and tactics keeps the journey fresh even across multiple playthroughs.

The storytelling remains as cryptic as ever, told through fragmented dialogue, item descriptions, and environmental details. Rather than guiding players through a linear narrative, Dark Souls III encourages discovery and interpretation. For those who invest in piecing together its lore, the story of fading flames, cursed cycles, and doomed heroes is as profound as it is tragic.

Visually, the game is stunning. The contrast between burning skies, ruined landscapes, and gothic architecture creates a constant sense of awe. Complementing this is a haunting soundtrack: melancholic strings, booming choirs, and somber piano themes underscore every moment, from quiet exploration to climactic boss encounters.

Its flaws are few but present. Difficulty spikes can feel punishing to newcomers, and the technical performance occasionally falters. Yet these are minor blemishes on an otherwise masterful experience.

Ultimately, Dark Souls III is more than just a game—it is a requiem. It closes the series with dignity and brilliance, celebrating everything that made Dark Souls iconic while pushing its formula to new heights. For veterans, it is a bittersweet farewell; for newcomers, it is a daunting but unforgettable initiation into one of gaming’s greatest legacies.

Score: 9.5/10 — A dark, beautiful masterpiece that stands as a fitting end to a legendary saga.

After beating the main game and all the DLC, I'll say that this was a very fun and challenging game. However, it gets a 9/10 for one reason alone, why did they nerf the binoculars?! Dark Souls 2 basically let you use them as a scope for your sorceries and yet in Dark Souls 3 they gave it the Dark Souls 1 treatment. Free-aiming sucks so much without the DS2 binoculars lol, but at least bows get aiming reticles now (yes I leveled DEX and INT, get over it). Overall, getting some cool spells in this game is making this a 9/10 instead of an 8/10. The whole "FP" bar thing was weird at first since I was used to casts per sorcery via scrolls you find or purchase but now it's not too bad (try leveling your INT to 60, you get damage good). Yes, tying cast speed to DEX and not ATT was another strange departure from DS2 but I like using bows and greatbows so it worked out for me (also katanas were cool). After I wipe some bosses in NewGame +1, I will start another playthrough using STR, DEX, and FTH so I can hurl lightning bolts and maybe join the Sunbros (very clutch for one DLC boss in particular, shout out to SchildMan who helped me beat Midir, you're the best Sunbro ever).

For reference, I played as a glass cannon sorcerer with only 9 VIG, which meant I had less than 500 HP even when I was "embered'. I got one-tapped by pretty much most enemies and especially bosses, but I thought it was fair due to my insane damage potential. It was interesting to see what bosses I thought were easy and which were hard because it was generally the opposite compared to most players, except Midir (He is probably the hardest boss in the game and has the most health and maybe damage too, but almost all his attacks one-tap me anyway so I'm not a good judge on that part. I'm not fighting him again lol, maybe as a Phantom to help someone else out). I actually ended up helping someone as a Phantom for the Slave Knight Gael battle and got them their victory on their first try (I exchanged the death blow with Gael and died in the process, still got my souls and ember lol). I got better at the game as a result because I can't parry to save my life so I just learned how to dodge really good against most bosses (looking at you Pontiff Sulyvahn).

A lot of people say the game is linear, which it is if you play it as intended but I learned there's certain parts where you can go to different paths and get stuff before you're supposed to have it. I will try that in my next playthrough as atonement for my glass cannon build (Sorry Sword&Shield, I vaporized you in the DLC boss battle, I suck at PVP but my homing crystal soulmass and crystal soul spear came in clutch lol).

P.S. you get opportunities to respec your character in case you want a different build in your current playthrough unlike Dark Souls 1 but similar (and not as often) as Dark Souls 2.

P.P.S. Deacons of the Deep has the best boss music and I unironically died to them so many times because I didn't have enough DPS lol.

P.P.P.S. The hardest bosses (in my opinion, in this order) were Darkeater Midir, Sister Friede and Father Ariendel, Oceiros the Consumed King, Pontiff Sulyvahn, Demon Prince, Twin Princes, Aldrich, Deacons of the Deep. The easiest bosses (in my opinion, excluding the tutorial boss, in no particular order) were King of the Storm/Nameless King, Dancer of the Boreal Valley, Champion Gundyr (the only way I beat him was due to dealing so much damage that he failed to enter his second phase and he kept attacking me lol), Dragonslayer Armor, Slave Knight Gael, Ancient Wvern, Yhorm the Giant, Old Demon King (literally 3 tapped this poor soul). The difficulty of a boss is highly dependent on your build (except Midir, he's just evil incarnate no matter what you play).

P.P.P.P.S. The most fun boss I ever fought was between Pontiff Sulyvahn and Slave Knight Gael. My most hated boss is tied between Oceiros and Crystal Sage (ironic since they're both magic resistant lol). Crystal Sage was the first boss that I ended up switching to a Katana in order to increase my DPS and win.

P.P.P.P.P.S. I was able to beat Midir, Halflight Spear of the Church (RIP Sword&Shield who served as the boss), Slave Knight Gael, King of the Storm/Nameless King, Soul of Cinder, and Iudex Gundyr (in New Game +1) within the same day, that's how powerful a glass cannon sorcerer build becomes by the end game. When you deal around 700-1000+ damage per blast with a crystal soul spear, the boss fights get cut dramatically short.

However, I have done a terrible thing and ended up cheesing 3 bosses: Twin Princes, Soul of Cinder, and Ancient Wvvern. All 3 can be killed with a bow, although I ended up using magic to finish Ancient Wyvern and Soul of Cinder. That is my true sin as a filthy sorcerer main, my intelligence had lead me to discover evil thus I choose a suitable ending. Ancient Wyvern actually is a terrible boss which serves no purpose other than gate-keeping the player from one of the coolest boss battles (easiest if you're a glass cannon like me lol) in the game.

Alright, that's quite a long review which ended up turning into a confession lol. May the flames guide thee, unkindled one, should you choose to get this game (and its DLC too, which has good gear and boss fights but questionably difficult areas even for someone overpowered like me).

As unbelievable as it may sound, I absolutely loved Dark Souls 3! The funny thing is, this was my very first Souls game, and even though I only picked it up again six years later (back then I gave up because it felt way too hard and I wasn’t experienced with games), I have to say—I couldn’t have chosen a better Souls title to start with.

What surprised me the most was how much it hooked me and how much I enjoyed it! Of course, once or twice I did roll my eyes a bit when I died, but come on—it’s a Souls game. My controller survived intact, I didn’t rage scream, so 99% of the time the game only gave me joy and excitement.

I know the older entries or some other titles in the series are much, much harder, but since this was my very first Souls experience, I can only recommend it! The levels and the level design are simply brilliant. It was such a joy exploring every nook and cranny to see what kind of items they were hiding.

Even in 2025, I think the graphics still hold up and are enjoyable. The monumental feel of the bosses, their attack patterns, and that incredible sense of accomplishment when you finally land the finishing blow—it’s truly breathtaking. And the music? Just genius. I LOVE it! I still get goosebumps from some of the boss themes, and even just sitting in the main menu listening to the music already tells you—you’re in for a tough journey.

The controls do take some getting used to (at least for me, coming from games like Batman and similar ones, it felt strange), but once you get the hang of it, there’s really no problem. I loved this game so much—even though I wasn’t originally a Souls fan—that I just had to 100% it! The game deserved the time I poured into it.

That said, for anyone who wants to max out the achievements: brace yourself, because you’ll need to farm an insane amount of items for a very long time. If I remember correctly, there are seven different item types, and you need about 40 of each. And the drop rate? For me, it took around 40–50 minutes just to get one drop, killing 1–2 enemies every couple of minutes. And this was with a build specifically made for item farming!

So for anyone looking to max it out, I strongly recommend checking YouTube for easier farming methods. Even so, I still replayed the game three times because you need to do that for the rings. But honestly, I enjoyed every second of it—it never felt like I was replaying out of obligation, trudging through every boss again.

At the end of the day, all I can say is: I can only recommend this game. It’s a masterpiece.

After watching a lot of Soulsborne content and putting 1,000+ hours into Elden Ring, I decided to return to Dark Souls 3 for a fresh playthrough. I had already finished Dark Souls 1 and Bloodborne years ago. After finishing the base game it became clear that Dark Souls 3’s greatest strength lies in its world-building, aesthetic design, and atmosphere. Few games capture such a sense of desolation and beauty at the same time while having such precise, weighty, and (un)fair Combat.

For this run I went with the classic Claymore strength build, infused with heavy, while keeping stamina, health and strength at roughly the same level. That balance made me tanky and powerful enough to cut through most bosses without much trouble. I killed every main boss (except Midir and the Nameless King) in under five tries simply because my damage output was absurdly high. That’s where Dark Souls 3 differs a bit from Elden Ring: while bosses look amazing and feel intimidating, their mechanics aren’t overly complex once you have a strong build.

Still, the game thrives on atmosphere and exploration more than on pure mechanical difficulty. It demands patience and punishes recklessness, but rewards persistence with unforgettable moments and some of the best boss designs in the series. For me, Dark Souls 3 is a game that shines less in raw challenge and more in how its world makes you feel—a haunting, brutal, and strangely beautiful journey. I also love New Game + as a feature in any game where you dont need to unlock every mechanic again and can vary swiftly between build without compromising power.
It requires patience, persistence, and a willingness to learn from failure. In return, it delivers one of the most intense gaming experiences out there: a unique world, memorable bosses, and the unmatched satisfaction of overcoming impossible odds. For those willing to endure the challenge, it stands as a masterpiece of the genre.

Dark Souls 1 is my favorite, 2 is a dumpster fire, and 3 is a solid all-around game with the best combat mechanics in the trilogy. I can't imagine any souls-like enjoyer not liking this. Even when finally finishing this game in 2025, it is head and shoulders above most souls-likes out there.

Pros
The new gem system allows you to add elemental and stat affinity bonus to weapons which does wonders for build variety.
All weapons have "weapon arts" that are special attacks that consume focus points. They aren't all unique but it's a very welcomed addition even if it just gives unga bunga builds a reason to even have focus points at all.
The weapon variety and combat mechanics in a vacuum are the best in the series.
I love the enemy designs, figuring out some of their odd mechanics, and dealing with their frequently diabolical but fair placement.
As usual, bosses are the star of the show. Their visual designs are cool and the quirks of each one keep you on your toes.

Cons
There is a persistent stutter that shows up maybe once a minute that you can both see and feel regardless of of what stupidly overpowered hardware you're running this old 2016 game on. Dropping a frame here or there usually is just mildly annoying but this one can and will eat your inputs pressed during the stutter. For example, you can be 100% positive that you pressed the dodge button but have it very inconveniently have been pressed during that stutter so your character just loses half of their health bar instead of dodging. This is the kind of thing people will throw their controllers over.

My personal score: 8/10

The mother of souls games and the best Dark Souls in the series. Without a doubt, one of the most challenging and frustrating souls games I've played.

Fantastic quality game if you're looking for a challenge. The game has no difficulty setting and it's auto set to "hard" and "very hard" I guess haha. The weird thing is, after playing souls game, you will eventually become a much better player overall. You build up patience, resilience and a "I try and die handful of times and I will eventually win mindset".

The good stuff:

+ One of the best gothic style in modern games. The art direction and setting is pretty much spot on
+ Amazing enemy and boss designs
+ Plenty of weapons, spells and stuff
+ Some fights are satisfying

The bad stuff:

-Inconsistencies and unlogic placements of bonfires. One of the best examples is Lothric Castle:
The distance between Dragonslayer Armour and Grand Archives are literally just 20-50m apart. What the hell????

Then there are plenty of times you've wished for a bonfire next to a huge event or boss fight.

-Insufficient explanation and hints. Everything is ridiculously vague and obscure.

- For some the difficulty is like the opium for their gamer hearts and for me, the line between challenging and too difficult has been crossed a few notches. The frustration factor was way ahead compared to NIOH or Lies of P. Dark Souls 3 was the hardest souls game. Hard not because I died a lot, but hard because frustration levels hit the roof.

- Lackluster ending(s). Not only were the endings very short but it felt very unsatisfying. I went through sweat and hell just for THIS?! No proper ending or explanation after the DLC finale, other than a few dialogues.

- I don't expect a great story or lore but… at least a clear motive that drives motivation and passion to go from A to B.

A must game for all souls fans!

I don't typically play these high-end games. A major reason is that the graphics simply does not combine well with the gameplay, even though we are paying a hefty portion of money for it.

I replayed it for ~4.5 hours recently. Some problems are just so prominent:
1) The lock-onto-target system is counterintuitive, so I like to play without locking onto enemies. In many maps, however, this causes the camera to keep rotating uncontrollably, which makes the game extremely unenjoyable. The camera logic overall is bad.
2) There are significant disparities between what the visuals suggest and where the hitboxes really are. The most noticeable example is parrying. If you have not bought the game, you can simply check on YouTube that the game's parry does not check the relative positions of the weapon and the shield, but rather the animation frames. Do not get me wrong - this is a viable solution. Perhaps I am simply one of those few who do not feel satisfied thriving in a game after learning all its details and specifics.

If this isn't my favorite game, I don't know what is.
Every few years I come back to it, throw together a new build, and it's as magical as the first time I played it.
The refinements to the Souls formula are so close to perfected here. All it could benefit from is something like the Elden Ring stakes of Marika, and the mechanic where you can duplicate a boss soul of your choosing via the mausoleums, for the occasions where both choices benefit you.
The weapons feel great. All armor pieces are viable in some way. The progression is just linear enough where you have options of where to go a few times, without being too under-leveled for one of them.
There's lots of room for skill expression here. Wanna go to the final area of the game early? Prove it, beat the Dancer.
I rolled up to Farron Keep and dagger quickstepped through it, treating it like a bullet hell.
Fromsoft could easily patch out stuff like the Firelink Tree Jump that gives you acess to trading and silver serpent ring early, but even that trick is somewhat balanced by the inability to access the rest of the things you'd normally get with the tower key.

Dark Souls III stands as a masterclass in game design, seamlessly blending challenge, atmosphere, and artistry into a cohesive experience. From the moment players step into the decaying, beautifully realized world, it is clear that every area has been crafted with meticulous attention to detail. The environments are not just visually stunning—they tell a story of their own, drawing players into a hauntingly immersive universe.

Graphically, the game is exceptional. Lighting, textures, and environmental effects combine to create moments of breathtaking beauty that contrast sharply with the world’s pervasive darkness. Whether navigating the ruined halls of Lothric Castle or the eerie depths of the Farron Keep swamp, each locale feels distinct, alive, and meticulously curated.

The true heart of Dark Souls III, however, lies in its boss encounters. Each fight is a masterful blend of strategy, skill, and tension. From the staggering spectacle of the Abyss Watchers to the imposing grandeur of the Nameless King, every boss demands mastery of timing, observation, and patience. These battles are not mere obstacles—they are memorable, cinematic experiences that reward careful planning and relentless determination.

Overall, Dark Souls III exemplifies what makes a game timeless: a world rich in detail, a challenge that feels fair yet formidable, and moments of pure triumph that linger long after the screen fades to black. It is a triumph of design and vision, deserving of every accolade it has received.

Posted: September 7
The true souls-like game from FromSoft imo. When you think of a souls-like, you think of DS3 and with ER out, now ER has taken the throne arguably, but for ER to run, DS3 had to walk. This game is great by itself... from a platinum perspective, it's quite the pain.
Disclaimer: I relied on the ds3 wiki a lot, especially when it comes to finding all the crap in the nooks and crannies of all the maps in the game. I gave it my best shot on NG but all subsequent runs, I just used the wiki. There were a lot of missables and so I used the wiki to route my gameplay in as optimal as I can so there's that. I went with a STR build using the Claymore mainly but started out as deprived. Oh and I did not play the DLCs.

Pros:
- fighting is as rewarding as you'd expect. Developing a sense of patience and reading the behaviours, and getting stomped by a boss only to have the run where you finally pull it off, this is the souls-like experience that's so well praised. And it's vindicating to see where the whole mechanic really took off.
- story is phenomenal, albeit, a lil confusing but the whole idea is exploration and putting the pieces of the lore together.
- sfx and music are immersive. The voice acting is also good.
- a lot of versatitlity and viability when it comes to choices. I have noticed that most of the weapons are usable and most of the classes are viable. I didn't try any sorceries, miracles, pyromancies (aside from CFA). At the end of the day, it just comes down to skill issue.
- you don't need DLC to platinum the game which I really like as a principle. I don't intend to get the DLCs but I'll def watch some let's plays/walkthroughs of it.

Cons:
- mechanics aren't as smooth as ER. No standalone jumping mechanic (only a run jump), no midair attacks (only landing attacks). Going from ER to DS3 took some time getting used to, that's for sure. Fast travel is only available at a bonfire, levelling up only available at the shrine. If you lost your souls in the world, you best know how to get back there bc there's no map or indicator to tell you where it is. There's also a lot of running and you always have finite stamina even if you're not in combat. Ofc these mechanics being cons is minor but really, it's moreso an annoyance which can interfere with the "Gaming Experience". Especially for players new to FromSoft souls-like, it's smth to at least be mindful of.
- visually speaking, I'm not a fan of the constant 60 fps and the fact that you either play fullscreen or windowed (and no borderless) is lame. Doesn't matter that it's a 2016 game bc elden ring was that too (except it had borderless). But with that said, bc of the static frames, you can really max out your graphic settings on any system. Other than that, the game is beautiful.
- there's no steam cloud saves? it's literally a free service for developers to use. Also, $80 for a game in 2016 is nuts.
- the covenant farming is ofc the bane of everyone's existence. It took the longest overall. I did most of my farming on NG++ using both PVP and PVE. In total, I probably spent 20-25 hours total? Hey at least you can farm souls and grind up your level like this....
- from a platinum perspective, I kinda like the whole planning out your route and your gameplay and paths to find the most optimal playthroughs. But if you're just playing the game bc it's fun and then you decide to platinum it, it will be frustrating. So many missables and you really need the wiki for this. All this effort can be daunting for many and so from a platinum side, be wary. \

It's a good game. No wonder it won goty 2016. Just probably don't platinum it unless you really want to...

Because of my nostalgia and personal attachments to this game (the characters, the philosophy, the tone, the Gothic mapping, the lore, I could go on) I may not be the best person to ask to leave a review. Its a hard game. But that was what made it substantial for me. With my whole heart I recommend this game to anyone interested in playing it. The Soulsborne series are legendary for a reason- and DS3 will forever be important to me (ignore the lack of hours Ive put into this game on steam, Im old now and mostly come back for wondering around the maps).

It was an escape from a bad situation- made me feel like I could accomplish something when I was trapped otherwise. I played Dark Souls 1 on my first console, beat it twice to get both endings, played DS2 once (barely beat it, didnt care for the mechanics), and earned platinum achievement on the PS4 for DS3. Not for completionist reasons. I was compelled to find everything, talk to everyone, do everything. And... DS3 was the end. I havent found a game like that since.
Everyone at the shrine, all those characters who were struggling alongside me when I felt the most alone in my life, during a foundational part of my life, impacted me in a way that was unexpected. It is something that I know I will never find again in any other game because of when I played it, but also because this game is simply that good.

Thank you to the creative team of FromSoft who crafted this game.
And screw Sony for limiting support on old gen Playstation consoles.

I won't just talk about Dark Souls 3, but about what the Dark Souls franchise has given me.
These games are cool, challenging, and excellent RPGs, but that's only a small part.
These games can heal your wounds, they can transform you. I know it might sound corny, but Dark Souls 1/3 helped me through some difficult times. It teaches us that to overcome adversity and change, you have to step outside your comfort zone. Sometimes we're afraid to start a game, we think it's too hard and we'll die. But in life, when we want something, do we just say, "Oh no, this will be hard," or do we do our best to get it? It's through discomfort that we sometimes improve, and these games can relieve depression. You could not believe me and stick to the superficial idea of this game, but when you think about it and take your time, you realize how much this game gives you.
"Don't you dare become hollow"

Rating 9.5/10

This has to be my favorite game in the Dark Souls trilogy. The bosses are fun and engaging, the level design is fun and varied enough not to get boring, unlike the previous two titles, and while I will say that it is probably the easiest of the three games, it doesn't bother me too much, as there were still a couple of bosses and areas that gave me a challenge.
I also can't be too harsh on the game for not being difficult, as a lot of the loss in difficulty comes down to the game's responsiveness and polish, as far as the combat goes. Unlike the two previous titles, I did not have nearly as many moments in this game questioning why I died or got hit or blaming the game mechanics for my deaths. This installment has the smoothest combat experience of all three games, and that is one of the main reasons I enjoyed it so much more than the other two.
Overall, I love this game, and I wish it weren't the final installment in the series. I also wish we could have had some returning features from the previous game (I feel like bonfire assetics could have solved most of my issues with the game's difficulty, please add them back, Fromsoft), but I am still extremely excited for what will come next.

It's 2025. You have heard of Dark Souls. You have heard it is a great series. You have heard it is difficult. There will be spoilers to certain aspects of the game in this review, but as I said, it is nearly a 10 year old game by this point.

There are a lot of things I can say about Dark Souls 3, and Dark Souls as a series in general. I believe that Dark Souls 3 is tied with Dark Souls 1 for the "best" Fromsoft game behind Elden Ring. There are several reasons for this, including lore/world-building, combat/mechanics, and balancing. The score and art direction are both fantastic as well.

DS3 is certainly more similar than Dark Souls 2 to original game and the world created by it, but it also shared a surprising amount of connects to DS2 that I, as a fan of DS2, really enjoyed seeing. From the giant memory tree outside of Firelink Shrine, to the Drang and Faraam sets, to the implications of who the Nameless King really is. I had always thought that DS2 was the ugly stepchild of the series, and I felt almost sad for it while playing it. DS3 honoring it in the ways that it did, and even building off of it in certain ways, was one of my favorite parts of this game.

But, my favorite part has to be the connections to the original Dark Souls. I fell in love with Dark Souls the first time I played it. The world was dark and dreary, as was to be expected, but also so much more full of hope and compassion than I could have ever imagined before playing it. It had a sense of quiet and somber melancholy to it, as if you were witnessing the dying breathes of something once beautiful. DS3 carries on this tradition. It builds on Lordran (and Drangleic) with Lothric in ways that make the world feel both incredibly void of hope and also brimming with heartfelt stories. I loved seeing places like Anor Londo, the Firelink Shrine, and the Kiln of the First Flame again, and seeing how they had been warped over the countless ages.

All in all I could go on about this game for hours, but I will spare this review of that. My final verdict is that Dark Souls 3 is a perfect ending to the masterpiece that is Dark Souls.

Dark Souls III is a game that can absorb you for countless hours and leave unforgettable memories. FromSoftware has once again proven that they can create true works of art in the world of video games. The atmosphere is dark, mysterious, and full of hidden stories that the player gradually uncovers, often piecing together fragments from item descriptions or the architecture of the world.

The combat system is pure satisfaction – dynamic, precise, and demanding. Every encounter requires focus and patience, and defeating a tough opponent gives a sense of triumph that is hard to find in other games. The variety of weapons and combat styles ensures that everyone will find something to suit their playstyle, while character customization offers great freedom in choosing how to approach the game.

The graphics and level design are breathtaking. Dark cathedrals, abandoned castles, and burned villages build an incredible atmosphere, while the music enhances the dread and epicness of boss battles. Each location is crafted with attention to detail, and discovering shortcuts and hidden passages feels like a well-earned reward for exploration.

Dark Souls III is not an easy game, but that’s precisely its greatest strength. The challenges it presents teach patience, determination, and reward perseverance. It’s an experience that not only entertains but also provides deep satisfaction from overcoming personal limits.

In conclusion – this is an absolute masterpiece of the action-RPG genre. If you enjoy demanding games that reward dedication and don’t hold your hand, Dark Souls III is a must-play.

Level Design Extraordinaire

Dark Souls 3 boasts some of the most intricate and interesting levels and locations I have seen in any video game. While the world design in Elden Ring and even Dark Souls 1 is more intertwined and elaborate, Dark Souls 3 makes up for it with its genius level design. Each location has its own personality, not just through appearance but also in how it plays out. In this aspect, it is Souls at its best.

Boss design is strong, but compared to Elden Ring it is lacking. Apart from a few bosses, most of them feel like elite enemies rather than true bosses when compared to Elden Ring. This really shows how much FromSoft has improved when it comes to boss design.

It also reminded me how much I have missed the PvP in FromSoft games. The PvP system in Elden Ring just does not do it for me. Spontaneous 1v1 duels in a compact and meticulously designed level are a far better experience than duels in vastly larger spaces with an invasion system that mostly forces players to invade those in co-op. I hope this style of PvP makes a comeback in The Duskbloods.

Most players are not very fond of the Ashes of Ariandel DLC, but I think it was good. While it is obvious that the level design took a turn for the worse, it was still enjoyable nonetheless. Also, the final boss Sister Friede in that DLC was pure cinema. It might even be in my top 10 FromSoft bosses. I am sad that I did not get to play the Ringed City DLC, the one most players love, because of the lack of a cloud save system. I lost my save, and I simply will not be replaying the 30 hours I had already played and enjoyed.

Dark Souls 3 is FromSoft at one of its peaks, a game with tight and intricate design and little to no downtime. Even though I prefer the masterful world design of Elden Ring, it has been a while since I experienced such a brilliant and tightly designed game.

This is the most modern-feeling entry in the trilogy; the combat is fast-paced and the visuals feel like they're trying to make you say "Wow!" a lot. Out of the three, this one's my least favorite, mostly due to the story and characters feeling too rehashy. Plus, despite this game overall having the best combat, I still have some pretty serious gripes with it.

The big one is how pointless it feels to wear heavy armor. The additional damage reductions they offer over light and medium armor are pretty minor, and poise, the stat that prevents enemies from staggering you when they hit you, is almost not a mechanic. I tried min-maxing my poise, and weak attacks from enemies that would take at least 10 hits to kill me were staggering me just as much as if I were wearing light armor. Add in the fact that light armor will naturally allow you to roll further when you dodge, and it felt like a really straightforward choice to simply wear lighter armors with "good enough" stats and maybe the Ring of Steel Protection to close the gap on resisting physical attacks.

My second issue is that the weapon skill Perseverance trivializes most of the boss fights. You push L2 while using something like a mace or a morningstar to instantly activate an effect that reduces all damage you take by 45% and gives you infinite poise for a second or two. Basically, you no-sell many of the strongest attacks in the game, with the only exceptions being grabs and the occasional attack that seems to get around it for no discernible reason (though the latter instances are extremely rare). This ability, plus the extra healing at the start, is why I think the Cleric is the best starting class.

This is still a VERY good game as a single-player experience if you have the energy and motivation to learn it. Plus, thanks to the Seamless Co-Op mod, it's an amazing multiplayer/co-op game now, too. The mod might even be worth using if you just want random invasions, since there's an item that "calls" for other players to invade you included in the mod, and the original servers are very underpopulated at this point.

“Silent Hill f” is, undoubtedly, the best entry in the series. This game, Silent Hill f, absolutely shakes up everything we know and love about the series and redoes everything from the ground up. Subtle horror, psychological probing, subtle and deep symbolism both are cast aside for “in-your-face gore and action. This is such a welcome change! The intentional frame delay of combat (upwards of 17 frames) was scrapped in exchange for reaction time and straight up action. Why be the everyman/woman when you can be a kung fu master that can fight and tackle anything Silent Hill… I mean, a village in Japan, can throw at you? Lets trade psych horror and body horror, lets trade disturbing imagery and cosmic elements for something less subtle.

Yes, like Silent Hill 3 and 4, this game takes place outside of Silent Hill! The twist? Unlike Heather carrying a demonic ocult god in her womb, or a mass murdering serial killer Walter who gained power from the actual town, this game has nothing to do with that. It’s just Silent Hill f for the sake of it! Plot twist! I love it!

Remember complicated puzzles that firced you to pay attention and focus? Well, with the dawn of smart phones and chronic ADD, that’s been dropped for something easier.

Now, I know what you’re thinking, “but how do I level up?” Easy. You get skill points and a bonfire equivalent to Dark Souls.

This game may look like a Dark Souls rip off, but it isn’t! It’s actually Silent Hill! But it’s in Japan and not set in Silent Hill. Because who cares about titles anymore?

Look buy this new game, Silent Hill f, now!

★★★★★ “577 Hours of Tax-Free Trauma”
After 577 hours, I can confidently say I have no idea how to parry.

Dark Souls 3 is a masterpiece of emotional abuse, where every door is locked from the other side, every chest is a mimic, and every NPC I’ve grown to love either dies tragically or tries to murder me mid-conversation. It’s like Pokémon, if every Pokémon was Malenia’s cousin and the professor handed you a broken sword and a lecture on despair.

Combat? Sublime. Like ballet, but if all the dancers were 8-foot knights on fire and you’re barefoot in a swamp full of crabs the size of minivans.

The lore? Oh it’s deep. So deep, in fact, that even after 577 hours I’m 70% sure I accidentally married a spider god and 100% sure I committed at least one war crime against a tree.

PvP? Yes. I was invaded by a naked man wielding a broken straight sword named "69" who promptly destroyed me in 6 seconds. I felt fear. I felt awe. I felt the uninstall button calling.

Bosses? Incredible. Each one has three phases, an anime intro, and an inexplicable hatred for me personally. I once spent 9 hours fighting Sister Friede, and I now flinch whenever I hear a woman sneeze.

Fashion Souls? 10/10. I've worn a helmet shaped like a mushroom for 40 hours because it gives me vibes. My armor provides no protection. Neither does therapy.

In conclusion:
After 577 hours, I’ve learned that fire is both a gift and a punishment, the true enemy is gravity, and nothing beats the high of backstabbing a gank squad while dressed like a confused jester. Would I recommend it?

Yes. Absolutely.
But only if you hate yourself just enough.

Steam Review: Dark Souls III
Overview

Dark Souls III remains one of the finest action RPGs of the modern era. It refines the formula from its predecessors with faster combat, gorgeous gothic environments, and unforgettable boss encounters. The game is brutally difficult yet deeply rewarding, and every victory feels earned. Even nearly a decade later, it holds up as a masterpiece of design. That said, the PC version is not without its flaws, as multiplayer connectivity and frame pacing issues can cause frustration.

Category Breakdown (1–10)

Graphics: 9/10. The world of Lothric is bleak yet stunning. From ruined castles to fiery wastelands, the level design is breathtaking. Some textures show their age, but the art direction overshadows technical shortcomings.

Performance (PC): 8/10. The game runs well on modern hardware, maintaining stable high frame rates. However, occasional frame pacing issues and rare crashes are still reported.

Gameplay: 10/10. Combat is faster than Dark Souls I and II, with influences from Bloodborne. It feels fluid, intense, and precise, rewarding skill and punishing mistakes.

Story & Quests: 8/10. Lore is rich and deeply layered, but much of it is hidden and requires community interpretation. NPC quests can be easily missed or failed, which can frustrate new players.

Sound & Music: 10/10. The orchestral score during boss battles is unforgettable. Atmospheric sound design makes every location oppressive and immersive.

World & Immersion: 9/10. The interconnected environments are masterfully designed. The mood, art direction, and enemy placement create constant tension. Immersion can be broken by occasional odd enemy AI behavior.

UI & Accessibility: 7/10. The menus are serviceable but clunky. The game offers minimal accessibility options, and newcomers face a steep learning curve.

Content & Replayability: 9/10. Between New Game Plus, multiple endings, DLC expansions, and diverse builds, the replay value is extremely high.

Technical Stability: 7/10. Stability is mostly fine, but online connectivity issues are frequent. Invading or summoning can take too long, and hackers remain a problem on PC.

Value for Money: 9/10. With both DLC expansions included in the Fire Fades edition, the content is vast and well worth the price, especially for fans of the genre.

Pros

Fast, fluid combat refined to near perfection
Incredible boss fights with unforgettable music
Beautifully designed environments full of atmosphere
Massive replayability with builds and New Game Plus
A high point of FromSoftware’s RPG design

Cons

Online play is unreliable and plagued by hackers
Steep difficulty and hidden lore can alienate newcomers
UI feels dated and lacks modern accessibility options
Occasional frame pacing and stability issues on PC

Final Score: 8.6 / 10

Dark Souls III is a brutal masterpiece that rewards persistence and skill. Its combat, atmosphere, and bosses remain unmatched in the genre, and it stands as one of FromSoftware’s finest achievements. While online play and technical stability remain weak points, the single-player experience alone makes it an essential RPG.

CPU: Intel Core i5-13600K (13th Gen, 20 CPUs)
GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4080 SUPER 16 GB VRAM
RAM: 32 GB

🔥 “The final flame burns brightest before the age ends—and Dark Souls III is an inferno of pure, unrelenting peak.” 🔥

There are endings…
…and then there is Dark Souls III—a swan song so devastatingly beautiful, so unapologetically punishing, and so godlike in execution, that it doesn't just close a trilogy—it ascends it into myth.

This isn’t a game. It’s a cathedral built from ash, steel, and shattered dreams. You don’t just play Dark Souls III…
You kneel before it.

⚔️ Combat Refined by Fire

From the deliberate brutality of the original to the frantic precision of Bloodborne, DS3 fuses it all into flawless flow-state warfare. Every swing is poetry. Every dodge is survival. Every boss fight is a duel against destiny. You want to feel like a god-hunting warlord wearing the sins of the world? This is where it happens.

Weapons don’t just hit—they sing. Weapon Arts add a layer of finesse that turns combat into a ballet of blades, and every build feels viable, deadly, and delicious. Faith builds? Nuke enemies with lightning from the gods. Dex builds? Slice faster than their screams. STR builds? Become the earthquake.

🏰 Worlds Forged from Nightmares & Dreams

Lothric is decay incarnate—a land crumbling under its own legacy. Every area bleeds lore, atmosphere, and pain. From the desolate grandeur of Irithyll to the soul-splitting horror of the Ringed City, DS3 flexes its level design harder than anything before.

You’ll walk through castles of light, catacombs of rot, and cities that remember the ages before you. And when you look up at a burning sun or down into a festering abyss, you’ll realize: this world is dying—and you are its final hope and final curse.

🧠 Lore So Deep It Rewrites Itself

You thought you understood Lordran?
You thought the Age of Fire was just a cycle?

Wrong.

Dark Souls III doesn’t explain—it echoes. And in those echoes, you find answers, tragedies, betrayals, and truths older than the flame itself. Every NPC, every item, every environment is part of a masterfully woven funeral dirge for a dying world—and you're the last mourner.

🎵 Music That Haunts Your Soul

Yuka Kitamura and Motoi Sakuraba summoned literal gods when they composed this OST. Every boss theme is a soul-rending masterpiece, from the tragic swell of the Abyss Watchers to the divine apocalypse of Sister Friede’s final phase.

💀 DLC? Nah. These Are Final Bosses of Reality.

Ashes of Ariandel and The Ringed City are not expansions.
They are eulogies, apocalypses, divine punishments wrapped in fire and filth. Slave Knight Gael isn’t a boss. He’s the wrath of mankind given flesh. And when you face him, it’s not just a battle. It’s an ending.

🛐 Verdict: 11/10 – A Glorious End to a Glorious Age

Dark Souls III is not just the best in the series—it’s the culmination of everything FromSoftware has ever stood for. It is a masterstroke, a requiem, a final prayer screamed into the void. And when the credits roll and the flame fades, you won’t feel empty…

You’ll feel reborn.

"Ashen one, hearest thou my voice still?"

Yes. Always. 🖤
Praise the damn Sun. ☀️
And embrace the End.

Positives:

Literally everything except the slightly linear level design. I don't have to write a huge essay about the positives, as nearly everything is a positive.

Negatives:

The slightly linear level design. While it looks great, the actual route through every area feels more linear than other From Software games, and it's the weakest in the Dark Souls triology. And even if I put this under negative, just because I say it's the weakest of the Dark Souls games still leaves it in the top 0.1% in terms of games world design overall. The worst designed world by From Software standards is still better than 99.9% of games you will ever play. The only reason why I even created a negative section is because it would have broken my own rules for reviewing anything to not do so, and to find anything to complain about I had to go into the realms of extreme nitpicking.


I almost never write a short review like this, but the thing is, Dark Souls 3 is the ultimate Dark Souls experience, there is almost nothing at all to complain about. I may rate Dark Souls a bit higher as I enjoy the world and atmosphere more, but Dark Souls 3 is the perfect Dark Souls experience. It's 100% worth the money at full price, even if it was 80€/$80 it would be a bargain.

If I were to give it a points score, Dark Souls 3 would land in the 95 out of a 100 points range, it really is that good. Just... Play... It.

Dark Souls III is quite simply my favorite game of all time. It was my very first Souls experience, and it immediately pulled me into a genre I didn’t even know I was missing. The sense of accomplishment after overcoming such a demanding challenge was nothing short of a revelation. The genius mechanic of retrieving your lost souls, with the looming risk of losing them forever on death, perfectly embodies the tension and reward at the heart of the game.

Beyond the gameplay, I was struck by the universe itself. I’m not usually drawn to medieval settings, but the way Dark Souls III blends that atmosphere with a fantastical and almost Lovecraftian dimension was breathtaking. Every area felt like a world unto itself, steeped in mystery, despair, and beauty. The environmental storytelling is second to none, pulling you deeper without ever needing to over-explain.

This game is a monument in video game history and one that will forever remain in my heart. With Dark Souls III, I discovered not only the kind of player I am, but also the kind of games I truly want to play. It was a transformative experience, the one that defined me as a gamer.

TLDR: A great entry to Soulsborne that is plagued by small technical issues and a price that never seems to drop for a decade old game. Still a good experience and should not be missed if you enjoy the Dark Souls universe.

➕Legendary world design
➕Intense combat
➕Good online player interactions
➕Benefits for ember vs humanity (DS1/2)


➖Targeting/lockon is generally awful and gives poor camera angles
➖FPS drops/stuttering
➖Subtle narrative and terrible quest “Tracking”
➖Poise is almost completely useless (aside from hyper armor)
➖Double-bound keybinds are SO ANNOYING (forward R1 kick when you mean to attack)

Series reviews:
DS1: /



Dark Souls, to me, is a series renowned for its excellent level design, and that tradition continues here. Especially when coming from DS1, you’ll end up seeing many familiar locations that bring a great feeling of nostalgia. The levels encompass the pinnacle of design for a “Dark fantasy” genre. It's grim, it's brutal, and aside from looks, the levels function exceptionally well. There are many twists and turns, shortcuts, connections, and paths with the game never holding your hand and telling you where to go. The world conveys a level of hopelessness with moments of triumph each time a player beats an area/boss like few other games can compare.

The narrative is….very subtle. I’ve mentioned before in reviews that nobody really plays the Soulsborne series for the narrative, but after DS3, I feel like that’s kind of a half-truth. There is extensive lore hidden in this world with the flames of a dying world, but the traditional part of Souls is that it never really helps the player “Properly” learn the story. Everything is hidden in item descriptions, vague dialogue, and confusing cutscenes. So while I did enjoy reading the lore of places like Untended Graves, I had to go out of my way to learn things about it via the wiki. Information is not well contained in game, and furthermore any sort of quest tracking is just not a thing, so it's absurdly easy to miss small steps in quests or places NPCs may be.

Another famous aspect of Soulsborne is its combat, and while DS3 does mostly deliver on this, I am left feeling like the combat is at some weird middle ground between Dark Souls traditional “Weighty” combat and Bloodborne’s excessively fast speed combat. At times some enemies are way too fast for this game, while the player is too slow. A good example is the spear and shield Ghru enemies. Their 5-fold combo attack is just so fast and with tracking, that a player has no ability to roll between hits and must outrange it or face numerous hits. Another example is the Boreal Outrider Knights and their insane aggressiveness. I think DS3 would do good from a 10-20% speed decrease in most enemies.
Aside from that, the combat is good and fun. I played as both a 2h strength and Sellsword Twinblades (Dex) builds. I found that each build has its advantages and disadvantages in certain parts of the game which seems like a good balance. There are some enemies and bosses that were a joke with strength and others I couldn’t beat easily, vice versa for Dex. I like how DS3 gives you more freedom to experiment with builds via limited respects versus having to restart the game.

Needless to say, this is a very difficult game. The difficulty is somewhat helped by embering and summoning players/NPCs for boss fights, but not all bosses have an NPC, and if no players are there waiting for help, you may be stuck for a while. Even aside from the bosses, there are some absolutely brutal treks between safe points (bonfires). With no difficulty slider, other than the jokes for health, this is a very difficult experience.

Graphically, DS3 is the best of the trilogy. Everything looks good, though at this point it is almost a 10 year old game, so keep that in mind. It’s fairly cemented as looking like a PS4-era game.

I experienced very poor technical quality with DS3. Perhaps it's because I preferred to play mostly on windowed, but it was terrible at points with stutters. I didn’t have these issues and played the same way with DS1 and DS2.
Also, I encountered numerous bugs like smacking enemies into walls and being shot/hit through walls.
Also, this might be more of a mechanical issue, but the camera and targeting is generally awful. With bosses/enemies flopping around and flying all over your screen combined with your lock on range being like 10 feet, there’s soooo many hits I took due to poor camera angles and unlocking targets.
i9-13900KF | RTX 4090 | 64GB RAM | 1440p @ 240hz | Windows 11 installed on SSD

Good audio - music ranges from generally silent while exploring areas to a full-blow orchestral explosion during bosses, creating some epic moments, but nothing that really stuck in my head.

There is good voice acting present, all dialog is fully voiced.

Some good replay value with NG+. You can change your build around a few times per playthrough and experiment as well.
Aside from NG+ though, the experience would be the same, just with harder enemies/higher number values.

Dark Souls 3 is probably my favorite of the DS series. DS1 is starting to show its age, and DS2 is good, but a significant departure from DS1. While DS3 is definitely not without its own issues, I feel that it offers a solid experience, one that is steeped with nostalgic tie-ins from DS1.

The most glaring issues to me in DS3 are the technical issues of stuttering, targeting, camera issues, as well as the bugs with walls/collision. The camera issues and targeting are annoying and range from a non-issue to almost game-breaking, depending on the enemy encounter. Nameless King or Old Demon King come to mind offering terrible targeting and camera views. I died so many times simply because I couldn’t view the enemy enough which led me to just roll spamming.
And the stuttering? I can’t figure out why the game is like this but it's terrible, every ~15-30s I get a half second pause. It may not sound like a lot, but in intense combat where every millisecond counts for dodging, it's super noticeable.

Overall, yes I would recommend DS3, but the technical issues I experienced brought it from an 8 to a 7 for me. It’s a fantastic world, deep lore, and more quality-of-life than previous entries that keep me coming back, playing multiple times, and trying out new things. I still think that Elden Ring is better, but DS3 is not to be slept on.


The price for this game is frankly ridiculous. An almost 10-year old game should not still be $60. Also, the DLC is still equally expensive. I would strongly recommend only getting this on sale, but even then it rarely goes under $20.

Dark Souls 3 is my favorite Souls game. It strikes the perfect balance not too difficult, but definitely not too easy either. It hits that sweet spot where the challenge feels fair and rewarding, making it a great middle ground compared to the rest of the series.

In my opinion, it’s the best game to start with if you're new to Souls games. The combat is fast and responsive, the boss designs are incredible, and the world is dark, mysterious, and beautifully connected. It teaches you the core mechanics without overwhelming you right away, and there's a nice flow to the difficulty curve.

What I really appreciate is how it respects your time. You can make steady progress without feeling stuck for hours, but it still gives that classic Souls sense of accomplishment when you overcome a tough area or boss.

Overall, Dark Souls 3 is a fantastic entry point and a solid masterpiece in its own whether you're new to the series or a returning fan.

Posted: August 22
This is the game that broke me.

Heed my tale and be warned, for this is no mere game you gaze upon.

I have played and beaten the Dark Souls games of yore. When I approached the third installment I believed, as many did, that I knew the path ahead of me.

I thought myself prepared. I believed myself worthy of the challenge.

I knew not the danger.

Heed my story. Heed my warning.

I'm a veteran gamer with numerous decades of experience behind me and this game still managed to burn me out. The Ashes of Ariandel DLC was the most difficult video game experience of my life. The final fight with Sister Friede is the most overwhelming boss fight in the entire trilogy by far. Anyone who buys this game needs to get that DLC and try it for themselves. Get them both, actually. You can't say you "beat Dark Souls" until you've beaten Sister Friede. I don't think I've ever experienced such a degree of shock and exuberation after overcoming anything else in a game before.

So imagine my despair after finally taking her down only to realize that the journey had just begun, that the fight with her was only the end of the first chapter. The Ringed City DLC was just beyond.

I stared into the abyss. I had no words.

After getting through the main game and suffering through The Painted World of Ariandel I soldiered on through The Ringed City DLC, thinking that my pain was finally coming to an end.

I approached the gateway to the final boss when it occurred to me to go online and check to be sure I had discovered all the areas and beaten all the bosses, just in case. To my horror I discovered that there was...

One. More. Level.

One hidden area of the world map that I'd never found: Archdragon Peak.

I nearly cried.

I pushed through the area and got to its boss: The Nameless King. He killed me handily, of course, as most bosses do on the first encounter. But after a few cracks at the King I just...broke.

I couldn't go on.

I had gotten through the entire game with only The Nameless King and the final boss before me. Just two boss fights and I would be done. But I just...couldn't. I couldn't muster the strength to carry on and I just dropped the game.

I kept telling myself that I'd go back. Maybe in a few weeks. Maybe a few months. I'd eventually get to it, I thought. But the very phrase "Dark Souls" made me sick. I just couldn't do it. It's like I had eaten ninety-nine pancakes and I only needed to eat one more to finally break the World Record.

But I couldn't bring myself to open the game. The game had completely and totally broken me.

I had moved on to other games in the meantime but Dark Souls 3 was always lurking in my library, gnawing at the back of my mind.

After a year and five months I was considering opening up a new title when it occurred: I needed to finish Dark Souls. I couldn't let it sit anymore. It had been too long.

So I did it. I opened the game. I picked up the controller.

I wasn't expecting much. I took on The Nameless King and poked around for a weakness or a sustainable strategy. I found one and nearly killed him on the second try. My spirit was back. After only a few more attempts I managed it. I took on the final boss and he wasn't bad at all.

After nearly a year and a half and two hundred hours...I had finally done it. I had beaten Dark Souls 3.

What an unbelievable ride. A thing of legends.

This game is truly one of the best games I've ever played and I think it will go down as one of the greatest games of all time. It's an absolute masterpiece.

It is traumatizing and exhausting. It is thrilling and beautiful. It will extinguish your soul and then reignite it.

This game will make you into a man.

Buy this game if you quest to become a True Gamer, one worthy of the title.

But be warned: you will never be the same man again.

DARK SOULS 3

Combat – 7.5/10
Combat in DS3 is smooth and responsive, giving you a strong sense of control. The fights feel clean and satisfying. That said, the game removed much of the build freedom from DS2, which is disappointing, there’s less choice in how you approach combat. Despite that, the core combat is excellent, and it’s easily the most refined of the series, so it earns a high score.

Bosses – 6/10
DS3 has decent bosses overall, with interesting designs and encounters. That said, some fights can be incredibly frustrating, sometimes not unfair, but annoying enough to make you consider quitting. The variety and creativity are there, but the inconsistency keeps it from being truly great.

World/Level Design – 3/10
DS3 largely reuses the world from DS1, updating areas and changing some details. While I appreciate it and understand the story context, it feels very familiar and doesn’t take full advantage of the series’ potential. With the concept of time and location shifts, I wish they had included DS2 locations or completely new areas unconnected to previous games. It’s competent, but far from the creativity of earlier entries.

Atmosphere – 5/10
The atmosphere is fine, but nothing stands out. I didn’t feel nearly as immersed as I did in DS1 or DS2. It’s competent, but it lacks the memorable vibe and emotional pull of the earlier games.

Difficulty – 5/10
Some bosses can be incredibly frustrating. A standout example is the Twin Princes, combining multiple gimmicks into a single fight felt unnecessary and annoying. While the game has its tough moments, the difficulty is inconsistent and sometimes leans more toward frustration than challenge.

Replayability – 5/10
Replay value depends on the player. There are multiple endings, including a secret one for those interested, but beyond that, there’s not much to drive repeat playthroughs. It’s decent, but not as compelling as DS1 or DS2 for replaying.

Performance/Controls – 7/10
DS3 has the best movement and controls in the series. Everything feels smooth, responsive, and precise, making combat and exploration much more enjoyable compared to the previous entries.

Overall Score – 5.5/10
Dark Souls 3 has the most refined combat and controls in the series, but it falls short in world design, atmosphere, and boss consistency. While enjoyable and polished, it doesn’t quite capture the magic or originality of DS1 or the world-building and freedom of DS2. It’s worth playing, but some design choices may leave veterans wanting more.

Easily the worst of the Dark Souls Trilogy but by no means a bad video game, Dark Souls III is a complicated animal. This is probably the only Dark Souls game to play now in 2025 if you have no experience with the series as 1. the stories aren't really related at all so release order literally doesn't matter, and 2. if you have played Elden Ring, this game is the only one that will not feel like walking through the Boston Molasses Flood in comparison. Dark Souls III did a lot of new and innovative things while largely abandoning many new features, story beats, and world building ideas introduced in Dark Souls II, most of which were revived in Elden Ring and now garner praise as key aspects of that experience. This game looks amazing, but is just as guilty, if nor more so of things like artificial spikes in difficulty, creating difficulty by throwing more mobs or even blatantly unfair mechanical challenges at the player, as Dark Souls 2. It's a decent game and if you can't stomach the much slower pace, older graphics and lower frame rates of 1&2 this is the game to play to get what "X is the Dark Souls of Y" means through more than just context.
Solid 7/10 because I played all of these games when they came out and actually remember the experiences and those feelings were reinforced by playing the entire trilogy this summer.

I did encounter 2 performance issues. While not making the game unplayable, they were hindering the experience slightly. Luckily, I was able to find fixes for both.

Issues encountered:
1. Area loading stutters - not terrible, but very noticeable and did ruin the immersion when they occurred.
2. Sound overlapping issues - sometimes ambient audio would restart or certain audio cues would be cut short. (This issue only happened after installing the DLC's)

Fixes found:
1.
- After launching the game, open Task Manager
- Find "DarkSoulsIII.exe" in the Details tab
- Right click and select "Set affinity"
- Unselect CPU 0 and click OK
- Open "Set affinity" again
- Reselect CPU 0 and click OK
- Tab back into the game and it should be fixed (this has to be done with each launch, but it's very quick)

2. Disabling any unused audio channels in windows completely fixed this issue. I had multiple audio sources and DS3 just didn't like that.

All in all, .

Posted: August 13
Things I liked:
-the story
-the gameplay
-the enemies designs and world
-the return of older locations
-the endings
-the ability to turn off online and play offline

Things I didn't like:
-the lack of cloud saving
-the lack of bonfire aesthetics
-the linearity
-some extremely annoying areas
-the jailers
-the teleporting dogs
-the lack of location variety
-performance
-the covenant item grind

I'd give this game a 8.5/10. There are a lot of things that I liked about this game and a lot I disliked about this game, however I generally loved my experience with Dark Souls III. I feel like a lot of my critique is self-explanatory, so I will not waste your time restating all of that information. While I think I enjoyed Dark Souls III more, I do miss the exploration of Dark Souls II. Exploration and hidden secrets are obviously still in the game, however this game is a lot more linear compared to Dark Souls or Dark Souls II. For that reason, I feel a lot more willing to replay either of the previous entries compared to this one. I think the addition of bonfire aesthetics would help with the ring grind, however questlines are more complicated in this game so I could see why they wouldn't work. Regardless of whether or not the bonfire aesthetics are in the game, you would still need to play through this game a total of three times to see every ending. My last talking point is the performance. The performance in this game is strange. There is a lot of framerate dips and vsync issues and I am not alone. These are extremely common issues in this game with no definitive way to fix them. You can make you situation bearable but not completely fixed. It is frustrating and insulting that these issues have not been fixed and they are still asking for $60. It doesn't help that Dark Souls Remastered and Dark Souls III reportedly still have hacking issues in multiplayer. I know I focused a lot on the negatives and I only tend to do that when its a game I love or hate and thankfully I was really close to loving this game.

Posted: August 13
I went into Dark Souls III thinking I was prepared. I’d heard all the stories about its difficulty, its cryptic world, its “git gud” culture. I figured I could handle it. How hard could it be?

The first few hours humbled me instantly. Every enemy felt like it had been sent specifically to test my patience. I’d block one attack, only to get stabbed in the back by something I didn’t even see. The bonfires felt miles apart, and dying meant watching all my progress slip away. It was punishing, but never unfair. Every mistake was mine.

My most vivid memory? The fight against Pontiff Sulyvahn. I went in cocky, thinking I’d take him down after a few tries. Instead, he shredded me. Over and over. I learned his moves, then got greedy and paid for it. I walked away from the game for two days, frustrated. But something pulled me back, that stubborn urge to prove I could do it. On the night I finally beat him, my hands were shaking. I didn’t even realize I’d been holding my breath until the victory screen appeared. The feeling wasn’t just relief, it was triumph.

That’s Dark Souls III in a nutshell. It’s not just a game, it’s an experience that demands patience, observation, and persistence. The world is hauntingly beautiful, crumbling castles, frozen valleys, and twisted cathedrals, all telling fragments of a story you have to piece together yourself. The soundtrack swells in the most intense battles, making victories feel almost cinematic.

Yes, it’s hard. Yes, you will die, a lot. But each time you get up, you get better. You start to see patterns you missed before. You start to trust yourself. And when you finally overcome what seemed impossible hours ago… there’s nothing quite like it.

Dark Souls III didn’t just test my reflexes; it tested my patience, my determination, and my ability to keep going when everything told me to quit. And honestly? I’m glad it did.

If you’re willing to embrace the challenge, the reward is worth every moment of frustration.

10/10 — Would die, and rise again, countless times.

Verily, the journey through the decaying lands of Lothric proved to be a formidable and rather commendable undertaking. The very essence of the realm, though steeped in ash and despair, held a certain allure, drawing one deeper into its somber embrace.

The clash of steel and the dance of evasion, while not without its familiar rhythms, offered a serviceable challenge. One might find their rhythm against the wretched denizens, though the precision of engagement felt, at times, merely adequate. Yet, the true marvel lay within the bountiful armories that the dying world presented. A discerning warrior, regardless of their chosen path, could find an implement suited to their grim purpose, for the variety of blades, hammers, and strange contraptions was truly vast. Moreover, the arcane arts and divine blessings offered a diverse tapestry of enchantments, allowing one to imbue their chosen instrument with potent, unholy, or miraculous power.

A particular boon, much appreciated by those who seek to embody the might of fallen champions, was the ability to reclaim and reforge the very souls of vanquished lords into formidable armaments and resolute raiment. These trophies, born of triumph over daunting foes, served not only as symbols of victory but also as potent tools in the relentless pursuit of one's grim destiny.

Alas, even in a world shadowed by fading flames, flaws may yet be found. The staunch shield, once a stalwart companion against crushing blows, now feels but a cumbersome adornment. Its promised riposte, a swift counter-attack after a guarded strike, remains stubbornly elusive. Furthermore, the spear-wielder, accustomed to a quick thrust from behind their iron visage after a parried assault, finds such an action tragically denied. This singular failing renders the defensive bulwark far less practical in the relentless skirmishes that define this accursed land.

Despite this minor vexation concerning the utility of the bulwark, the overarching experience of Lothric's twilight was undeniably magnificent. The flame, though fading, casts a captivating glow.

A few years after its release, I gave this game a shot, drawn in by the hype. What I found was pure frustration. No tutorial, a cryptic story, and a first boss that felt just to punishing to be true. I quit.

Then in 2019, I saw it on sale and thought, why not? I bought it, gave it another chance… and everything changed.
Since then, it’s become one of my top three favorites (out of a library of over 800 titles). I’ve finished it multiple times, explored New Game+, and kept coming back for more.

Now it’s 2025. I upgraded my PC, as I do every few years, and discovered my progress was gone. No Cloud Sync support, which I’d say is its only flaw.

But I didn’t mind. I started over, and once again, I was hooked. Nearly a decade later, it still feels fresh, still surprises me, still pulls me in. And I know I’ll keep returning to it.

A jewel among jewels.

This is maybe the best game I've ever played, otherwise definitely among them. This is every strong point of the series without the glaring flaws from the previous two. No development rush that ruined aspects of the game, no useless areas, and minimal ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Yes, the game is hard. But not unfair like the previous two. It is challenging, but rewarding, and exhilarating. A masterpiece of game design and art direction.

The combat is satisfying and generally never straight up unfair. The weapons are well designed and allow for many unique playstyles. The character stats don't have some of the bs from ds2/ds1. Super customizable and fun challenging gameplay which ties perfectly into the Dark Souls narrative of earning your place through honed skill.

The art direction is unmatched. One of the most beautiful games I have ever had the pleasure of experiencing. Mesmerizing sights from various vantage points, unbelievable level design and world design, beautiful paintings, intricate architecture, unique and intriguing enemy and NPC designs.

I could go on, but there is little to be said besides that this game is nearly flawless, in my opinion. At least it improves unimagineably from the previous two such that I could never genuinely dislike this game. It is the best dark souls, by far. And it is the most beautiful one. Oh and did I mention the OST? Because it has some of the best music in all of video game history. Hands down. Main title theme and ending credits theme are absolute masterpieces.

I have just today finally finished the game, the first one I've beat out of the three souls, after hundreds of hours in the series and many hundreds more in Elden Ring, Sekiro and Bloodborne. Playing through this game after nearly finishing DS1 and playing through a decent amount of DS2 is insane. The amount of lore built up, of recurring themes and symbolisms.

All this to say that this is, without the shadow of a doubt, peak.
Ashen one, hearest thou my voice, still?
