Posted: August 7
Dark Souls III is a game that solidified that I do, in fact, like Dark Souls. I was unsure, after having a generally positive opinion of the first game, if i would enjoy the Souls series as a whole - and I'm honestly happy to report that, in fact, I do. The wonderful flow of combat, the feeling of mastery when you actually dodge a hard attack, the general rush and variety available to you is wonderful - while it was hard, and punishing at times, it was generally a fun blast, and I felt very happy with the time I spent.

The level design is a stark improvement over DS2 - even the shorter levels were far more involved than the single corridors of Heide's Tower of Flame, and while some zones were just... painful (Farron Keep and Irithyll Dungeons spring to mind), the vast majority were both aesthetically interesting and nicely complex, most finding that sweet spot for me between too short and too long. Enemies... well, regular enemies, were a mixed bag. Playing as a dedicated Reaper user (because i thought Friede's Scythe was cool AF), the swarms of rats and other smaller greeblies that would interrupt my attacks were a pain, but most of the rest of the enemies were solid and good fun. Knights especially were hard at first, but fun once you figure them out. That being said, some enemies (Like crabs, jailers, elder ghru) were an absolute nightmare of suffering and frustration. Especially jailers, screw those guys.

Boss wise, i was originally a bit concerned, given how many less bosses this game had compared to DS2, but they did not skip on the quality in comparison. We don't have a Dragonriders, Twin Dragonriders situation here. True, there are some rather dull and easy gimmick bosses like Ancient Wyvern, Curse-Rotted Greatwood and High Lord Wolnir - the vast majority were exciting, fully fleshed-out fights with interesting dodge patterns and involved attacks. My standout favorites were Dancer, Nameless King part 2, and Soul of Cinder - but Gael deserves an honourable mention, same as Friede. Going toe-to-toe by myself, going from angry, to frustrated, to calm and ready was a wonderful experience. I learned, and when i overcame that boss that had been giving me trouble, it was almost a euphoric feeling. I've come a long way, and it feels earned.

DS3 is the best souls title i've thusfar played. Granted, I haven't played the original, but - it would be hard for that to stack up to this. While at times the game can feel a bit... needlessly punishing with runbacks or with enemies specifically designed to push you off of ledges (which is ass, that is not a good bit of game design FromSoft), the actual problems were a lot less than I was dealing with before. I'm excited to see what the other titles are like, and see if this one is truly the peak of Souls - or if it's simply an amazing expression of a difficult, but rewarding, journey.

9.4/10. That’s right: I cheaped out and gave it the same rating as my review for Elden Ring because I can’t decide what I liked better. Dark Souls 3 was much better than Dark Souls 1 in most respects (especially visuals and bosses) and keeps what Dark Souls 1 did better than Elden Ring, so… really, it should be higher than Elden Ring but somehow I can’t get there, mostly because I now think I rated Elden Ring too low (it should be at least a 9.6, but maybe that’s the nostalgia talking).

I played a med roll dex/faith sharp two-hand build focused on either Lothric Straight Sword or Sellsword Twinblades (I switched off Twinblades mostly because I heard it was too Winblades but then realized after that LSS is also OP, lol). Wore stam backpack and did FAP/Havel/Estus/Phys. Reduction or Silver Serpent ring or stam ring. Wore Lothric Knight Armor/heavy greaves/gauntlets and also went no-helmet the entire game (mostly because fashion souls and also as a further challenge – apparently this has a disproportionate impact on your resistances), no summons (obviously), no ember (mostly).

The Good:

The gameplay, obviously. I’m not going to explain why it’s good – for that, check out my Dark Souls 1 review – so I’m just going to go into what I thought it did better than DS1. I’d describe it as near-Elden Ring customization and build variety and thought that there was a nicely varied number of zones (more populated too, imo) that were fascinating to go through. It’s harder than DS1 in terms of the actual overworld as well as in terms of bosses, which I appreciated, and I would describe the balancing as mostly fair (outside of the DLC areas which I thought were atrocious).

Basically, everything I said about DS1 and how I prefer the style over Elden Ring’s still applies – just now with better and more varied bosses, more interesting NPCs, cooler enemy designs, and much, much better graphics. I kind of appreciated how the game also put you right in the middle of it without the usual opener of fighting baby enemies, with the High Wall of Lothric and the Lothric Knights. I also liked the voice acting a lot more in this game and the boss designs were far more interesting to me than DS1 and rivaled the excellent design in Elden Ring. Finally, as the god-gamer who beat Promised Consort Radahn Week 1 (which I’m obligated to flex in every Elden Ring-adjacent review), I thought the difficulty in this game was perfect in that the challenge was mostly in the bosses and not the overworld, and most if not all the bosses felt really fair – there honestly weren’t that deaths to me that felt undeserved, which is shocking coming from DS1 cheesiness and Elden Ring random two-shots.

The Bad (Trying to be objective):

Some of the design choices that I disliked in DS1 are back, and – shockingly – I still dislike them, including the invasion system (which is just griefing imo), some annoying enemy placements, the camera targeting for flying enemies (I just finished Midir today wow that boss sucks camera-wise), the placing important stuff behind illusionary walls (like, why?), the overly-convoluted NPC questlines (I only did Anri’s out of all the hard ones basically), the kind of strange progression (getting 4 cinders which you only start getting like 60-70% of the game, basically I felt as if I was progressing through zones and fighting the boss at the end and reaching a new zone with no real idea why or what was going on), the covenant system (makes no sense and doesn’t really do anything), the Ember stuff and the need to be Embered to summon (which also opens you up to be griefed – oops, “invaded”), the poison swamp/overly cheesy zones like the ballista in the Smouldering Lake…

The Ugly (YMWV):

Hmmm… not much? I think my main problem is that the plot to me was utterly incoherent – which sucked, because watching the lore videos was super interesting and I thought the overall world/plotlines were cool once I understood them. I also think that the game was overall too murky/monochromatic in its color scheme, where every zone seemed a bit washed out/undersaturated and a bit dull, especially compared to the vibrancy of DS1 and Elden Ring – which might be an aesthetic choice, but it wasn’t one I particularly liked. I thought the bonfire placement (with having a generous amount compared to previous ones) and less of a runback for the large majority of bosses were welcome changes in that it removes artificially increasing the difficulty with frustration measures, but I’m not sure if I’m in the minority there or if that’s a hot take. What I suspect is a hot take is the use of Embers, which I firmly believe is cheesing and was surprised to find out that the sentiment online is that popping an Ember to fight bosses is perfectly okay (hur dur “boss has a 2nd phase? My second phase is the Ember B)"). Using Embers felt really, really cheap and cheapened any bosses that I killed with them – like when I struggled with Midir, ran out of flasks, thought “hey the boss is 1/3rd HP let me pop an Ember just to see more of his patterns”, then survived an unexpectedly long time before staggering him and basically taking a quarter of his HP with a crit. Nameless King I also beat right away after using an Ember, and the victory felt… hollow (heh). Ringed City and the other DLC were way too challenging overworld-wise imo, annoyingly so. Finally, the constant callbacks to DS1 I actually enjoyed for the most part (since I really liked DS1) but I can see why some people don’t like how this seems kind of like a “greatest hits” Dark Souls – the only parts that really made me (fat) roll my eyes were the Soul of Cinder fight which was just Gwyn again or Anor Londo which somehow felt way more on the nose than the other references.

And now, the rankings:

Bosses based on how much I liked (honestly the average boss I thought was good): Friede >> Gael >>>> Pontiff >> Nameless King >>> Abyss Watchers >>> Champion Gundyr >> Twin Princes >> Soul of Cinder >>>> Demon Prince >>>> Aldrich >>> (All other bosses not mentioned, as in, they were all decent/alright) >>>Midir >>>>Halflight>>Oceiros >>> Wolnir >>Crystal Sage>>Greatwolf>> >> Deacons >>>>>>>>> Curse-Rotted Greatwood

Hardest bosses: Friede >>>>>>> Twin Princes (I know) >>> Midir >> Gael >>>> Nameless King >>>> Demon Prince >> Soul of Cinder >> Aldrich >>> Gravetender/Greatwolf >>> Ancient Wyvern (I know) >>> Champion Gundyr >>> Pontiff >>>> Dragonslayer >>> Everything else >>>>>Oceiros>> Wolnir>> Halflight

Best Zone: High Wall of Lothric probably or Archives or Undead Settlement

Worst Zone: the Jailor dungeon, followed by Ringed City (which actually made me stop playing for a while), poison swamp, Anor Londo, or Profaned Capital or Consumed King’s thing or Smoldering Lake in no particular order (wow I’m realizing I hate a lot of the zones)

Posted: August 3
The souls games always improve from the previous version. I went from DS1 to DS3 since DS2 seems to be universally hated.

I 100% DS1 in 153 hours, DS3 in 199. That does include the DLCs as well.

DS3 is a lot of fun and allows for differences in play styles depending on how you want to play it. Boss fights are challenging with more than 1 way to beat them for the most part depending on your playstyle. The Bosses are unique and many have me wanting to go and fight them again just for the fun of it.
The Min/Max options work great with a range of weapons encouraging players to try different builds. Boss soul weapons/spells range from worthless to wanting to change my playstyle to fit this weapon/spell in somehow.
Timing for parrying is crucial so go with a wired or 2.7Ghz wireless controller, don’t bother with bluetooth it’s too slow. I did some parrying but gave up since some enemies have an annoyingly long delay to their attack so I opted to dodge and slash with my build.

The DLCs are fun, though I will say the Ring City is the better one IMO. The final fight is truly epic and fun.

The only gripes I have are these:
Lock-on breaks way too easily. I have had fights where I move/rotate behind a pillar and the lock-on breaks. So when I dodge I change direction right into the enemy’s attack. I have taken so much unnecessary damage from lock-on breaking so easily. Also some boss animations will take the lock-on point too far or high up and break lock-on as well.
Animations with delayed attacks. Some attacks have delay animations and it can be tough to see when you need to roll or parry. I recall just dealing with this on the nameless king with some of his phase 2 attacks.

Overall these are not game breaking changes, just things to note if you start playing. I’m glad I got through the game and went through to reach 100%.

Posted: August 3
Trigger warning: This review includes a long rant about Dark Souls III pricing because it really grinds my gears. If you’re a die-hard fan who thinks DS3 is flawless and worth sacrificing your soul, house, and both legs just to play it, you might want to grab an Estus flask, a Dr. Pepper, or whatever helps you chill before diving in.

If you want a little more discussion around the game see below. Otherwise,

In a nutshell - the good:

- Exploration is fantastic. While the level design doesn’t quite reach the interconnected brilliance of Dark Souls 1, it’s still top-tier. I spent about 55 hours completing the game and DLC on my first run, then blasted through it in 5 hours on NG+. That contrast really shows how much of the game’s magic comes from discovering new areas, secrets, optional bosses, and stunning scenery. Even if the loot isn't always rewarding in itself, the act of uncovering "hidden" things definitely is. Firelink Shrine is a great example fon that and my favorite Dark Souls Hub (even better than Majula yes)

- The soundtrack is phenomenal. It even surpasses previous titles (I mean just the menu theme).

- Art direction, boss design, enemy variety, and gameplay loop are all superb. FromSoftware truly mastered their formula in this game. The variety in enemy move sets and the depth of weapon mechanics, including the new weapon art system, add a lot of replayability. Trying different builds or trying to do challenge runs with various constraints forces you to adapt your playstyle each time which is very rewarding. There are already hours-long deep dives explaining why Dark Souls III and the Dark Souls formula is great so I will not spend too much time on this.


In a nutshell for the cons :

- Me no like pricing strategy
This is going to be a bit of a long rant because I have somewhat strong feelings about this, I find this game far too expensive and I really dislike FromSoftware’s (or I guess maybe Bandai Namco’s) pricing philosophy. From mid-2018 to early 2022, the Dark Souls III Deluxe Edition (which includes the base game and all DLC) was often available for around 15€ on official digital stores during. Which is definitely a good price for such a game even today and made the full experience accessible to a wider audience.

But something clearly changed in FromSoftware’s (or Bandai Namco’s) financial strategy. Since early 2022, the same edition has been locked at 60€ on official stores. And since June 2023, it has consistently stayed at that price or even higher, with rare and shallow discounts dropping it to 30–40€ during major sales for like, two days. This change feels blatantly exploitative. We're talking about a game released in 2016 that now costs more than it did when it was still relatively "current".

And FromSoft (or it's publisher Bandai Namco) is not some struggling indie studio. They have been thriving, especially with the rise in popularity of Soulslike games over the past few years. So when you're asked to pay 60€ (let’s be honest, most players will want the DLC since it is basically part of the full experience), for a nearly 10-year-old game, it feels absurd. I put about 70 hours into Dark Souls III and enjoyed it very much, but I cannot in good conscience support this kind of pricing. I would personally recommend getting it from a keyshop if you are on a budget (and I really f****ing hate keyshops), or better yet, just take the scenic route through the high seas. These practices do not deserve consumer support.

To make things worse, this isn’t limited to Dark Souls III. The same pattern has been seen with Dark Souls II, which also used to be regularly available for around 10€ or less with DLC on official stores, but now sits at 30€ most of the time. And then you look at Elden Ring, a much newer and larger game, which is often priced similarly or even lower than Dark Souls III (I got it for like 27 bucks on sales during summer sales). That pricing makes no sense at all. Why should an older game with fewer features and less content cost more than their latest title?

If you want to support the developers, absolutely buy Elden Ring on an official platform. But when it comes to Dark Souls III at €60, I just cannot endorse that kind of pricing. It feels like a blatant cash grab and I find it disrespectful to both long-time fans and newcomers. Dark Souls Remastered is also fairly priced (generally at 30 bucks and relatively often at 15 bucks on sales, both are worth it imo depending on your budget).

- I found certain passages or even whole areas (DLC mainly) quite annoying to explore because of the amount of ennemies and how much damage they dealt (and the fact that those are late game areas so the moveset are not the easiest to dodge) and I hate how Fromsoft sometimes tries to force you to fight multiple ennemies (sometimes you can aggro just one with a bow and deal with the group one by one but the further you go the more From tries to make that impossible and it is just dumb to me as the gameplay is atrocious when you have to deal with multiple opponents at once)

- As per usual one of your biggest enemy will be the camera, Some boss fights and cramped areas become messy simply because of poor camera behavior. Learning to avoid lock-on in these moments helps, but it's not ideal. Using claw grip might help too, but I am no Brachyura so ♥♥♥♥ that. On the bright side, hitboxes are much improved compared to DS1 and DS2, so unfair hits are far less common.




Final thoughts:

Dark Souls III is an amazing game. I played it right after DS1, DS2 and, of course, Lords of the Fallen (2014), and I can confidently say it’s my favorite in the trilogy. That doesn't mean it's superior in every way, though. DS1 still has the best level design, and DS2... well, maybe wins for fashion and dual wielding. But overall, DS3 was just a joy to play.

The boss fights, in particular, are where this game truly shines. While DS1 had mostly solid bosses with a few amazing ones (notably in the DLC) and DS2 struggled outside of its DLC bosses, DS3 delivers memorable boss encounters across the board. Even the weaker ones are at least visually and thematically engaging. Some fights may be mechanically simple, but their presentation, music, and lore elevate them. And the best ones are simply among my favorite fights in all of gaming and some of my peak experiences playing videogames.

I strongly recommend getting the DLC. The bosses are mostly excellent (even though I am not a big fan of Ashes of Ariandel I think it's definitely worth doing), and the new areas are beautiful, if sometimes frustrating in their design choices.

As a side note, the final boss of the last DLC isn’t just my favorite fight in the Souls series, it’s my favorite fight in any video game, period. I hope I'll have the luck to experience some similar fights in Elden Ring and other Souls like I'll get my hands on.

Dark Souls 3 | Perfect Game Review
(100% Completion at 87.6 Hours)

I booted up Dark Souls III thinking I was on some grand quest to link the flame—but most of the time, it felt like I was just flailing around trying to keep a cursed BBQ lit in a hurricane.

Gameplay: If you’re looking for a power fantasy, look elsewhere. Dark Souls III is all about learning from failure, dying repeatedly, and slowly chipping away at progress like you’re carving a statue with a spoon. But once it clicks, the combat feels genuinely rewarding and earned, which is rare these days. ★★★★★

Graphics: Dark, moody, and occasionally striking. Some areas are beautifully designed, others just feel like recycled ruins with extra gloom. It fits the vibe, sure—but Dark Souls III isn’t a world my eyes will be missing anytime soon. ★★★☆☆

Sound: Kicks in when it matters, and boss themes are memorable and hit hard. The cryptic voice acting fits the tone, but can feel a bit flat at times. Nonetheless, the music and ambient sounds carry the atmosphere. ★★★★☆

Story: There’s a plot and decent lore buried under cryptic item descriptions and vague NPC mumbling—but if you’re not into lore videos or reading everything twice, good luck. Put simply, you are told to kill some lords, and the rest is left to guesswork. Mysterious, but often feels unnecessarily confusing. ★★★☆☆

Tons of builds, secrets, and endings to chase, with NG+ adding some extra challenge. PvP is a hit-or-miss depending on matchmaking. Worth a second run if you enjoyed —or if you’re chasing achievements like I was… just be ready for the grind. ★★★★☆

Like other soulsborne games, demands patience, persistence, and a dash of masochism. When it clicks, it’s fantastic—but it doesn’t always respect your time, and some design choices feel downright unfair. However, there’s plenty to appreciate if you stick with it and the boss fights alone are worth picking up this game if you enjoy the soulsborne genre.

Revisiting Dark Souls III years later, I still consider it an excellent conclusion to the trilogy - nearly on par with the first game, though not quite as groundbreaking. It feels smoother and more refined than its predecessors, with improved combat, better visuals, and boss fights that are more varied and creative than in Dark Souls II. I also appreciated the return to a less linear world design, with clever shortcuts and plenty of optional areas to explore. Despite feeling a bit shorter and some bosses having frustratingly bloated health bars, I found it easier than I remembered - likely thanks to experience from the earlier games. While the original Dark Souls remains the high point of the series, Dark Souls III comes close, and revisiting it reminded me why this trilogy is considered a modern classic.

As a player who enjoys the Dark Souls series, I can fully recommend Dark Souls 3, both for veterans of the series and newcomers. Overall, the game feels like an effortless blend of the strengths of the other games in the series, without letting any of their weaknesses hold it back. Similar to DS1, there's incredibly strong level design that rewards curiosity and the joy of exploring every corner. The various weapons are well-balanced in terms of their strengths, a strength that was present in Dark Souls 2. The pace of the combat system has been increased compared to games 1 and 2.

Dark Souls 3 is also a feast for the senses. The world is both graphically and aesthetically impressive, and the sound design is by far the strongest in the series to date. (This only applies to Dark Souls, not Elden Ring, etc.) Every sword strike sounds strong, loud, and powerful; it simply feels good to strike an enemy while remaining extremely cautious in the face of every enemy blow. All of this comes together to create this incredibly well-functioning package, where sound and visuals are not just good in their own right, but actively enhance the atmosphere of the game world.

The Souls series is known for its difficulty. While many descriptions of this difficulty are exaggerated, there is definitely some truth to it. Dark Souls 3 is no exception in this regard; the game's difficulty is very comparable to other games in the series. The first boss is surprisingly tough and demands a lot from new players. If you get stuck here at the beginning, don't feel bad; I think it's perfectly normal. Just try out a few playstyles and maybe it will work out better then. One thing that is different from the other installments is that the final bosses in the game are actually the hardest. Traditionally, the hardest bosses in Souls games are often found in the middle of the story, while bosses towards the end are often easier. Not here; the hardest bosses are definitely the ones at the end and some optional ones that can only be fought towards the end of the game.

Of course, Dark Souls 3 isn't perfect.There is always room for improvement, but it is difficult for me to list them because I enjoyed the game very much. While I often find the level design very good, I have to say that Dark Souls 1 had a significantly more interesting game world with more interesting options to start with in the early game. Dark Souls 3 is significantly more linear in this regard, but still great.

Overall, the game is brilliant and definitely a recommendation.

Back when in 2017 I played Dark Souls 1 on a hacked PS3. I didn’t fully understand what I had in my hands, but something about it hooked me. The atmosphere, the music, the constant sense of danger… it was a world I always wanted to return to.

One year later, a friend let me try Dark Souls 3 on his PS4. I only played for a few hours, but it was enough to leave a mark. I dreamed about it. I searched for that same feeling in other games—but nothing came close.
A couple of weeks ago, after months of saving and finally having a decent PC, I’ve bought Dark Souls 3. I can’t express how happy I am. It’s more than just a game—it’s an experience, a masterpiece. Every fight, every corner, every sound… simply brilliant.

I’m starting over, this time fully, and I can’t wait to dive into the DLCs soon.

Thank you, FromSoftware. And if you’re still unsure about getting it: do it. It’ll change you.

Dark Souls III is a triumphant and emotional conclusion to the legendary series. It masterfully blends the refined mechanics of Dark Souls II with the faster-paced combat of Bloodborne, creating a gameplay experience that feels tight, aggressive, and incredibly satisfying. Every dodge, parry, and swing feels precise, and the boss battles are among the most cinematic and challenging in the series.

The world design is stunning—dark, gothic, and filled with decaying beauty. While not as interconnected as the original Dark Souls, each area still feels distinct and rich with lore. The game is full of callbacks and references to previous entries, giving longtime fans a bittersweet sense of closure. The soundtrack and visual storytelling enhance the feeling that this is the end of an age.

The lore is deep and tragic, building on the themes of cycles, fire, and fading gods. NPCs are fascinating and mysterious, and every item has a story behind it. Dark Souls III truly rewards players who seek to uncover its hidden depths.

That said, the game isn’t flawless. Some areas—especially later in the game—can feel rushed or less inspired. Enemy placement occasionally leans into unfair territory, and PvP balance can be inconsistent. Additionally, while the callbacks are meaningful for fans, newcomers might find some story elements hard to follow without context from earlier games.

Still, Dark Souls III stands as a powerful farewell to the series. It’s visually breathtaking, emotionally resonant, and brutally fun. For veterans and newcomers alike, it’s a must-play—and a fitting end to one of the greatest trilogies in gaming.

Dark Souls III generally has a very positive reputation. It is considered a strong entry in the series, often praised for its challenging gameplay, intricate level design, and rich lore. While it can be punishing, many players find the sense of accomplishment and exploration rewarding. Some criticisms include occasional performance issues, a few underwhelming boss fights, and a potential over-reliance on nostalgia.

Here’s a more detailed breakdown:

Positive Aspects
Challenging and Rewarding Gameplay:
Dark Souls III maintains the series’ signature difficulty, offering a strong sense of progression and accomplishment when overcoming obstacles.

Intricate Level Design:
The game world is praised for its interconnected environments and visually striking locations, even if some areas borrow from earlier titles.

Rich Lore and Atmosphere:
The game delves deeper into the established lore of the Dark Souls universe, creating a bleak yet captivating atmosphere.

Strong Boss Battles:
While some found a few boss fights underwhelming, many are lauded as among the best in the series.

Positive Reception:
Both critics and players generally view Dark Souls III positively, with many considering it a fitting conclusion to the trilogy.

Negative Aspects
Performance Issues:
Some players experienced frame rate drops and other technical problems.

Disappointing Boss Fights:
While many encounters are praised, a few are criticized as less engaging or creative.

Over-Reliance on Nostalgia:
Some reviewers felt the game leaned too heavily on familiar elements from previous titles, potentially hindering its originality.

Steep Early-Game Difficulty:
The early game can be particularly unforgiving and lacking in clear direction, which may discourage new players.

In conclusion, Dark Souls III is a highly regarded game, celebrated for its challenging gameplay, intricate world, and rich lore. While not without its flaws, it is considered a strong entry in the series and a worthy conclusion to the trilogy for many fans.

Dark souls 3, a challenging, dark fantasy, story rich game.
After completing Sekiro, I was impressed by FromSoftware's unique design, so I decided to try Dark Souls 3. I quickly fell in love with it. The world design is intricate and rewarding, constantly encouraging exploration through its hidden paths, interconnected areas, and environmental storytelling. I really felt like I didn't want to miss out on certain parts or areas and explored every corner.

The boss fights is what sells this game the most. The difference in move sets between bosses make every fight so much more refreshing, where you have the feeling you want to keep trying till you defeat the boss to end your session with. And when you finish all of the bosses throughout the main game and loved to fight those, there are even better ones waiting for you in the DLC.

The only downside for me is the typical archer melee combo on tiny ledges and the radius of mobs following you when they are aggro'd. Some of them will follow you through the entire game if they were able to use stairs.

Overall, Dark Souls 3 is one of the most rewarding gaming experiences i've had. It challenged me and kept me coming back for more. Even when I was getting wrecked. If you're a fan of tough but fair games with rich worlds, this is absolutely worth it.

In terms of gameplay, DS3 fixes most of the problem of the previous games in the series: bosses are much more fun and harder to cheese; it's much harder to accidentally become completely overpowered, and enemies are much more varied.

However, there are some issues I have with this game:
1) It's completely unoriginal. 90% of areas and characters in DS3 are clear references to DS1 and 2; and honestly that feels like fan-service: instead of designing new and original content they just took what seemed to work in the previous games.

2) It seems to forget what made DS1 great. The original Dark Souls had an iconic feeling of loneliness and mistery that dark souls 3 just ditched in favour of adding more dialogue and exposition and making areas much more linear. While DS1 feels like a game about exploring a mysterious, decaying kingdom, DS3 just feels like a game about figthing bosses and walking in a straight path to the next bonfire.

So, to wrap things up, I recommend this game if you're looking for glorious battles and flashy gameplay, but avoid it if you're looking for a "deeper" experience similar to DS1.

"The softest echo could be enough for me to make it through" - Bandito (Twenty One Pilots)

I hated this game, I profoundly DESPISED this game. Coming out of Elden Ring I was aching for more Fromsoft and everyone tends to recommend DS3 as the next stop for getting into the soulsbourne franchise. I started it, made my way through the first few bosses no problem and then hit a brick wall that stopped me from progressing for the next 6 months. I hated this game.

Then, I was told to git gud by a friend and out of spite reinstalled the game and broke through that brick wall after 6 attempts and promptly steam rolled the rest of the game.

I now love this game.
The atmosphere, the bosses, the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ art direction. It's all phenomenal, Fromsoft took what they'd learnt from every prior entry and polished it to a mirror shine. Not only finishing the franchise perfectly but setting a precedent for themselves as a development studio for every Souls-Like game after this one. It's all so perfectly hopeless, and while I originally hated that hopelessness I think learning to envelop myself in that feeling and that darkness allowed me to appreciate what the game was trying to tell me.
This isn't a game about happiness or light, it's a game about pushing through and learning to accept the light you're given even as you're smothered in the dark.

Posted: July 16
The third titles of the Dark Souls series that many people call "roll spam simulator" or "nostalgia bait."

Let's ignore that and instead talk about what this title brings that the other two did not.

1. Ashen Estus Flask: Effectively "mana" for the various magics within the game called Focus Points. No need to attune multiple copies of spells for more charges! However, I hope you like less Estus for your health.

2. Non-upgradeable armor: Good or bad depending who you ask. Meaning, the armor you find will be the best it will ever be, so no need to farm titanite to upgrade a full set.

3. Specialized dual weapons: While powerstancing was not brought back from Ds2, there are pairs of weapons that can be found across this game that innately function similarly.

4. Skills: Each weapon now has specialized skills at the cost of Focus Points(FP) that can increase poise, defense, damage, or add new movesets for you to play around with.

5. More linear levels: Did you get lost and accidentally stumble upon Blighttown or Heide's Tower of Flame immediately? Well, that's next to impossible here since the early level doesn't allow that unless you're a monster...

6. Purple Phantoms: A brand new covenant in the way of the Mound Makers! A very odd PvP related covenant that makes you purple instead of the classic red.

7. More immersive NPC interactions: With few exceptions, most NPC's along their quests were more or less static in their interactions with the player in the first two titles. The interactions are much more detailed and involved, even being allowed to be summoned by an NPC for their respective questlines.

8. Unique infusions: While the previous games had almost strictly elemental infusions, Ds3 adds a few new ones to the table in the way of Heavy, Sharp, Refined, Blessed and Hollow.

That's all I can think of. If you enjoyed Dark Souls 1 and its story, you'll probably love Dark Souls 3 and the many call-backs this game has to offer.

My personal favorite Souls game, and where I got started, though if you're unfamiliar with the lore it can be a little confusing getting started, but that's fine. You know why? Because Souls games are all about having a steep learning curve. The insane difficulty is meant to challenge you. There is absolutely no hand-holding, no quest markers, nothing. You get a tutorial on how to do basic things then you get thrown into the frying pan. This game is not easy, Dark Souls became its own genre for a reason. You WILL die, hundreds of times. You will want to quit. You will be frustrated with the mechanics, and the combat, and you will feel like quitting. But you will feel the need to come back, to complete it, to finally beat that ONE annoying boss. And after 50, 100, or even 1000 failed attempts you will finally beat it, and the feeling of accomplishment will be like nothing you've ever experienced. Because you earned it, you took your time, you trained, you practiced your moves, and in the end you won. You can respawn an infinite number of times, the only way you can lose is if you give up.
And that's the poetic beauty of Dark Souls. You decide when you lose. You decide how hard you're going to make it for yourself. Not with a regular difficulty slider, but through hard work, dedication, and summoning a bunch of Sunbros who are better fighters than you are to beat up the big mean hard bosses for you.

10/10

Review: Dark Souls III | The Peak of the Series – And I’ll Die on That Hill
Playtime: 102.8 hrs (Real total? Probably 600–700+ 😅)

This review is coming from someone who has played DS3 a lot. Multiple accounts, multiple platforms, countless playthroughs – challenge runs, randomizers, co-op, you name it. I’ve seen everything this game has to offer, probably twice.

And honestly? I still think this is the best Souls game.

The atmosphere is incredible. That signature FromSoftware blend of haunting world design, cryptic lore, and eerie calm-before-the-storm moments is here in full force. The art direction is top-notch, and the soundtrack? Just... chef’s kiss – as memorable and emotional as ever.

The gameplay? Smooth, responsive, and satisfying – even after your 80th run. DS3 has that perfect blend of weight and speed. It's more refined than DS1, more grounded than Elden Ring, and more fun (for me) than any of them. It just feels right.

Balance-wise, the game does a great job. Leveling up takes serious time, especially early on, and you’re kind of forced to follow a linear path, which keeps things tight and fair. That said, the game gives you plenty of tools to make your life easier – from powerful gear to summonable Phantoms and NPC allies. You’re never truly stuck unless you choose to be.

NPC questlines and the multiple endings are actually really well done – some are emotional, some are strange, and a few are incredibly easy to miss. But they add replay value and flavor to a world that’s already dripping with atmosphere.

Once you’ve finished the game once, though, there’s not much in terms of new content... unless you make your own fun. Thankfully, I did – with challenge runs, build experiments, and the occasional chaos of PvP or co-op.

Now, for 100% completion: it’s honestly pretty chill unless you're going for it offline. Farming Covenant items from enemies instead of online PvP is soul-crushing. Like, actual pain. But other than that, it’s manageable.

There weren’t many surprises for me, but that’s probably because I’ve played it to death. Still, the world holds up beautifully even when you know every shortcut, trap, and boss move.

If you're into Soulslikes and haven’t played DS3 yet – stop reading this and go fix that. It's fast, it's brutal, it's beautiful, and it’s arguably the most polished entry in the series.

Highly, highly recommended.

“Ashen One and the Fire Keeper – A Farewell Etched in Flame”
If Elden Ring is a masterpiece of evolution, Dark Souls III is the final symphony of the legacy.

This game was not just another Souls title — it was the closing chapter of a saga that defined an entire genre.

From the haunting music of the Firelink Shrine to the melancholic skies of Lothric, every frame feels like poetry written in blood.

Combat is brutal, precise, and deeply rewarding. Each boss fight pushes you to your limits, making your eventual victories feel earned, not given.

Dark Souls III polished the formula of its predecessors while keeping the weight and brutality intact. The movement is faster, the visuals are stunning, and the level design is a love letter to fans.

I came here as a fan. I left feeling like I’d walked through fire and come out… reforged.

After playing this, no other challenge quite hits the same. And that says everything.

what happened man

i originally made a really snarky and petty review poking fun at the people who play these games For The Bossfights (and I'd be remiss to deny ds2's bosses and its general approach to boss design was something I liked a lot about that game) and not much else but everything about Dark Souls III just makes me sad. I didn't fw ds1 and 2's level design that much and they had their fair share of moments that had me rolling my eyes or raising an eyebrow but I at least appreciated what they were going for. They at least felt purposeful. They had pacing, enemies were designed to be not be super threatening alone (with some exceptions) but deadly in groups - discouraging running through areas before scouting them out and learning the layout lest you get quickly overwhelmed.

according to Our Infallable God, Miyazaki, scrapping all of that seems to have been the natural next step. clearly we needed MORE gank squads with enemies that have far MORE aggressive ai and attack patterns. this is at least compensated for a little bit with just how much more effective rolling is, but that insistence on abusing i-frames also seeps into the boss design and ultimately kills most of the nuance to the combat that these games had. with just how incessant some of these attack patterns are, it feels more like attrition, hitting the epic invincibility button over and over again to win, than "being patient and waiting for an opening". It's all spectacle, it's boring, and it's designed to waste your time. Of course people don't play these games for the exploration, or the world building, or the character building, or the atmosphere! With just how much you're expected to roll, investing in endurance is a given, and, that also leads me to my next point:

almost all of the ♥♥♥♥ you people gave to ds2 is here and way, way, WAY worse

dark souls ii sucks because i have to level adp to make any use out of dodging. dark souls ii sucks because i have to go back to majula to level up. dark souls ii sucks because of gank squads. dark souls ii sucks because its locations are uninspired, repetitive and ugly. dark souls ii sucks because it ignores dark souls i (iii has the inverse problem!). dark souls ii sucks because it's more linear.

aside from scholar's enemy placement (which, is very different to base ds2 and should be considered a different beast in this type of discussion), I found none of these to be issues.

I don't find problems with iii's equivalent problems.. in theory. The rolling and enemy placement I've already talked about, and I mean, I love Metroid Fusion even as someone who loves Super, so linearity isn't too much of an issue to me, but there's something so cynical to me about making a "direct sequel to dark souls i" after dark souls ii, especially with the context of miyazaki's absence from the directoral role. like ii bears some kind of mark of shame for daring to exist and be developed without him at the helm - a sentiment already echoed by many Soulsborne Fans™ and, to me, made apparent by just how little this game dares to reference it. There's a few items, like the drang equipment, but the ds1 stuff just feels so in-your-face, bringing back the exact same npcs and place-names wholesale in a series lauded for making the history of its world a mystery, anticipating players to sift through line after line of dialogue and flavor text for merely a hint of connections to the past. DSIII just screams fan-service to me and not the good kind.

If anything, the thing that crossed my mind the most playing this game was "maybe I'd been too harsh on dark souls i. maybe I should revisit it." Maybe part of why I enjoyed dsii so much is because a lot of the skills and knowledge gained from playing dsi is applicable there too. Maybe I did just need to Git Gud as they say, I'm still not sure, but I'm still left wondering:

What happened to Dark Souls.

Posted: July 8
I first played DS3 back in like 2017, I'd never played a souls game before, so I got stuck in the high wall of Lothric, got pissed, said the game sucked, and deleted it. Several months later I thought "I didn't give it enough of a chance" and played it again - got pissed, and deleted it again. Then, a few months after that, I heard some people say how amazing it was, so I redownloaded it AGAIN with the intention of forcing myself to play the entire game. I played, died, got pissed, but kept going. Eventually, I progressed a little. I played more, died even more, but progressed a little more. Eventually, I got to the boss of the location, beat him after several tries, and I felt pure ecstasy.

Then I just kept playing, kept dying, getting invaded, falling for traps, and being misled by player messages. It was chaos, but - I endured. I started to love the game. I told my friend who had also downloaded it and deleted it, claiming it was a bad game. I told him "bro, just force yourself to play it, It's going to change your life". And so, he did. We beat the first boss together, and after 90 some hours, we beat the entire game including the DLC's.

This made me want to play other souls games, and is what sparked my obsession with the series. To this day, I've never played a better game series. Each game is unique, challenging, but rewards perseverance above all. I'm now almost 1,000 hours deep into the series and I still learn something new with each playthrough. When Elden Ring came out, it was like being a little kid on Christmas again. When the Elden Ring DLC came out, it was christmas again. When Nightreign came out, CHRISTMAS AGAIN. I'm going to be playing these games the rest of my life, and I genuinely look forward to it. I'm going to have a kid just so I can put him onto soulsborne. But especially Dark Souls 3 - the game that inspired me to persevere and do try your best in all that you do, something most people overlook nowadays.

You can never lose if you never quit. Even faced with oblivion, It's our nature to endure - always.

Posted: July 7
First Souls game. Won’t be the last.

I came into Dark Souls III with zero experience in the series—and got completely hooked. What started as frustration turned into obsession. And eventually, mastery.

🗡️ The combat is punishing but fair. Every swing, dodge, and parry matters. You don’t button-mash—you learn patterns, stay patient, and pick your moment. When you finally beat a boss after 15 tries, it feels earned.

🌒 The world is haunting and gorgeous, like a dying kingdom full of secrets. It doesn't hold your hand—there’s no quest log, no map—but if you love piecing things together and getting lost (in a good way), this is for you.

📜 The story and lore are deep but hidden. You won’t get cutscenes every few minutes. Instead, you’ll find strange NPCs, cryptic dialogue, and item descriptions that hint at something bigger. The mystery keeps you thinking long after you stop playing.

🛠️ The RPG depth surprised me—there are so many builds to try. I started with a sword-and-shield knight and later experimented with dexterity weapons and pyromancy. Every style feels different.

🔥 Pros:
Challenging but addictive combat

Incredible boss fights and enemy design

Atmospheric world with exploration at the core

Deep, mysterious lore

Satisfying build variety and character progression

⚠️ Cons:
Steep learning curve (but worth it)

Some areas can feel unfair at first

Minimal guidance—can be confusing if you expect traditional RPG handholding

There is no other way to say it - Dark Souls 3 is an utter masterpiece, and the true successor to the brilliant original Dark Souls.
Importantly for Elden Ring players who want a taste of the infamous 'Souls' trilogy but aren't sure where to start, look no further. This is the perfect gateway into the world of Dark Souls.

I've put in hundreds of hours across a few accounts/consoles, and it just gets better every time I play.

Take your time to explore, get stuck into the amazing hidden content, and within a few hours you'll be underway on a truly exceptional adventure through Lothric and beyond. It goes without saying but Dark Souls 3 is home to some of the best bosses FromSoftware have ever made. Combat is fluid and fast, enemies are varied and unpredictable, and the environments are simultaneously harrowing, beautiful and awe-inspiring.

I cannot recommend this title enough, Dark Souls 3 is an experience that simply cannot be missed.

This is my first game review ever, and I believe that this is the only one I will ever write.
I have been playing Dark Souls 3 for 4 years now, started on playstation 4, proceeded to a cracked version on pc, and eventually buying the game on steam and I can confidently say, that this is the best game I have ever witnessed. The world feels like it was really lived in, and eventually destroyed due to the fire fading, and the world getting corrupted. The gameplay seamlessly combines all the elements from the previous games made by Miyazaki, and makes it feel swift, responsive and fun. When it comes to the bosses, I can easily say that this game has the best boss design of any fromsoft game, even Elden Ring. The bosses are not just fighting for the sake of fighting, they really look like they are trying to stop you on your quest, each attack feels powerful, yet fair.
I love this game, every single aspect of it is just perfect, at least in my eyes. Buy it. Play it. I beg you.

Dark Souls 3 — это как если бы абхазец решил научить тебя играть в шахматы, но вместо пешек — бутылки водки, а каждое поражение отмечается криком и пинком под зад.

Ты входишь в игру с уверенностью, что будешь героем, а выходишь с диагнозом «психологический мазохизм», потому что спустя время тебе будет только нравиться, что ты видшь надпись "YOU DIED"

А боссы — это не просто боссы, а группировка местных братков, которые собрались, чтобы устроить тебе самую жёсткую пьянку без алкоголя, зато с кучей адреналина и слёз. Попытался сделать рывок — "YOU DIED", попытался увернуться — "YOU DIED", попытался выпить эстус - "YOU DIED", попытался "YOU DIED" - "YOU DIED".

И самое главное — каждый раз, когда ты видишь “YOU DIED”, это как попробовать заново сойтись с бывшей. Все равно в конце "YOU DIED"

В общем, если хочешь почувствовать себя живым трупом, которого никто не хоронит — играй. Если хочешь нормального отдыха — посиди с абхазцами на рынке, они тоже умеют веселиться.

9.5/10, потому что абхазцы могли бы и побольше водки взять, но и так весело!

Dark Souls 3 is known for having the trilogy's weaker world design. It is true that areas don't connect together as well as in DS1 and they don't feel as tentacular as in DS2, it is in every way more linear. But I think this decision to make the world more straightforward also gives a clear insight on this game's intention. By being simplistic on the spatial progression, Dark Souls 3 allowed a new population of players, unfamilar with the genre to discover the formula - linear level design is not a regression but another more accessible method of handling world progression.

As many players, DS3 was the first souls game I really had the chance to grasp. The combat is the culmination of 3 title's worth of experience, it feels simple, tight and punishing but also so meaningful. The enemy design is terrific, we always talk about the complexity and tension that monsters in the Dark Souls games bring but I also find an elegance in the design of some humanoid enemies that felt universal and re-used throughout the game. The Lothric Knight is a perfect example of that, this is a triumph of enemy design, it is re-used from the first to the last area of the game and always feels tense.

Narratively, this is the peak of the trilogy. the game is a critic of the licence itself, sequels upon sequels, upon sequels... Until there is nothing more than the husk of what was there before. This world is a fan service, even almost a parody of both Dark Souls, the world is dying again, there are still lords of cinders, the cycle has continued for ages, but eons of repetition have gradually eroded the original purpose of the world. And here we start as a player, asked to save the world once again, for a purpose faded in time. The theme of decay feels more than right in the case of this game, it feels fitting for the universe, but also for the licence. Once I put down my controller after beating the last boss of the DLC I felt satisfied to leave Lothric behind, alongside Dark Souls to let it rest.

Speaking of bosses, there are some of the most memorable encounters I ever had in the series, The Dancer Of The Boreal Valley, Sister Friede and Slave Knight Gael probably stand among my best gaming moments.

This is undoubtedly one of the greatest games of all times.

I wanted to wait until I 100% the game to make a review, so low and behold more than 2 and a half years later I finally decided to go for it.

Dark Souls 3 is what I consider to be the best instance of the soulslike genre at its roots (of course, right). The horror element of the game is perfectly done in both the anxiety you've learned to have as you walk into countless narrow corridors and the frustration of failing time and time again.

This is a game best played blindly, every instance of failure or getting lost in the world is a puzzle to figure out for yourself; which will either relieve you or have you remembering the hatred you have for the situation. Regardless, every part of this game is so incredibly memorable. From maybe finally beating a boss you feel is beneath you, getting grabbed by a disgusting looking enemy in the Irithyll Dungeon, finding an NPC throughout your playthrough as you continue their questline without even meaning to, or just recalling a very particular shortcut you found to create a pathway from a bonfire to the upcoming boss you're about to die to, I can't even try to think of a point in this game that isn't incredibly memorable.



You may recognize I haven't even mentioned the combat, reason being I am actually pretty mixed on it. The bosses in Dark Souls 3 are absolutely incredible, I can't truly think of an actually BAD boss type in this entire game. I'd say even the bosses people would consider irrelevant are still designed perfectly as intended, to be irritating and to teach you to respect what you don't recognize you should respect or have been taught to disrespect, or is more of a cinematic moment rather than an actual fight. And that's just from what is considered BAD by the majority of the community, in terms of the best I don't think there is anyone who would deny that this game has some of if not the most amount of S tier bosses. From Champion Gundyr to Pontiff, Sister Friede to The Pygmy, I've only named half of some of the best fights to the entire genre. There is so much love and care put into every single swing, how the developers perfectly created a system that you can either play to their decided tune, or just entirely flip them on their head if you exploit their weaknesses, and as much as you may feel they don't, they DEFINITELY have those weaknesses to exploit.

Besides the bosses now, this is where I am pretty mixed on. On one hand, as opposed to Elden Ring, which those who are reading this review likely have already played, straight swords and axes feel incredible in this game, they have their own identity and use as weapons that feel strong and not just as a longer dagger or an objectively worse hammer.

On the other hand, the looping attack string of this game is incredibly cheesy. Dexterity scaling weapons like straight swords and katanas when one handed are made to be incredibly stamina efficient and absurdly effective when combined with again, the looping attack string. When you recognize the game's inner workings the game is incredibly trivial. Infusions combined with some of these weapons basically break the game, and you can realistically just get what is basically the second best weapon in the entire game in the first 20 minutes, perfect to infuse at each part of the game where you unlock the ability to infuse or a new coal to infuse different elements. It's bizarre to me because the game is almost serving you on a silver platter these tools to entirely cheese the game, making a majority of challenge in this game tantamount to a fart in the wind. If you recognize I'm not being entirely specific on HOW these infusions effect gameplay, that is on purpose because as much as I want to be honest I also dont want to ruin someone's first playthrough of the game through exploits.



The setting of the Kingdom of Lothric is so incredibly masterful in its design, the enemies whether of The Deep or righteous in their own secretive ways are some of the best in the entirety of the soulslike genre, and the combat although incredibly exploitable, all culminates in an incredible game. I don't think its possible for anyone to not recommend Dark Souls 3 if they are truly someone who can recognize the craft behind making a project like this.

I had a fantastic time with Dark Souls III, and I absolutely recommend it—especially if you’re new to the Souls series like I was. It’s challenging, yes, but not unfair. The atmosphere, exploration, and boss design all come together to deliver a truly memorable experience. While it’s not without a few quirks, the highs easily outweigh the lows.

--Minor Issues--

- Some NPC questlines are a bit too obscure. Without a guide, it’s easy to miss entire character arcs or outcomes based on small, missable steps. It adds mystery, but it can also feel unintentionally punishing for first-timers.

- Multiplayer and summoning can be inconsistent. Sometimes it works flawlessly, other times it feels like you're fighting the system just to summon a friend or invade. It's not a huge issue, but worth noting.

- A few late-game areas feel less inspired. While most of the game is excellent, some of the final zones (like the Profaned Capital or parts of the Grand Archives) don’t quite hit the same highs as earlier areas, especially artistically.

--Highlights--

- As my first Souls game, it struck a perfect balance. The world is layered and intricate, but never overwhelming. There's always something to discover, but it never feels bloated or aimless.

- Level progression is mostly linear—but in a good way. The game guides you forward naturally, while still rewarding curiosity and offering branching paths when it matters most. It never feels like you're stuck in a hallway, but you also won’t get lost for hours.

- The bosses are phenomenal. They’re a true highlight of the game—each one memorable in its own way. My personal favorite was the Abyss Watchers, both for the music and the fast-paced, stylish combat. There's something uniquely satisfying about learning a boss's pattern and finally overcoming it after a tough battle.

- The atmosphere is unmatched. From Lothric Castle to Irithyll of the Boreal Valley, the game nails the "end of the world" tone perfectly. It’s bleak, haunting, and absolutely captivating.

- Challenges always feel fair and rewarding. The difficulty is real, but the sense of accomplishment when you push through a tough fight or area is unmatched. The game doesn’t hold your hand—but it respects your effort.

Despite a few minor gripes, Dark Souls III completely won me over. It's a tightly designed, atmospheric action RPG that’s rewarding in ways few games manage to be. Whether you’re brand new to the series or a returning veteran, it’s absolutely worth playing—if not mastering.

After my Elden Ring journey, I wanted to share my experience with Dark Souls 3! (I haven’t hit 100% achievements yet, but I’m super close once I finally get there, I’ll update this review with my final thoughts!)

Honestly, the Dark Souls series as a whole is just a masterpiece. That makes it really easy to gush about the good parts… but finding things to criticize? Not so much. But hey, I’ll give it a try.

Things I really liked:

Boss fights, atmosphere, world design, and that sweet sense of progression.

The boss fights are just so satisfying and fun. Well, except for Champion Gundyr. (No offense, but I’m convinced he exists purely to test my patience.) The clever design and unique mechanics kept me hooked all the way to NG++. Just one more playthrough and I’ll finally be able to 100% this beast!

The atmosphere and map design are genuinely breathtaking and super detailed. I got lost in Anor Londo’s dreamy vibes more than once, and honestly? Having soup with Catarina was such a wholesome little break. The dark, moody vibe perfectly captures that classic Souls philosophy, and I loved every minute of it.

Feeling yourself get stronger and actually seeing your progress is so rewarding. Every step forward feels like an achievement, especially after hours of suffering.

Things that felt a bit “meh”:

Champion Gundyr. I hate you. That’s it. Purely personal. Please go away.

When I found out the “collect all rings” achievement actually means collecting every +0, +1, +2, and +3 ring in the game… I nearly uninstalled out of spite. Having to go into NG+++ just for that 100% is honestly a little evil. (Still gonna do it though. Sigh.)

I’d say I’m about 75% through my Dark Souls 3 journey. Once I finally reach 100%, I promise I’ll come back and update this review!
Until then, praise the sun! ☀️🛡️

Update after getting 100% achievements:

Okay, let me be real this game absolutely broke me. It was way, way harder than I thought it would be. I used to think collecting all the rings was rough, but farming covenant items? I swear, I lost half my braincells doing it. Whose idea was it to put a 1% drop rate item and make you collect 30 of them for an achievement? Seriously?

I’m not kidding, after finishing this grind I don’t want to see another Souls-like for a long, long time. I am so tired. (All that’s left now is Dark Souls 1, and maybe, maybe if I ever recover, I’ll try DS2. But don’t count on it.)

Honestly, if you’re thinking of going for 100% in this game, my only advice for the sake of your mental health: Don’t. Just… don’t. Save yourself.

Review is below, but first:

the absolute hollow brains of the developers have a servers and have all the means to enable steam cloud saves for this game. yet there is no way for this game to have my saves stored in any form of cloud. I had to format by main disk and reinstall windows, and lost 151 HOURS of my life. it took me 239 hours in total to finish this game

I shall not fully speak how I feel about this, not due to decorum, but just because I don't want to risk my account getting banned. this negative review will follow them until the end of days.

REVIEW:
LORE-WISE: absolute disappointment. this game gave me ONE single answer to ONE of the hundred questions I have been accumulating since I first started this franchise. all endings are short and anti-climactic, not showing me any of what I expected. only the Fade ending with the Fire Keeper talking into the dark was acceptable. I understand that every franchise must always have a few secrets that must never be answered. to always keep up driving thought and theories. but this damn franchise is 95% questions without answers.

WORLD DESIGN: cool, but there are so many symbols here and there with no explanation. and places that should have specific designs and decorations rather than random meaningless designs, like happens will decorated walls of churches, tapestries, decorated glass windows, etc. and why is it so hard to give us a map of the world in general?

MUSIC: as always, little to no music. the music is part of the soul of a game. and these are almost always quite hollow outside some bosses fights.

RAMBLINGS: why is there no single talking cat in this game? you had one job! and Yhorm was an absolute waste. I was expecting him to be the son or younger brother of the Giant King from DS2. and for we to visit what I thought would be the giants' capital, ''profaned'' by Vendrick. but no. it was nothing of the sort. and why could we not enter into that building on the island in front of the Ringed City? why make something so interesting and obvious to have meaning if it was to end up pointless?

LAST WORDS: it is clear that DS2 is by far the best game of the franchise. if I had not known that DS2 had a troubled development and had unfinished and changed content, and just had now been told that one game went through these troubles, I would have been sure that it would have been DS3. for this game is clearly the one lacking proper direction. Elden Ring better not disappoint me like DS3 has.

The third and final stop of the souls trilogy to be played, this is the game I had played before all this, and going back in felt like a huge wave of nostalgia for my second fromsoft game, but first souls game. Many of the areas brought back vivid sweet memories of the little girl I was when I first played through them. I had an amazing time travelling through this world again, with fresh eyes that were open to understand all the wonderful nods to the games that came before it, truly an amazing series that I love with all my heart. Seeing this game's pvp alive and well in this day and age was wonderful, I found myself invaded quite a few times in this playthrough, and even did a bit of invading myself. What a beautiful game.

Class Mechanics
I played through as a sorcerer, for one last time. I found the magic in this game to be seriously weaker than the games that came before it, I found myself struggling quite a lot early game, but once I got my hands on my spells like Great Heavy Soul Arrow, it started to make the game feel much better. The introduction of the MP bar surely was different from DS1 and DS2, and I don't think I entirely love it, moving cast speed to dex instead of attunement is also a serious downgrade from DS2, but I coped and made do with what I had to still have a good feeling build. Spell diversity did feel good, and by end game I had multiple spells I was using comfortably to get through areas.

Bosses
Dark souls 3 is a flawless game when it comes to its bosses, not a single flop, they were all amazing and fun and felt rewarding to learn. I found myself not particularly stuck on any of them this time around, I have grown a lot since I was a child stuck on Nameless King, and Midir for entire days haha.. I beat Midir on my third attempt, and Gael on my first this time around, exhilirating combat that felt well paced and rewarding. The bosses in this game are a cut above their younger counterparts, openings feel tight especially with a spell like GHSA! I loved this game to bits in the boss regard.

I beat my final bosses, Midir, Gael, and Soul of Cinder, at SL123-125, but could have done it earlier, as my last 39 levels or so were entirely for more HP and Stamina, I found that this game it was much harder to make do with base health, as enemies hit harder, so unlike in DS1 and DS2 where being at 5 vigor was no detriment to the experience, being at 9 vigor in ds3 felt horrific as soon as I stepped foot in the princes boss arena, they were my biggest roadblock by far. I ended with 60 int, 50 att, 27 vig, 20 end, and 20 dex. Needing to get so much dex for max cast speed felt bad, it made me feel really bad actually, but sometimes we do what we must for our build to feel good. 1000 HP felt like plenty, and 1300 with ember felt beefy, I was once again able to comfortably run a light roll setup, using my trusty court sorcerer's attire with the crown of dusk to really pump out damage, every single ring slot was filled with damage boosts or cast speed boosts as well, it really felt like I got to go all in on damage in my build by offhanding the cleric candlestick as well, felt very dark souls 2 in that regard. Which is a high compliment.

The zones in this game are very nice, they never overstay their welcome, the enemy density is always very lax and easy to deal with, this game does a good job at giving you enemies to deal with should you like, while also technically letting you just run past them all if you are so inclined. I also found myself loving Hidden Body + Spook fro getting through areas when I just couldn't be bothered to kill the enemies, the zones are pretty, if not a bit frustrating at areas like the Cathedral of the Deep.

This part of the trilogy is one of it's strongest. It is so very enjoyable, and feels very modern which means a lot of that jank in DS1 and DS2 is missing, but part of me missed it, a lot of me missed DS2 while playing this. Which leads me to my final ranking on the original three dark souls games. DS2 is the best one by far, and you should play that if you haven't yet. Followed by DS3, an amazing modern game that just feels good to play and well balanced compared to its janky easy to break predecessors. Leaving DS1 at the bottom of the list, a foundational game, that just doesn't have some of the oomph it's future entries will have. Fromsoft has made something truly special with the souls series, and this game is no different. Everyone should play them at least once, just for the experience.

This was my first souls-like game so I remembered it quite fondly. After replaying it now for the first time like 4ish years later and playing the DLCs for the first time my opinion has changed a bit. For the worse, sadly. I still think it's better than Elden ring, no doubt, the combat is better and all, the aesthetics are better and the bosses are among the best. It's more the areas that feel kinda boring. And there are a lot more 'Dark souls 2' moments (which is what I call slamming a ♥♥♥♥ ton of enemies on one spot and telling the player "have fun mate") than I remember. I really really really ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hate the Ashes of Ariandel DLC. Like wholly, it's awful. The only thing I kinda enjoyed about it was Friede's 3rd phase. Everything else was either, a 'Dark souls 2' moment, a bad boss or both. All in all, I think it's no more than a 2/10, I do not see myself ever replaying it. The Ringed City is waay better , although the bosses still feel like a bit of a let down, especially Midir, who has been so hyped up by the community that I was expecting a lot more than the same 4 attacks and way too giant of a healthbar. It's a slog, bigger than Friede. Twin princes are cool, Halflight is among the worst bosses FromSoft has made, since it commits the cardinal sin of forcing you to play dark souls PVP. Thankfully, Gael slaps and is among my favorite bosses in FromSoft's catalogue. He doesn't beat Lady Maria and Orphan but I'm happy to put him next to Martyr Lugarius on the 3rd/4th place.

It's still a great game and among my favorites but not as magical as I remembered it. Still a solid 8/10 (dropping from a 9).

Posted: June 25
I didn't get it -

I came to DS3 after playing Elden Ring recently and Bloodborne when that first released years ago. I LOVED both of those games and was sure I was going to love this - I didn't, it felt so different and challenging in ways I didn't expect that it quickly got the better of me and I gave up. Blamed the game for being too challenging and just moved on to playing other things. Then I saw a video on YouTube called "Dark Souls 3 will save you" (Or something like that, it's worth the watch) - I came back to the game, did the tutorial area and felt confident. Then the challenge kicked in, I was dying, over and over but something had clicked - I'm playing it wrong, instead of trying to rush through each dungeon and just MAKE IT TO THE NEXT BONFIRE - I was taking it room by room, clearing, learning and improving as I went, getting more confident in my build and enjoying the game so much more. I still die, plenty... But each death is something to learn from, not a moment to give up, but to move forward, learn attack patterns, learn when to be aggressive and when to be defensive. I learned most importantly, to slow down. It's not about rushing and hoping that you will just about make it, it's about learning how to dominate an area, how to be better - Dark Souls 3 is IMO, the most challenging FromSoft game I've played (I know people say Sekiro is a whole different beast - That's downloaded ready to be played at a later date) - It's methodical, aggressive and requires plenty of patience and practice. But that's the hook that keeps me thinking about it when I'm not playing it, keeps me dying to get some time to jump on my PC and work my way forward. It's beautiful and I'm so thankful I gave it another shot, it's worth every penny it cost, and every second you put into it.

Truth be told, I probably still don't 100% get the game, there's so much more to learn. But I wholeheartedly endorse that experience and recommend this game to EVERYONE. I know I'm late to the party and this game has been out for a while, but as someone who is on their first playthrough of the game, maybe this might be of some use to someone who's deciding whether to play or not. Play this game, it's gaming at it's absolute finest.

This is gonna be a long review but for those who dont wanna read the whole review or dont get any spoilers here is a short version:

Dark Souls III is for me a perfect game. I think you could even argue that it is better than Elden Ring which is my favourite game of all time. It has a ton of great bosses also optional and many cool regions and in most parts perfect level design. It can be a bit frustrating like all souls like but thats part of the deal. But nonetheless it is a clear 10/10.

Long Version:
FYI I now almost got 100 hrs, all achievements and 3 playthroughs. The aspects that i really like about this game are:
The graphics still are in such a good shape now 9 years after release. Even some games today would be more than happy if they got these graphics. For me its always the amazing cutscenes. There is also so much variaton between the regions. For me personally Irithyll, the Cathedral of the Deep and the Archdragon Peak are espacially brilliant. And i am always surprised how many different enemy types are in ds3. In each region its totally different. The vibe and music also have to be mentioned. And of course it would be a crime to not mention the detail. So many fcking secret routes and stuff it really is phenomenal.

Bossranking:
Probably the best part in DS3 are the bosses and to appreciate them i did a little ranking.
I take this really serious so there are four criteria: Arena(6) + Looks(6) + Lore(6) + Gameplay(8) = 24 max points
And there is always a quick note.
Remember this is very subjective and dont be sad if your fav boss is in dead last, you're just wrong than kiddo.
(Lore is mostly rated on the cutscenes or other things the bosses do. the rest should be obvious)
Nr. A. L. L G. ALL
25. 2 3 1 0 06 = 0,25 Yhorm the Giant
24. 1 4 0 2 07 = 0,29 Old Demon King
23. 1 1 3 2 07 = 0,29 Spears of the Church

25. Yhorm the Giant: (the first time it might be fun and the hits are kinda satisfying but after the first time its kust boring, his attacks dont even change in the second phase really and predictible)
24. Old Demon King: (there are many similar enemies all around and not that spectacular, his attacks are also not the best, though i gotta say they kinda stole a lot for rykard here: the arena, the skull fly attack)
23. Crystal Sage: (much spamming but cool idea wtih illusions but for me as a bit colorblind and dumb its not so easy to track her down)
22. Spears of the Church: (NPC boss not much to explain)
21. Deacons of the Deep: (very random and boring, there are two ways this fight is gonna go: you get clapped or you gonna clap them ez)
20. Ancient Wyvern: (the kill is satisfying but thats all, its so frustrating dying on the way up when you climb that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ladder)
19. Gravetender Champion & Wolf: (kill 5 wolves, a NPC and a big wolf where you can defeat a weaker version before, honostly it lacks so hard of creativity)
18. Curse-Rottet Greatwood: (you always hit wood and dont really see what he is doing)
17. Demons (Ringed City): (nice fight but duos isnt the best)
16. Iudex Gundyr: (solid first tutorial boss)
15. Vordt of the Boreal Valley: (more solid boss but doesnt reinvent the wheel)
14. High Lord Wolnir: (cool jumscare and idea but on 2nd run its just annoying when you die of the mist)
13. Champion Gundyr: (very good moveset but why no real 2nd phase)
12. Darkeater Midir: (honostly very annoying, bridge part is nice idea but long bossrun, also in the arena its a long boss run with dumb attacks like breath attacks under him which is always the thing about dragons: you cant really escape them)
11. Aldrich, Devourer of Gods: (he looks cool but the fight in general is a bit lame)
10. Oceiros, The Consumed King: (such cool dialogue and fun fight)
9. The Abyss Watchers: (great fight, cutscenes and vibe)
8. Pontiff Sulyvahn: (what a perfect fight but no cutscene)
7. Dragonslayer Armour: (not very special fight but it feels so damn good, idk why but the flying things are unnecessary)
6. Soul of Cinder: (satisfying end fight and brilliant music, not challenging unfortunatelly, i did it first try in every run)
5. Dancer of the Boreal Valley: (the movement is so smooth and the twist that you fight in that place)
4. Sister Friede: (Almost perfect but the second phase can be a bit annoying, best cutscenes in game: "FRIIIEDEEEE")
3. Lothric Princes: (i really like this fight, because they used the tp mecanic really well and it feels epic)
2. Nameless King: (Iconic, though i gotta say his first phase is a bit dumb, with the dragon breath attack below him and always hitting the small hitbox of the dragon head)
1. Slave Knight Gael: (Perfect arena that for sure influenced radahn, perfect looks, perfect cutscenes and lore and of course perfect gameplay with such a fun and fair but also tough fight)

DLCs:
Of course the DLCs are something different, genius and definitly worth buying.
ASHES OF ARIANDEL:
the starting area is a bit frustrating with way too less bonfires and a frustrating tower but the legacy dungeon part is so good. With cool shortcuts like the tree felling and the way up the village is a perfect part. But you cant deny the "cool" vibe. also dont forget the entrance to the dlc which is also nice. i think if it werent for the bosses i would like this dlc more than ringed city
RINGED CITY:
here its also the starting area i dont really like that much with the butterfly spamming. But the second part is way
better with this swampy area, much stuff to explore and great shortcuts. And the cutscene when you first see the ringed city, is so gooooood


Over all : It was perfect down to the last minute detail. Perfect.
10/10 and must play.
