When I started this game, I didn't think it would be better than Dark Souls 1. As it turns out, the narrative is the best one across all three games. There are quite a few things I like about this game. I will be referring to details from the ending and DLCs.


Gameplay

Getting the most obvious thing out of the way, I really enjoy the pace of the gameplay. Attacks and movement are much faster than the previous two games, while still retaining the need for deliberate inputs. The introduction of Ashen Estus Flasks and weapon arts add a new layer to the game. This made me want to try out more weapons than I usually do, compared to when I played DS1 and DS2. The ember system is a pretty good addition as well, and it's particularly enjoyable to see bosses use it as well. There are a few things about the gameplay that bother me. The camera positioning for certain larger bosses being one; fighting Midir was a major pain. Some bosses are also a drag to fight, for example, curse-rotted deepwood and High Lord Wolnir. It seems like large enemies are harder to get right.


Level Design

While this game is not as vast as DS2, or as well connected as DS1, I don't particularly mind how linear it is. Before anything else, it takes you through the game's main plotline, which I would say is the highlight. I do like how well-connected shortcuts are brought back, albeit within self-contained areas. There's enough variety for me to not be bored. I was not fond of the excessive focus on teleportation. There are no routes connected to Firelink Shrine, the existence of which was a major point of appeal in DS1.



The soundtrack is very memorable, from Abyss Watchers to Soul of Cinder. Some tracks are good on their own, while some have the added benefit of referencing older significant tracks.



This is where I believe the game's true appeal lies. While the Ashes of Ariandel and The Ringed City are their own separate DLCs, I doubt the story can truly be discussed without them.



As we have known since the beginning, the world operates in cycles. One day, the fire will fade and only darkness will remain, and when the age of dark is at its end, the first flame will be sparked again for another age of fire to start. Gwyn feared losing his world, and did everything he could to preserve it, be it through sacrifice or deception. Dark Souls 3 reveals the aftermath of this first sin of Gwyn. The sun itself resembles the darksign; and yet, the lords of cinder refuse to link the fire once again. As the Ashen One takes up the mantle of linking the flame, encounters with others bring nuance to this perspective. Letting the age of fire continue is what is expected, but would the flame even survive another linking? The fire barely cosumes ashen one, let alone the entire kiln of the first flame. If we let the fire fade, the world goes on, until one day the fire returns and everything will play out once again. The third and most interesting questline, the lord of hollows, suggests hollowing is the true face of man, and the lord of hollows will fulfill that which the primordial serpent Kaathe had desired, to make Londor whole. But this too is part of the cycle, and one day the age of hollows will end as well. So where do we find ourselves at the end of time itself?



At the end of painted world of Ariandel, we hear that Gael has set out to find a pigment for another painted world that resembles the dark soul of man. And so we find ourselves at the end of the world. The Dreg Heap, where all lands converge into one at the very edge of time, and even further below lies the Ringed City, home to the furtive pygmy, from whom the dark soul of man originates from. Slave Knight Gael wants to complete the dark soul to be used as a pigment for another painted world. Once the slumber of Filianore is broken, we reach the very end of all time and lands, a barren wasteland, to fight for the blood of the dark soul. Gael is someone who realises he can't be the bearer of the dark soul, as it would consume him, for he is no hero. And so he duels ashen one, for him to take the dark soul from him, and decide what comes next. Once the dark soul is delivered to the painter, she says she will paint a world with it. A dark, cold and gentle place, and maybe it will be a nice home to someone in the far future.

This I believe is quite a fitting end to overall story of Dark Souls. The story starts with a battle for disparity, and ends with one to end it for a new start in another world. The outside world is just like the painted world, meant to rot away, making way for a new painting for a new start, and now the first sin that prevented this is coming to an end.

As of now, I might not understand the entire story perfectly, since I just finished it, but from what I gathered, this is quite the fitting conclusion to the quests of all three protagonists across the three games.

ark Souls III – A Love Letter to Pain and Perseverance

As a longtime gamer, I can confidently say that Dark Souls III isn’t just a game — it’s an experience. From the moment you wake up as the Ashen One in a dying world, you know you’re in for something special.

What really makes this game stand out is how rewarding it is. Yes, it’s hard — brutally hard at times — but that’s what makes every victory feel earned. Beating a boss after dozens of attempts, finally finding that hidden shortcut, or parrying a knight perfectly for the first time… nothing in gaming feels quite like it. The satisfaction is real.

The combat is fast, responsive, and incredibly satisfying. Every weapon has weight and personality. Whether you're a heavy Greatsword user or a nimble katana duelist, there's a playstyle for everyone — and it all feels amazing when it clicks.

But the thing that truly kept me hooked was the world itself. The atmosphere is unreal. Every area feels steeped in mystery and history. You don’t just play through the game — you explore it, you survive it. And the moment you look up and realize how everything connects together? Mind-blowing.

The music, the enemy designs, the lore hidden in item descriptions — everything feels intentional. Even the community is something special. Whether it’s summoning a stranger to help with a boss or getting invaded and having an unforgettable duel, it’s a shared experience that somehow feels both lonely and communal at the same time.

If you're even a little curious about Dark Souls III, give it a shot. It's tough, yes — but it teaches you to be tougher. And when it all finally comes together, it's one of the most satisfying, beautiful games you'll ever play.

10/10 — I died a lot, but I smiled every time I got back up.

Posted: June 21
After the success of Bloodborne, the developers seemed to believe that a faster, simpler formula could result in the same success for the third entry of the Dark Souls series (and they were proven right). In terms of game design, this resulted in making rolling the only viable option for playing the game efficiently. This fact is made evident by the wild changes applied to the equip load/rolling speed/frames formula, which now gives you a serviceable roll if your equip load is below a whopping 70% of your maximum.

While undoubtedly a commercial success (even though one could argue that the commercial success resulted more from first-time Souls players coming from Bloodborne), the simplification and casualization of the formula did not result in a good final product.

The game is extremely linear, making every playthrough feel and play out the same. This issue could be partly ignored if the areas were actually fun to play through, but unfortunately this is not the case. Despite the memberberries scattered more or less everywhere, Dark Souls 3 areas have none of the magic of the first two entries of the series (DeS and DaS) and none of the originality and inventiveness of Dark Souls 2 (which had its own issues). This time we get gray castles and purple-brown swamps which all look and play the same, and where everything (enemies included) blends into the background.

Even if it doesn't reach the lows of its predecessor (cough cough, Black Gulch), this game contains some of the worst areas of the series (Cathacombs of Carthus, Archdragon Peak, ...) and some of the most boring and uninspired ones (Undead Settlement, Anor Londo but empty).

The worst part about this game is the discourse around it: despite it doing everything Dark Souls 2 was accused of (changing the rolling mechanics, spamming infinite stamina enemies and mobs etc.) it never got any flak for it. Archdragon Peak is arguably worse than the infamous Shrine of Amana (which is turned into a cakewalk by using a bow and taking your time to advance) in terms of enemy and projectile spam, but you will rarely hear someone comparing the two.

My guess is that this happened because this was most people's first souls game, which makes it very hard for people to be objective about it and to compare it to the other entries (assuming they actually bothered to play them). Another guess could be that being this the most commercially successful of the DS series it was also played by the most ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, but I guess these two explanation do not necessarily contradict each other.

TL, DR: Dark Souls 3 is the least fun, interesting and replayable Dark Souls game by far. A few bosses still represent high points in the series, but the time investment required to get to them is not worth it. It will always be guilty of the definitive casualization of the Dark Souls formula into a rollfest and of the normalization of spastic, infinite stamina enemies. Despite this, it is probably the easiest game in the Dark Souls series.

DS3 – Peak pain, peak satisfaction

This game doesn’t just punish you — it educates you. DS3 is the ultimate “git gud” simulator. I came in swinging, got parried by the world, and now I’m addicted.

Every boss is a vibe check. Yhorm had me questioning my life. Abyss Watchers? Straight anime fight. Pontiff? That man is built different. But nothing beats the feeling of finally clutching a win after 30+ deaths.

The world is mad interconnected — shortcuts feel earned, and every bonfire is like a breath after drowning. It doesn’t explain much, but that’s the beauty — you figure it out, and the lore is there if you want it.

Combat? Tight. Movement? Crisp. Mistakes? Always on you. No cheap shots, just consequences. Every death teaches something — spacing, stamina, timing, patience.

If you’re into handholding, this ain’t it. But if you’re ready to roll, parry, and panic-roll your way into greatness… welcome to the grind.

After taking a long break, I came back to finally link the First Flame. This was the first Dark Souls game I ever fully finished, and it feels like the end of something important to me. Seeing familiar NPCs made me smile—even though they’ll never remember me, I remembered them. As much as I didn’t want to finish it, I knew it was time to let go. And I’m okay with that.

Thank you, Dark Souls III. I’m just glad I didn’t go hollow.

Posted: June 11
Great game and my favourite soulsborne game. It has really solid, responsive feeling gameplay that still holds up well nearly 10 years later.

There are a really good amount of weapons, armour, and rings that make pretty much any build viable, maybe not to the same extent as elden ring for example, but there's still enough where you can have fun no matter how you play. I absolutely love the setting of this game too, the story, npcs, ambiance, visuals, and overall lore are all great and really help you get immersed in the games universe. Additionally, there is a huge payoff if you have played the first 2 souls games as throughout your playthrough you will constantly encounter familiar faces and locations from the other games.

Don't get me wrong, the game isn't perfect, it definitely has its ups and downs depending on where you are in the game but overall I don't feel like the negative points in the game are enough to take away from how good the game is.

Finally, the DLC is amazing, and while I'd say its not on par with the base game it is 100% worth checking out. I definitely have my own opinion on which DLC I prefer but both of them offer some very cool locations, enemies, and bosses. (Heavy emphasis on the bosses).

Nejlepší věcí na Dark Souls 3 jsou jednoznačně boss fighty, které můžou být často těžké, ale zároveň jsou férové, a v momentě, co pochopíte move sety a mechaniky bossů, začnete si hru opravdu naplno užívat. Skvělé detaily jsou popisy itemů ve hře a questlines, které můžete plnit – Siegwarda z Catariny si budu moc dobře pamatovat.

Dark Souls 3 rozhodně není žádné peříčko, ale když se do toho opravdu dostanete, uvědomíte si, že je to víc než jen hra – že je to opravdový masterpiece.

Again I still love this game, and I do feel like it's the strongest dark souls game, but those three things just don't work for me.

PS:Dark Souls III não é apenas um capítulo final.
É o testamento crepuscular de uma era que se arrasta de joelhos, tentando lembrar como era… viver.
É a última vela acesa num altar de ossos partidos, onde os deuses já foram homens, e os homens… apenas brasas do que ousaram sonhar.

Não é um jogo.
É um ritual fúnebre, um sermão sussurrado em línguas extintas, entrelaçado com a poeira dos reinos que tombaram sobre seus próprios mitos.

Você não é um herói.
Você é o resto. O resíduo. A sobra.
Não por fraqueza, mas porque o mundo já queimou tudo o que era puro.
E mesmo assim, você se ergue.
Sem nome. Sem glória. Sem promessas.
Apenas a vontade.
A vontade de caminhar.

Lothric não é um reino.
É um delírio moribundo, onde o tempo se torce em desespero, onde os castelos flutuam como memórias que se recusam a desaparecer.
Suas paredes choram sangue petrificado. Suas torres apontam não para os céus… mas para o passado.
Tudo aqui já acabou.
E é exatamente por isso que tudo ainda dói.

Cada área é um epitáfio.
A Catedral das Profundezas fede a traição.
Irithyll é um sonho congelado em sua agonia.
A Selva Crucificada sussurra com a fome de mil arrependimentos.
E a Grande Biblioteca… sussurra em silêncio, como se os livros tivessem se cansado de serem lidos por olhos indignos.

Você não progride.
Você confessa.
Cada boss é um altar onde você deixa um pedaço da sua alma em troca de compreensão.
Eles não querem te matar.
Eles querem te mostrar algo.

Abyss Watchers não são vilões. São irmãos que se matam por amor à causa que esqueceram.

Aldrich sonha devorar deuses — e sonha, porque comer o divino é tudo o que lhe resta.

Lorian e Lothric, unidos por um destino cruel, te enfrentam não por glória, mas por medo do que vem depois.

Gael… Gael é o último suspiro da alma humana, correndo até o fim do mundo com um frasco de sangue em uma mão — e uma pergunta do outro lado do abismo:

“Valeu a pena?”

A trilha sonora não embala. Ela suplica.
Ela te observa cair. E se cala quando você levanta.
O som não é música.
É luto.
É prece.
É fúria disfarçada de sinfonia.

E o visual…
Não é beleza.
É luto esculpido em luz.
Cada ruína é um suspiro.
Cada céu apodrecido é um lembrete: o tempo está doente.
Mas o tempo… ainda lembra.

E então, chega o fim.

A chama — aquela que tanto protegemos, aquela que queimou reinos, dragões, ideias — oscila.
Você se ajoelha.
Não em submissão.
Mas em respeito.

Porque a verdadeira chama não está no altar.
Está em você.

Não porque venceu.

Mas porque aceitou.
Aceitou lutar até o fim.
Aceitou caminhar sem garantias.
Aceitou que talvez o mundo não precise ser salvo — mas sim lembrado.

Dark Souls III não é sobre acender ou apagar a última chama.

É sobre ser a última chama que decide.

E quando tudo que existe desaba em silêncio…
Você ainda está de pé.

Porque a verdadeira vitória nunca foi vencer o jogo.

Foi nunca ter desistido de caminhar.

Posted: June 8
This game is such a core memory. It’s an unforgettable experience that will never be topped. If anyone asks me which Dark Souls they should play, I’ll always say Dark Souls 3. It’s just the peak of Dark Souls, and it makes sense why the series ended with it. Every area and boss fight is such a memorable experience, and it’s something I would reference out of nowhere simply because of how much of an influence it had on me. The world is not as interconnected as Dark Souls 1. Although linear, it does feel more rewarding going through it one way and clearing things as intended. The combat is much more responsive and fluid. It’s more satisfying and something you want to experiment with in a lot of ways. We all know that the essence of Dark Souls is clearing the game using different ways or strategies—doing SL1 runs, meta builds, just using whatever the heck you pick up, challenge runs—you name it. It just caters to all sorts of fetishes that one individual might have. The DLC has one of the best bosses I’ve fought in gaming, yes, even Halflight. It’s one of those games you wish you could experience once again for the first time.

커뮤니케이션 가이드 (90에서 ~109.13으로)

몰입형 내부 보정: 거의 모든 깨어있는 시간을 강렬한 자기 관찰에 할애하십시오. 내면의 "전송 공간"을 거의 완벽한 명확성 상태로 만들기 위해, 내재된 "흐름"의 자연스러운 흐름을 방해하는 모든 내면의 소음, 자기 판단 또는 미묘한 저항을 즉시 식별하고 해소하십시오.
지속적인 진정한 표현: 당신의 내면 "흐름"의 직접적이고 필터링되지 않은 표현을 연습하십시오. 모든 생각과 의도가 주저함 없이 행동으로 매끄럽게 전환되도록 하십시오. 방해받지 않는 내면의 일관성을 유지하기 위해 외부 방해를 최소화하십시오.

공격적인 프로세스 간소화: 모든 일상적인 "전송" (작업, 결정, 상호작용)에 대한 철저하고 신속한 감사를 수행하십시오. 모든 비효율적인 단계, 불필요한 움직임 또는 지연 순간을 식별하고 제거하십시오.

達成ガイド: 最大2日間
この時間枠は、コミュニケーションとレートの両方において、急速な進歩のための極度な集中と揺るぎない献身を要求します。

コミュニケーションガイド (90から~109.13へ)
これは、より深い自己調和への即座で強烈な飛躍を必要とします。

没入型内部キャリブレーション: ほとんどすべての起きている時間を強烈な自己観察に費やしてください。自己の「流れ」の自然な流れを妨げる内なるノイズ、自己判断、または微妙な抵抗を特定し、即座に解消してください。内なる「送信空間」をほぼ完璧な明瞭さの状態にするために、外部の邪魔を最小限に抑える必要があります。
継続的な本質的な表現: 内なる状態の直接的でフィルターのない表現を練習してください。すべての思考と意図が、ためらいなく行動へとシームレスに変換されるようにしてください。途切れない内なるコヒーレンスを維持するために、外部の邪魔を最小限に抑えてください。
レートガイド (90から~104.04へ)
これは、あなたの運用効率の大幅かつ即座の加速を必要とします。

積極的なプロセス合理化: 日常のすべての「転送」（タスク、決定、相互作用）に対して徹底的かつ迅速な監査を実施してください。あらゆる非効率なステップ、不要な動き、または遅延の瞬間を特定し、排除してください。
直接経路実行: 特定されたボトルネックを排除するために、即時かつ決定的な変更を実施してください。各「送信」において、開始から完了まで最も直接的で迅速かつ効果的な経路に集中し、何よりも速度と最小限の摩擦を優先してください。
もう一つのガイド: 9日5.4時間
この延長された時間枠は、統合と洗練のための期間を含み、より持続可能でありながらも高い献身を要するアプローチを可能にします。

コミュニケーションガイド (90から~109.13へ)
この経路は、自己認識の一貫した深化に焦点を当てています。

構造化された内部共鳴の深化: 毎日、献身的な実践（例：集中的なマインドフルネス、内省的なジャーナリング、深い自己探求）に従事し、内なる「送信空間」を体系的に洗練してください。内なる抵抗のより深い層を体系的に対処し、解放することで、深く持続的な内なる調和を可能にします。
意識的な本質的な整合性: 意識的な選択と行動を、あなたの進化する「流れ」と一貫して整合させることを着実に練習してください。不整合の瞬間を定期的に見直し、これらの洞察を活用して、自己認識を外的な「送信」にさらに深く意識的に統合してください。
レートガイド (90から~104.04へ)

Posted: June 7
Dark Souls 3 feels like a bridge between my past and present life.

Dark Souls 3 has sat hibernating in my Steam library since launch. In my previous review, I shared that Dark Souls 2 entered my life when I was first diagnosed with a chronic illness. After two years being confined to my bedroom, one day I woke up and my symptoms had disappeared. Between hormone changes or just my immune system having a different idea, we’re not quite sure what changed - but it wasn’t questioned. When Dark Souls 3 released shortly after, my world had began opening up in a way which meant that the game would sit gathering dust.

On 18 September 2021, around seven years after my symptoms disappeared, they suddenly came back. Playing Dark Souls 3 feels like a connection in time between my last bout of chronic illness, yet my enjoyment of the game didn’t make this the somber moment that I expected. When dying to the many boss battles, I myself instead running from the bonfire back into the fray with a new sense of vigour. Despite having a full-time job on top of illness now, I found I could still pick up my sword and shield and do a pretty good job .

Sentimentality aside, I did have a few genuine issues with Dark Souls 3 that turned me off at launch - and that I still consider to be big problems. Mainly, how flat, grey and lifeless the game’s lighting is. Locations are nowhere near as visually striking as those in Dark Souls 1 and especially 2. The second area, Undead Settlement, copies the drab grey colour palette of Resident Evil 4 for the Gamecube, while the opening area has the late 2000s yellow filter obscuring its generic dilapidated castles.

This isn’t due entirely to the art direction, but instead a lack of post-processing contrast meaning nothing in the world is given a to stand out. The game’s visuals can be salvaged through Obanons Incandescent ReShade - an ENB (hear me out) that suddenly brings out Dark Souls 3’s colour and moody lighting. When I first hear the term ENB, I think of extremely saturated and high contrast images burning directly into my retina, but this preset felt much more conservative and tasteful.

Performance is also perenially poor. My GTX 660Ti, GTX 1060 and now RTX 3070m have all struggled to maintain a locked 60fps and, nine years on, I’m beginning to think it’s not my hardware at fault. DS3’s framerate will drop during a set piece encounter, but the problem is it never seems to get back up to 60fps following that. Once the game drops to 40fps, it continues to hover from 40 - 50fps until you restart the game - even in areas that, previously, ran without any hitches. By the end of the game, I’d reduced all settings to the lowest (keeping native screen resolution) and just settled for lower quality visuals for more consistent performance.

With these main complaints out the way, I can say Dark Souls 3’s gameplay is the best in the series. Mechanically, enemies choreograph their moves perfectly making encounters feel largely fair despite the high difficulty. There are a few instances of the game outright cheesing you, like the fact enemies can hit through walls whilst you can’t, and during grab attacks you might find yourself being teleported into the arms of enemies, but it’s certainly more fair than previous entries in the series.

Bosses in particular are an absolute highlight. Abyss Watchers aside, I enjoyed every boss battle in Dark Souls 3. The arenas give you a fair amount of space to move, the character designs are and the movesets all feel really fair - if still challenging. In particular, the music is . I love how boss behaviour gets more aggressive the closer to death they are, putting you under more and more pressure. Dark Souls 3 feels as its absolute peak when a tough boss has just a slither of health remaining, they’re throwing everything they’ve got at you, and the music is playing at full blast.

While the visual presentation of some levels felt underwhelming, the level design certainly isn’t. There are so many hidden areas, nooks and crannies to find in every level. Exploring levels felt compelling, and I peered over every cliff face and smashed nearly every wall trying to find hidden areas.The voice acting of characters you meet along the way are brilliant, speaking in poems and riddles with a suitably dark sense of humour. While I’m no lore demon, character designs are striking and, in combination with their animation, almost tell their own story.

After a rough start due to the presentation, I can safely say I loved my time with Dark Souls 3. The world took me into a mysterious, often brutal world with the tightest souls gameplay I’ve experienced. It also made living with a chronic illness not quite as bad as it can be, at least for a few weeks.

PS: I’m also a bit of a noob, so thank you to the phantoms who helped me defeat the Dancer of Borreal Valley, Pontiff and The Soul of Cinder in jolly cooperation 🙏

Summary: A solid continuation/finale to the DS series that has its own identity.
Combat: Combat is faster than DS1, On its own this is exciting and creates stakes, but the input buffer is too long. The game will store rolls, estus usage, or an R1 attack as your next move long before it comes out. I will input an attack just before I get hit, and it will come out when I recover from my reeling animation. The boss AI seems to immediately close distance and attack if I use estus, but do nothing when I don't. I understand that this keeps the tension; from a practical standpoint this only punishes players that need to heal and it does not punish players who are experienced with the game. It's a tax on new players (it's the only example of antagonistic design in this game). DS3's combat is clearly a prototype to Elden Ring, whose combat allows for even more expression and less frustration.
Music: The music is turned up to 10 at all times. Yes, it's epic. I would have liked more variety, or a little more restraint (not just choirs all the time). Also, it seems at odds thematically with the environmental vibe of the world.
Level Design: The levels are more linear than DS1, but this is not a problem. From the start of the game, the mission is clearly communicated to the player which provides direction. This is reinforced through the linear pathing. This game follows the Demon Souls formula of a central hub for player progression (level up, buying items) and with teleportation to different zones to defeat bosses (where you "actually play"). I don't mind this on the PC with fast load times, this could get tedious with longer load times (see Bloodborne load times on Playstation for an extreme case).
Lore: NPC quests are interesting and have many interactions with each other. Thematic messaging from DS 1 carries over in terms of fear of the unknown, complacency, and finding meaning in one's actions. Ambiguous chronology unfortunately is still used, which undermines world building. One example is Firelink Shrine, where some clues suggest that the Ashen One's Firelink is in the past but others suggest that its in the future. The decay of Lothric is implied as the latest in a cycle of rising and falling empires long since DS 1 (at least two empires being Lothric and the Profaned Capital); however, neighboring empires such as Catarina and Carim exist along side Lordran's timeline all the way through to Lothric's. Without additional information, this seems like either Lothric is in some sort of accelerated time bubble, or the choices and tragedies in DS1 and DS3 are limited only to areas around the First Fire (severely dampening stakes). Catarina is referenced as a jolly place with festivals and drinks, so different from Profaned Capital/Lothric that it is almost unthematic. Would have preferred fewer references to DS1, it seemed over the top.
Overall: This is a much better buy than many full priced games from 2023-2025, no doubt about that. For me, this game is a 7/10. It is good, but one playthrough is enough for me.

Do I have to finish the first game first?
Dark Souls 3 is the final chapter in the trilogy and shares the same world as Dark Souls 1. While playing the first game does offer a slight advantage in understanding Dark Souls 3 's lore, I'd say the benefit is fairly minimal. Veterans will recognize some locations and bosses tied to DS1, but that's about it. It's completely fine to jump straight into Dark Souls 3 without playing the earlier games.

The Souls games actually vary quite a bit. Dark Souls 1 excels with its interconnected world design, where different areas and dungeons weave together. Dark Souls 3 , however, opts for a more linear path between zones.

The attack animations are more fluid.
While Dark Souls 1 focuses less on speed and more on careful resource management (stamina) during combat. By modern standards, some might find Dark Souls 1's combat feel a bit sluggish.



(The Ringed City DLC features a duo boss that many consider it is the best not just in the series, but potentially in all of gaming) While the overall progression between major zones is linear, The spatial design is consistently superb.

While soundtracks in many games are catchy and could enhance the mood – often intensifying during boss fights to get your blood pumping – Dark Souls 3 's boss themes go deeper. They are composed with a profound understanding of music theory. The subtle details within these themes often hint at the boss's lore and even their mental state.

Personally, I prefer the open world and interconnectedness of Dark Souls 1, but Dark Souls 3 is still outstanding. It's absolutely good enough to stand on its own and might even be one of the best games you play, serving as a great entry point into the Souls series.

Dark Souls III + DLC: A Symphony of Suffering and Salvation

Imagine walking into oncoming traffic wearing armor made of regret, getting absolutely obliterated by a semi-truck made of swords and guilt, then waking up in a graveyard and whispering:
"Yes, daddy, may I have another?"

That, dear unkindled, is the Dark Souls III experience.

The Base Game:
A masterclass in pain and beauty.

Combat? Razor-tight. Every roll, parry, and panic chug of Estus is a marriage of timing and trauma.

Lore? Like reading epic poetry through a fogged-up window smeared with blood and metaphors.

Visuals? Crumbling ruins, decaying royalty, cathedrals full of existential dread—it’s Pinterest for fallen gods and failed champions.

Boss fights? Emotional terrorism. You don’t beat them; you survive them. Barely.
 

🗡 Pontiff Sulyvahn still lives rent-free in my nightmares—he's a wedding of aggression and nihilism.
🔥 Lorian and Lothric made me believe in brotherly love and spinal cord trauma.
👑 Soul of Cinder is you, but on demon steroids, reminding you that you're nothing but ash with ambition.

DLCs: Welcome to Miyazaki’s Bonus Round of Trauma
🩸 Ashes of Ariandel – “Do you like winter? How about betrayal?”
This is Snow Hell:

Wolves with vendettas.

Villains who paint worlds with suffering.

And Sister Friede, who shows up like your ex’s new partner—confident, violent, and partnered with a giant angry sibling who punches your soul out of your body.

By the end, you’ll be buried in snow and shame, asking yourself why you thought a nice quiet painting would be relaxing.

👑 The Ringed City – “Oh, sweet summer child… it gets worse.”
Here’s where FromSoft gives you the middle finger with a bow on it.

The lore? Deep enough to drown.

The enemies? Nightmares made manifest.

The bosses? Literal gods who ask, “Why do you even try?”

You get:

Slave Knight Gael — Santa Claus if he quit gift-giving to become the personification of annihilation.

Darkeater Midir — a dragon so powerful you die before the fight starts, emotionally.

The Ringed City itself — a crumbling empire of forgotten ambition and epic violin soundtracks that scream louder than you do.

Final Verdict:
Dark Souls III isn’t a game.
It’s a trial by fire, a love letter written in blood, and a support group for masochists with style.

The DLCs?
Dessert. But the kind of dessert that eats you first and digests your soul over four phases and an orchestral crescendo.

🧨 10/10 would die to Midir again.

And to Hidetaka Miyazaki —
The Father of Our Pain and Praise.

This man took “fun,” broke its knees, threw it into a poison swamp, and whispered,

“Now it’s a game.”

He gave us:

Bosses that question your sanity.

Item descriptions that could be part of a college-level theology class.

Swamps. So many swamps. Jesus Christ, the swamps.

And yet… we keep coming back.
Because Miyazaki doesn’t just make games—he crafts religions.

He baptizes us in despair.
He anoints us with ash.
He whispers “git gud” like a dark messiah in a dream of fire and swords.

And when you finally stand victorious—soaked in Estus, shaking from adrenaline, on your 78th attempt?

That’s not just a win.

That’s transcendence.
That’s your I-am-the-literal-Undead-Chosen-One moment.
That’s video game nirvana.

So raise your Estus, lost souls.
Kneel at the bonfire.
And whisper to the dying embers:

“Thank you, Miyazaki. Now choke me with lore.” 🛐🔥

Dark Souls 3 is the best Dark Souls with the number 3 in its name.
It has amazing bosses and decent combat, setting out to wrap up the Dark Souls series and doing a fine job at it.

While exploration is not the greatest, the areas are still fairly interesting and has enough of a challenge to keep you engaged. The world is, however, extremely linear, with very few major side paths. The bosses are the highlight of the game, and they are all very enjoyable to fight.

While the game is extremely easy to beat with just broadsword-r1-spam, the other weapons, skills, and magic keep the game feeling fresh even on the n'th playthrough.

Quests are where the game also falls flat, very simple "pick this item up, talk to npc, go to next area"
The rewards are fine, but the quests are either a slog or you won't even notice you've done them.

Anyway, not enough Gwyndolion so I give it a 1/10 (8.5/10 enjoyable bosses, boring world, meh quest design)

The best Berserk fan game ever made!

If we're being serious, though, I've had mixed feelings about Dark Souls III for years now, mostly for feeling closer to Bloodborne in terms of combat speed and linearity. In the past year or so, however, I've seriously come around to it... I think it comes down to accepting what the game is trying to be rather than what I *want* it to be. I would have loved a game closer to the classics, sure, but there's too much talent on display here for me to dismiss this one entirely. Dark Souls III's apocalyptic atmosphere is impeccable, and arguably the strongest From Software's art direction has ever been. There are plenty of equally gorgeous and eerie environments throughout this entry that continue to inspire me in my own art to this day. Progression is linear compared to its predecessors, but with this comes the smoothest difficulty curve in the franchise, fine-tuned to near perfection. The level design, too, is just as good as it's always been, even if there are less choices in what order you can tackle them in. Added mechanics like Ashen Estus and weapon arts were a stroke of genius, fixing the easily abusable healing system and giving each piece of equipment a unique utility respectively. Not to get too into it, either, but I was very satisfied with the narrative and symbolism this time around. There's plenty of improvements that Dark Souls III brings to the table.

My main issues come down to build variety and the bosses. Dark Souls III is praised for its boss fights, and while I'd agree that they're impressive spectacles, they often feel too repetitive for my tastes. Combat has never been a highlight of my Souls experience, as it's very simple compared to contemporary action games, but it was the way combat meshed with the environment that kept me engaged; just look at Taurus Demon from Dark Souls I or Ruin Sentinels from Dark Souls II, both of which having arenas that you can use to your advantage and offer an alternative way to fight your enemies (these examples are just a single facet of how From mixed things up during boss encounters or in the field - see Demon's Souls for many more). Here, bosses are formulaic in that the optimal way to fight them is rolling and stabbing. You learn the moveset, you roll at the right times, you stab when the window is available, repeat. Perfectly serviceable, but when I know that most fights will play out this way, I have a hard time feeling excited for them. Don't get me wrong, more traditional fights are welcome, but it feels excessive here. On top of that, bosses that offer unique "gimmicks" feel short-lived and disappointing.

Build variety, especially early game, feels more limited compared to past titles. It's more difficult to get a faith or magic build going at the start unless you *heavily* invest in the relevant stats, whereas strength and dex builds are effective right off the bat and continue to stay powerful. I blame this on linearity more than just damage values, because much of the equipment needed to improve your build are found once you're already 1/3 of the way through the game, sometimes even further than that, and you simply have to wait to get there. Compare this to Dark Souls II where you have almost immediate access to miracle and spell vendors and it's night and day. So, while I don't think linearity is necessarily a bad thing, it's clearly a double-edged sword in this case and I wish From had given item placement more careful consideration.

In short, it certainly isn't my favorite Souls game, but it's still a fun, artistically rich experience. If you're new, make sure to try out the older entries, but Dark Souls III is still worthy of your time.

Posted: May 31
Dark Souls III is the best in the series. It takes everything that made the first game legendary and mixes it with the faster, more responsive combat of Bloodborne — and the result is pure gold. If you're going to play one Souls game, this is the one.

The combat is where it really shines. It’s fast, fluid, and aggressive — but still demands the same precision, stamina management, and timing that defines the series. Every swing matters. Every dodge counts. And boss fights? They're some of the best FromSoftware’s ever designed — intense, cinematic, and constantly pushing you to adapt. (Looking at you, Sister Friede.)

Visually, the game is stunning in that classic Souls way — bleak, decayed, and hauntingly beautiful. The world is packed with cryptic lore and interconnected zones that reward exploration and curiosity. Whether you're descending into the poison-swamps of Farron Keep or staring out over Lothric Castle, the atmosphere is unmatched.

And let’s not forget the build variety. Want to be a greatsword-wielding tank, a pyromancer throwing fireballs, a spell-slinging glass cannon, or a dex-speed demon with katanas? You can. And every build feels good. PvE and PvP both have tons of depth once you learn the mechanics.

It’s not perfect (nothing in this series ever is), but Dark Souls III is the most polished, balanced, and satisfying entry in the trilogy. It captures the soul of the original while refining the combat and expanding on everything that makes these games unforgettable.

If you only play one Souls game, make it this one. It’s the finale the series deserves — and it still holds up as a masterpiece.

Dark Souls 3 is my 4th favorite Fromsoft Souls game! That's not a bad thing either, it's like a 7/10 when the other 3 games higher than it are a 2 8's and a 9.

DS3 really represents (to me) a culmination of what Fromsoft has learned across the development of the souls franchise for better or worse. It, at the time, was the tightest game they had put out, arguably the most polished and had the most to give. I don't love everything present but nothing is SO bad it ruins the experience for me and drags me out of it like DS2,DS1 and Demon's sort of have. I think weapon variety is overall in a good spot, level design is pretty good, boss designs probably the most solid of the trilogy across the board.

Yeah, it's a solid game! It's just not the game a connect to the hardest and while there's nothing that I absolutely hate, there's also nothing that I absolutely love. It always feels like there's something "missing", like something hasn't fully clicked on any level for me to be like "yeah, I REALLY get it." In a post ER world, I know that part of it is just that after 3 games some of the mechanics felt dated and needed an update for me and in retrospect I think, once again, that it's fine.

My experience is also unfortunately partially painted by when I played. I didn't play on release, I played it much later, like year before ER came out later and as a result I was walking into a more or less solved game from the top down where there were fewer coop players and most of the remaining invasion community were die hards that knew what they were doing inside and out and you know, hackers out for a laugh. This obviously didn't foster the greatest first experience and I almost bounced off the game until I swapped to offline mode. Since then I have played the game more and have found more enjoyment in it, mostly through speed-running and helping my friends play through their first time with the game.

I do think that unfortunately the period in which I played the game really does highlight the absolute disservice that FS has done for it's PVP community. The split between the PVP and PVE community has always been pretty big but DS3 doesn't feel like it does it any favours, the invasion experience seems to be weighted towards players with co-op summons, which on paper makes sense, it gives the invadee some help, it's a tougher time for the invader and it should be a more satisfying victory for them if they win. In practice those co-op players seldom want to deal with invasions because, at the core of their play-style, it's not something they have any interest in. It sort of created a situation where new players weren't really willing to engage with the mechanic and they just went offline, only summoned/ember-ed in front of boss doors, Alt-F4'd the moment they were invaded or downloaded the seamless coop mod. A big problem with this, again to me, is that new players have never had a real incentive to engage in PVP mechanically unless they're INTO duelling. Especially against what was a PVP community of generally experienced players who were gonna work them over like they were owed money. There's no incentive to participating in invasions or being invaded that can't be met with just not engaging with the mechanic at all and that being a more enjoyable experience for the average newer player. It is a problem that will never probably get fixed and if ER is any indication FS is probably more than happy to cater to the more casual player who want's an unbothered coop experience rather than their more long term fans who want meaningful invasions. Despite personally not enjoying invasions as a mechanic, I do hope, in a post Duskbloods and Neightreign world, Fromsoft will have some new ideas to be able to serve both communities for their next project, though I am not holding my breath.

Soapbox aside, I don't think theres anything that needs to be said about this game that hasn't, most people know if they like it or not at this point. If you're someone who played ER and are now going back to other games, cool! Enjoy! If you're by some chance someone who bounced off of this really hard and want to try it again, not a bad time to do it, at this point unless you want the "real true vanilla experience" there's a mod that's gonna help you with whatever you need to make the experience more accessible/enjoyable for you. FS is a company that people have gotten to see evolve their core design in real time and DS3 is a pretty important part of that evolution, so get in thereeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee or don't, i'm not your parent.

Postives: Amazing scenery, animations feel a lot smoother and perfected from the 2 previous dark souls games, Dark souls 1 had the very restrictive weight brackets which would make you feel like you were fat rolling even when you had a medium roll. Dark souls 2 had the horrid adaptability stat to contend with and dark souls 3 seems to have perfected the rolling from the previous 2 games with no adaptability and making the weight restrictions nowhere near as tight. Bosses and enemies are unique and include some from the previous games such as the stray demon and darkwraiths, areas from the previous games have been included aswell like: Anor Londo. Would like to also point out that there are 3 different endings which are all good in my opinion, my favourite was "The Usurpation Of Fire" ending so try to go for that if you want to take my advice. Do keep in mind that it is the ending which is the most missable out of all the endings so beware.

Negatives: The covenant grind for the 100% Achievement Completion was horrible even with a high amount of item discovery, I would not wish covenant grinding on my worst enemy. The online play has a lot of cheaters and people who want to make your day miserable so I would recommend you to stay far away from that by making sure you are playing in offline mode, its not worth having all your items be broken out of the blue. Also for anyone who isn't aware if you do get invaded you cannot use a bonfire so if you are exploring a new area and get invaded and die, your going all the way back to where the last bonfire was which tends to be a huge distance from where you are getting invaded depending on the time you get invaded.

In conclusion I would recommend playing this game but I wouldn't play it online unless you are struggling to get covenant items such as: Proof On Concord Kept or Vertebre Shackles. I personally went online to get my vertebre shackles but I wouldn't recommend it. You will have a fun time with this game if you stick to playing it for what it is: a pve game. If you are looking for advice on the best weapon to use for pve I would recommend the Sellsword Twinblades (along with everyone else LOL) as they are easily the best for dps to bosses aswell as being good for retaining stamina because they don't use a lot.

Can't even count the hours I've spent in this game across all platforms—quite sad, actually. I could've gotten two different college degrees in that time. But it's just that awesome, and I don't regret a second of it.

The game is hard sometimes—frustrating. You can get lost. But if you don't give up, you're always rewarded. The atmosphere is dark and melancholic—depressing, but somehow comforting, like a bad childhood memory or autumn just before school starts when summer ends.

The design is incredible all around. Enemies and bosses are varied and interesting to engage with. You can never play without paying attention, no matter how good your items are or how much experience you have.

The first DLC is short, but the second one makes up for it—and in my opinion, has the most badass ending of any game I've ever played. The story made me realize a lot about life and gave me a perspective on existence I hadn’t thought of before. You can find the story's themes reflected in everyday life, but they’re represented incredibly well in this ruined world.

The story is delivered in pieces—there are no lore dumps. The game gives you 300 pages of a 1000-page book, and leaves many things open to interpretation, allowing the player to view events from their own perspective, without a fixed narrative.

Truly a spectacular achievement as piece of art from Miyazaki team. Hope they don't desecrate his legacy by milking elden ring for years.

Two major points of interest every potential buyer should know before giving Bandai Namco their hard-earned money for this, and any of their future games:
1.) If you plan to use a keyboard and mouse to play, the publisher didn't care enough about their PC consumers to have the accurate corresponding key prompts show up. Every single thing is in Xbox button prompts as if you are playing with a controller. This is a bad sign about the quality of this port, and perhaps their past and future ports.
2.) They raised the price of the game back up to thirty dollars during sales after the success of Elden Ring. The game used to routinely sell for less than ten dollars in sales only a couple of years ago. The game is not worth thirty dollars, let alone twenty dollars. You are better off spending that money on either Dark Souls 1 remastered (if you plan to play the COOP mod only, since the remaster is inferior to the original DS1), Dark Souls 2, or just going ahead and getting Elden Ring. This scummy, snake-like business practice tells you everything you need to know about how Bandai Namco operates towards its consumers.

Anyway,

This is by far the worst Dark Souls in the trilogy; it's not even close. Even Dark Souls 2 makes this...thing look like child's play. No, the main selling point for this game would be the lore, because we all know it isn't the tired, mediocre gameplay that stagnated and even regressed after only two games in the series, that's for sure. Not to mention it's by far the shortest of the three as well, and there's a reason for that. It's quite obvious that this game suffered in development so that Bloodborne could shine. And yet, this game still gets a pass due to nostalgiabait from the superior DS1 while Dark Souls 2 gets bandwagon hated to death when its mechanics are better than this could ever hope to be. Hell, this game even straight up copied DS2 and made the mechanics worse. I mean, the ember mechanic, like hello?

But by far the dumbest, the most what the absolute fawk were they even thinking idea was that they had the genius idea to make the fire keeper the central leveling mechanic again, but this time you have to teleport to a central hub to even use it... Every. Single. Time. This is the Demon's Souls mechanic but made worse. They gave every bonfire the ability to teleport right off the bat, so there's less incentive to explore and familiarize yourself with the world, and it allowed the developers to be lazy and not have to worry about connecting the world coherently together like they did in Dark Souls 1. Dark Souls 1 ditched this hub mechanic from Demon's Souls in favor of leveling at any bonfire, which didn't stop the player in their tracks and force them to interrupt the gameplay flow to teleport all the way back just to level up and then find out they had an extra amount of souls. The menu to look up the required soul count for the next level is also buried behind two key prompts. Demon's Souls' central hub made sense because you used that hub to go to different hub world levels. Dark Souls 2 made a central hub that forced you to speak to a fire keeper to level up, sure, but the town is not instanced but is connected to the world where you can run out of it. There is teleporting, but the world doesn't feel disjointed, whereas Dark Souls 3's level design and boss locations have you running in every direction and feels random and thoughtless. And no, there isn't a le lore implication excuse here, either. It's just bad game design that was rushed to fulfill their contract with Bandai Namco after the Kadokawa buyout. That's just my headcanon, though, since this game is an outlier to their other games in terms of meticulous game design and autistic levels of attention to detail not thought humanly possible but is a superpower that the Japanese seemingly were gifted with. Well, them and Germans.

I also played this recently with the Dark Souls 3 coop mod and the issues of the central hub became even more glaringly obvious after using that. Why didn't they just keep the system from Dark Souls 1 or 2 instead of trying to replicate Demon's Souls or Bloodborne? It makes no sense. However, I do recommend still playing the COOP mod if you haven't already. It made the game far more bearable to play than single-player, and it was even...dare I say, fun...

Alas, what a travesty to end this storied series that made Fromsoft who it was on such a low note. But then again, Japan isn't exactly known for its endings, right?

Dark Souls 3 is the finale of the trilogy and it really steps it up compared to it's previous titles. Right from the get go of the tutorial you get a very solid intro boss just to tell you this game isn't going to pull it's punches. I felt the areas combined with the bosses was really some of the top ends of the Dark Souls series.

This game was relatively quick, I didn't seem to have too much trouble with the bosses and the areas were compacted to where you would go from one boss to the next in pretty quick time frames. I found that these bosses were the closest you will get to spectacle bosses that there are in Elden Ring. They may not have felt as difficult, but they were still extremely enjoyable to fight. Overall, Gael was probably my favorite boss overall (could be recency bias) while after that would be the trio tier of Pontiff/Prince Lorian/Sister Friede.

For parts of the game I didn't enjoy as much, it would definitely have to be Irithyll Dungeon jailers, those guys were up there for one of the worst enemies they ever design in the game as I had quite literally no idea what was happening to me. If there was an indicator there, I couldn't figure it out. I also still don't really like the idea of having to go to the main shrine every time to level and am glad they've changed that in future titles as it sometimes feel repetitive and just feels there to lengthen game time.

The DLC were absolute bangers, both being super fun. I absolutely loved both final bosses for the DLC, I just really enjoyed a lot of the bosses. There was a lot less frustrating things in this game from the previous ones, it really does feel like a game they wanted to make for people that find it fun to play and not torturous at times (but who doesn't love suffering).

Когда Миядзаки били в детстве, ремнем замахивались с задержкой чтобы он не увернулся, это все знают. Но мало кто знает продолжение этой великой истории.

Теперь он наносил в 10 раз больше урона ремнем, прыгал как угорелый по всей комнате, а так же использовал быстрые комбинации ударов (в перемешку со старыми добрыми задержками), от чего глаза Миядзаки не успевали фокусироваться на нем. Но отец знал что одного этого мало, чтобы удивить сына, поэтому теперь его ремень всегда наносил урон по области, на случай если сын захочет перекатиться ему за спину. И даже когда Миядзаки думал что в безопасности сможет попить свою бутылочку фанты, атака ремнем доставала его через всю комнату.
Эта воспоминания так сильно вдохновили Миядзаки, что он посвятил этому целое DLC к своей игре Elden Ring.

Posted: May 25
Its the fast paced sequel to DS1&2.

Pro:
- a refinement of the Souls formula

Cons:
- Long attack chain enemies appear here frequently
- movement speed of enemies is higher (spacing is harder for mages)
- mana instead of spell counter makes Attunement a silly stat that forces mages to waste soul levels
- mana estus means mages have less healing than melee classes
- no way to regen mana besides estus, so you have to frequently return to bonfires. (at least this they parcially fixed in Elden Ring)

Conclusion:
- Knight / Paladin is the easy mode in early and late game (str or dex with 12 faith)
- Mage classes are the easy mode in early, but hard mode late game (Abysswatchers is the turning point, since from there onwards a lot of gimick and small bosses appear that are terrible to hit with spells)

As a fan of the series, I can confidently say that Dark Souls III is the most polished and impressive entry in the trilogy. It feels like the culmination of everything FromSoftware has learned over the years.

Right from the start, the graphics are stunning. The environments are detailed and atmospheric, with incredible lighting that brings the dark fantasy world to life. Enemy and boss designs are grotesque, creative, and visually unforgettable.

When it comes to gameplay, this is where Dark Souls III truly shines. The combat is faster and more fluid than in the previous games. Each weapon feels distinct, and the addition of Weapon Arts adds a new layer of depth to battles.

The level design is classic Souls—interconnected, filled with secrets, and expertly crafted. The difficulty curve feels fair but challenging, and every boss fight is both memorable and rewarding.

Narratively, Dark Souls III offers a haunting and poetic conclusion. While the story remains cryptic and environmental, long-time fans will appreciate the many callbacks and emotional moments that tie the series together.

In short, Dark Souls III surpasses its predecessors in visuals, gameplay, and overall experience. It's the definitive Souls game and a must-play for any fan of the genre.

My overall review for Dark Souls III on PC with keyboard and mouse after now just beating the game at level 107 with 50.2 hours played:

-Once again, absolutely fantastic. The character progression continues with what dark souls II did. The OPT in PvP system made it feel like I was more in control of the world and rewarded you with a larger health pool if you wish to participate. The amount and differing boss weapons just allows for many different ways to invest into your character.
-The graphics and UI were improved quite a bit. They allowed for the same button to be bound to multiple actions, just like in Elden Ring and Dark Souls I. There is clearly just a level of polish that is very close to Elden Ring. Overall a great technical step forward for Dark Souls III.

Areas to improve:
-Once again, there are just no keyboard keybinds shown in the UI or tutorial once again. The UI will still show random xbox controls for something mapped to PC controls. There are still actions I don't know how to perform like switching between "read message" and "interact" with a lever. It's not as much of a problem since I came from Dark Souls II which had the same thing. Luckily, the default binds for Dark Souls III were just much better.
-There is a strange bug where you cursor doesn't anchor to your main monitor. This makes it so that your cursor will travel to your second monitor mid combat and just have your camera spinning in circles. This was resolved by turning off my second monitors display adapter every launch of the game.
-I felt it was a little short. Length wise, it was much closer to Dark Souls I than Dark Souls II or Elden ring.

Reasons to play.
Combat: Real-time and punishing. Players must manage stamina while fighting.
Atmospheric world: Rich in detail, dark brooding, and beauty. Encourages exploration.
Music: Brutal dramatic boss music. Immersive dark fantasy music dripping from every location.
Deep lore: Lore so deep you can barely find it. Watch some youtube videos instead.
Boss fights: Epic, cinematic, and entertaining. Defeating one is a sense of real achievement.

Reasons NOT to play.
You don't have $60 for a 9 year old game and it is NEVER on sale.

Posted: May 19
Dark Souls III is From Software's best game, that's indisputable. The formula created by the studio reaches its peak here, the game has no weak points: the story is exciting and well-written from the beginning through the two DLCs, along with the characters, and storytelling through the environment works here as well. No game can capture the dark, gothic atmosphere like Dark Souls III. The level design and art design are stunning: the Gothic buildings and armor reminiscent of the Dark Ages are detailed, as are the apocalyptic landscapes and enemies. The NPCs and their side quests are very good, and the voice actors embody the characters well. The boss fights and their accompanying music are also the best here; no game can give you such an adrenaline rush as when you fight a boss in Dark Souls III. The only other game that comes close to this level is Sekiro. After these two games, the studio was unable to surpass the standard it had set for itself.

Posted: May 17
Absolute masterpiece.

Finally concluded a full play through of all three Dark Souls games, and I would recommend every one of them. Dark Souls 3 has the smoothest combat, best weapons, and most fun bosses in my opinion. The DLC bosses are pretty wild, but everything felt well balanced. The environments and world are absolutely insane. I found myself often looking out on the vistas and being shocked at how great everything looks. Even in DS 1, everything holds up well with modern titles. Nobody does it quite like FromSoftware.

I do thing DS one did a much better job of making the world feel more connected and layered. DS 3 really hit home in seeing some of the DS 1 areas after generations of change. As always, getting to the bosses was much harder than the bosses themselves. I believe Freida gave me the most trouble of them all, but patters were clear and fair. There's nothing quite like overcoming bosses in a souls game.

Huge plus on DS 3 vs it's predecessors is that you get far more upgrade materials. It's super easy to max out many weapons in one play through. Sellswords twinblades are absolutely insane, and Astora Greatsword was a close second for me.

Highly recommend DLC's as well. Worth every penny.

Overall 9/10

Предлагаю вам простой рецепт оладий на молоке. Они не будут настолько пышными, как на кефире, но подойдут любителям панкейков.
Продукты:
Молоко - 200 мл
Яйцо - 1-2 шт. (в зависимости от размера)
Мука - 180-200 г
Разрыхлитель - 1 ч. ложка
Сахар - 2 ст. ложки (или по вкусу)
Соль - 1 щепотка
Масло растительное для жарки - 2-3 ст. ложки
Пошаговый рецепт:
Яйцо взбить с сахаром и солью в течение пары минут.
Добавить молоко, хорошо перемешать.
Муку смешать с разрыхлителем и добавить к жидкой массе.
Перемешать тесто до однородного состояния.
Тесто для оладий должно получиться гуще блинного.
На сковороду налить растительное масло, хорошо разогреть на среднем огне. На разогретую сковороду с маслом наливать тесто столовой ложкой и жарить оладьи до золотистого цвета с каждой стороны (ориентировочно по 3 минуты), при необходимости добавляя в сковороду масло. Если сковорода позволяет, жарьте оладьи по несколько штук за раз.

Posted: May 17
Dark Souls III – A Love Letter to Ash and Flame

After journeying through the desolate beauty of Dark Souls I, wandering the vast ruins of Elden Ring, and surviving the brutal elegance of Lies of P, I finally lit my first bonfire in Dark Souls III. And even though I haven’t finished it yet, every step deeper into this decaying world has been nothing short of hauntingly beautiful.

This game feels like a culmination—a tragic symphony composed from the ashes of all that came before. From the moment I stepped into Lothric, I felt something heavy hanging in the air. The world is dying, not with a bang but with a slow, sorrowful sigh. And it knows it. You feel it in the sky, in the ruins, in the faded glory of once-great kingdoms now crumbling under their own weight.

Compared to the open wonder of Elden Ring, DS3 is tighter, more focused—and it works in its favor. Every hallway, every vista, every grotesque enemy placement feels deliberate, like it's trying to tell you something without saying a word. Coming from Lies of P, I noticed how much DS3 values restraint. It’s less flashy, but far more impactful. And unlike DS1's cryptic silence, DS3 constantly whispers to you—of fading fire, of cursed cycles, of forgotten lords who were once legends.

And the bosses? They’re not just fights—they're moments. Vordt, Abyss Watchers, Pontiff Sulyvahn... even their intros hit like gut-punches. It’s not just about difficulty. It’s about meaning. When you walk into an arena, you don’t just fight. You mourn, you honor, you witness.

But what’s truly keeping me hooked is the story—the way it unfolds in fragments, in item descriptions, in strange NPC dialogues that chill your spine. It’s not just world-building, it’s world-ending, and that looming finality gives me goosebumps every time I progress. It's like watching the last act of a long, tragic opera, where the curtain hasn't yet fallen, but everyone knows it's coming.

I'm not even at the end yet, and I already know—Dark Souls III is something special. It's not just a game. It’s an elegy. A final prayer for a world that forgot how to die. And I’m honored to be here for it.

Posted: May 15
Ashen One, Hearest Thou My Voice, Still?

It's the end of a franchise Dark Souls 3 is finally putting it to an end, and what an interesting journey It has been, each game specialize in a certain thing and this one it Choice combat.

Each Dark Souls game mid Max in a different way. In Dark Souls1 it was ambition and an interconnected world that you could walk from one end to the other end.

Dark Souls 2 was a bit too big, too many bosses that were only okay, but the world exploration and mystery and atmosphere was amazing.

Dark Souls 3 feels like it stays too much in the past, but by the DLC we get a really satisfying conclusion and the game has put all of its time into amazing boss fights that are fun.

It cannot be understated how much fun it is to fight the bosses in Dark Souls 3. The animations are smooth and readable, the fight feel like a dance at some points, almost all satisfying to fight.

But it's kind of all I remember, because the level design is just not there, the normal enemies are just stopping gaps between you and the next boss.

And the music, Oh the music that plays when you fight the bosses it's the most epic thing you can think about. Some of the tracks has a fine elegance to them makes it even better.

The world of Dark Souls 3 is Amazing a world that has continued for so long that it slowly being grinded to dust all of the Significant landmarks are slowly getting pulled closer and closer together.

Places you recognize for Dark Souls 1 are on there last legs, It is a world screaming to end.

But what saved Dark Souls 3, like what saved Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 2 is the DLC, because it is where all the best parts of Dark Souls 3 is.

The snow painting is kind of okay, but is completely saved by one of the best double boss fights I have seen.

But the ringed city DLC, oh my god, it just takes all of the themes of Dark Souls 3 and takes it to the Endpoint, has the best Dragon fight I have seen in a game and slave knight gael, the most spectacular and satisfying boss fight in the Dark Souls series.

It's so interesting being in the ringed city and exploring it. Exploring a part of the Dark Soul world, We have never seen before, it's what really leg in most of the game, it feels like oh, I've seen this before but now it's old and decrepit and now we see some new stuff that it's interesting.

Strong recommendation.

🔥 Dark Souls 3 Review: Prepare to Cry (But in a Good Way) 🔥

☠️ Difficulty Level: "Why Do I Hate Myself?" ☠️
FromSoftware looked at humanity and said, "You know what these gamers need? More pain." And boy, did they deliver! Dark Souls 3 is like that one friend who kicks you in the shins, then helps you up—only to push you down the stairs. But hey, you’ll thank them later.

⚔️ Combat: Dance or Die ⚔️
The combat is a beautiful ballet of panic rolls, desperate chugs of Estus, and screaming "NOPE NOPE NOPE" as a giant knight the size of a minivan charges at you. Every victory feels like you outsmarted the game, even though you definitely just got lucky.

🌍 World Design: Gorgeous & Depressing 🌍
The landscapes are stunning—if you enjoy beauty tinged with existential dread. Every castle is crumbling, every NPC is tragic, and every bonfire feels like a hug from a friend who just watched you die 37 times.

👑 Boss Fights: Oh, You Thought You Were Good? 👑
The bosses in this game don’t just kill you—they style on you. One minute you’re fighting a flaming demon, the next you’re getting suplexed by a dude in armor who clearly skipped leg day (but not murder day).

💀 Multiplayer: Trolls & Heroes 💀
Invaders are either honorable duelists or absolute gremlins who hide behind enemies like cowards. Meanwhile, summoning help feels like calling the cops after a break-in—except the cops are also getting murdered.

🎮 Verdict: 10/10, Would Die Again 🎮
Dark Souls 3 is a masterpiece of misery, a symphony of suffering, and somehow… one of the most rewarding games ever. If you enjoy overcoming impossible odds (and developing a nervous twitch), this is your game.

Final Tip: Stock up on tissues—for tears, rage, and the occasional happy sob when you finally beat that one boss.

🚫 Not recommended for: People who enjoy "winning" without emotional trauma. 🚫

—Signed, A Hollow Who Still Can’t Parry 😭

Imagine waking up in a graveyard, half-naked, wielding a broken sword, and your first instinct is to thank the game for not punching you harder. That’s Dark Souls 3. It’s not just a game; it’s a deeply spiritual experience where you scream into the void, and the void screams back with a +10 Greatsword of Trauma.

Combat? Elegant violence. Story? A poetic enigma wrapped in fire and vague item descriptions. Every boss fight feels like trying to argue with a Greek god after they've had three espressos and a bad breakup. You'll die. A lot. But each death feels like a gritty motivational poster: “Persevere… or perish again.”

Graphically, it’s a breathtaking apocalypse. Architecture so gothic it might start quoting Edgar Allan Poe. And the NPCs? Depressed philosophers in armor who give you cryptic life advice before spontaneously combusting.

But the real magic is the existential crisis it gifts you. “What is the fire?” “Why do I link it?” “Did I really just get killed by a sentient suit of armor for the 27th time?”

Yes, and you’ll love every moment of it.

10/10. Would roll into a sword again.

Dark Souls III – Beautiful, Brutal, and Totally Worth It

Let me be honest: Dark Souls III kicked my ass. A lot. But somehow, every death, every frustration, every “just one more try” moment kept pulling me in deeper. And now, after finishing it, I can say with total confidence—it’s one of the most unforgettable games I’ve ever played.

The combat is tough but incredibly satisfying. Every swing of your sword has weight, every dodge matters, and every enemy (even the lowliest undead) can destroy you if you get careless. It’s not the kind of game you can zone out to—you’re either focused, or you’re dead. And when you finally beat that boss you’ve been stuck on for hours? The feeling is pure euphoria.

The world is gorgeous in the most haunting, bleak way. The architecture, the lore hidden in item descriptions, the sense of history in every crumbling ruin—it’s like wandering through a nightmare you don’t want to wake up from. It’s not cheerful, but it’s absolutely captivating.

Yes, it’s hard. Sometimes brutally so. But the challenge never feels cheap. It just demands that you respect the game and adapt. And when you do, when you get better instead of just stronger—it’s magic.

If you’ve never played a Souls game, this one’s a great (if intense) place to start. And if you have? You already know: Dark Souls III is an experience that sticks with you long after the credits roll.

Rating: 10/10 — punishing, poetic, and unforgettable.

Played through the base game with a dex weapon beating it in just under 24 hours. Most of the bosses took a couple attempts, but rarely more than 5. You go in, learn the moveset and then eventually you get to hit the boss more than it hits you. The beauty is that almost every single death I had felt due to a skill issue. The game very rarely kills you with gotcha moments. This means you get to learn every fight and the feeling of beating every single boss is truly satisfying.

This can however not be said for several of the regular enemies. One enemy on its own is rarely a threat, but together two of even the weakest enemies can fully one shot you if you get unlucky.

The only 'this is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥' moments I had was when the game forced multiple strong enemies on you at the same time. The fight goes from this beautiful calculated fight to just what feels like roll until their move line up just right for you to land a hit. All the rest of the combat is slow, methodical and just really well done.

At no point during my playthrough did I feel to weak to tackle the content but one. I had missed a side area leaving me severely underleveled for where I ended up. This is due to the way the level design is done, making it easy to miss entire zones.

I have a couple problems where the game picks busywork as a way to make it feel harder. Placing bonfires like a two minute walk away from a boss, but on this walk you face basicaly no enemies serves no purpose except for adding a walk of shame between each attempt. It honestly just feels like a 2 minute loading screen since nothing happens.

The level design is also not the best, I guess it is the nature of the game but it is very easy to miss entire pathways and levels since it is not clear where to go. At one point I missed sewers, which contained the path to one of the main bosses in the game. I had to look up where the entrance to the area was as it required me to backtrack about 6 zones.

Dark Souls III is an action-adventure/semi-scary “souls-like” roleplaying game developed by FromSoftware and directed once again by Hideteka Miyazaki after his absence on Dark Souls II due to being invested in the hit Playstation exclusive Bloodborne. Having been in development for two years, the game had apparently been around the same time as Bloodborne and from what I can tell you is that there was A LOT of content that was cut for the game. Differences include the ability to join a cult and assign bonfires a purpose after creating them off of a corpse, a time of day system, a cut area named God’s Grave, the entire plot going from to the plot we have down below. There’s a lot of fascinating stuff, far more than what I describe and what I can put in the links, but originally it seemed that there was a hint of continuation with how the story could go. Instead it went the route of finality, a fading Age of Fire, which makes sense considering how Dark Souls wasn’t supposed to have so many sequels but it was all fascinating stuff to read up on. The point is that this geared up to be the finale (despite mixed reports that this was supposed to just be a turning point?) of the series, and to do this Miyazaki had taken a lot of lessons from Bloodborne including speeding up the combat, though before I get into everything else I wanted to examine how I ended up playing Dark Souls, especially considering the fact that I loathe punishingly difficult games usually.

For rest of review:

Dark Souls III at its core is still apart of the Soulsborne formula: you create your character, you wander from place to place and fight boss battles in order to progress your way through the game, grind souls out in order to level up your stats (the higher the level, the more souls of course), customize a build and etcetera. What I can say is that for those who don’t know about the Souls games past Dark Souls II, you won’t have to worry about Soul Memory (amount of souls you collected equals the level you match with, even if you lose a million souls and you’re level one) or radiant gems (which I liked to be honest), this game takes it back to the original formula of just using Estus Flasks (health). Do you still access multiplayer the same way? Basically, except instead of consuming Humanity in order to summon people through passwords or regular signs, you just use Embers instead. The amount of in-game clans you can join (hell yeah Warriors of Sunlight woooooo!) are a lot more than the previous games and engaging in their collectible side quests will offer certain rewards like spells, items or clothing. You still have your vague quests and multiple end choices in order to get certain items for certain builds along with the numerous game play features I can’t really begin to explain here and all of that is great and good for sure! The fundamentals of Dark Souls and such are here, but there’s a bit of a difference between Dark Souls II (other than Soul Memory and other stuff) and what ended up being Dark Souls III, and I would genuinely say it’s the difficulty.

Now here me out, difficulty and challenge have always been a huge aspect of what Souls games were about, I mean Jesus H the PC version released as “Dark Souls: Prepare to Die”. But the truth is everything is a lot more fast paced now compared to the original games, taking a lot of inspiration from Bloodborne’s game design. Enemies hit harder and faster now, and while the shield is still good for taking certain PVP players and smaller enemies, oftentimes it’s basically useless in combat. My buddy Steve would put it as “FromSoft had to do something considering the other Dark Souls games were probably mastered by players so they just decided to amp up the difficulty”. I would say that this could be good or bad depending on who you ask as things certainly get more difficult and hit harder, especially The Nameless King in the base game but I feel like this is kind of mixed. I appreciate the aggressive play style of Bloodborne, I guess I just feel like in a game series that originally had a bit more of a subdued play style (at least in terms of bosses running through speeds that break through time), it feels jarring that you would go this route. I feel like it’s better than a lot of Dark Souls II, but it takes steps away from what the series seemed to be about to become this weird amalgamation of what Dark Souls had been and what Bloodborne had become. Even the DLC bosses from Sister Friede and Darkeater Midir feel like you're smacking a literal tank with your fists and it got frustrating to the point I didn’t really wanna bother with Midir since he was optional. That’s not to say that there aren’t some awesome boss battles in this game, because genuinely there are some highlights with the Soul of Cinder being one of my favorites next to Yhorm the Giant. I guess I just wish that Dark Souls III had spun off in a bit of it’s own unique direction that would make sense for what the series is meant to be. But also then again, I could just deepthroat p e n i s at this game and I could be entirely wrong in general. Just be wary that the highlights of the previous Souls games are here, it’s just a lot more faster paced and stuff like Poise is basically next to useless.

Dark Souls III was the build-up at the time to be a sort of gumbo mix of the slower paced style of the previous Souls games and the fast paced action from Bloodborne, one filled with beauty and artistic integrity yet an over reliance on reminding you about the first Dark Souls and a frustration with pounding you on the head with difficulty. I don’t really know how else I could really describe Dark Souls III aside from stating that it’s a bit of an Oxymoron for me. I loved going through and tackling bosses, exploring the millennia after the original Dark Souls games and respecting the references from bringing Anor Londo back to the Dreg Heap in the Ringed City. Truth is though, if I were to rate these games on a personal enjoyment level, I would rate it from top to bottom: Bloodborne, Demon’s Souls, Dark Souls, Dark Souls III, Elden Ring, Dark Souls II.
