Dark Souls III – The End of Fire, the Peak of Souls

Some games end with a whimper. Dark Souls III ends with a symphony of ash, blood, and fire. It’s not just a conclusion to a legendary trilogy – it’s a cathedral of despair, triumph, and haunting beauty. And by the time the credits rolled, I knew I’d experienced something timeless.

WHY THIS GAME IS A MASTERPIECE:

✅ Combat honed to perfection.
DS3 takes everything learned from previous Souls games and turns it into a razor-sharp ballet. Fast, responsive, deadly – it rewards precision without forgiving arrogance. From curved blades to colossal greatswords, every weapon feels unique, and every duel feels like a dance with death.

✅ Soundtrack straight from a broken heaven.
I’ll say it: Yuka Kitamura and Motoi Sakuraba composed one of the greatest soundtracks in gaming history. The music doesn’t just accompany the game – it elevates it. From the melancholic swell of the Twin Princes to the apocalyptic thunder of Soul of Cinder, each track cuts through your soul like a flaming blade.

✅ A story told in ruins and whispers.
DS3 doesn’t hold your hand – it challenges your curiosity. Every shattered throne, every cryptic NPC line, every faded banner tells a tale of kingdoms long dead and heroes long forgotten. And the lore? Richer than ever. If Dark Souls was about cycles, this is the final turn of the wheel.

✅ Bosses as both monsters and martyrs.
From the frozen fury of Pontiff Sulyvahn to the tragic brilliance of The Nameless King, DS3’s bosses aren’t just fights – they’re legends. Their themes, their designs, their mechanics… it’s all crafted to leave a mark on your memory (and your broken corpse).

✅ Visual storytelling at its peak.
The world is collapsing – and you feel it. Lothric isn’t just falling apart, it’s burning with dignity. As you climb twisted castles, haunted gardens, and forsaken cathedrals, you don’t just see the end of an age – you live it.

A Few Brutal Truths (that I still love):
❌ It’s hard. No sugarcoating. It will destroy you if you go in mindlessly. But if you embrace the challenge, DS3 gives back tenfold.

❌ The story is hidden. If you don’t pay attention or dig deep, you might miss the heartbreak, the brilliance, the subtle genius behind every name, every item description.

FINAL VERDICT:
Dark Souls III is the final crescendo of a masterpiece trilogy. It blends blistering combat, unforgettable music, and one of the most intricate and tragic fantasy worlds ever created. If DS1 was discovery and DS2 was deviation, DS3 is destiny – the finale that burns bright before fading into ash.

My Score: 10/10 – Ashen one, link the fire… or let it fade. Either way, you’ve earned the right to choose.

A Legendary Conclusion

Dark Souls 3 is everything I wanted from the final game in this trilogy. It has amazing combat, some of the best boss fights in gaming, and incredible levels to explore with a wide range of weapons to use depending on how you want to play the game. To put it short, it's the best in the trilogy and an absolute must play game. Everything great about the previous games in the series is here and it's elevated to new levels. The faster paced combat feels incredible and is the best in the series in my opinion. The story here is actually incredible as well, and truly builds off of and is a worthy conclusion to the other games. Overall, Dark Souls 3 is an incredible experience that is well worth the money.

Combat

Dark Souls 3 uses the standard FromSoft stamina based combat system but at a much faster face than Dark Souls 1 and 2. It feels very similar to Elden Ring in terms of how fast and fluid it is. This system makes facing off against even fodder enemies fun, and engaging. Every action needs to be though about before you do it since running out of stamina can come at the cost of death. Where the combat really shines though is in the boss fights. Nearly every boss in this game is an absolute banger, and some of them are what I consider some of the best in the series, especially in the DLC. There's lots of weapons to use from greatswords, to katanas, to magic, to giant torch hammers and everything in between so there's something for everyone here, and unlike other games, Dark Souls 3 actually gives you enough resources to fully upgrade multiple weapons in any given playthrough so I was able to use 5 different weapons during my playthrough of the base game and DLC without sacrificing power or stats to do so.


Much like the other games in the series, the world of Dark Souls 3 is dark, gritty, and wonderfully executed. This world is much farther gone than others in the series and it feels like it. Everything feels like it's teetering on death, doing everything it can to hold on for just a little bit longer. Dark Souls 3 has some truly stunning locations to explore as well from castles, to cities, to the infamous Dark Souls swamps. I loved exploring the world and I always felt like I was rewarded for doing so whether it be with loot or more story telling and world building. I felt like there was a good variety of places to explore, and I never got bored of any of the areas. There's plenty of secrets to uncover and it was always rewarding to find them.


In conclusion, Dark Souls 3 is a near perfect end to a truly incredible series of games. I can't recommend it enough, but I would say to definitely play at least Dark Souls 1 first. Some of the bosses in this game were true tests of skill and overcoming them felt amazing. I wish I could forget my time with this entire series to experience it all over again, and Dark Souls 3 was the best of them all. For more reviews, follow my

Dark Souls III isn’t just a game — it’s a haunting, breathtaking conclusion to a saga that transcends the medium. It’s Hidetaka Miyazaki’s love letter to those who walked the long, painful path through Lordran and Drangleic — and to those of us who, somewhere along the way, got lost in our own darkness.

This game came to me at a time when I was spiraling. Life felt like a series of failures. Nothing held meaning, and I was sinking. But Dark Souls III, with its bleak beauty and punishing honesty, offered something I didn’t expect: not escape, but understanding.The world of Lothric is decaying, tired, and full of sorrow — but it's also stubbornly alive. Every enemy encounter, every fallen knight, every quiet moment at the Firelink Shrine whispered something deeply human: You are not alone in your suffering. The game doesn’t pity you. It respects you enough to challenge you — to let you fail, and fail again, until you rise.

That rhythm, that fight, mirrored my own life at the time. And somehow, pushing through the fog gates in-game helped me push through the ones in my mind.Dark Souls III is a masterpiece — mechanically refined, narratively rich, artistically unforgettable. But more than that, it’s a beacon for those caught in their own cycle of despair. Through ash and flame, it reminded me that perseverance has meaning, even when the world seems hollow.
Miyazaki gave us more than a game. He gave us a reason to keep going. And I’ll always be grateful.

An absolute masterclass of the series. I know i’m doing a disservice to myself and the lore aspects but i’m playing all of the soulslike games in reverse. Elden Ring was my first game and I was hooked & wanted to see what the rest of the titles had to offer. ER’s exploration was immaculate & rewarding, then enter Sekiro with the best combat system, 0 cheese, sword to sword, incredible fights that test your limits. then Bloodborne, that mid century Lovecraftian horror aesthetic, absolutely beautiful. i can’t gush enough about all of them.

Now enter Dark Souls III, taking all of my favorite elements of the previous 3 and smashing them into one game, brilliantly executed. it’s gonna kick you in the teeth and you’re gonna have a good time doing it. Given that i have two games left in the series, with DS2 being known for cheesy environments and DS1 being a lot of people’s favorites as the pioneer of the series. i’m looking forward to playing both of them and learning the story in reverse, but it’s going to be hard to top this. if you’re a fan of Elden Ring, give this one a shot. the cinematics, the swelling soundtracks, the meticulously crafted areas that reward your curiosity, and bosses that will beat you down, but can be overcome with the enough willpower. both the base game and DLC are so memorable. Sister Friede was a fight that absolutely tested everything i’ve learned as a player of these games until this point. I have no complaints.

DS3 is the PEAK soulslike experience and one that will stay in my heart forever.

Aye, good gentles and gentlewomen! Lend thine ears, for I shall regale thee with a tale of a game most wondrous: Dark Souls III.

The art style, a tapestry of shadows and despair, doth enrapture the very soul. 'Tis a world where beauty and ruin perfom a morbid dance, a feast for the eyes that doth simultaneously delight and terrify. I, myself, have often found myself lost in contemplation of the crumbling castles and grotesque creatures, a willing prisoner of its grim embrace.

And the music! Oh, the music! A symphony of sorrow and triumph, a score that swells and crashes like the very tides of fate. Each note, a testament to the game's grandeur, doth stir the heart and embolden the spirit. Verily, I have wept, I have cheered, and I have felt the very essence of the game coursing through my veins, all thanks to the sonic mastery.

The sound design, too, is a marvel. Every clang of steel, every guttural roar, every whisper of the wind carries weight, pulling thee deeper into this realm of darkness. 'Tis a world one can hear, a world that speaks to thee with the voices of its many horrors and its fleeting moments of grace.

In short, Dark Souls III is a masterpiece, a jewel in the crown of gaming. Go forth, brave souls, and experience this epic tale. Thou shalt not be disappointed!

高牆斷垣風聲咽，
破曉劫火冷如鐵。
龍息未歇王已亡，
刀痕劃過群星裂。

亡者無語，鐘聲再鳴，
薪王不歸，餘燼孤行。
舉劍之時天地靜，
血火之中步虛冥。

城廓沉沉藏異像，
深處教堂聖女亡。
腐泥沼澤埋誓約，
罪業深牢掩瘋狂。

遙見霧都伊魯席，
名為神之高嶺城。
冰階白翼悲歌處，
法王之火染深紅。

轉戰古老至高塔，
擲下命運喚三王。
洛斯里克弟懷猶豫，
哥德里克劍染殤。
拒薪之子終伏劍，
一切仍歸灰燼荒。

其後夢入寒絕境，
亞莉安德爾畫中霜。
雪落鴉羽無聲寂，
烈焰織女喚斷章。
冰原斬鎖舊神血，
劫火又燃破紙牆。

隨後墮入輪外地，
城市千層倒時光。
空洞古神哀無語，
終見教堂殘畫廊。
劍鋒斷雨揮殘夢，
血泊無聲埋瘋狂。

至此赤天燃終焰，
蓋爾逐光至盡方。
老奴持劍畫外走，
背負人類暗魂長。
終戰斷崖血與淚，
唯餘畫筆寫新章。

問我何求何所歸？
不為成神，不為王。
願為灰中一微塵，
化作筆尖落冷霜。

Posted: May 4
Dark souls III
What is this : RPG, with unforgiving combat involving melee weapons and various magic type. You WILL die.... A lot.
95/100
Death : 239
Controller support : Yes
Replayable : Yes
Playtime breakdown: about 60 hours 1st playthrough, 20 hours NG+ and NG++ for items (achievements), 20 hours of tedious grind for covenant items (achievements, not completed yet...)
50$ (DLCs included)

* Rich and beautiful world/setting
* Bosses are most of the time an amazing and challenging experience.
* So many ways to play the game
* Movement is pretty good compared to previous installments of the game
* About 90% of the bosses have fair fights instead of crappy gimmick like those Dark Souls 2 was filled with
* A lot more bosses than previous games are high speed adrenaline filled fights keeping you on your toes. (bosses like Artorias in the 1st game)
* If you take it slowly and methodically, most areas are fair without needing range or consumable cheese. Also the game doesn’t require farming souls to progress: the bosses can be beaten with what you have by just playing the game your way.

* Some gimmick boss remains, most notably Yhorm the giant which .
* As per DS usual, lore is a mystery. I didn’t understand half of it until I watched a youtube lore video at the end of the playthrough.
* Environmental hazards : Lot of bosses arena were made to look pretty. The downside is you end up having crappy benches/chairs/candlesticks in the arena which block your movement. If you are walking through an area that would be fine, but during a high speed boss fight, your eyes are on the boss and getting killed because you backed up in a stupid bench is enraging. In some fun cases [sarcasm] () it evens blocks your view, but not the enemy’s attack which just goes straight through and hit you...
* Camera work. Somewhat related to the environmental hazard, some arenas are just designed in a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ way where you end up in walls where your camera starts acting up and blocking your view of the boss. In some other cases, the boss is just so massive that you can't see it right and the target lock focusses you high in the sky where you can’t hit anyway. Sometimes, it’s also a boss that flies constantly over your head making camera tracking a nightmare.
* ♥♥♥♥♥♥ hitboxes. Some boss’ attacks have real crappy hitbox where the visual of the sword clearly doesn’t touch you, yet you get hit. Or a grab that clearly misses just to have your neck teleport to the boss’ hand. (slowed down replay as proof) Yes, with practice you can get the hang of the perfect timing and direction to avoid those attacks, but I believe the visual should match the attack as this is how you are meant to instinctively know when to roll out of the way and it is the only way you can have a fair fight on the 1st attempt. Luckily this is not a problem with every boss. Additionnal note on hitboxes: some bosses have a body hitbox as well as one for their weapon. Combine that with their massive size and you can get stuck in between the 2 of them with no escape when you come close for a hit, making you just be dragged along until the boss decides to smash you with a free hit.
* Covenant item grind : in classic DS fashion, for 100% achievement you need to beat the game 3 times and run a collectathon of all magic and items, which requires you upgrading covenant for the rewards. The covenant items can be obtained from random drops in the world if you do not want to play online, BUT the grind is worse than both previous DS games. 1st, barely any of these items can be found in the world to alleviate the grind a bit as you run your 3 playthrough. 2nd a lot of the enemies that drop the items needed are not particularly easy to kill, requiring you to take them one at a time and slowly [hence increasing the time required to grind the items]. 3rd the drop rates are horrendous, even with a max item drop build [which requires you fighting with a specific weapon instead of whatever you like].

* Better than DS2 for sure
* At least as enjoyable as DS1
* Lots of fun and value in the game
* Current price may be a bit high for what I believe it is worth.
* Boss move set and design generally nailed, but some work remains to be done on arenas and how it affects the player for optimal game experience

My fondest memory was facing the Nameless King and getting destroyed time and time again. I was filled to the brim with anger, and after hours of deaths I decided to place my summon sign down to both help other players and better understand the boss without penalty. I went in again and again, helping dozens of players, learning the patterns and how many slashes I can get in. Finally, I went in alone in my own world and defeated the Nameless King almost flawlessly (one sweep attack got me sadly.)

Defeating the Nameless King gave me a feeling of catharsis I will never forget. It was like I realized that getting angry over a video game is futile; the game is not an enemy, but a challenge to overcome. Every death is a lesson to learn, and anger will only get in the way of learning.

This game will always hold a special place in my heart, as it shaped how I thought of every game I played afterwards.

FromSoftware’s Dark Souls III is less a game and more a gauntlet—a bleak, punishing, and strangely beautiful pilgrimage through ash and anguish. Released in 2016, the final chapter in the Souls trilogy feels like a greatest hits album of everything the series has done best, wrapped in haunting gothic architecture and steeped in the melancholy of a world on the verge of final collapse.

The gameplay is refined to a surgical edge. Combat is fast, fluid, and brutally fair. Every swing, parry, and dodge has weight and consequence. This is a game that demands your full attention; button mashing is suicide. Yet, once its rhythm clicks, battles become balletic—deadly dances with enemies that punish overconfidence but reward patience and precision.

Level design, always a hallmark of the series, is exceptional here. Lothric Castle, the Road of Sacrifices, and Irithyll of the Boreal Valley are just a few of the unforgettable locations—each interconnected, meticulously detailed, and dripping with lore. Secrets hide around every corner, and exploration feels rewarding, if always treacherous.

Boss fights are both a highlight and a trial by fire. From the towering abyssal beast Pontiff Sulyvahn to the poetic final confrontation with the Soul of Cinder, Dark Souls III delivers spectacle without losing its soul. These aren’t just enemies—they're stories in motion, often silent, but rich with implications for players willing to dig.

The narrative, as ever, is obtuse—told through item descriptions, cryptic dialogue, and environmental clues. It's not spoon-fed, but pieced together like a shattered stained glass window. It’s deeply rewarding for those who engage with it, especially fans familiar with the broader Souls mythos. As a finale, it carries a quiet sadness, a sense of inevitability and reflection, that hits hard by the end.

If there’s a critique to be made, it’s that Dark Souls III sometimes feels like it’s playing it a bit safe. Some bosses echo past glories, and veteran players might find fewer surprises. But what it does, it does with such grim majesty and polish that it's hard to fault it for consolidating rather than revolutionizing.

Dark Souls III is not just a game—it’s a trial, a sermon, a farewell. It's about perseverance, discovery, and the strange satisfaction of being broken and reforged in fire. A fitting and poignant end to one of gaming's most influential sagas.

Score: 9/10

Dark Souls III – A Brutal Masterpiece

Dark Souls III is one of those games that just pulls you in and refuses to let go. From the very beginning, the atmosphere is absolutely unmatched—dark, haunting, and weirdly beautiful. Every location feels carefully crafted, like you're walking through a dying world with secrets in every corner.

The gameplay? Smooth and satisfying. Combat feels mostly tight, responsive, and fair (well, mostly fair). When you die—and trust me, you will—it never feels cheap. It just makes you want to get better. And the bosses? Stunning. Each one is a memorable challenge, both mechanically and visually. They’re like twisted works of art that want to crush your soul.

If I had one gripe, it's that the game doesn’t support cross-platform play. That would've pushed it into true legendary territory. Imagine summoning or invading friends across all platforms—chef's kiss.

Overall, Dark Souls III isn’t just a game, it’s an experience. Brutal, rewarding, and unforgettable. Highly recommend it to anyone who loves a challenge and wants to lose themselves in a world that’s as punishing as it is beautiful.

Unforgettable Experience
I've become a bit of a souls fanatic over the past few months after always avoiding this genre like the plague and lazily grouping Elden Ring and Dark Souls along with all the other generic medieval RPGs. But FromSoftware seriously know how to build an immersive world.

Progression. Peak
You start off weak and thin as a piece of paper, while straight off the bat enemies are ready to group you down and b*tch slap you. At first, it feels unfair—frustrating, badly designed, maybe even boring. But what you’re not realizing is that the harshness is deliberate: the game is telling you to . You cannot mow through hordes of enemies and just get to the next place. You realise you have to stop, observe, be methodical, and plan ahead. , but you persevere. Things eventually start to click into place (after a crap tonne of deaths) and before you know it you're tackling enemies head on that you would have thought unthinkably impossible previously. The way in which DS3 teaches you this progression is masterful and (in hindsight) rarely ever feels unfair. This quality alone makes playing DS3 an engrossing and worthwhile experience.

there are times when you are unfairly pinned against a corner with literally no other option but to watch yourself get beaten to death. This is indisputably unfair and badly designed

Everything in DS3 has been . Enemies, bosses and environments are often sad and disgusting, really pushing the player's imagination into an interesting space, reinforced by the detailed and stunning visuals. When you're precariously tiptoeing into a new area, before wanting to figure out what you're going to do, you just stop and take it all in.

if you're a fan of horror, there is a lot to enjoy here.

DS3's are wonderfully immersive. It's ultimately fairly linear but has labyrinthine elements making exploration not only fun but critical. When you finally unlock that door or kick that ladder down revealing a shortcut you really feel like you've earned it. There are castles, forests, swamps, dungeons, ruins, cliffs and catacombs that have been , rarely feeling stale.

you take damage from falling onto ledges. While this has been implemented to intentionally deter you from just cheesing past levels, it sometimes hinders exploration.

The star of the show. Boss fights are an amalgam of . Early to mid-game bosses teach you, late game bosses test you. There are a few lame fights, but on the whole they are extremely tightly designed. They are but finally defeating them gets you that euphoria few other games reach. A few of the bosses in this game have been the most memorable for me out of all of Elden Ring, Sekiro and Dark souls. Elden Ring indulges a little too much in the spectacle of it all, but .

there are one or two bullpoo fights which feel a little pointless/unfair.

I have never really vibed with the way FromSoft tell their stories (or lack thereof) and this game is no different. The world building through the environments and item descriptions are fantastic for feel and backdrop but the actual main story is vague and sparsely told in a way that makes it difficult to grasp what is actually going on. Maybe I needed to pay more attention and link things together more, but I feel the game should've helped me do that a little better. The main strokes of the story are clear though, but never feel important. This wasn't too much of a detraction; DS3 is about struggles and progression, at least for me anyway.

has been in Spain a full seven years:

Posted: April 28
I don’t know what more I could possibly add to the discourse surrounding this game besides saying that Dark Souls 3 is my favourite game and the best game ever made. Lothric may not be interconnected like Lordran but this isn’t a bad thing when the level design is consistently amazing. The level design doesn’t reach the heights that the b team reached with the DS2 DLCs but this game exhibits the best level design in the series overall. The bosses are undoubtedly the best Fromsoft has ever made and Slave Knight Gael is the best boss fight ever created hands down. The overall pace of the gameplay feels amazing, the mechanics seen in the first two games taken to their extremity. Lore wise, this game is the perfect ending to the series, so good in fact that I really don’t want Fromsoft to ever resurrect this franchise; something something linking a fire etcetera. The art direction is yet again superb and the soundtrack is the best they’ve ever done with Yuka Kitamura ascending to musical divinity. I don’t wish to minimise Motoi Sakuraba’s work as I love him as a composer and think he had some excellent songs especially the Nameless King’s ost but what Yuka Kitamura did for this game is beyond generational. The only real gripe I have with this game besides Fromsoft’s obtuse quest design is how derivative of DS1 this game is. Seeing Andre still being alive for the first time was quite jarring and there are plenty of other examples of obnoxious fan service all the while much of what transpired in Drangleic has been left forgotten. The fact that Aldia is heavily implied to be prince Lothric’s mentor is tasteful but besides this there aren’t very many meaningful callbacks to DS2. But nonetheless, this game, and this series is absolute kino.

Posted: April 28
Dark Souls 3 isn’t just a game — it’s a slow, agonizing unraveling. A world rotting from the inside out, where the smallest, simplest lies poison everything. Promises of salvation, of light, of purpose… they’re hollow echoes in a place that’s long since forgotten what hope felt like.

Every step forward feels like a memory being erased. One by one, the things you thought you knew — about safety, about victory, about yourself — are killed off. It’s a game that doesn’t just challenge you physically, but emotionally. And after hours of pushing through its darkness, you start to feel it: maybe it’s time to put this heart out of its misery. Because you’ve been dying. Dying for too long.

But that’s what makes Dark Souls 3 unforgettable. It never tries to hold your hand. It lets you take what you want and leave when you please. The world doesn’t need you — it never did. And if you walk away, don’t expect it to wait. It won’t. The ship has sailed.

Still, you can’t help but feel the weight of what it gave you. I swear, it offered everything — every little piece of itself. Every haunting vista, every perfectly timed boss fight, every cryptic NPC interaction. But so often, we looked away. Missed the meaning. Got caught in the struggle and forgot to admire the artistry.

And when we finally hit that wall and whispered, “I’ve had enough. I’m moving on,” the game answered in its own cruel, poetic way: “My love was never lost.” But we only realized the depth of that love — the design, the story, the pain — once it was too late. Once all of it was gone.

There’s a constant ache that runs through the experience. The kind that makes you question whether the journey is worth it. The pain and the fights never seem to end, and some nights, you genuinely wonder if you’re still in it for love, or if you’re just stuck. Maybe it’s time to put this obsession out of its misery — because it’s been dying for too long.

And yet, strangely, that’s where Dark Souls 3 finds its identity. In the struggle. In the sorrow. If you’re in pain, that’s how you know you belong here.

Looking back, I gave it everything. Hours, energy, emotion. Every little piece of me. But there were moments when the game felt distant — indifferent. And when I finally decided to let go, it echoed back that same sad truth: love was never lost. Just… forgotten. Until everything faded.

All the years. All the effort. All the silent nights staring at a bonfire’s glow. Those memories — they’ll haunt me. Maybe I’ll take some regrets to the grave. But I won’t be missing this game.

Because Dark Souls 3 didn’t just challenge me. It broke me. Rebuilt me. And in the end, it taught me what it means to let something go after dying for too long.

Posted: April 27
Dark Souls III – Get Good: The Game

You wake up in a grave.
No tutorial. No hugs. Just some guy with a sword the size of a truck waiting to slap you back to the main menu.

Dark Souls III doesn’t care about your feelings. It doesn't care about your hopes, your dreams, or your Wi-Fi connection.
You either git gud or get reintroduced to the "YOU DIED" screen every 30 seconds.

Combat? Tight, fast, brutal.
Story? Hidden in item descriptions written by sad poets.
Enemies? Pure nightmare fuel. Even the trees want you dead.
Bosses? Giant, screaming, multi-phase tests of your sanity.

You will rage. You will scream. You will Google "how to beat Pontiff Sulyvahn" at 2 AM.
And when you finally beat that boss after 43 attempts?
You will feel like an immortal god... until the next hallway slaps you back to reality.

12/10 – Died 500 times. Smiled once. Worth it.

Posted: April 27
I've never played "Elden Ring" or "Lies of P" as of time of writing. Aside from possibly those, DS3 is the best souls-like game ever made in my opinion!

The music elevates all the fights to the greatest heights of spectacle in the whole series. The artistic aesthetic turns even mundane exploration into a visual feast. The combat feels like perfect blend of the best features from all of FromSoft's previous soulsborne games. Nothing about this game stands out as being "bad" to me aside from loading screens after death; I don't think corpse runs are bad, only things that increase wait time between failure and regainung control, especially in games where failure is so frequent.

Thee are four things I subjectively would have liked to be different, but are not necessarily flaws.
-First, the parry timing is a lot less intuitive than in DS1. It feels like it's working as FromSoft intended, but intended awkwardness is still awkwardness.
-Second, boss attack combos don't have very engaging counter-play. I found myself just running away and waiting for the boss to finish their combo, aside from doing something fun like memorizing their patterns and perfect-deflecting each attack, like in Sekiro.
-Third, I somewhat miss the magic attunement system from previous games. Mana-like systems are fine and all, but sometimes the crunchyness of spell-slot-like systems psychologically trick me into liking my magic more thanks to the extra time I spend factoring them into a mage build.
-Fourth, I welcome the special weapon abilities, but I don't know how to feel about them consuming mana. Assuming spells return to the DS1 system, I think I'd get rid of the mana bar and ashen flasks and turn these weapon abilities into "reckless" attacks that cause recoil damage, and just stick with the estus flasks.

Anyway, overall a spectacular game that any enjoyer of the genre should own!

Posted: April 22
THIS GAME WILL CHANGE YOUR LIFE

Before i start my review i have to say a couple of things... wow... what a masterpiece. Also let me address some question you may have from the game.
Do i need to play other Dark Souls to understand Dark Souls 3? Yes and no. If you don't care about lore (which you should) then no, but if you care then you only need to play Dark Souls 1. There are so, many, and i mean like more than 50% of the game is references to Dark Souls 1, so it's a shame to play Dark Souls 3 without that context. Also IMO if you play Dark Souls 1 you can appreciate the upgrades of Dark Souls 3. (For lore, don't worry about Dark Souls 2 keep this one to play after this one).

Anyways i will make the review for people who never played the game.
Okay so, Before i go on a rant, yes it is single player and it has online functions like CO-OP and PVP. Now the co-op is not what you might expect. You need to consume ember and place a mark on the ground so that another player interact with it and then he will come to your world on specific areas so that you both can play together. PVP (non dlc) is used in various ways, the most common is by invading a player, which means, its just invading someones world and killing them.

You see, Dark souls 3 is a game that gives the player the freedom to choose where to go next with almost zero help. Most of the time the path is linear but it can sometimes split in two. Don't worry because you will go back to where you were before so you will have to do both paths anyways.
It's a hard game, so if you like that great, if not maybe not so great but really, give it a try. The graphics are amazing (also turn the brightness in the settings to 0 because the game puts a filter on the game that make it grey), the music is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ epic, the bosses, the ambience, the game is amazing in my view. After you finish the game you can continue a new game with same character but with most difficulty. This goes up to +7 levels of difficulty! So lot's of content.

You will rage quit, you will want to say "this is totally not fair", you will be frustrated. All this things are there because you don't understand the game really. Once you understand the game, how it works, how the animations work, how everything is connected and you know all the places and where to go, then this is a easy game. So with hard work the game will become easy.

Amazing lore, but the lore is not given with cutscenes. Every item you will pick will have a story behind it. So always read the item description and learn more about the fading of the fire.

GAIN THE COURAGE AND BUY THE GAME AND FINISH IT LIKE THE BOSS YOU ARE! If i can finish the game so can you!

I highly recommend. Praise the sun.

Posted: April 18
Amazing game and I think the best one of the original Dark Souls trilogy from gameplay perspective.

It has breathtaking views and scenery that is almost the same level as Elden Ring, I made countless screenshots from views in Anor Londo, Lothric Castle, Archdragon Peak and Irtythill Valley. The artististic implementation of each level is good, and level design is also decent, without unlogical locations like in DS2.

I liked every boss in the game, they are all balanced and very fair in terms of hitboxes, timings - their attack intentions are very clear and even small puzzle bosses like Ancient Wyvern are easy to understand. There were some issues with the camera with some large bosses, but they are neglible and FromSoftware seems to explicitly make camera your second enemy, as we see from Elden Ring DLC.

Most importantly for me - it does not make previous games mistakes, fixing really dumb mechanics, present in previous games that annoyed me there, like placing bonfires really generously, and placing them near bosses or providing shortcuts that really make any boss runback take not more than a minute, which is not the case for DS1 and DS2. Enemies are diverse and placed really well, without hordes attacking and breaking your camera as in DS2. You can really see how FromSoftware, little by little, improves game experience with each new release, how Sekiro and Elden Ring improved such utility tasks even more (with Statues of Marika or even more generous checkpoint placement).

As with all other games of that developer, the music and sound design are also very well made, they never disappoint in that regard.

The things I didn't like are minor and opinionated:
- Overuse of fog walls to hide secret passages, sometimes multiple in a row. There are a lot them in this game, some leading to really rich or lore-improtant locations. Without messages from other players, I would miss 90% of them. I do consider this to be a partially lazy game-design mechanic, as usually there is little clue that a fog wall is present somewhere. I bought the game to enjoy full content of it, but if it's hidden that way, I am not liking that much.
- Very cryptic lore and story communication. I do understand this is FromSoftware style and such lore messaging was one of the core principles of Dark Souls series, still I did not understand most of it in the game, even by reading item descriptions. I watched the credits roll without significant emotional impact, which I had for example in Sekiro or Elden Ring, because I just do not understand if what I did is good, bad or something in between. For that reason I focused mostly on enjoying gameplay and locations. omitting the plot or storyline mostly.

In summary, it's for me the perfect and most finished true Dark Souls game, that I really enjoyed, and definetely recommended to play.

First of I'd like to say that this review isn't based off my enjoyment of this game. I really do enjoy it. However I just can't recommend it to other people to play. I enjoy this game, in spite of the faults that I'm about to list, but I know that for a lot of people who would (justifiably) be turned off from these things which is why I want to make an honest review so that if someone wants to play this game they do so knowing what they are getting into.

One of the most important aspect of a soulslike game is its gameplay. The Most important thing about gameplay (for me) is the responsiveness of controls, and the camera. This game has issues with both.

(Controls) - Key issue with this game has to do with what I think is fromsoft's greatest mistake, the fact that the dodge button is on a key release. This adds what is essentially more 'input delay' between the tactile feeling of you pressing a button and your character making a dodge. While you can train your brain to perceive the correct timing to be on button release with practice, it still takes more time to impliment than it would otherwise. instead of dodge being 1 action (button press) it now takes 2 (button press than release). This adds to what can already be rather significant input lag from the controller cable, processing the input, the time that you do not have i-frames as the animation is starting up, and what you see on the monitor. In practice it can feel like a half second delay or more that you have to do ahead of things in order to properly dodge. This makes things very annoying because you constantly feel like the timing windows are extremely early compared to the animations of bosses. This is particularly a problem for bosses that have very short windups for their attacks, or bosses and enemies who have attacks with several different windup timings.

For a game where dodging is one of the most critical aspects of the game (and running is not), the fact they sacrificed the control scheme when it comes to dodging is inexcusable and it is also poor game design. Additionally, this game punishes alternative builds as the game is very dodge focused.... parrying/blocking are just not as good in this game as they are in other fromsoft games such as dark souls 1, sekiro, or elden ring respectively.

(Camera) - The Camera in this game also tends to have a lot of issues. It is very buggy up close to walls, and it often times just breaks if you are trying to lock onto a taller enemy, which becomes a problem with some bosses. Additionally, sometimes the lockon button will just cause you to immediately look behind you even if there is a nearby enemy. Additionally, lockon completely breaks with any enemy that moves quickly, teleports, or is moving past you.

This significantly effects many bosses in the game such as the nameless king, yorm the giant, the lothric twins, or midir. While Elden Ring had its camera issues with dragons, it had so many bosses that it didn't effect a significant amount of the total. However because Dark Souls 3 has much fewer bosses the impact of camera issues is just much more significant.

(Grinding for 100%). Grinding for the 100% of this game is also not worth it in this year, I'm not sure why I put myself through this at times. Out of the ~75 hours I have in this game... a good 30 were just spent covenant grinding. Because the multiplayer in this game is inconsistent the really best method is to farm covenant items from enemies, which, even with max discovery might only be at a 3% droprate (which you need 30 of).

This is really sadening to me because the rest of the game is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing. Like, Dragonslayer armor or sister fried are some of my all-time favorite fromsoft bosses. Soul of Cinder is such a good final boss as well too. But like, I just can't recommend this to someone, especially someone who might not be experienced with soulsgames... and that sucks... I wish it weren't the case but it is.

As someone who played Elden Ring as my first Souls game, this is the closest thing to Elden Ring outside of Elden Ring that I've played. Don't listen to the people saying it's hard, if you've played Elden Ring it's incredibly easy in comparison. The only boss I couldn't beat first try (I'm an average player at best) was Nameless King, and it took be under 10 attempts. Every boss is fun and well-designed, and although there are still some areas that will get you with that classic Dark Souls stun lock, as long as you're taking your time and only agro'ing 1 enemy at a time, it's not bad at all. If you're having trouble with the boss, there are NPCs available for summoning at almost every boss and/or other players willing to help you out. Of course dogs still suck and enemies will be waiting behind corners to jump you but its expected and once you know whats coming its very manageable. If you've never played a Dark Souls game, then I would recommend starting with Dark Souls 3. The combat is smoother and more fluid compared to DS1 and DS2, and there are tons of options, whether you want to cast magic and keep your distance or rush your opponent with a massive sword. The only negative I have to say about this game is its community. If you are ember'd then EXPECT to get invaded, especially in Anor Londo, but the good thing is there is no shortage of Embers to be found, and you can just kill yourself to avoid being invaded. The people who invade are either good and just want to fight, or (this is the more likely situation), are terrible and want to waste your time and ruin your day. What I mean by waste your time is that they will come and hit you with your back turned, and then as soon as you deal good damage to them, they will run and hide behind mobs so you can't reach them, and then repeat until either you or them have no heals left and if they have no heals left they will just keep running and hiding so you can't rest at bonfires. If you're on the fence about getting the game, I'd wait till it goes on sale, as full price is asking a lot for an older game. Overall, 10/10 game would highly recommend, most fun I've had since playing Elden Ring for the first time. May the Flames guide you!

Dark Souls III is, in my opinion, the best of the entire Souls trilogy. It is the definitive Souls experience and probably my personal favourite.
Compared to the other games in the trilogy, everything is more grounded and features incredible boss fights and annoying combat encounters as they are brutal. The game is very enjoyable despite the obvious difficulty curve. If you are looking for a casual game, don't play this.
Dark Souls 1 is lovely and still a really good game to this day; Dark Souls 2 is a great game despite its many many shortcomings but Dark Souls 3 perfectly rounds off the franchise.

Posted: April 16
This was my first "souls" game and couldn't be happier that I chose this one. Dark Souls III is the game I would recommend to someone that is looking to get into the genre for an accessible but still challenging experience.

Dark Souls III by far has the fastest, most polished, and least janky combat in the trilogy, while also(in my opinion) having the best lineup of both bosses and levels in the trilogy.

The game is also very linear, which may turn some people off. But this allows the game to maintain it's balancing and difficulty curve with less options for broken builds and cheese strats. This is expressed at the start of the game where leveling up is locked behind a relatively hard tutorial boss.

If you're looking for a first souls game that is more open-ended with more options to tune difficulty, then I would recommend Elden Ring, obviously. But for those seeking a truly challenging experience, Dark Souls III is the perfect choice for getting into souls games.

The game does have flaws, such as lacking color palette, and a few bosses being underwhelming. But despite that, this game remains as one of my all time favorites, and experiencing it for the first time is something that I'll never forget.

9/10

What a great end to the Dark Souls Trilogy. This game definitely has the best boss fights in the series, and the graphics are still amazing 9 years later. The music is also pretty good throughout. While I personally enjoyed the world and inter-connected level design of the original Dark Souls, the areas in this game are still fantastic and grandiose. The combat is as refined as possible and the build variety makes fighting enemies and bosses very fun and rewarding. As is expected in a FromSoft game, the story and lore of the world is hidden, but creatively told in an engaging and meaningful way that manages to tie the entire trilogy together into a fittingly dramatic and powerful conclusion. Some people claim that the game relies too much on nostalgia, but I would argue that it is used in a way to bring the series to a meaningful end that rewards those who have invested time to seek out and learn the lore.
While I was initially underwhelmed by the quality of the early game bosses and areas, by the time I neared the end of the base game and the DLCs I would find myself blown away by the quality of the bosses. Each boss somehow managing to top the previous one in difficulty, design, and fun. This is by far the most rewarding game I have played. None of the bosses really feel unfair, and even the weaker bosses still have something memorable about them. I could not have asked for a better ending to my favorite video game series.

Great way to end the trilogy. Easily the best boss roster in all 3. Great music with beautiful tracks. The credit song almost made me cry. It would be easier to say which bosses I don't like (Oceiros & Crystal Sage). The environment story telling is top tier. The NPCs are all memorable and some are even returning from the previous games. The level design isn't bad either and surprisingly Grand Archives, Irythyll of the Boreal Valley & Cathedral of the Deep are my favorite places.

I don't know the reason why but the Silver Knight Sword and Spear are not obtainable by players which is disappointing condsidering they are obtainable in DS1. Most of the early bosses are gimmick fights which can be annoying at times. I would have loved if there were a little more references to DS2 but that's just me.

Overall an amazing experience and do recommend after finishing the previous games.

Posted: April 12
Dark Souls 3 has the best combat in the series. (At least up to its release.) That alone makes it one of the best video games of all time...

...which is good, because that really is the only reason to seek out and play it.

Dark Souls 3's world is linear and its stages, while impressively constructed, blend together. The story, while sincere, is literally a Metal Gear Solid 4 style parody of itself. And there really aren't a huge variety of situations that can be overcome in vastly different ways. It's almost all different flavors of "git gud and duel your enemies by learning their reaction times". 3D fantasy Punch-Out, in other words.

And my God do I love 3D fantasy Punch-Out.

I find pretty much all the FromSoft games replayable, but I usually don't like losing to bosses. I want to craft a new journey through their worlds, do things in different orders, overcome challenges in different ways with different builds, etc. The boss fights aren't so fun that I want to fight them over and over and over, clearing the same runback each time... except in Dark Souls 3. Dark Souls 3 bosses are the best duels in the series. After the halfway point, you're effectively facing Artorias over and over and over. Is it samey? Yeah. It's also insanely fun! In Dark Souls 3, losing isn't a setback. Losing means I get to spend more time playing the game's terrific boss content. It helps that runbacks are usually the perfect length... just enough time to mentally reset between boss attempts, but not so long that they become grating.

Dark Souls always had brilliant action-adventure combat. Compare it to a character action game and have the player fight an empty room full of enemies, and it might be boring. But craft a brilliant adventure RPG where the player builds up unique characters and faces unique challenges, and Dark Souls combat is perfect. Dark Souls 3 is the game where that combat gits gud enough to sustain itself even in fairly samey situations. You can make a whole game about one-on-one duels with this combat and it's insanely fun. So that's what From did.

Dark Souls 3 is "the linear Dark Souls", but it's also "the brilliant boss fight Dark Souls". Everything here is in service of that experience. This isn't Dark Souls 1, or 2, or Elden Ring. It's Dark Souls 3. Judge it for what it is, and it is excellent.

There are very few games that have managed to truly captivate my attention. To the point I am playing the game on late nights and getting up early to play it in the morning.

Dark Souls 3 managed to do that.

This game is brilliant. Fantastic gameplay, bosses, enemies, locations, Dark Souls 3 has it all. If I had one flaw for it, It would be the story. I'm told I have to "find" the story, but I'm just not a player that works that way. That being said, I didn't buy this game for the story, I bought it for the combat.

I picked one of the strongest META builds in the game, being Mercenary with Twinswords. I choose this because the closest Soulslike I have played would be Jedi-Survivor on Jedi Master difficulty. I assumed I would be getting my ass handed to very often.

I was correct, but not as often as I thought.

Funnily enough, the earliest locations were the toughest for me. This is due to me playing too aggressively. That paired with not enough healing items and super tough enemies made it very difficult to start with.

The game got easier for me when I started using my bow. Luring in enemies into one-on-one confrontations. Still, I would get handed to fairly easily.

After I started using my shield, everything changed completely.

Instead of rolling around like a headless chicken, I lured the enemy to me, got the shield up and circled the enemy in their inner-guard. While I would still get hit the odd time it made my one-on-one encounters super easy. And that was when I started to feel good at Dark Souls.

Most of the bosses were fairly easy using this playstyle. Circle, shield up, wait for opening. For some of the early bosses, using an ember allows for summonable friends to join in the battle.

I died a total of 102 times from bosses, 39 of them was from the Abyss Watchers, which was my true skill check of the game. I really wished I knew about being able to summon NPCs beforehand, because it would have made my battles a heck of a lot easier!
This game can be truly stunning. There are so many beautiful areas in this game, that are so fun to explore. I rarely do this for games, but allow me to talk about each area one by one.

We start with Cementary of Ash, and I think its a great place to start the world. The enemies aren't too tough, and it's a great area to tackle your first Soulsborne. While there aren't too many tough enemies, you now know you are probably going to get overwhelmed fighting too many and some are best to be flat out avoided. They loved it so much, they brought it back in Untended Graves!

Lothric is such a beautiful place, the castle and they surrounding area. It has a brilliant view and filled with tons action. Like the first dragon in the game!

Undead Settlement keeps it up, a brilliant location with enemies and areas that feel straight out of Resident Evil 4.

Road of Sacrifices kind of halts this. Half the area is battling in a forest like area, the other in a forest like area with a Swamp and a small keep. A problem with it though, is it's not a very fun area to explore.

Cathedral of the Deep is an area I don't like because it scares me. It's filled to the brim with loads of tough enemies and the Cathedral itself has a brilliant atmosphere. And fighting outside on the roof of the Cathedral was really fun as well.

Farron Keep annoys me. Poison Swamp. Poison swamp with not much fun to do.

Catacombs of Carthus is the start of a truly awful trend for this game. Underground areas. But not just that. Underground areas that aren't fun.

Because Smouldering Lake is the next area you go to, though it is optional. And that has the same problems that Catacombs has! But the enemies are even tougher!

Then you come across Irithyll of the Boreal Valley and you feel your breath being taken away. When you see it in the distance, you just stop for a moment to take in the scenary. And then you start battling in the are and you are having so much fun.

And Anor Londo is very similar again. It's another hard area, but stunning in visual design. While I do think the enemies are a bit too difficult here, it is still a blast of fun to play.

Then Irithyll Dungeon comes along and your previous cheers vanishes into thin air. This is the worst area of the game for having mobs that reduce your health WHEN THEY SEE YOU. Not only that, they have an attack that can easily be a one hit KO if you haven't levelled up much of your health. The game doesn't give you much of an idea on where to go as well, and you will be using your Estus Flask quite often to heal up. While I do believe there is some bad level design in this area, it's the amount of enemies parred with the abilities of these enemies that make the area is a struggle to get through, and I dreaded going back to it on repeat playthroughs.

When you are done trudging through the Dungeon, you come across Profaned Capital, which while not being nearly as bad of an area, but I really don't like it either. It has a nice visual, but that's all I see it as. The runback to the boss is also really annoying, so I started having a dislike for the place, even though it's only a small part of the game.

The Consumed King's Garden is like Farron Keep. Swampy area with poison. Some hard enemies and a bit of an annoying runback to the boss. This area would be so much better, if there was no poison.

Lothric Castle is another very touch area, I enjoyed going through it a lot. And while there was a lot of enemies, and I was constantly getting low on health, the game felt fair. I was dying due to carelessness rather than cheap tricks that make the game less fun.

The final optional area of the base game is Archdragon Peak, and it's one of my favorite areas in the game. While not my most favorite, it feels the most unique, and I really enjoyed the bosses here especially as well. The final boss of this area is so much fun (when the camera isn't messing with you), and it's second phase is just absolutely peak.

And that brings me to the final area, Kiln of the First Flame. After going through so much of the game, it was great getting to the end, and I was thinking "This is it." It's a great feeling when you finally beat the game and put the controller down.

Basically, a lot of the areas that take place in caves, dungeons or underground just generally suck. And is a sucky experience to play through.

There are enemies that feel unfair to fight. Giant Spiders, Giant trees, general, but they aren't cheap to fight. Mobs are either pushovers that can be challenging in groups, challenging on their own, or straight up be Mini-bosses.

So my final thoughts?

This is a fantastic game. The combat is amazingly addictive, and while playing through it, I was constantly thinking about it, and how I should change my style of combat while battling. However, It is not perfect. It has great combat, and it very fun fighting one-on-one, but any more than that, and it starts to feel very unfair. That paired that there is some very frustrating level design brought my enjoyment down. I wished the story was a bit more direct, my enjoyment of a movie is brought down when I don't understand what I'm watching and it felt like that playing this. However, again, I didn't play this for the story, I played for combat. And the combat is just amazing. From normal enemies to bosses, they are fun to fight.

At the very least, I believe you should try this game. It won't be for everyone, but it is very good to me. If you don't like it, that's fine, but it would be criminal not to try.

Dark Souls 3: La Crónica del Elegido Inútil
O cómo un muerto viviente desnutrido salvó el mundo a los tropezones

Todo comenzó una mañana cualquiera. El sol no brillaba, los pájaros no cantaban, y nuestro protagonista despertó en una tumba poco higiénica, rodeado de niebla, lápidas y un ambiente que gritaba "te vas a morir en 5 minutos". Así nace nuestro héroe: El Chosen Noob. Desnudo, con una espada rota y la actitud de alguien que no leyó el tutorial.

Capítulo 1: Cementerio de la Ceniza y el Tutorial que Ignoraste
Nuestro valiente muerto andante dio sus primeros pasos, esquivando mensajes brillantes en el piso que decían cosas como "¡Cuidado con la espalda!" o "Prueba rodar" y que, por supuesto, ignoró olímpicamente. Apenas giró la primera esquina, un hombre lagarto gigante (también conocido como "el susto que me hizo tirar el joystick") lo mandó de regreso a su checkpoint con un grito tipo Wilhelm scream.

Pero no se rindió. Volvió. Y esta vez... también murió. Y otra. Y otra. Hasta que por puro cansancio, el lagartorreptil se cayó de un barranco y nuestro héroe se adjudicó la victoria como si hubiera planeado todo.

Capítulo 2: Iudex Gundyr, o "el tipo que no sabía cuándo parar"
Llegamos al primer jefe: un caballero dormido con una lanza clavada en el pecho. Error #1: sacarle la lanza. Error #2: no correr.

Gundyr comienza como un combate de entrenamiento. Pero luego dice:
"¿Y si me convierto en una cosa de aceite con tentáculos y rabia acumulada desde 2016?"
Y así empieza la pesadilla. Tras 18 muertes, 3 gritos y un par de memes mentales, el Chosen Noob logra la victoria pegándole con una antorcha y rodando como panqueque con miedo.

Capítulo 3: Santuario del Enlace de Fuego y la Trampa de la Confianza
— "Bienvenido, Ashen One..."
— "¿Puedo guardar aquí?"
— "Sí. Pero también podés caerte por ese pozo de atrás si te pinta."

Aquí conoce a la Guardiana del Fuego, una señora amable que jamás parpadea (lo cual da un poco de miedo) y que te sube de nivel a cambio de almas. La relación es algo así como:

Vos: "¡Quiero más fuerza!"

Ella: "Traéme 5 mil almas y lo hablamos."
Un clásico.

Capítulo 4: Alto Muro de Lothric y el Club de los Dragones Intolerantes
Nuestro héroe llega al castillo más gótico que vio en su vida, repleto de caballeros de 3 metros, perros con hambre y dragones con mal carácter. Acá el juego decide que ya es hora de dejar de jugar y comenzar a sufrir. Cada esquina es una trampa. Cada enemigo, una excusa para romper el control.

Tras una maratón de muertes, llega al jefe: Vordt del Valle Boreal, un toro gigante con asma. El combate se resume en: correr en círculos, esquivar ataques con delay de 2 segundos y, finalmente, golpearle el trasero hasta que caiga de tanto girar.

Capítulo 5: Aldrich, el devorador de dieta líquida
Para este punto, el Chosen Noob ya es un poco menos inútil. Tiene una espada decente, una armadura que no es un barril y aprendió a rodar con dignidad. Entra a la Catedral de lo Grotesco y se enfrenta a Aldrich, un ente viscoso que solía ser un cura… y ahora es una sopa de gente.

Lanza flechas mágicas, invoca lluvias de fuego, se teletransporta como tu ex cuando le pedís explicaciones. Pero, tras 47 intentos, una guía en YouTube y mucha, mucha rabia... ¡Victoria!

Capítulo 6: Los Príncipes Lothric y Lorian: hermanos con WiFi compartido
Estos dos son como los mini jefes finales del sufrimiento. Uno es mudo, el otro es cojo. Pero juntos tienen una química infernal para hacerte perder la fe en tus reflejos.

Cada vez que matás a uno, el otro lo revive. Es como jugar a los Sims pero con fuego, espadas mágicas y ansiedad. Eventualmente, lográs derrotarlos con una técnica ancestral: correr en círculos y gritar como un pavo real.

Capítulo Final: Alma de Ceniza, o el resumen de todas tus frustraciones
Y finalmente, llegás al último jefe. El Alma de Ceniza.
Una criatura que no solo tiene 4 fases distintas, sino que parece tener el moveset de todos los jefes anteriores juntos.

Espadachín, mago, piromántico, guitarrista de heavy metal (bueno, casi).
El combate es largo, intenso, y hace que te preguntes por qué no jugaste Animal Crossing en su lugar.

Pero después de una hora, dos termos de café y una introspección espiritual...

¡Victoria!
“¡Has ligado el fuego!”
“¡Has salvado el mundo!”

...O, si elegiste la otra opción, simplemente lo dejaste apagarse para siempre. Porque “el mundo no merece mi esfuerzo”.

Y así termina la odisea de nuestro no-tan-inútil elegido.
Murió 542 veces.
Gastó 312 Estus.
Rompió 1 joystick.
Pero vivió para contarla.
…más o menos.

FIN.

Dark Souls 3 is the perfect culmination of everything the series is known for. From the hauntingly beautiful environments to the punishing yet rewarding combat, it is a game that demands patience, strategy, and a willingness to learn from every death. Every area feels meticulously crafted, with intricate level design that rewards exploration and offers numerous shortcuts and secrets to uncover. The lore is rich, and while it’s often subtle, piecing together the world’s history adds a layer of depth to the experience.

The combat system is fluid and satisfying, with a wide range of weapons, spells, and tactics to suit different playstyles. The bosses are where the game truly shines, each one a test of skill and endurance, often with some of the most memorable and epic encounters I’ve ever experienced in a video game. There’s a real sense of achievement when you finally overcome a difficult boss or navigate a perilous area after countless attempts.

However, the difficulty is relentless. Dark Souls 3 does not hold your hand, and it’s not for the faint of heart. The high difficulty is part of what makes the game so rewarding, but it can also lead to moments of frustration, especially when you’re stuck on a boss or an area for hours at a time. Still, each failure feels like an opportunity to learn, and the satisfaction of finally overcoming those challenges makes it all worthwhile.

Dark Souls 3 is a masterclass in challenging, rewarding game design. It’s not just about overcoming obstacles; it’s about pushing yourself, learning from mistakes, and finally earning the sense of triumph that comes with conquering this brutal yet beautiful world.

FromSoftware is well-known at this point for an incredible lineup of games. Elden Ring takes the Souls-like system and excellently (surprisingly, even) turns it into an exploration game. Sekiro is mechanical perfection - I've still never played a game that matches the precision of its combat. Bloodborne oozes with atmosphere and has this visceral feeling that's so unique to it.

So what does any of this have to do with Dark Souls 3? I love all of those games to death - as well as all of the previous souls games (yes, even you Demons Souls) - but there's something so special about DS3 that easily makes it my favorite out of any FromSoft game. Every element of it - its visuals, combat, level design, soundtrack, bosses - everything comes together to create the best example of video game "art" I can think of. It's really hard to put into words but they were able to capture a magic in this game that I don't believe they were able to do again, despite how incredible their succeeding products were. To top it all off, it has probably the best ending to a series I've ever experienced in the Ringed City DLC.

Dark Souls 3 is timeless. Maybe it's just nostalgia or maybe there's just something about the game that appeals to me. There's tons of discussion on the best FromSoft title and people criticize this game for being too linear or being too roll-heavy. I honestly don't feel like these are issues about 15 playthroughs later (my Steam playtime is about 1/4 of my total across platforms).

Just turn off your lights, put on some headphones, and try the game out. No matter what happens, persevere. I think you'll get it.

Having started with Dark Souls for Xbox 360 on launch day (with no idea what I was getting into) and going all the way up to and through Elden Ring: Shadow of the Erdtree (skipping Sekiro since that is more Tenchu than Souls tbh):

I think Dark Souls 3 (with all DLC, esp. Ringed City) is probably the best of the Souls games. Bloodborne on PS4 is the best iteration on the general gameplay concept, but DS3 is *the* Souls game pinnacle of design and quality.

Elden Ring has some polish, such as swappable Weapon Arts/Ash of War, but also has led too far into the trap of "faster enemies == better challenge." SOTE really showcased how comfortable FROM is to just let the white knights force a game to be considered good despite obvious flaws.

DS3 absolutely has flaws, but it also isn't up it's own ass about it yet. Ashes of Ariandel is a 'meh, that's neat but weirdly short and awkward' DLC, as an example. Dropping 1mm from a ledge makes you land-stumble, which is annoying but mainly a PVP area issue (unless you attracted... everyone in an area).

DS3, ultimately, feels like they took the design lessons from Demon's Souls, Dark Souls, and Dark Souls 2 (including DLC for DS1 and DS2) and meshed *most* of them very well together. They also tried a few new things, and they had variable success (Weapon Arts are either underwhelming or the only value to a weapon, for example). It has multiple endings that... don't have the Mass Effect 3 ice-cream-flavor feel. Areas feel *generally* well designed and thought out. Bosses are, generally, very good and often challenging without just needing to have untelegraphed attacks, fake out windups, or ambiguous openings.

That's probably the biggest thing: The Bosses still feel like they are designed to contradict other developers' approach to 'Challenge/Difficulty.' It rarely uses cheap tricks to create tedious situations and claim that's "difficulty." It has the standard unnecessarily annoying poison swamp area, but this swamp actually has stuff in it. Bosses feel awe-inspiring not because they do the old MadTV 'Look what I can do!' spectacle, but rather because they follow the paradigm of the Souls games: They *are* awe-inspiring, and either don't care about you or are too mentally degraded to properly acknowledge you.

Nanana, juegazo Dark Souls 3. Mal. Es uno de esos juegos que si te agarran en un día donde estás aburrido y no sabés qué jugar, lo empezás y de repente se te pasaron tres horas sin que te des cuenta. Pero ojo, no es cualquier jueguito. Es de esos que te hacen sufrir, que te hacen putear a la pantalla, pero cuando finalmente matás al boss que te tuvo una hora trabado... man, es una sensación única. Como que te sentís invencible, ¿viste? Depende un poco de qué tipo de juegos te gustan, pero si te va la onda medieval, oscura, con espadas gigantes, magia loca y enemigos que parecen salidos de una pesadilla, este te va a volar la cabeza.

Lo que tiene de bueno es que podés jugar solo tranqui, y es re inmersivo. Cada zona está llena de detalles, secretos, caminos alternativos, y lo mejor: un montón de objetos escondidos. O sea, hay niveles donde hay como 18 cosas ocultas y algunas están tan bien escondidas que ni con guía las encontrás fácil. A veces da fiaca buscarlas todas, sí, pero muchas veces te encontrás con una espadota zarpada, o una armadura toda fachera que decís “amigo, esto es otra cosa”. El tema es que capaz no la podés usar porque tus stats están en la lona jaja. Entonces ahí te toca farmear, mejorar tus puntos, subir de nivel, y eso cada vez cuesta más caro, así que te la tenés que rebuscar, farmear enemigos, y pensar bien en qué invertir tus almas.

Y hablando de eso, si te matan… perdés todas las almas que tenías encima, y te querés cortar las venas. Pero te dan una chance: si llegás al lugar donde moriste sin que te maten de nuevo, las recuperás. Eso genera una tensión tremenda que te tiene todo el tiempo al borde del sillón. Cada decisión cuenta.

También se puede jugar online, y ahí se pone buenísimo. Podés invocar amigos para que te ayuden a pasar una parte difícil, o incluso meterte vos en el mundo de ellos para dar una mano. Lo malo es que solo avanza la historia del que te invoca, así que si los dos están en la misma parte, después vas a tener que hacerlo de nuevo en tu mundo. Pero posta que se arma un cago de risa con los pibes, porque se tiran chistes mientras esquivan enemigos o mueren de formas absurdas. Y si encima usan micrófono, mejor, porque se vuelve muy social y divertido.

Otra cosa piola es que tenés que investigar un montón: dónde encontrar ciertos objetos, cómo mejorar tu equipo, qué rutas seguir, cómo vencer a cada enemigo. No es simplemente ir y apretar botones, tenés que pensar, esquivar, estudiar los patrones de ataque, y saber cuándo dar el golpe justo. Además hay diferentes estilos de juego: podés ir full fuerza con una espada gigante, o ir a distancia con magia, o usar builds más rápidas tipo ninja. Eso lo hace muy rejugable, porque cada vez que lo empezás podés probar una forma nueva de jugar.

Eso sí, el juego está carito. Yo lo agarré en oferta y fue la mejor decisión de mi vida. Ahora está medio saladito, pero si lo cazás con rebaja, ni lo dudes. Es de esos juegos que valen cada centavo, porque tiene contenido para rato. Y lo mejor es que no es lineal: vos podés elegir el orden en el que hacés ciertas zonas, ir por diferentes caminos, hablar con NPCs que tienen historias ocultas re locas, y hasta sacar distintos finales.

En fin, Dark Souls 3 no es para cualquiera, pero si te la bancás y te gusta el desafío, es una joya. A veces vas a putear, otras veces vas a sentirte un dios, y otras te vas a reír con amigos mientras mueren de formas ridículas. Pero siempre, siempre, te va a dejar con ganas de más. Es como un vicio. Cuando le agarrás la mano, no lo podés soltar. Ultra recomendado. 🔥🗡️💀

This is the best installments of the Souls series in my mind, and also the best finale to this epic series. The Age of Fire will one day come to an end, and in this brutal and gradually crumbling world, filled with human emotions, there have been characters of all shapes and sizes, flesh and blood. The story of DS is not only about the gods, but also about these flesh and blood characters. They are often the characters that move us players the most.

Perhaps the same is true in the real world, where we all have beautiful visions that we want to realize, and beliefs that we want to carry out, but are mercilessly drowned out by the tides of the times. In a world of sudden changes, you and I, every ordinary person is up and down.

But even so, don't easily give up on the creed you hold fast to. Even as times change, don't forget the important things that lie deep within you.

This game stands as the grand finale of FromSoftware’s legendary trilogy, a melancholic swan song that perfectly encapsulates the series’ themes of decay, futility, and fleeting embers of hope. It is, in many ways, a love letter to long-time fans, tying together lore threads that have dangled since the original Dark Souls. However, while the narrative and atmosphere reach impressive heights, the game’s approach to build variety feels like a step back, especially when compared to the sprawling arsenal of Dark Souls 2.
On a technical and artistic level, Dark Souls 3 is a masterclass in world design. Lothric’s interconnected, ruined beauty is a haunting sight, echoing the best of the first game’s level design philosophy while improving upon it with smoother traversal and verticality. The lore is rich, weaving in references to past games in a way that feels reverent rather than redundant. Gwyn’s legacy, the Undead Curse, and the ever-repeating cycle of fire and dark all find resolution here, bringing a sense of finality that Dark Souls 2 never fully embraced.
However, where Dark Souls 3 falters is in its gameplay variety. Compared to the sheer level of options in Dark Souls 2, which boasted an almost overwhelming number of weapons and builds, Dark Souls 3 feels streamlined to the point of limitation. The removal of power stance mechanics, the reduced dual-wielding options, and the emphasis on a faster-paced, aggressive combat style cater more to a specific meta rather than the ‘choose your own playstyle’ philosophy that defined the series. While the introduction of Weapon Arts offers some variety, they often feel underwhelming compared to the adaptability and unique movesets from previous entries.
Magic builds also suffer from this streamlining. The separation of FP from individual spell casts allows for more sustained magic use, but the nerfs to spell damage and the overall slower casting times make them feel underpowered, especially in PvP. Meanwhile, faith builds—once a cornerstone of versatility—are left with little to stand on outside of buffs and healing.
Despite these limitations, Dark Souls 3 still provides an immensely satisfying challenge. The boss fights, from the relentless Nameless King to the poetic tragedy of the Soul of Cinder, rank among the best in the series. The combat is more fluid than ever, encouraging a mix of aggression and careful stamina management. While the game may not offer the sheer experimental sandbox that Dark Souls 2 did, it refines what it does have to near perfection.
Ultimately, Dark Souls 3 is a worthy send-off for the series, closing the loop in a way that respects its past while pushing forward into a bleak yet beautiful unknown. It may not offer the same level of build variety as its predecessor, but as a final journey through the dying embers of this world, it remains unforgettable.

After Elden Ring being my first souls game and playing it for over 1000 hours Dark Souls 3 is a pretty fun experience. I used a straight sword through most of my playthrough and infused it with bleed or mainly used the frost sword that came with frost which was really good through the entire game.

I loved a good bunch of the boss fights namely, Peak Gundyr, Abyss Watchers, Dragonslayer, Nameless King only second phase tho, Soul of Cinder and my fav Slave Knight Gael. only a couple of times bosses made me change my play style with Pontiff being my first challenging boss, making me switch to light roll and Midir making me use a thrusting ultra greatsword also with light roll.

Some bosses I really didn't like one bit (nearly all the duo fights and npc fights) Greatwood was didn't need the other enemies in the arena, crystal sage was barely a boss cause I no hit them by mistake, Deacons was easy but boring, Wolnir was easy when he didn't spam spawn a bunch of skeletons in a crampt ass arena, Aldrich just was a spell spammer, ancient wyvern was just total wipeout to insta kill them at the end, then the dlc well Sister Friede should have been a 2 phase removing the phase in between and was just an annoying fight cause she just kept running away or going invis, Gravetender was bearly a boss and died way to quick the wolf was cool just needed more health and a second phase to be more of a fight and Halflight was basically pvp gank.

Finally I have been going for the 100% achievements for this and man ♥♥♥♥ covenants, pvp in this game is not fun 1 bit you either get triple teamed on or you get a pvp sweat that has all the best pvp weapons and just destroys you or even worse you get killed by a hacker which happened twice during the grind, which after grinding 10 for one covenant i decided i'm gonna find another way only to see every covenant item has a 1% drop chance in pve, but ♥♥♥♥ it, doing that was way more enjoyable than doing the ♥♥♥♥ show that is souls pvp.

The environments in this game are amazing the Boreal Valley being hands down one of the prettiest areas i've seen in a fromsoft game and i feel that Firelink Shrine is a miles better hub than the Roundtable Hold but there are some places that just suck being the Catacombs of Carthus and the Grand Archives I also didn't really like the Painted World of Ariandel but it does look cool tho,

The weapons are pretty cool shame theres no ashs of war system in this cause i'd love to use some different skills on my weapons and not be just stuck with one, the boss weapons are really unique but some don't really fit the boss like idk why midir gives you a katana just a weird choice in my eyes. The armour wow they went crazy with the armour in this game 100 times better than elden ring for sure, Ringed Knight armour Peak, Dancer armour peak, Dragonslayer armour also peak, Fallen Knight Armour perfect.

I give Dark Souls 3 a rating of 8 Desert Pyromancers out of 10 :) time for ng+
