If you've never played a souls game and want to get into the Souls series, do not under any circumstance start with this game. Start with Dark Souls 1, or Demons Souls if you can.

I'm someone who has been playing all the souls games for almost 13 years (many hundreds of hours across everything from Demons Souls to Elden Ring, including Bloodborne), and was excited for this game and bought it day one on PS4, and this is by far the most frustrating (not "difficult" frustrating) game I own. I love all the souls games and think they are all fantastic even with some of their faults. Dark Souls 3 just has too many problems for me to enjoy it and consider it a good/enjoyable game. I'll start with the upsides.

The return of the MP bar from Demons Souls is by far one of the best decisions they made. I love DS1 and DS2, but the spell quantity in those games feels clunky and bad to play with for the most part.

The introduction of weapon arts gave some very welcome variety in moves for weapons. I don't think it's fair to compare it to Elden Ring expanding on and nailing the ash of war system. It is very limited in DS3, but it is a very good addition nonetheless.

There are a few bosses that are simply peak design, some of the best FromSoft has ever made (although most of them are in the second DLC)

The second DLC is probably the third best DLC FromSoft has made. I do put it above AotA from DS1, but well below SotE from ER and Iron King from DS2.

It's a quality built game in that it runs well, not any bugs that mess with normal gameplay, and doesn't crash. I personally think the graphics of the game look really bad compared to what came before (and after) but that's a personal opinion and I don't think people are wrong for liking how the game looks.


Now the problems.

It takes many steps back on mechanics and design choices from what came before and it just creates a bland and boring game.

It's incredibly linear. That cool open DS1 map in the first half of the game and the freedom to choose 4 paths at the start of DS2 is gone. You are on basically 1 path from beginning to end.

This game has by far the worst balance of all the souls games. Magic is mostly a joke, and any melee weapon that isn't fast and R1 spammable is significantly outperformed by ones that are (that goes for magic too actually).

The amount of souls given to you by enemies and bosses is so incredibly low. In all my other first playthroughs of the other souls games I finished at level 100-120, in Dark Souls 3 I finished at ~80 (including doing all bosses).

The weapon upgrade progression is so...weird. It's not a nice and smooth progression where you are constantly becoming stronger as you advance to new areas. The moment you hit a new area that will give you materials to upgrade your weapon, you are flooded with enough to fully upgrade it basically immediately. (Bloodborne has this problem as well, but that game is much shorter, so it doesn't feel as bad to deal with this issue)

I feel like I should also mention Poise at some point, it's in the game after all even if it does basically nothing.

The general enemy design is so....just bad. It's like they looked at how successful and popular Bloodborne was and wanted to put something from that game in DS3. That kind of enemy works with with the faster paced combat in that game, but is incredibly frustrating to deal with in this one and they are all over the place, especially in the first half of the game. (most enemies have more health than you, at all points in the game)

Bosses mostly feel the same way. There are a few really good bosses in this game (final DLC2 boss might be the best boss FromSoft has ever made), but most are just unoriginal and feel all the same. Another gripe I have with the bosses in this game is that they seemed to have a huge boner for multi phase bosses in this game. That's not inherently an issue, my problem mainly comes from when I kill one boss health bar, then I get another phase 2 with a whole new boss health bar. I'm ok with it sparingly, but it's everywhere in this game. (side note, Sekiro's system of multiple health bars I'm ok with. Not only do you see how many health bars a boss has except for one boss, but the fighting in that game is also totally different anyway so it's really not comparable)

Speaking of unoriginal, this game is in many areas (mainly boss design and some areas). There is nothing wrong with the original idea, but having similar (and in some places the exact same) content as previous games with all the other stated issues really hurts the expierence. I would much rather have cool new bosses and areas in all those cases.

The first DLC is beyond awful and overpriced for what you get. Horrible lack of meaningful content. It's much smaller than it might seem at first glance. There is only 1 real boss. There is a second (optional) boss that is literally just a combo of a NPC and enemies that already appear in other places in the DLC. Going back to the actual real boss, it's beyond horrible. I agree that I'm in the minority on this opinion, but it's just a not fun 3 phase DPS check where you're basically forced to sit and wait and bait one attack for an opening.

The second DLC is very good, but you can only access it either at the very end of the base game, or very near the beginning if you choose to enter and beat the first DLC. Forcing completion of the first DLC to access the second immediately is a very odd design choice. Locking normal access to the second DLC so late is very lame. All the other DLC in the souls games can be accessed pretty early for the most part.

I can't be the only one that feels this way, but armor feels very useless. In DS1 and DS2 when you wear and upgrade big heavy armor, it is very apparent how much it reduces damage taken. In DS3 it feels like you take the same damage from everything regardless of what you wear. Another minor point is that it feels like enemies in general do a lot of damage at pretty much every point in the game, basically forcing a lot of early levels in Vigor to not die in 2-3 hits to most things.

With how awful the general enemy design is, you are essentially forced (and never punished) for skipping and running past literally everything in the game. The best way to play this game, is to play as little of it as possible. This isn't unique to 3, but general area and enemy design in DeS, DS1, and ER feels much more fair and well thought out (and you are well compensated for doing so in the other games, not really so in 3.

PvP is terrible. I did so much PvP in DS2 and that was beyond peak PvP souls. To have 3 have a frustrating invasion system, terrible weapon balance, and a complete lack of respectful behavior from all the new players completely killed my desire to ever PvP again.


Anyway that's most issues I have with this game that I can immediately come up with. I love all the other souls games that From has made, and I really want to like this game, but it's just not likely to ever happen. This game is unfortunately most peoples first experience with the souls series and I wish so badly that it never is the case. I have many friends who played this first and hate all the other games because they expect it to be like DS3 (because it's what they're used to) and they are put off by the other games being different.

I do still play Dark Souls 3, because I'm addicted the series and marathon play them all sometimes, but this is the lowlight of the series for me and many people. Contrary to what steam reviews may indicate, the community is surprisingly divided on opinion on this game. I want to like it, I want it to be a good game, but that's just not the case.

Setting and Themes
Dark Souls III brings the series full circle, set in Lothric, a kingdom at the brink of the end of the world. The theme of rekindling the flame versus letting it die is at the forefront, making it feel like a culmination of everything the series has built. The lore is richer and more intertwined with the original game, featuring callbacks to characters, places, and storylines from both Dark Souls I and II.

Refined Combat and Movement
The combat is noticeably faster and more fluid than previous entries, drawing inspiration from Bloodborne. Weapon Arts, unique abilities tied to specific weapons, add more depth to combat and create opportunities for unique build strategies. Stamina management feels more balanced, allowing for aggressive yet calculated approaches.

Bosses are the highlight of Dark Souls III, offering some of the most epic and visually stunning encounters in the series. Fights like Pontiff Sulyvahn, Abyss Watchers, and Soul of Cinder push the limits of both gameplay mechanics and visual spectacle. The DLCs, especially The Ringed City, add even more challenging and lore-rich content.

Visuals and Atmosphere
The game’s aesthetic is grand and haunting, blending gothic architecture with a world on the verge of collapse. Areas like Irithyll of the Boreal Valley and Anor Londo are visually breathtaking, providing a sense of tragic beauty. The dynamic lighting and improved graphics engine give the world a somber and eerie feel.

Multiplayer and Replayability
Dark Souls III features a robust multiplayer system, with a vast array of covenants that encourage both PvP and cooperative gameplay. New Game Plus remains a strong feature, offering additional challenges and allowing for deeper exploration of lore and character builds.

Alright let's get this going, and I have to get something out of the way really quickly, I'm not exactly the biggest lover of the souls games I beat the first one way back on the 360 and absolutely did not enjoy my time doing so.
I also tried to play the second game and hated it (I've later learned this isn't exactly an unpopular opinion for the second game).
I played through about half of Bloodborne, enjoyed it but didn't find it to be a masterpiece that people say it is and never had a desire to finish it.
Played through the Demon Souls remake, enjoyed it but same deal as Bloodborne (I don't know how the remake stacks against the orginal as I never owned the original).
I played like 3 hours of Elden Ring and absolutely hated it (yes I know that's a very hot take).
That said one of my friends INSISTED I try DS3, and for awhile I was like "why bother it's going to either be like DS2 or ER" but I finally said f**k it and sat down to play.

As I played I finally understood the appeal of the souls games, now again I don't think this is a masterpiece by any means, it has it's problems and they are plenty, but I did have the desire to keep playing, I even created a second character to try a slightly different playstyle preferred that playstyle so I redid the areas I did again, then I went for an even more aggressive playstyle and enjoyed that the most so I opted to keep going on that third playthrough)

This review was originally made at around 14ish hours in, I'm now 37 hours and I completed my first playthrough of the base game (like 1/3 of another and 1/5 of another) I can tell you it's great.

Breakdown:
The gameplay loop is extremely tight it feels like Fromsoftware learned from their mistakes with Demon Souls all the way to BloodBorne and crafted this out of the best elements from them.

The story is amazing, honestly at first I didn't love the ludonarrative aspect but it slowly grew on me and towards the end of the game it does give you more of a cinematic approach, I do still wish there would have been a little more cinematic approach to the narrative since the story is *so* good, but I get that ludonarrative is what Fromsoftware is known for.

The Pathfinding is I think where the game falters a bit, it took me some time to figure out how to get to a few areas and there's some backtracking which I'm not a huge fan of.

Overall the level design is great though there's a few places with objectively bad level design but one of them is optional and the other is on the shorter side of the levels so it's forgivable (Smoulder Lake can get f**ked)

The enemy placement is a different story though it is such a crazy wide range that it's hard to really put a word to it, there are some places where the enemy placement is straight up perfect and others where it's absurdly asinine throwing everything and the kitchen sink at you with stupid sh*t.

The Difficulty curve is pretty decent BUT there's a HUGE difficulty spike with the last two bosses it feels a little cheap especially with the last boss since there are (I counted) 6 moves that can insta-kill you from a full 1000 hp health bar, I get that it's meant to the final boss but damn it felt like the game just hated me at that point, there's also a few annoying things that certain bosses do but if you can get your moves right to burn them down it's fine.

Tech - Whoo boy, this one is rough, this port SUCKS I don't own this game on the console so I can't speak for how it is there but DAMN did I have to do alot for this game firstly it's locked at 60fps, the problem being my monitor doesn't divide perfectly into 60, alright, downloaded borderless window mod to run at 141 with Gsync, then around half-way through the game I started getting extreme stutters and hitches, alright downloaded a LOD mod to help the stutters and dvk for the hitches... game crashed, uninstalled dvk and ran a fix to make the game use more than one core, fixed that. Amidst all of this the game crashed 3 times, numerous bugs and around half-way in despite me being in offline mode I had invaders (yeah idk either) I'm assuming it's a glitch either way one of them cleaned my clock like it was nothing (I think it was a high level invader as he had some strong stuff), which was extremely annoying since I specifically chose to be offline so that I could enjoy the game as a single player experience.

Overall despite my annoyances and gripes and the many flaws the game has (including the ones specific to the port itself) the game is great a great solid 8.5/10.

This game may actually convince me to at least retry Elden Ring, DS1 and Bloodborne to see if my opinion would change

For anyone who enjoys these types of games, this is a must-play. This game is on the same level as Elden Ring on difference being that Elden Ring is an open world game.

FromSoftware’s grim masterpiece concludes the trilogy with ferocious elegance. This action RPG plunges players into the fading Kingdom of Lothric, a gorgeously macabre world of ash-choked castles, twisted cathedrals, and cosmic horrors. Combat is refined to a lethal dance—faster, tighter, and more versatile than predecessors, with weapons offering unique skills and spells crackling with visceral impact. Bosses tower as titanic spectacles of despair, each duel a heart-pounding test of reflexes and resolve.

Lore remains deliciously cryptic, weaving threads from past games into a melancholic crescendo. While level design leans linear compared to Dark Souls’ labyrinthine sprawl, environments drip with oppressive beauty and hidden secrets. Multiplayer thrives through jolly cooperation and ruthless invasions, though technical hiccaps (occasional frame dips, sparse QoL updates) linger.

A relentless, poetic ode to decay and defiance, DSIII isn’t just for masochists—it’s a cathartic pilgrimage for those craving triumph carved from failure. Essential, if exhausting. 💀🔥🗡️

Dark Souls 3. Oh, where do I even begin? It’s like finding a golden ticket, but instead of chocolate, it’s a ticket to a world full of monsters that really don’t want you to have any chocolate. That’s Dark Souls 3. Think of the combat like a slap fight, but each slap leaves a mark. Simple? Yes. Effective? You bet your soul. The bosses? Oh, they’re not just bosses. They’re like if a nightmarish art exhibit and an angry god had a baby. And somehow, each one’s better than the last.

The gambler in me saw the Luck stat and instantly thought, ‘LETS GO GAMBLING!’ I dumped every point into it like I was betting my last dollar at a roulette table, knowing full well I’d probably end up with nothing but a bad story and a broken controller. What does Luck do? No idea and still don't know. But it sure as hell felt like I was betting my soul every time I pressed ‘confirm.’"

Do I recommend it? Absolutely. 10/10, would make terrible decisions again. A wise man once said, 'Quit while you're ahead.' I am not that man, I'm the man that doubles down. Luck stat? All in. Victory? Unlikely. Consequences? Irrelevant. The house always wins, but I refuse to leave the table.

Dark Souls III boasts an amazing roster of boss fights in the later end, but some earlier bosses are far less polished.

To be more specific, I am talking about the , , and . There is also a somewhat late game boss that i'm sure many would agree is a pain, .

These bosses are either complete gimmick fights, or are constantly teleporting around and spell spamming. These fights become a chore to complete when going for 100%. (you must fight each of them at least three times)

Now for the glazing portion.

Every other boss in the game serves to elevate the experience.

My favorite boss in the game, , is an absolutely beautiful fight. The combination of soundtrack, design, and overall atmosphere around this fight makes up for all of the bad boss fights single-handedly. When fought at the correct level, this fight feels like a proper back and forth, fitting for a final boss.

Conclusion

After sinking over 50 hours into this game, I was able to achieve 100% completion. Many more hours can be spent in this game, even after 100%, due to multiplayer, and countless builds/challenge runs you can attempt.

Overall, despite the negative parts I highlighted above, I would rate this game a solid .

This review will be mostly a rather lengthy anecdotal story about how I somewhat recently became a fan of Fromsouls games (I don't know if that is a thing that people say, but I am going to say that for the duration of this review, so I guess you're just going to have to live with it), basically for the purpose of any of the uninitiated who might be reading this, wondering if it's really worth it to play through these notoriously challenging games, but I will start this review by at least mentioning that Dark Souls III is an absolute work of art that offers tons of rewarding and awe-inspiring content to those who are interested enough to put the time and effort into it (and as blindly as possible if you want to get the most out of it). I will talk more about that in a bit, but first, a little background on how I ended up becoming a rather obsessive fan of the nightmarish hellscapes of the Fromsouls games.

I am more or less getting into the Fromsoft soulslike games kind of late in the game. I vaguely remember briefly playing some King's Field game and Demon's Souls a long time ago as video store rentals back before society had deteriorated into the pale imitation of human experience that it is now (I can't leave reviews on those titles so I'm mentioning them here, whether you like it or not), but I bounced off of those entries pretty quickly, as I really didn't know what kind of games I was getting into, and probably just wrote them off as being alright looking games, but probably poorly designed, or something, though after somewhat recently actually dedicating time and effort to a few Fromsouls titles, I have since decided to collect and play through every one that I can get my hands on.

Fast forward to circa 2015, I see a friend playing Bloodborne and I think it looks amazing (another one I can't leave a review on here, so I'm mentioning it now as well, whatever...). Loving the intricate world design and dark gothic setting of it, I really start wanting to try it out myself, but I remember hearing at this point that these games are incredibly punishingly difficult and I probably concluded that that was why I bounced off of previous titles, so I'm not sure if it's for me, but I go ahead and give it a go anyway. I end up loving Bloodborne and finally play through my first Fromsouls title from beginning to end, though not without dying a lot and seeing just what people were saying about the challenge of these games, but also finally realizing why those who put the time and effort into them end up loving them so much, because they are actually outstanding games, unparalleled in artistic design and world building, and because overcoming some of the greater challenges is an experience more heart-pounding and exhilarating than most any horror game (or otherwise) can seem to offer.

From there, I felt confident enough to jump right into the first Dark Souls game, thinking that since I played through Bloodborne essentially blind , I figured I should be able to just dance around the challenges of Dark Souls at this point, right? Well, apparently not, as making almost no progress in Dark Souls for the better part of a day, I then started to think that Bloodborne was an anomaly in the Fromsouls lineup that just happened to cater to my playstyle in particular. While this might not be entirely untrue, I did realize later that I can make certain builds in other games that also worked for me and also that if I gave more time to more titles, I could sort of naturally "git guder," as it were...

So, a few years later, I get tired of neglecting the recent massively popular Elden Ring, and decide to get it around the time that the Shadow of the Erdtree DLC was first announced, and I give another concerted effort to a Fromsouls game, now trying to go for a character design that more closely resembled that of my experience with Bloodborne. I end up loving Elden Ring even more than I did Bloodborne (though I do still love Bloodborne too) and I dedicate several months of my time to it, clocking in at around 230 hours for a single offline playthrough (making Elden Ring by far the most hours I've spent on any game ever), finishing everything I could possibly think of to do in a blind run of the game . Aaand then I am left just yearning for more... So, a little while later, I decide to give the Dark Souls series another go with the most recent title, Dark Souls III, thinking it might be the most comparable to my experiences with Bloodborne and Elden Ring (this is as far as I've got with Fromsouls games in general so far, so I'm still not sure how true that might be, but I do know now that I want to play through all of them).

Well, as I sort of inferred at the beginning of this review, I ended up absolutely loving Dark Souls III, considering it a masterpiece of a game, and really being the title at this point that has lead me to start collecting and playing through every Fromsouls game that I can, no matter how difficult they are supposed to be, with the intention of playing through all of them blindly, offline (you don't have to do that, but I just prefer solo offline gaming experiences myself), and trying to accomplish everything that I possibly can in a playthrough in that way, which is what I did with Dark Souls III, beating every optional boss from the DLC, and finding every item and completing every quest that I could on such a playthrough, which took me around 130 hours to do, and was more than worth every moment and every penny that I spent on this amazingly memorable experience.

It was definitely challenging at times. There were a few parts that I died on dozens of times . So, yeah... Maybe you're on the fence about diving into these allegedly unforgivingly challenging games, maybe you've tried some for a little bit and thought it might not be your thing but are still a little on the fence about the idea, maybe you want to start with one of the later titles or a spinoff of sorts like I did, but whatever you do, I would say to do it as blindly as possible, to know that overcoming the challenges in these games and playing them long enough to see the majesty of their artistic design can be incredibly rewarding experiences, and that you can "git gud" one way or another, either through mastering some kind of ideal build, through pure stubborn tenacity, or from just a desire to see more of the tons of content, incredibly intricate and immersive design, and unparalleled beauty that these nightmarish hellscapes have to offer. 10/10 would spill the blood of a dark soul again.

The 2008 Subprime Crisis in Dark Souls Terms
Imagine Lordran’s economy is based on Souls, and Firelink Shrine’s bankers (Lothric Bankers) start handing out Soul Loans to undead who clearly can’t pay them back. These are the “subprime mortgages.” At first, everything seems fine—Hollow merchants are buying castles (houses) with these loans, and traders are bundling bad loans into Soul Relics (mortgage-backed securities) and selling them to bigger banks like Anor Londo’s Silver Knights.

But soon, the Hollows default on their loans. The Soul Relics turn to Dark Sign ashes, and panic spreads across Lordran. Lothric Bankers collapse, the Firelink economy crumbles, and even powerful banks like Anor Londo’s go Hollow. The gods (government) scramble to rekindle the economy, but the damage is done—the Age of Dark (recession) begins.

Basically, everyone got greedy, bet on unstable undead, and triggered an End of Fire moment for the global financial system.

Coming to this game from the masterpiece of Elden Ring, I have absolutely enjoyed every second of my 165 hours of this game. While elden ring excels in exploration and the open world, this games level design is much more linear, so it has more of a focus on combat and build creation. If you want to get 'more' Elden Ring, definitely download this game. The combat is near identical, with the exception of jump attacks (you can still jump though) ,having many different types of weapons, movesets, skills and magic. Another postitive about this game is that you dont need to do a near identical 2 hour long setup every single playthrough, like in elden ring. However, the game is slightly harder in the fact spirit ashes dont exist (you can still summon other players, but the boss gains increased health). Overall, this game is wonderful, and one of the best fantasy RPGs I have ever played. Thank you, fromsoftware, for this!

Best souls game in the entire franchise without a doubt. Absolute must-play if you call yourself a Gamer.

It fixed almost every issue that DS1 and DS2 had. It's definitely so much more re-playable too. People complain about the "linearity" but I personally don't mind it at all. It makes the game so much more approachable, and bare-bones. especially for new players.

Not an easy game, but It's not TOO hard either. I'd say this game and Sekiro has the best balancing out of all the games. The game controls marvelously, and is extremely responsive. You will die many times without a doubt, but none of your deaths should really feel cheap or unfair.

All of the bosses have amazing move-sets, along with their own set of strengths and weaknesses. Vast majority of bosses attacks are readable, and well telegraphed. So I'd say ANYONE could beat this game, even those who suck at video games.

Forgive my kiss-up-ery. I just love this game so damn much, man. It's my favorite game of ALL TIME. It's just that simple, down-to-earth, souls formula that makes it so good. You make a character. Pick a starting class. Run around the world collecting souls and defeating bosses. Dying a bunch of times. Finding new weapons that you like. Learning. Getting stronger. And finally, Replaying the game in NG+ with all your gained knowledge from your previous play-through(s). It's simply addicting. 9.5/10 game for me.

Posted: March 25
After my first playthrough of Elden Ring, needless to say, I wanted more FromSoftware. I initially wanted to play through the Dark Souls games in release order, but after playing Dark Souls: Remastered for a couple of hours, I made the decision to skip forward in the library of Dark Souls games to something a little more familiar, and I'm so glad I played Dark Souls III when I did. Dark Souls III proved to be one of my favourite experiences with a game, providing me with a whole new perfectly crafted world to explore, new characters to meet, while also introducing me to my favourite bosses of all time.

Gameplay:
Dark Souls III is probably the easiest game in the series to get into for a fan of Elden Ring, as the gameplay is almost identical, minus features like jumping, crafting, summoning, and guard counters (something that really should be in every FromSoftware game going forward). The lack of these features surprisingly wasn't a problem for me, and if anything, made the experience feel more grounded; as if the fat was cut, leaving only the best of the Souls-like experience.

Side Content:
Dark Souls III doesn't feature as much side content as later FromSoftware games like Elden Ring, but the quests it has are all extremely solid. The quests, for the most part, are very cryptic and will definitely require a guide if you intend to do any on your first playthrough. Dark Souls III continues the FromSoftware staple of an endearing cast of characters, most of which have their own side-quests rewarding the player with special items and emotional moments. Greirat and Siegward especially stand out, with both quests ending tragically, but providing a memorable journey for anyone who accompanies them throughout their journeys. Some quests even influence the end of the game, with Anri of Astora in particular offering a unique ending.

Bosses:
If there was one aspect in which Dark Souls III excelled most in, it would be with it's boss fights. Unlike later FromSoftware games which featured a significant number of filler bosses, Dark Souls III features absolute banger after banger, with only a handful of underwhelming or bad fights; such as the Curse-Rotted Greatwood, Deacons of the Deep, and the Ancient Wyvern. Dark Souls III instead offers some of the most well crafted fights in any game I've ever played, with the biggest highlights for me including Iudex and Champion Gundyr, the Abyss Watchers, Pontiff Sulyvahn, Dragonslayer Armour, the Nameless King, and the Soul of Cinder. All the bosses feel fast, but fair, remain well paced, and lack many of the BS attacks that began to appear following this game.

Levels:
Dark Souls III also contains some of the best areas in any game I've played. The following are my thoughts regarding what I consider to be the game's best areas. This game's version of Firelink Shrine, while unfortunately completely separated from the rest of the world, is a well made hub area, with easy to access merchants and locations. The High Wall of Lothric is a great first area, with lots of locked doors, challenging early-game enemies, and lots of great secrets to find. The Cathedral of the Deep is a fun place to explore, with falling from highs being a constant problem to manage. Irithyll of the Boreal Valley is perhaps the game's most visually striking area, the gorgeous blue really setting the city apart from the rest of the game. The return to Anor Londo, although brief, is an unforgettable experience. Lothric Castle is a pretty fun time so long as you run past the big knights with swords that can two-shot you. The Grand Archives is another area that masterfully uses elevation to it's advantage, and in hindsight, likely inspired the Shadow Keep from Elden Ring: Shadow of the Erdtree, one of my favourite legacy dungeons in that game. And finally, the Untended Graves is a great area, flipping the familiar into something else entirely, hinting at major themes later explored in The Ringed City. With all the positive stuff out of the way, some of Dark Souls III's areas do fall a little short of the greatness other areas achieve. The Undead Settlement is not necessarily a bad area, but is rather underwhelming following the grand spectacle of the High Wall of Lothric, the Catacombs of Carthus are just sorta underwhelming, and worse, a little boring; yet another tomb filled with skeletons, how original. The Smouldering Lake is interesting visually, but there really isn't much to the area outside of disabling the ballista and dying to the giant worm thing. Finally, the Irithyll Dungeon is straight-up terrible, and every second I stay there, the more I want to pull my hair out.

Story:
Dark Souls III continues the Dark Souls trend of ambiguous storytelling, while also making many of its themes, particularly those in relation to the cycle of light and dark, more easily accessible. Once again, I'm not familiar enough with the game's lore to comment on the story too much, so once again, I'll let Vaati do his thing.

Soundtrack:
Dark Souls III is home to the best soundtrack in the Dark Souls series. Every track feels so epic and impactful, with very few songs that I don't enjoy listening to. I highly recommend listening to the soundtrack, especially if you are a fan of orchestral choir music. Highlights for me include the main theme, Vordt of the Boreal Valley (specifically the second phase), Pontiff Sulyvahn, Dragonslayer Armour, Nameless King, Abyss Watchers, Lorian, Elder Prince and Lothric, Younger Prince, Spear of the Church, Darkeater Midir, Slave Knight Gael, and Epilogue. One thing that I need to mention is how the final boss theme reveals so much about the game's lore. The first phase of Soul of Cinder represents every being that has linked the fire previously, using their techniques to fight you. But upon the defeat of the first phase, the Soul of Cinder will unleash the power of the first to link the fire; Gywn. The orchestra fades away, and you are left with Gywn's tragic piano accompanying the boss, who now wields his moveset. It is the perfect end to the fight, telling the game's story though just a couple of notes.

Ashes of Ariandel and The Ringed City:
Finally, Dark Souls III offers some of best FromSoftware DLC we've ever seen. Ashes of Ariandel, the first expansion, although short, makes up for it with it's final boss and visuals. Set against the backdrop of a beautiful snowy landscape, Ashes of Ariandel feels like a natural extension of the base game. Sister Friede, the final boss of the expansion is one of the most difficult bosses I've ever fought, boasting three separate health bars across three distinct phases. The satisfaction that came with my first victory over her is one that I've rarely felt, even when playing other FromSoftware titles. But Dark Souls III's DLC hasn't hit it's peak just yet. The Ringed City is some of the best content made by FromSoftware, containing some of the most visually appealing environments the game has to offer, but also three of my favourite Dark Souls bosses; The Demon Prince and their super unique duo mechanic, Darkeater Midir, FromSoftware's second best dragon fight (until Bayle the Dread), and what is perhaps my favourite boss of all time; Slave Knight Gael. Slave Knight Gael does everything perfectly; a perfect arena, perfect music, perfect moveset, perfect story, perfect visuals, perfect difficulty, I cannot stress enough just how good this fight is, a clash between two insignificant specks of ash at the end of the world. The perfect way to close out the Ringed City DLC, and Dark Souls III as a whole.

Conclusion:
Dark Souls III was a perfect game for me to go to after Elden Ring, and there are parts of my first playthrough that I will never forget. Dark Souls III isn't my favourite game of all time, but there is nothing that will ever be able to remove it from it's place within my top ten.

Both DS1 and DS2 are flawed masterpieces in their own way. DS3 deviates from this however. It's got an extremely polished gameplay experience with a lot of the idiosyncrasies (both good and bad) no longer being present in the game. This does lead to far fewer moments of frustration and more moments of genuine skill expression but it looses the charm and *soul* the earlier games have. That being said it sill compiles many of the best parts of earlier games. Combat feels fluid and bosses feel challenging but fair, the environments have fantastic atmosphere, and each level has an interconnected feel despite the games more linear design.

This is my first soulborn game...
Like people say it should be, for tho it is hard, its still more accessible.
And tho not the hardest, it aint the most casual either, far from it. A perfect introduction to these type of games I guess.

Well lemme tell you if this game actually worth your money-> and my answer is IT DOES, but on sale.
For this is a 7.4/10 game
A hard, frustrating, relatively long experience that youll never forget.
(this is my rating where the best game ever made will probably be 9.5)
And he is a summery down below if you care to read, peace yall..

PROS:
Artstyle- awesome, nothing bad to say about it just an epic grand and gothic.

Visuals, enemy and weapon design- Beautiful and cool AF, so many weapons and different scenery all through out, this game really makes you feel small.

Story- Im not sure if its a con or a pro to most people but liked it, and thats because its VERY ambigious.
Like really so, buts thats kind of the charm of it, I personally definitly prefer it over a complicated plotline which is in your face. And if you really look into it its actaully have an highly unique and fascinating lore, and I think it adds to the game positively.. Just makes it biblical, just makes it more... "EPIC"

Performance- good, the graphics are not the best Ive seen but the visuals compensate on that, there is some stuttering rarely and maybe one of two crashes in character creation... its simply good enough.

And now....
Combat- ... Yeah this is the entire point of this game, extremely punishing combat in modern game standard.
Its addicting, to say the least. For when you open up the game you are not thinking before hand on the awesome story and beautiful place you wanna go, you are thinking of the horrid time you are going to experience and the relief youd get after while doing so in a knight costume.
Just if you havent played anysoul game just know there is something called stemina..
There are some stuff about specifically that I terribly dislike, specifically how little control you got over your character, know that when an attack or a dodge is clicked, it will be attempted and theres no going back.... despite all that, what is good over all outweights it more or less.
For the adrenline you get after beating a boss or an annoying enemy is what makes it soo addicting.
Some of you may feel unexplained joy while concoring this challanges...
P.S Tho I myself only felt that joy when beating nameless king, I actually love that boss, hated every other XD


CONS:
compatibilty with m&k- zero basically its just inexcusibly terrible, you can misclick tons of action you dont want to, and the action tutorial CANNOT be translated from controller to keyboard and its god aweful, you gotta learn it externiliy online, which reminds me.

Tutorial- There is none, the "tutorial" explains to you dog shii, and thats so with many things in the game specifically with items and quest items are just too abigious or unexplained, which now reminde me of...

Questline- Just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that you would not discover on your own, they are basically easter eggs rather then quests, they can also be completely ruined over the dumbest things. same thing with achivements.

Camera- I dont need to explain it lol, its the most godawful camera controls Ive ever seen in a video game.

Replayabiliy- surprisingly not as much as I expected, I guess its just too leanier, its still replayable just not that much and certain areas definitly feel like "did this done that", just a tiny bit of a con for me.

LEARNINESS- this is the major issue that is going to strike you, this game has a grind but its just neutrual part of the game, the real reason why your first playthrough is going to be 60 hours is because this game is SUPER LEARNY.
Remebered how I said this game doesnt explain ♥♥♥♥, well this is very true with the combat as well and its way the game stretch on, you just need to learn pin point accuracy things that you cannot guess.
And in some ways it is a good thing, but other times like with common enemies and all it just feel like time's wasted and you cannot be asked...

So yeah BE WARNED I would say its overall a good game, even a legendary game with its infemey.
But its not a nice game, In a pretty bad way actually.

IT'S FINALLY FINISHEDDD!!!

After a long and grueling 33 hours, 33 minutes, and 21 seconds I have conquered every single Dark Souls 3 achievement! I've loved this game for many years, but this challenge was no short task.

To anyone wanting to 100% Dark Souls 3, here's some words of advice: There will be several covenants that you must do to obtain all rings. This will require several hours committed repeatedly killing the same enemies over and over going for a <5% drop rate. But there is one saving grace, some covenants allow you to be summoned to get their reward! This means you're allowed to put a red summon sign and have a friend summon you and repeatedly die to you to get the reward. Drastically reduces the time it takes although does not yield any souls.

GL to whoever is next!

Posted: March 19
Dark Souls 3 isn’t a game—it’s a roaring blaze of a tale, a final bow dripping with ash and beauty, the kind of peak fiction that sticks with you like a scar. Lothric’s world is a graveyard of dreams, all twisted towers and smoky skies, so pretty you almost forget it wants you dead. The design is pure genius, a maze of gloom that feels like it’s mocking you with every step. Combat’s a wild dance—fast, fierce, and unforgiving. Every swing’s a thrill, every dodge a victory shout against a world that’s already given up.

I practically cheered when I cut down the Abyss Watchers, their chaos matching my own, and I stood slack-jawed at the Nameless King’s lightning show. The story’s a tangled mess of fire and betrayal, so thick with meaning it’s almost too much to unpack. Five stars, because it’s a masterpiece of dark beauty that nearly steals the crown—but not quite. It’s still kneeling at the altar of Dark Souls 2, the holy grail of fiction that outshines them all.

I enjoy all Fromsoft games but this one leaves a salty taste in my mouth after coming back to it.
Each version from Demon souls to Elden ring has its ups and downs and I don't think any one of them really deserves a negative review.

Most would say Dark souls 2 is the worst, to me it ain't great either.
So the subjective statement is which one do you like, because I think each one is different enough to attract a different audience as a whole. Each is similar, but some designs or mechanics change everything.
Everyone casually completes each one made, then they move back to the one they enjoy the most.

I came back to DS3 cause I wanted to 100% every souls game, yeah that ain't happening lol.

I find this versions combat irregular.
Some enemies just never stop, like they have no stamina bar.
Poise is meh and it feels like enemies have a magic number of poise not based on armor.
Shields feel very useless except for parrying, even when you have a lot of stamina.
Hit boxes are ♥♥♥♥♥ terrible, being hit behind an enemy completely facing away from you.
The hit scans are so ♥♥♥♥♥ funny, literally roll behind an enemy mid swing and watch them turn and hit you.

The Iframes are funny as well, all people do in pvp is roll around because blocking and parrying is almost a waste of time. Oh and back stabbing is the worst in any souls game. I have caught many people / enemies in back stab animation, but for some reason the camera gliches and they break free from it and I stab the air while they are free to move. It's not that you can't back stab, but why would I wont to chance having that happen to me mid fight.

One thing that really got me was the claw enemies in painted world, their leap attack can literally go through walls and still grab you.

That being said it is still a good game, some people enjoy this version more than the rest.
For me I don't think I will be coming back to this version.

I enjoy Dark souls and Elden ring, so I will go back to them.
If you enjoy DS2 or DS3 then play and enjoy them.

Don't forget to GitGud

Posted: March 17
This review may contain spoilers.

Pretty much every “gamer” knows the dark souls series, even if you haven't played it yourself.
It was the same for me until i streamed it once for a friend.
I played the “seamless coop” mod that was released 2 weeks ago with this friend.
Even though I'm still new to the game myself, I've seen a lot of content (streams & videos) about the series and other games like Elden Ring and Sekiro.
Dark souls 3 is a wonderful follow-up to the other games.
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬⠀▬▬▬⠀▬▬⠀▬
Positive things

+ Fabulous boss, mob and level design.
+ Wonderful music that contributes a lot to the atmosphere.
+ Interesting side quests that tell more about the lore.
+ Timeless graphics that still impress almost 10 years later.
+ Countless weapons and different spells, giving you a wide choice of play styles.
+ More content through 2 DLCs - Ashes of Ariandel and The Ringed City.
+ Achievments.
+ Replay value through NG+, PvP and other things.
+ Mod support.
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬⠀▬▬▬⠀▬▬⠀▬
Negative things

- There's not really much for me to criticize about the game and if there was, it would only be the very long grind when farming to complete the different oaths. (important to get 100% achievments)
▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬⠀▬▬▬⠀▬▬⠀▬


Souls Like games are usually never designed for co-op, but I would have liked something like this to be available.
The mod offers a great opportunity, even if some things don't work quite as well in NG+.
We are really looking forward to the new Elden Ring, because there is an existing coop.

Really really difficult game. The world building and level design and sense of atmosphere are, go figure, second to none. I come back to this from time to time to start a new run and play through the first like 4 or 5 location/levels because DS3 is just stupidly immersive and fascinating and, when you win, satisfying. I don't get much further than Irithryll before I get exhausted by the grind. This is some of the best world building I have ever seen in a game, and probably the hardest game I've ever play.

Very steep learning curve. Highly demanding combat. Demanding of your determination and demanding of your focus under pressure. Even demanding of your willingness to research strategies online because the game tells you very little other than You Will Die. Bosses will tear you down regardless of your level scaling or build. This is fun for some, for me it feels like work once I get to the mid game. The levels and enemies are phenomenal. The bosses are absolutely exhausting.

S-tier game, but not for everybody.

Have a nice day.

**A Flawless Masterpiece – Dark Souls III**

Dark Souls III stands as the pinnacle of the Souls series, a breathtaking culmination of everything that made the franchise so legendary. It refines every aspect of its predecessors and delivers an experience that is both exhilarating and unforgettable. From its stunning world design to its tight combat mechanics, Dark Souls III is, without a doubt, the best title in the series and one of the greatest games ever made.

The game’s world is a true work of art, filled with stunning visuals, incredible level design, and a haunting atmosphere that keeps you fully immersed from beginning to end. Areas are beautifully crafted, with a mix of sprawling landscapes and intricate, interconnected zones that reward exploration. From the decayed beauty of Lothric Castle to the fiery depths of the Profaned Capital, every location is meticulously designed, offering secrets to discover and paths to uncover. The attention to detail is unmatched, and the world feels alive, with a sense of history and purpose in every corner.

The combat in Dark Souls III is the smoothest and most refined in the series. The movement feels fluid and responsive, with improved agility and weapon handling compared to previous games. The addition of new weapon types, magic, and combat stances adds depth and variety to the gameplay, ensuring that every player can find a playstyle that suits them. Battles feel intense and rewarding, requiring both precision and strategy. Whether you’re facing hordes of enemies or one of the game’s many epic bosses, every fight is a test of skill and patience that never feels unfair. The boss fights themselves are some of the most memorable in the series, from the emotional confrontation with the Nameless King to the heartbreaking battle with Gundyr. Each encounter is a work of art, challenging and thrilling in equal measure.

Dark Souls III also delivers the most refined and compelling narrative in the series. The storytelling is more focused than ever, with a sense of closure to the series’ long-running themes of fire, darkness, and the cycle of life. The lore is deep and rich, but it’s woven seamlessly into the world rather than delivered through lengthy exposition. It’s the kind of storytelling that rewards players who pay attention to detail and piece together the mysteries, but it never feels overwhelming. The end of the journey is as satisfying as it is bittersweet, providing a fitting conclusion to the saga that began with the original Dark Souls.

The multiplayer elements in Dark Souls III are outdated but still good. The online integration that allows players to easily invade, co-op, or engage in PvP. The dynamic between players—whether it's helping each other through tough areas or battling for supremacy in the arena—adds another layer of excitement and community to the experience.

Overall, Dark Souls III is a perfect storm of everything that made the series great, all delivered in a polished, refined package. It improves upon every aspect of its predecessors while adding its own unique flair, creating a game that is thrilling, challenging, and profoundly rewarding. From the atmospheric world design to the fluid combat, Dark Souls III is the definitive experience for Souls fans and anyone looking for a truly unforgettable gaming adventure. It’s not just the best in the series; it’s a defining achievement in the history of video games.

Easily the strongest entry in the series in both mechanics and storytelling, but hampered by its need to callback and reference the first entry and ignore the second one.

The game exemplifies the design philosophies that Fromsoft had nailed down to a science by this point. The important NPCs all are easily visited in a contained space, the world winds around itself and one area's skybox is another one's terrain, the lore is by turns cryptic and mysterious and grandiose and arch depending on how much the player pays attention, the design is more hideous and twisted while retaining the dignified air of fantasy Europe, and the usual list can continue from there. It's a masterpiece of third-person Action RPGs and a crown jewel of the Souls-like genre. Unfortunately, it is very concerned with reminding you of the other games in its series and often sacrifices new ideas and areas for straight up lifted sections of other games. Characters offer bread crumbs of old names and ideas to pursue their stories. So it begs comparison with the other shining gems of Fromsoft's catalog which shows its minor weak points.

It has much less backtracking than the other two. So while it moves faster and more linearly it still is about the same amount of content. The bosses are greater than ever and the enemy design, when it's not repeating older ones, is strong and interesting for the entire main game. There are some all timer dungeons, but there are several that are basically interesting hallways that I forget about completely before every play-through.

I'm not the biggest fan of the DLCs because they feel like rushed extensions of old concepts that hide their short length with difficulty hikes, but it is nice to see some more old concepts and mysteries revisited. If there's one place the game could use some padding it's near the end.

Overall, strong recommend and if it weren't for Elden Ring I'd consider it the best way to get into the series. However, it's worth revisiting the other two first just so all the references and repeated locations make sense.

Started the Dark Souls trilogy for the first time a couple months back and have now finally finished it. Dark Souls 3 is the actual sequel to Dark Souls 1.

It's a solid game overall, but of course with a lot of jank and unpolish (per From quality as always from their earliest days back with Evergrace, all the way to Elden Ring). Such as an incredibly high amount of constant pop-ins, and a lot of terrain not being properly put together, many times you can see gaps between bricks where you can look past them into the next room.

Design-wise, I dislike how they further encourage people to stick with faster weapons, since there's zero passive poise (huge mistake with nothing else to take its place), and hyper-armour (which is a fine concept, but not to be by itself). I likewise dislike the trend of From games post-DS1, where they've heavily sacrificed world building and in particular, the interconnectedness of DS1, in favour of their mediocre combat (of which From has and still isn't good at). So many enemies have insanely high tracking, combos, jumps, and gap-closers, meanwhile the player has nigh-nothing in all of these fields. Oftentimes, especially with particularly poorly designed and theatrical bosses (e.g. Midir and Gael) you feel like the NPC watching the 'player' doing all of these flashy moves and you're waiting 90%+ of the time. But, sadly, this is apparently that the vocal playerbase likes as From keeps going in this direction. So if you like the spectacle of spammy and messily-designed bosses, you may like the bosses in this game in particular. For what it's worth, Champion Gundyr and then Nameless King are by far my favourites in DS3.

A couple of suggestions for those who will be playing DS3: I recommend using a reshade of some sort (I made my own, very easy to do, I recommend you to make your own too instead of copying another's) as the game as a base is incredibly washed out and grey. And lastly, remember to perform some gestures in-front of the firekeeper–she has some cute reactions of her own!

Sheer brilliance. Besides Sekiro, I (like many) had heavily seen souls games as mires of grinding and pattern recognition, not fully understanding their intended dynamic of risk mitigation using all resources at your disposal (granted many mechanics have little to no explanation and I had to figure them out over years of various titles).

This is the game with which the design finally clicked, and I understood the dynamic fully. It's not bragging (my reflexes are not great) but a testament to the game's beautiful pacing and balance to say I was able to complete the whole game with no grinding, no guides, no getting stuck, and almost no bosses taking more than 3 tries (6 at most). By the lategame I was soloing most bosses on my first encounter, not due to godlike reflexes or luck, but due to most of their attacks being very survivable with suitable stat allocation and equipment. I even foolishly lost an estus upgrade when i tried to see if the game would let me trade it, and even with a slightly weaker estus still never felt starved for healing.

Maybe one day I'll make a more detailed review with better context for players going in blind, but all I can say is, if you find yourself bashing your head against a wall, the game is telling you there is another solution. If damage seems unavoidable, consider how you can minimze the threat. If you can't beat a group of enemies, you're not supposed to let them mob you; funnel them and pick them off one by one. If an enemy counters your weapon or uses a damage type you're weak to, the surrounding area will contain equipment or items to counter them; don't be afraid to switch up your equipment and approach. If your strategy feels reasonable but an enemy is just doing way too much or taking too little damage, they are a gatekeeper telling you to keep exploring for another path and return later; by the time you have exhausted other paths, your level will be high enough, or you will have discovered a scripted way to eliminate them safely. Cash out when you have the souls for a levelup, use your items, use your embers, and never be afraid to call on a Jolly Cooperator (Cordial Intruder?)

The game is pretty light on mechanical explanation however. If you feel too lost with character progression, I'd recommend looking up a basic explanation on weapon upgrading and stat scaling, and understanding the game is balanced around you making use of these mechanics. If you "mess up" your stat distribution early on when you don't know what you're doing, the game again gives plenty of ways to keep up your base damage until you understand which stats are relevant, by which point you will be given the ability to respec your stats how you want.

Also, if you keep just a general awareness of lore from item descriptions as you go along, you're in for a mindblowing revelation in the late game when threads begin to converge and all the seemingly mindless obstacles you've faced in the game suddenly gain context.

Brilliant design.

I left the game idling many hours, thoroughly explored first-time playthrough of base game is ~35 hours. Highly recommend for a thoughtful exploration-driven action rpg that revolves around player-driven discovery. Reflexes optional! (Don't overload encumbrance, Shields are your friend)

Dark Souls 3 is my favorite in the trilogy. It just feels much more polished than the first game (Bed of Chaos … need I say more?) and part 2 wasn’t exactly the best either :-S The environment is very well designed, and I personally like the bosses and gameplay the most out of all three games.

Achievements
Required time: 40-60 hours
Difficulty (in my opinion): 7 out of 10
DLCs required for 100%?: No

Going for 100% achievements is mostly fun again as well. You have to play through the game three times to get all three endings, and on top of that, you need to collect all gestures, rings, sorceries, pyromancies, and miracles. The rings in particular can be a hassle, as many of them are only available in NG++.

A lot of the collectibles are hidden behind obscure quests, so playing with a guide is highly recommended. As always, I recommend the excellent guides from Cynic 0055. The road to 100% also involves farming some really annoying covenant items. For some of them, you have to decide whether to get them online or offline. Farming them offline can take forever in certain covenants, while getting them online can be difficult due to the partially dead multiplayer. This can backfire quickly, especially if your level is too high and you can’t find online partners anymore.

Despite the grind, going for 100% was still fun for me the second time around (I had already achieved 100% on PS4 before).

Dark Souls 3 is the perfect ending to one of the greatest series of all time. The combat is very refined and tight compared to the previous titles. It is the most linear of the trilogy, which I think benefits the game in many ways. The difficulty curve is finely tuned and well balanced from start to finish, for example.

The world in DS3 is very bleak, which may not appeal to everyone, but it certainly works for me. I love the sense of hopelessness and decay the game provides. In the words of Miyazaki himself, "something beautiful needs something ugly - something that's depraved or tragic to heighten and embolden that beauty."

If you're on the fence on whether or not to purchase this game, just do it. It's an experience unlike any other. You won't regret it.

Posted: March 8
The most polished of all the Souls series, for sure. It delivers more than expected in terms of fan service (which I think is a good thing) with the correct dose of nostalgia in it, while also having the most exciting and fluid combat compared to previous Dark Souls games.

If you have already played previous Dark Souls games, something you should take note are:
It doesn't have the methodical, slow combat from Dark Souls 1 and 2
It doesn't have the same caliber of level design, lacking connected areas and freedom to roam anywhere in the world
This game is very boss fight oriented, the exploration being the less satisfying part of this game (in my opinion)
Please, consider that I'm comparing directly to previous Dark Souls games, and not games in general.

Now, for newcomers that had never played any souls games, for a long time I've recommended people to begin with DS3 if you are bothered by the graphics and clunkiness of DS1 and DS2, although I think you miss 50% of the experience if you had never played the other games before (you'll miss references, storylines that are continued in DS3, and also the overall glowup from the other games). But that's not the case anymore if you want a fresh Souls game that is appealing to play and watch, and ALSO are trying to learn the genre, Elden Ring is probably your best choice.
If you are interested specifically in the Dark Souls story, gameplay, legacy and dark fantasy theme, you should probably start with the first game instead.

Nonetheless, if you are looking for what the Dark Souls game could come to be, Dark Souls 3 is probably the combination of everything that is exciting about the series.

Buy the game if you can afford, summon friends, invade a lot, praise the sun, try to extract the best you can from the experience because it's something I believe everyone that loves games should have in this day and age.

Dark Souls III, while sadly a rather short game in comparison to it's previous two titles, was such a great experience, with so many cool bosses to fight and master, many different armours to try on, and some really cool characters and questlines (many of which I regrettably didn't finish or perhaps missed aside from Seigward and Lapp).
This is definitely a game I plan to come back to, either in NG+ or modded or what have you. I'd say this currently ranks 2nd in place for me when it comes to Souls games.

This is it. This is THE Souls-Game that has perfected the souls-formula. After over 200 hours in Dark Souls 3 as well as Elden Ring I can comfortably say that DS3 has the better gameplay.

In this game, you will experience the peak of boss design. DS3 bosses have something that makes them stand apart from souls bosses of other games. These bosses have been designed in such a way that fighting them is a real challenge while giving the feeling of a fair battle. For example, those bosses may delay their attacks but they give small but significant indicators which signalize when the attack is going to hit. Unlike in Elden Ring, these bosses do not overdo those delayed attacks. They have a healthy amount of such delays. It is quite a refreshing feeling. DS3's bosses also give you the time to punish. They WILL eventually end their attack and thereby give you the chance to hit. That is something that is missing in Elden Ring where sometimes bosses have seemingly endless attacks (Don't understand me wrong, I love the challenge of it but in the end DS3 gives you a much more satisfying feeling fighting the bosses due to the fairness). Moreover, these bosses allow you to use different builds which makes their design even better.
Another crucial aspect of DS3 is that these bosses are UNIQUE. They do not repeat occurring within the game which gives them such a special encounter every time. Comparing it to Elden Ring again, none of the bosses in DS3 is just a reskin of another boss. Each fight feels different and refreshing.
One more aspect that contributes to the uniqueness is the music. - Oh my god, this music is so GOOD. What have they done. I cannot describe how amazing the music is. The boss soundtracks are so distinctive, unmistakable and have all their own character. DS3 truly exceeds in the music design when comparing it to Elden Ring. Elden Ring has also good tracks but they are much more generic which gives them not such a special and unique sensation. On the other hand, DS3's tracks are so fitting within the fight itself and fit even with the lore of the bosses.
Generally, there is not a single bad boss - yes, here and there are some bosses that are not great but overall everyone does not give you a feeling of frustration. However, the good half of the bosses is just PEAK.

The linearity of the levels may be something that is not for everyone appealing. However, in my opinion DS3 has implemented the linear level design greatly. I had never the feeling getting bored due to the linearity. Moreover, the game provides some forks within the roads where you can choose to go first but in the end the path is mostly linear. The levels itself are engaging to explore and go through. They are interesting and the enemies in each level differ, giving a good amount of variety and interest. One aspect of the linearity that most people may overlook is that it gives the game great scaling of difficulty. Hereby, bosses as well as enemies are more perfectly scaled with your current character level, making the bosses even more fair. In contrast, the exploration of Elden Ring really hurt the bosses. While some bosses have been way to overscaled - which forced me to sit many hours on them - , other bosses have been wayyyy to easy due to being overleved which made the boss fights itself much more uninteresting.

Yet, there is one thing that lacks quality. Namely, online pvp. The problem of online pvp is that the delay is so huge which makes fighting almost unpredictable since out of nowhere the opponent may hit you even though your character has been away in a safe distance.

Lastly, I want to mention that Slave Knight Gael is the best boss ever made. It combines all the great features from every boss in the souls series. The fight is so epic with all the things going on - the attacks of Gael, the music in the background, everything is just perfect. No other boss comes even close to the design of this boss. It is worth buying DS3 just to fight Gael.

My steam hours are misleading, I have over 500+ hours on this game through other installations. Best game that I have ever played, I love it so much. Words cannot describe the amount of sheer impact this game has had on my life, sounds dramatic but it has gotten me through some of the hardest times of my life.

I love the semi-open world vs the full open world like Elden Ring, I value the structured experience vs free reign.

The world of Lothric is dark, hauntingly beautiful, and richly detailed. The environmental storytelling is exceptional. Every area feels alive with history, and the interconnected world design invites you to explore every inch. The bosses are challenging and memorable, each with their own unique mechanics that will keep you on the edge of your seat.

Dark Souls 3 also strikes a fantastic balance in difficulty: it’s tough, but fair. Each death feels like an opportunity to learn, and every victory feels earned. It’s a game that truly respects the player’s time and skill, and the sense of accomplishment you get from progressing is unmatched.

If you’re a fan of deep lore, tight combat, and challenging gameplay, Dark Souls 3 is a must-play. It’s a game that doesn’t just ask for your time, it rewards it in spades. Whether you're a seasoned Souls veteran or new to the series, this is a journey worth taking.

Posted: March 1
I came to this game straight after finishing my first ever Fromsoft game (Elden Ring) on the hunt for the same masochistic dopamine hits from defeating crazy cool and over the top bosses. Safe to say, this did not disappoint.

Even when considering this game is 6 whole years older than Elden Ring, it did not feel overly clunky or frustrating whatsoever in terms of gameplay. Obviously the graphics and overall scope of the game aren't nearly as impressive but they simply don't need to be. In fact, I'd even argue that certain elements of this game are done much better than Elden Ring in some regards.

For example, the main plus for this game has to be the boss designs, both in terms of aesthetics and gameplay. Besides the odd gimmick style boss towards the start of the game that fail to thrill or really leave much of an impression, the overall boss quality of this game is top notch, and probably the best of the three Fromsoft games I have now played (DS3, Elden Ring, Bloodborne) in terms of consistency.

Moreover, the level design, whilst relatively linear for the most part, is almost always engaging and rarely feels too frustrating or punishing.

Like with nearly any game though, there are of course a few cons. Admittedly though, DS3's are pretty minor for me:
- As mentioned, some of the early bosses could have the potential for someone less patient to stop playing. Not due to difficulty, but more so that a few feel quite uninspired/forgettable.
- The overall boss count is too low in my opinion, especially when considering how at least a quarter of them are pretty mid IMO.

Overall however, this game would be worth playing for a few of the best bosses alone, and the peaks of this game are so high that you will certainly not regret playing.

8.5/10.

Overall good game. Got it day one on console and got the soundtrack on disc as a preorder bonus. Great game. Mechanically, it might be the best Souls game.

Unfortunately, the amount of one shots and stun locks in the game keep it from being a better game than it could be. They really emphasized more "punishment" in the game than they did difficulty. A lot of fans get those two terms confused for the same thing and apparently the developers did as well. Not really a hard game, but one screw up can delete your entire health bar even when it's more than halfway across your screen.

Input Reading is still stupid and enemies should have a more normal reaction to your character healing or attacking. Seeing the enemy dodge away at the exact same time I press the attack button is dumb. Seeing the enemy start an attack at the same time I press heal is also dumb. This makes the game feel more like a guessing game. "What inputs am I allowed in this fight?". This limits the player to only being allowed to use heal or R1/RB(Basic attack) when the enemy is already in the middle of another animation.

Arbitrary rules like "This merchant disappears from Firelink Shrine if you progress past this point and you can no longer access their shop until the next playthrough" is still in. Another aspect I don't like about these games. This was old since the original Dark Souls but it's still in the game.

Best part: A lot of these issues are still in the most recent release(Elden Ring) but on the bright side, This game has probably the fewest amount of delayed attacks and it actually feels good. Not too many, but just enough. From Software doesn't seem to fix a lot from their games per title, it's more like after four sequels, you have all the fixes you should've had in one but it's better than nothing and at least you no longer are required to clawgrip the controller the play the game optimally in the upcoming game. It only took 16 years but I guess it's better than never.

I actually really do like this game, I promise. It's just that I recently Alt+F4'd from being one-shot by a boss and need to complain.

This was my third FromSoftware game after Elden Ring and Sekiro. Compared to my previous experience, the game offered a refreshing look at the classic of the Souls series. Of course, there are some outdated mechanics and the lack of a normal jump makes you get used to the game again. However, after a few hours of playing, the atmosphere and music completely immerse you in the gameplay and you forget about all the minor flaws. The design of the bosses, game levels and the world as a whole is at the top level. Of course, compared to Elden Ring and Sekiro, the bosses are much weaker and almost all the bosses in the game were quite easy to defeat. After 50 hours, I was able to complete the entire main game and both DLC and NG+ for all achievements. After 8 years from release the game stands the test of time and I definitely recommend this game to all newcomers to the world of Miyazaki games. 10/10

This is one of the greatest souls-like games i have ever played. There is tons of stuff to do in DS3, for example; Explore the entire map and find out secret locations/secret interaction, like a specific gesture on a spot, 100%ing the game and lastly starting many runs with different builds each time. The bosses of this game were incredibly difficult, and were made to perfection with design and lore. My personal favourite in the base game was Nameless King, as it was one of the most challenging bosses i have fought in the base game + the most fun to fight because of the amount of perfectly timed dodges you have to do, and trying to learn his moves with each death.

Now, onto the dlc's. I felt as though ashes of ariandel was kinda lacking in alot of aspects, like the bosses, and lore. It was kinda lackluster to me, as there wasn't much to do besides explore 95% of the dlc and barely get to fight any of the bosses (which there was only 2). Though, the Sister Friede boss fight was somewhat challenging, it just wasn't 'calling' to me, like how Nameless King was + the Champion Gravetender boss fight was, in my opinion, the worst boss fight i have ever done.

Onto the 2nd dlc. The Ringed City was such a massive upgrade than Ashes of Ariandel, as there was much more bosses to fight, and way less exploring needed to do to fight a singular boss. The Ringed City is way more enjoyable to explore, and the bosses in it were way better in every other way than Ashes of Ariandel.
My favourite boss in Ringed City is definitely going to be Gael, as he has so much lore, and one of my personal favourite boss fights throughout the souls-like Genre. This boss was very challenging, and was much more enjoyable than every other boss i have fought, because of the amount of perfectly timed inputs you have to accomplish to fight gael, without nearly dying in a few hits. Another thing i love about gael is his lore. His lore stretches so much about how he was in search for the dark souls, so that he can help his lady to finish her painting, but went insane after finding them, and even going as far to kill us for our dark soul.

Overall, playing this game was the best thing i have ever done. It was the most fun game i have played in a while, and i would highly recommend people to play this, if they want to have fun, or challenge themselves.

Lore: 10/10. Greatest lore i have ever seen.
Gameplay: 10/10. The graphics is amazing, and the fluid gameplay is even better.
Replayability: 10/10, As there is so much builds you can try with each playthrough.
Overall: 10/10. There is so much to do in this game, and it is highly recommended by me.

15/10 because I couldn't find any flaws (except for some funny hitboxes).

I might have taken the better part of a decade to finally get to this game, but I'm clad I finally did. While graphically not that "pretty" Ds3 is still visually a very pleasing game. Distant castles, and tall towers many of which you can eventually explore, and all the level design that fromsoft has become famous for.
Intervowen paths that take you in so many different directions it's almost overwhelming in the best kind of way. A vague yet very interesting story and lore the world is full of, left largely up to the player to discover. A massive arsenal of different weapons, spells, and armors for the player to wield and wear.
Boss fights while mostly not too difficult in the base story are still entertaining, and the game has optional bosses that present a real challenge. On top of all that Ds3 has a staggering amount of replay value with the different builds and amount of different armaments to use.
Great game would buy again, and will play again.

Before my experience with Dark Souls 3, the souls-like genre was very distant to me. I had played for perhaps an hour or two before wondering if maybe I was taking on more than I could chew. So I uninstalled it and walked away. The very next morning I was confronted with the thought of trying again, which was very prevalent in my mind. So I gave in and reinstalled it, this time my poise would not shatter so easily. I struggled against early enemies, slaughtered characters for insignificant items which I had no use for. My decisions were terrible and they burdened me with guilt, regret and sorrow. During battles my feeble attempts preceded uncountable losses. There were times when victory seemed unachievable and so out of reach, times that made me think: “maybe this will be my pitiful discontinuance.” but back down and retreat I did not. For I had been motivated to disprove the words of one deserter whom I had dismally slew. He spoke in a nihilistic tone: “We’ll never amount to anything. Not you, not I”.

I thank you, Chase, for bestowing such a divine gift unto me.

Abandoned by the kindling, Lothric the realm
Withers in beauties that haunt our gloom dreams;
This splendid ash forsakes the grave to whelm
The lords that hie: one god-devourer deems
Old fable-chanted Irithyll the feast;
By charnel gates the wolf-blood dancers spire ...
(In all these lands the fog, in all the beast;
Lorn wyverns sloom and fade amid the mire;
Our nights laden with Londor pilgrims; husks
Of dimmest hope bell-stirred) ... In coil some lord,
Reclusive in his hypogean hoard,
Awaits a friend's promise; and where it dusks,
In gardens, naves, consumed, prince Lothric's scorn
Crawls in the heart of nameless ash, grave-born.

To pluck them all, fell bloom, where homes converge
Fog-loved: to link the flames or let flames die;
Secret desires within some hollow wisdom surge
In this sad land, where, yes, indeed, souls twist awry:
For other gloamings.

Dark Souls 3 is the best title combat wise, but lacks when it comes to unique areas. Almost every area has been done before in Dark Souls 1, 2 or Bloodborne, but is more refined in this game. The World is very linear compared to the other titles, which has the benefit of getting a steadily increasing challenge, without difficulty spikes or lows. On the other hand, the aspect of planning a route to play through the game, based on prior knowledge and build, is completely absent from this title, making replays very samey and without variety.

The Gameplay is Dark Souls, faster than DS1 or DS2 but slower than Bloodborne. There are lots of weapons and Spells to choose from, Rings to get a variety of buffs and Armor that has almost no use besides looking good.

DS3's Story takes place after countless rekindelings of the first flame, Kingdoms have come and gone over countless centuries and still the flame keeps dying. You play as unkindled ash, once cast away by the flame, unfit to rekindle now reawoken to rescue it. Your goal is to defeat past Lords of Ember, scattered throughout the World and bring them back to the Kiln for the flame to be relit once more. This is the only bit of story told to you, the rest is lore hidden behind Items.
