There is not much to say about Dark Souls 3 that has not already been said. It takes the very best parts of the first two games and rearranges them into an incredibly satisfying whole that hasn’t really been equalled since (and yes, that means it’s on par or possibly even better than Elden Ring).

From Dark Souls it borrows the incredible interlocking world. No longer do you feel like you are bouncing around between a host of disparate locations like in Dark Souls 2. Each hellish landscape is bound to the next here, with some amazing vistas to boot. There’s nothing better than reaching the top of a cathedral and being able to see all you travelled through to get there, or climbing a series of ladders out of a swamp to arrive on the far end of a bridge you were whisked away from a few hours before. Progression is more open than ever, with a myriad of different paths available one you leave Lothric castle. It’s just so damn fun to explore.

Conversely, from Dark Souls 2 it steals the faster-paced, snappier combat, where dodging is just a viable tactic as the good’ol sword-and-board. Being aggressive is important here too, much more so than in the first game, and turtling up behind your shield is the best way to get backstabbed, thrown of a cliff, or pummelled into oblivion. That goes for the bosses, which are hands-down the best From Software have created.

Among my favourites are the Dancer of Boreal Valley, with her unpredictable, wavering combos, the Nameless King and his fire-breathing dragon … and my favourite boss of any souls-like so far: Slave Knight Gael. This three-phase monstrosity toes the line expertly between hard and frustrating. His moves hit like a truck, but can be learnt and avoided. His health-pool is big, but not so humungous the fight lasts 30 minutes (I’m looking at YOU, Darkeaster Midir!). A masterclass.

Then there are all the easter eggs, the links to earlier games, the melancholy sadness of the final boss, the excellent DLC … I cannot think of a single thing I would have done differently. Except perhaps maybe the NPC questlines. It’s a personal choice, but I always prefer to take on the areas and different bosses solo, and having to summon some NPCs to progress their quests or obtain certain gestures is a pet peeve of mine I’ve been harbouring ever since the first game.

But really, if you even have the very slightest of interests in the soulslike genre, you owe it to yourself to experience this game at least once. An absolutely exceptional title that will be talked about and played for years and years to come.

Prepare to Die. But have great fun doing it.

Chest ahead.

I bring this up because Vordt's greathammer with perseverance is like a OP Endure! I trashed Pontiff Sulyvahn on my second try using Vordt's greathammer trading blows on boss that is resistant to frost. Renllana Twin Moon Night in Shadow of Erdtree is way harder than Pontiff Sulyvahn. DS3 places a great hammer in beginning of the game with the best frost damage in the game and with an OP special ability perseverance! Perseverance gives a poise buff is 6 s twice that of Endure that they nerfed to 3 s and Endure is still OP against all bosses except Malenia. So, in DS3, the bosses are fun and none are as difficult so far compared to Elden Ring. However, the gank mobs in DS3 seem to be more difficult than the gank mobs in Elden Ring. I like open world games for leveling a bit better due to the freedom to go somewhere else and level up and come back. but once I got the Vordt's great hammer, the game got easier. Right now I am in the dungeon after Pontiff Sulyvahn and there is one bleed gank mob that is not fun! I have not seen a gank mob like that one in Elden RIng! I am 48 hours in and took out Pontiff Sulyvahn on my second try at level 60. I finally got used to not being able to jump and could do the first skip in the game to get the more souls ring in the rafters of the shrine after learning from a challenge runner that the ring was up there. At first I had to put the game down due to the gank mobs of High Wall area, not knowing how to get to the bottom without being taken out due to no flasks left, where am I going to the next bonfire? I finally reached the bottom and took out Vordt using a +2 broadsword, then I got that great hammer with frost and perseverance ability and the game became fun. The bosses just do not seem to be as difficult as those in Elden RIng base game and gank mobs seem more over the top in DS3 compared to Elden Ring. But also, perseverance is OP endure and having a 6 s poise buff is twice as good as endure in Elden Ring so that may be why DS3 bosses seem to be easier compared to bosses in Elden Ring. I like DS3 and that Vordt's greathammer! That frost great hammer is OP on the level of being STUPID!!! If you played Elden Ring and liked it and never played DS3, I recommend it and give it go! This game has stupid OP broken weapons and builds and one of the best weapons has frost and you get it in the beginning of the game. I like tank and trade play style using Endure or Perseverance and as soon as I learned the frost great hammer had perseverance, I leveled it and not looking back.....Oh, I also like the ring concept as they act like Elden Ring Talismans, cool touch. Anyway, give it shot if you have not played it before, and it does not take too long to adjust if you have played Elden Ring. Just purchased the DLC pack today as I wanted to make sure I liked the game before purchasing the DLC package. Vordt's great hammer might be my favorite weapon in DS3 and Elden Ring combined but I do like Venomous Fang and Ancient Meteoric Ore Greatsword,,,,,but frost and perseverance is JUST SO BROKEN AND FUN.....

In Lothric’s land, where echoes wail,
I set my foot, so bold, so frail.
With sword in hand and heart of steel,
I thought, "This can’t be such a deal."

Each foe ensured I'd die distressed.

As I respawned throughout the night.

Midir’s fire? A pure disgrace,
My patience died—I raged, I braced.

I screamed alone in my dark room.
Yet when at last the flames burned bright,
I wept… then New Game Plus felt right.

So heed my words, ye brave and bold,
This game is pain, yet joy untold.
You’ll curse, you’ll break, you'll lose your soul—
And love it, wholly, as a whole.

A great game, definitely worth playing, but You should keep in mind that it differs a lot from the DS 1 or DS 2.

Lets start with cons:
1. Technical issues:
- Game doesn't support 21:9 screens (You will get black stripes on the sides of the screen).
- All control hints are for controllers - they're not adapted to the keyboard/mouse.
- Mouse sensitivity is way too high even on the lowest preset.
2. In contrast to DS 1 and DS 2, throughout the most of the game, You won't get that experience when You don't know where to go or if You did choose a right path - just because there's only one path (with slight deviations and optional detours a few times).
3. Also noticed that I ended the game with all the starting equipment (sword, armor, shield). I didn't feel the urge to replace them with any other piece of equipment. That's a little bit off-putting.
4. For me personally the first 5-7 hours of the game were really disappointing, and the linear locations didn't help that at all.

That's all. Everything else is just great.
- Controls are comfortable (except mouse sensitivity).
- Enemies are interesting (except for the first 5-7 hours) to deal with.
- Bosses are indeed engaging + there's no repetitiveness, which we had in DS 2.
- Setting, locations and sound design, graphics quality, all that is fantastic.
- Everything else, which I did forgot to mention is great too.

Posted: February 15
TL; DR: rolling simulator 3k of abyssmal despair with glimpses of happinnes

Levels:
This game has the most boring level design of all the Dark souls games. The levels are linear, filled with hyper aggressive enemies on crack from the first level (except tutorial) making exploring incredibly boring and exhaustive (if there even is anything to explore in the level, usually isn't). Each level is a spam rolling simulator because every single enemy in the game (except wolves in the dlc) is made for a 1v1 but there are multi fights everywhere. It makes it incredibly boring to interact with the enemies through the level, encouraging you to really just run past them which is what i did sometimes if i ever had to go through parts of the level for a second time or more. The best level in in my opinion is the Grand Archives which had good shortcuts and a lot of verticality. Some areas like Irithyll made up for the boredom with some good lore.

World map:
The world is also incredibly linear, making you feel like you're walking a straight line all game. There are a few side areas and at the end it loops back to the start but they somehow still made it feel like you are just walking a straight line all game. The world also looks really boring, it's so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GRAY. I know ds3 is like nearing the end of the world and stuff like that but they could actually have made it more exciting. This game is also the shortest of the three dark souls games.

Bosses:
The bosses are probably the best part of the game, but they still have their problems. One thing i do NOT enjoy in bosses is where you have to memorize a timing of a hit because it's awfully telegraphed. Happens in a few really cool boss fights in the game and it kinda drags you out of the experience of the fight. One really weird thing about the bosses: WHY DO SO MANY OF THEM TELEPORT? I don't have a problem with a boss teleporting in itself, it's just that too many did it where it removed the uniqueness of the boss battles, which brings me to another point: Bosses felt the same. It feels like all you do is roll in this game, normal enemies and bosses included. Luckily the game gives you almost infinite stamina. Positioning does almost nothing in this game. The bosses do have really cool concepts and lore, they did a good job there.

The game has high quality movement and gameplay with a snappy feeling. There is a good quality in every part of the game really, but just know that a high quality poop is still just a poop.


This game is carried by its lore and bosses actually making the game enjoyable. The rest is kinda ♥♥♥♥. 7/10.

So... you intend giving Dark Souls 3 a try? If you have never played Dark Souls before and/or are even entirely new to the Soulslike genre. Be prepared to struggle, related to your skills, patience, flexibility, style of playing the game and mental stability... do not be afraid! Muster your courage and accept the challenges lying ahead of you. You will improve and grow with every mastered hurdle on your path. Play with friends or all by yourself if you prefer. Should you falter, stumble and be in need for help remember this... Even in the darkest places you can find a glimmering light of hope, like pure letters of sunlight who will offer you aid, helping hands who will give you a soft push over the obstacles on your path. Everyone who had a long journey coming here through playing Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 2 (like me on console), have probaly played the game on its release. In case you missed this one for some reasons, look forward to familiar sceneries and encounters who will wake your fading memories from the past which are still bound to your emotions... praise the sun my friends! Let´s dive into Dark Souls 3 without too much spoil. There is a captivating atmosphere till the end, music, theme and the little stories of all zones are well made. Traveler, self-proclaimed heroes, huntress or hunted, take your time and let your glance be embraced by the stunning view of many charming locations. The freedom to travel across the game world, visiting most of all places you can discover in the far distance is great and gives the world a large, meaningful feeling while traveling through. The composition and structure of the different world zones are really pleasant. A wide variety of creatures will come across your path throughout the game (most of them will point their bare fangs towards you, better be careful). The many boss encounters will put up an engaging, entertaining fight which could leave you breathless sometimes. You can chose and engage in PvP activities or skip it entirely, be careful with all of your actions and choices regardless of PvE or PvP, they will have consequences. With an impressive arsenal you may let the greatest creatures fall on their knees. Dark Souls 3 provides a world you can literally sink into for hours, experiencing many different emotions. Maybe you can take something into your real life... such as being able to overcome every struggle with a clear mind, thinking and a bit of help when needed. It´s been a long journey for me including the predecessors Dark Souls 1 and Dark Souls 2. Is Dark Souls 3 a worthy ending of the trilogy? If you ask me, probaly yes it is... it depends on the point of view. Personaly I looked forward to see and interact with a wider variety of places, encounters, characters who have caught my interest and shaped the lore of Dark Souls. Some of them were only introduced a bit or not at all (a missed opportunity in my eyes). I know... I´m picky but in my opinion a bit more at least would have made the Dark Souls 3 experience perfect. Criticism aside, enjoy this game to your heart´s content! ,throw yourself into exciting encounters, explore an astonishing world or search for glorious treasures but be careful and look out for those who may bite you...

*Semi-Spoiler review*
Just finished my first play through of the base game & Ashes Of Ariandel and my thoughts on DS3 are mixed but mostly positive.

Base Game:
I found the levels to be designed quite well and the overall aesthetic to the levels were fantastic. The main problem I faced was that some levels were either over crowded or under crowded which messes with the pacing and the skill consistency needed to pass the levels. Though this problem wasn't every single level it was still very noticeable when present.

I enjoyed mostly all bosses Though I did find the gimmick type fights (e.g Wolnir, Yhorm and Ancient Wyvern) pretty lazy/boring but their appearance designs were great.
I think they could have made the final boss a lot more impressive, I personally found him to be rather underwhelming and easy. IMO nameless king would've made a perfect final boss.

Overall score for the base game: 8.5/10

Ashes Of Ariandal:
I completed this DLC in around an hour so i'll keep this just as short.

The level design was pretty mid and just felt like a clump of snow with squashed in enemies.
Only having 2 bosses was kind of a let down but I personally found both fights a lot of fun so that made up for it a tiny bit.

Overall score for Ashes Of Ariandal: 3/10

Edit:
Finished The Ringed City within around 2 hours.
The Ringed City DLC level design was abhorrent and tedious with tight paths and over done enemies which could've been overlooked if there were some cool weapons or cool enemies.
The Slave Knight Gael fight was the only enjoyable part of this DLC personally.

Overall score for The Ringed City: 3/10

I greatly enjoyed Dark Souls 3, but it was not the masterpiece I was expecting.

For background, I haven't played any other Dark Souls games. I have played some Souls-likes, particularly Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice. I 100%'d Sekiro and it's currently one of my all-time favorite games. I came into this experience expecting a challenging, fair gauntlet of brutally difficult and interesting combat. That is mostly what I did experience. I play on mouse and keyboard for all of my games, so that is the experience I describe here. In short: I would not consider Dark Souls 3 to be on the same level as Sekiro, although it is still a very good game overall, with my main issues being with the consistency of the experience. I would give it a 7.5 out of 10.

To go more into detail, let’s draw some comparisons to Sekiro. First, DS3 is an RPG where you design and build a character as you play. Sekiro has an eponymous protagonist you play AS, rather than a PC of your own. This means that while you can learn different combat styles and play your own way to some extent, there is not the same level of customization and open-endedness of style that DS3 offers. I actually think this serves to Sekiro's strength. DS3 is a demanding game and forces the player into one of several playstyles rather early on. If you are not following a guide or planning your build from the beginning without changing it, you will suffer. Not knowing what to expect, I started out doing a balanced STR/DEX build with sword and shield. After about six hours of play, I was so frustrated at my inability to defeat enemies that I completely changed my build and went all-in on STR with a massive greatsword. This was really fun and got me through the midgame with very few issues, and forced me to learn a new combat style in order to succeed. I appreciate the game for this, but my poor early decision-making meant that at higher levels, my points in DEX, VIG, and VIT were useless. Not wanting to grind out more levels for hours, I had to face the final bosses with imperfect stats and gear. It took much longer than usual for me to beat the endgame bosses for this reason. By contrast, Sekiro's more streamlined combat style means that no player is ever harshly punished for the style in which they choose to play.

The camera sucks. It's total garbage, it ruined dozens of fights for me. If I could change one thing about this game it would be that. Let’s move on.

One thing that gamers often laud about the DS series is how punishing its combat is. I found there is certainly a learning curve in the early game, and lategame bosses are highly difficult unless you have an optimized build; however, for the midgame bosses, the predictability of their movesets and how well-telegraphed they are actually made the fights trivial for me. I have a list at the bottom of how many tries each boss took me; some of them I beat in one try. In Sekiro, I consistently struggled against every boss before beating it, having to spend a great deal of time learning their movesets and timings before striking. This consistency of struggle meant that I was rewarded for my time and diligence in a consistent way; whereas in DS3 my feeling was much more of a series of difficulty spikes, bounded more by frustration than by feeling rewarded.

DS3 is often touted as tough but fair. I would not characterize it as such, at all. Tough, sometimes. Fair, sometimes. Both, sometimes, but just as often not. There is very little consistency about when the environment is destructible and when it is not, first of all. Some enemies and bosses will eviscerate large stone pillars by brushing against them, but those same pillars are immutable when hit with my greatsword for 500 damage. Another gripe: sometimes enemies can be hit through the environment, such that you can hit or be hit by an attack that somehow phases through part of a wall or a staircase. To be clear, I like that this is exploitable in a game that often feels unfair against me; it means I can make the game unfair in my favor for a moment instead. The point here is that it's an issue with the fairness overall that I think the devs were shooting for.

Finally, collision detection. IMO this is not a small problem with the game, but the main one. In a fighting game which is ostensibly all about timing attacks and dodges and knowing exactly where your opponent is going to be at all times so you can hit them just right, I ran into dozens of situations over my playthrough where the collision detection was blatantly unfair, and never once was it unfair in my favor. For example: say you have a spear or lance, and you go to stab it at an enemy that is between you and your spearpoint. Your attack will miss, and rightfully so. However, take that same example and reverse the player and enemy, and suddenly their attack will hit you. I ran into numerous other issues where my attacks went through an enemy instead of hitting them. An illustrative example of this is during the fight with the King of the Storm, there are a couple attack patterns where his dragon's head can’t be hit even though it's right in front of you and you're swinging a greatsword directly through it. This is just bad game design.

Some positives: I really enjoyed the strange lore of this game, to the extent that I went out of my way to read wiki articles between runs. Also, the difficulty in learning how to play is not nearly as much as I thought it would be; perhaps that is helped by the fact that I've already 100%'d Sekiro. I liked nearly everything about the combat system, including the audio and visual feedback on strikes and kills, and the satisfaction of one-shotting enemies in mid to lategame was great. I really enjoyed the variety of enemies faced, the variety of environments to explore, and how even though the game is primarily one-track, like Sekiro, there are many alternate paths you can take. There were a few environments I greatly disliked, due to huge spikes in difficulty or frustration; namely, the swamp areas which force you to walk slowly, and the Smouldering Lake, whose enemies are much quicker than any ones you faced previously. I disliked annoying mechanics like the giant firing lances at you from a tower, only to agree to stop immediately once you talk to him for two seconds. I enjoyed the silly one-off mechanics like dipping your head in wax to avoid curse damage from spooky bookshelves. I found most of the environment designs beautiful, deeply immersive, and well-thought out. Once you get into the gameplay pattern of each new area, it becomes intuitive, and you stop worrying about what's next and start enjoying the ride.

At its best, Dark Souls 3 was immensely fun and satisfying. But other times, sheer frustration with the game's innate mechanical systems, quirks, or intentional design choices made it a chore to keep playing. When I finished Sekiro for the first time, I immediately hit New Game + and played again, excited to experience that combat all over again from the beginning. When I finished Dark Souls 3, I felt relieved that I wouldn't have to keep playing this game anymore, but pleased with myself for finishing. I think the absolute bane of this game is its lack of consistency--of difficulty, of expectations of the player, of enjoyment, even of the rules of combat. This, in comparison to Sekiro, is my main problem with the game and why I won't be playing it again for a long time.

As of writing this review, this is the only Soulslike game I have played (assuming Cuphead is not considered a Soulslike).

DS3 was - for me - a source of contention when was gifted to me back in 2019. I was streaming, and the chat goaded me to trying it while live. I couldn't even make it to Vordt without my frustrations boiling over with chat attempting to direct my every move. I put it down after 4-6 hours and had no interest in trying it again.

Fast-forward to 2022 and while no longer streaming, I was finding gaming itself to be boring. Elden Ring was being released, and the entire world seemed to pause what it was doing to play the game. Given my past experience with DS3 on stream, I did not want to join the bandwagon for a game I did not know whether I would enjoy or not. But the draw was there. I reinstalled DS3 and decided I was going to play it at my pace, the way I wanted, on my own and not with others. And wow, what a difference the experience was - very similar to watching a movie for the first time by yourself compared to watching it for the first time with someone who has seen it and is explaining it to you as it's going.

Being able to give the game my full attention, the gameplay experience, ambience, and aesthetics were captivating. The fights were fun to learn, and I was able to learn quickly how to pace the difficulty to my comfort and challenge levels. The controls felt smooth and responsive. The voice acting and item descriptions brought life into the world that prompted me to seek more information on wikis and youtube videos. I was engrossed in the lore and wanted to figure it all out.

As a result, I want to get all the other Souls games, including Demon's Souls and Elden Ring. I truly believe, based on what I've studied, that playing DS3 first was probably the best thing I could have done as well, given how the level design guided me to where I needed to go, and the gameplay was less clunky than earlier titles. I've been taught through this game how to tackle the earlier and later titles, whether it'll be the convoluted and intertwined world design of Elden Ring and DS1, or the various aspects of leveling up my character or items in DS2. Had I began with those games, I think I may have been less inclined to reach for the sequels or prequels.

Dark Souls 3 is, without a doubt, one of the greatest entries in the legendary Souls series and a perfect culmination of Hidetaka Miyazaki’s vision. From the tight, satisfying combat to the hauntingly beautiful world design, this game encapsulates everything that makes the Dark Souls franchise unforgettable. The level of depth and detail in every corner of the world is unmatched, with each area offering unique challenges and a rich, dark lore to uncover.

The combat is fluid and precise, with a variety of playstyles and weapon choices that ensure every player can find their preferred way to fight. Parrying, dodging, and strategic attacks come together in a system that rewards skill and timing. The difficulty, while unforgiving, always feels fair — every death serves as a learning experience, pushing you to improve and become stronger with each attempt.

Boss battles in Dark Souls 3 are truly a work of art. These monumental encounters not only test your reflexes and strategy but also carry with them an emotional weight. Each boss feels like a unique challenge, with beautifully designed movesets and lore that ties into the world. The standout feature, however, is the music — the boss themes are absolutely breathtaking, often sending chills down your spine as you face these towering adversaries. The music isn’t just background noise; it’s a key part of the experience, amplifying the intensity and emotion of each battle.

The world of Dark Souls 3 is a masterpiece, filled with intricate lore and environments that feel alive and connected. From the desolate landscapes to the fiery depths, every location is a work of art, with hidden paths, traps, and secrets that reward exploration. Miyazaki’s signature touch is evident in the way the world feels connected, with areas that loop back into one another, creating a sense of cohesion and immersion.

The atmosphere is further enriched by a haunting soundtrack, with tracks that perfectly complement the bleak yet beautiful world. The game’s story, though subtle and open to interpretation, pulls you deeper into its world, keeping you hooked until the very end.

For fans of challenging action RPGs, Dark Souls 3 is a must-play. It’s an unforgettable journey that pushes you to your limits, but the sense of accomplishment that comes with overcoming its challenges is unparalleled. This game is a true masterpiece and a perfect testament to the genius of Hidetaka Miyazaki and the FromSoftware team. 10/10 — an absolute triumph of game design.

I’m genuinely baffled that people associate FromSoftware and their games with the word "quality." There’s a mountain of problems with Dark Souls 3, many of which trickle down into their later titles like Elden Ring. Ultimately, Dark Souls 3 isn’t a terrible game, but it comes with glaring issues that overall make it a frustrating experience.

This is hands-down the worst PC port I’ve ever played. For a game that demands precision and focus it controls like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. It’s well-known at this point that the hardest enemy in these games is the camera, and and that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ camera is on NG+7 for mouse and keyboard users. Basic utilities like swapping items are bound to the arrow keys -so I’m expected to take my hand off the mouse just to use an FP flask? Sprinting and rolling share the same button, meaning you have to release the spacebar to roll, which makes timing feel clunky and unnatural. Even the UI is broken, constantly displaying controller icons instead of actual keybinds. If that wasn’t bad enough, the game is optimized like ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. At 1440p I'm forced to drop my settings to low on a GTX 2080 because a game from 2016 just cant keep up in performance.

Dark Souls technically has multiplayer, however it is by no means a co-op game. Summoning is more about helping someone kill a boss or invading their world, not exploring. As a cooperator, you can’t collect items and regions are separated by fog gates which makes the experience feel limited and disconnected. Fighting bosses and especially other players usually feels laggy. While many dedicated players have learned to work around it, it’s disappointing that network issues have become a staple of the series. The idea of embered invasions is cool, but the network performance sucks.

The gameplay is a massive part of the games identity, its clear the developers added deliberate attack delays and emphasized attack commitment in order to outplay bosses, however no amount of over designing ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ will ever make getting hit through a wall less frustrating. Bosses and even basic enemies can unleash entire 6 or 7+ hit combos and depending on your weapon class, it's very possible to start an attack before the enemy has even made a move and lose because they can start and finish an attack before yours even comes out. The entire core gameplay loop is designed around timing a single quarter second action and miss-timing that action means your dead. That's it.

This is a nine year old game that still costs $60 without the DLC. There's no way they had any quality assurance for this release of the game and still they somehow expect full price after all this time. If you really think the game is worth $60 how about you consider that these exact bosses will be copy and pasted into their new game later this year. How about you look up the January 2022 exploit when hackers could brick your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ computer by executing code via DS3's online netcode. How about FromSoft pull the stick out of their ass and come up with some real solutions to the same problems that just keep happening and happening. Do you really need to preserve the integrity of your games difficulty that bad?

It's not a bad souls game.
So if you like souls game and maybe you played demon souls and you really enjoyed that game this game is probably one you'll enjoy due to its return to a more fast paced combat with high attack speed, low stamina cost on rolls, and lots of hitstun from the player's weapon.
This fast paced "excitement" is translated into the boss fights as well, with bosses using long attack chains at high speeds.
Whilst this sounds exhilarating it actually turns out to be very boring because the only buttons you're really pressing are roll and R1. R1 out damages basically all other attacks unless you're using specific paired weapons like sellsword (which just end up always using L1), you're basically just playing a take turn action game where you roll through all the enemies' attacks and then attack yourself when they're done until the enemy attacks again.
The game is also just as monotonous in it's visual design, with it basically only using faded colors instead of the vibrant colors of dark souls 1. Interestingly whilst this game has a torch it's basically never used because there's way too much light everywhere, the only area with interesting lighting is the specific part in the Irithyl dungeon where the giant is.
You'll also often hear people talk about how the game is very linear which has merit to it compared to its two predecessors, but honestly it's probably preferable that the game is linear because it makes it faster to play through.
Of course it's still a fine game but pales in comparison to the previous installments and so I'd recommend anyone to buy this game when it goes on sale which it does every now and then.

I love Dark Souls 3. Easily my favorite souls, after Bloodborne. But the PC port is absolute hot garbage. There's a constant stutter and lag every few minutes, making it borderline unplayable. I've tried every solution from all the various threads talking about the issue, none of them work. I just finished Dark Souls 2, wanted to jump straight into 3, but it's just so laggy. If not for the stutters, 10/10 would recommend, but as it is, I wasted my money. At least I know a fit girl who can get me a working copy.

EDIT: The only way I could get the lag and stutter to mostly stop is by disabling all cores except 1-8, turning the priority of the darksoulsIII.exe to high, turning on my controller after launching the game, and disconnecting all USB devices. All that, after multiple attempts trying to get the other suggested fixes to work. The fact that I had to spend hours trying to find a workaround just to be able to play the game is insane. I have a new PC, the specs are more than adequate, and even now I still occasionally get frame drops and lag. It's playable, but frustrating. I wish steam gave a "yes, but" recommendation option tbh.

TLDR; great game, poorly optimised port, some people get lag and others don't, be aware before buying

Posted: February 7
Dark Souls III is what happens when an experienced dev team takes an already solid formula, takes it apart, then puts it back together in the most sleek, shiny and polished way you could ever imagine. That's not to say that all deviations from the first two games hit the mark completely, but by and large, this is the most refined Dark Souls experience to date.

Dark Souls games were never about fidelity, but lean hard into artstyle, and just like Dark Souls and Dark Souls II before it, Dark Souls III oozes style, charm and atmosphere. The presentation and sound are what you come to expect from such a game, and this one is no different, top marks in that respect.

As far as level design and world design go - some criticize Dark Souls III for being too derivative of the first game in terms of general style and vibe, and that's often completely warranted. Apart from Irithyll, it really feels like a decent variation on Lordran, and offers very little we haven't already seen before. That's not to say it's bad, it's just familiar, I'll let you decide whether or not that's a good thing. There's definitely no "Lost Izalith/Demon Ruins" moment and thank god for that. One thing I do feel is a downgrade from the first Dark Souls' tightly interwoven world design and Dark Souls II's messy, but extensive world, is the linearity of the whole experience. The satisfying shortcuts and loopbacks are still present (albeit within an area, rather than between them), and fast travel is once again available since the start. However, it's a much more straight line with the occasional detour than Dark Souls I or II. Like the familiar art direction, it's a matter of preference.

Before I start the all-out praise-singing, I just briefly wanted to touch on the story and lore. Unlike Dark Souls, which is unique for perhaps no other reason than being the first to do so, or Dark Souls II, which veered into different tropes and told its own rich tale, Dark Souls III kind of feels like a re-telling of the first game, and I found myself caring a lot less for the story and lore than the other two games.

Alright then - where this game absolutely knocks it out of the park, and then out of the continent, the atmosphere and into outer space, is the gameplay and above all, the bosses. The build variety is up there with all the FromSoft games, and if you're dogged enough, anything is viable. But - we all know what we're here for, the sweet, sweet "learn the attack pattern, git gud and prevail" high, and Dark Souls III delivers this in spades. I loved Elden Ring and I played it before playing this for the first time, but I often felt Elden Ring's combat, especially in the latter half, took a turn for the ridiculous in a way that I really didn't appreciate. Here, there's none of that. No overbearing particle effects or endless combos, no "oh you can dodge it bro just dodge left right up down diagonal back 71 times" AoE wipe attacks, when you die, you rarely feel like the game just decided to overwhelm you. The controls are the best they've ever been, the bosses are a clean, tight affair full of finesse and interesting attack patterns - if you fail, the reason is you done goofed 99.9% of the time. There truly are so many memorable encounters like the Nameless King, Sister Friede, Slave Knight Gael, Darkeater Midir, Dancer of the Boreal Valley, I could go on and on. There's the odd gimmick fight and a few rather forgettable bosses (I don't remember the Crystal Sage or Cursed Tree bosses at all, they just felt kind of... there), but the selection on offer is nothing short of stellar. The fights are faster and a little more emphasis is put on rolling, but I was extremely happy with the bosses and I will definitely be coming back for more.

In conclusion, having now finished all three Dark Souls games, I can confidently say that while the original Dark Souls will always be my favourite for purely subjective reasons, and while Dark Souls II is a great game in its own right despite its admittedly numerous flaws, Dark Souls III is definitely the best and most refined of the three in many, many aspects. If you liked the first two games and/or Elden Ring, you'll feel right at home here. Grab your favourite bonking implement, spammy slashy-pokey dex instrument, pew-pew staff or chime and prepare to have the YOU DIED screen burnt into your eyes. It's a great time.

A Soulful Masterpiece

Dark Souls continues to push the boundaries with the latest, ambitious chapter in the critically-acclaimed and genre-defining series. Prepare yourself and Embrace The Darkness!

Story
The First Flame that sustains the world is fading, threatening the cycle of light and dark. You are the Ashen One, an undead chosen to link the fire or seek another fate. Guided by the Fire Keeper and drawn to the Kiln of the First Flame, you must defeat the Lords of Cinder, past heroes who abandoned their thrones, and reclaim their power.

Gameplay
Dark Souls III is a third-person souls-like action role-playing game. Combat is a core part of Dark Souls III, deliberate and punishing, requiring precise dodges, parries, and stamina management. Combat and movements are faster and more fluid than in Dark Souls II. It includes melee, ranged attacks, defensive maneuvers, and magic. Melee weapons include swords, spears, maces, greatswords, and other fantastical arms, while ranged options feature bows and crossbows. Defense involves shields, armor, dodging, backstepping, and parrying. Magic—sorceries, miracles, and pyromancies—returns in the game with the Demon's Souls system revamped as Focus Points (FP). Dark Souls III introduces new combat features, including weapon and shield Skills, special abilities unique to each weapon that consume Focus Points. The game emphasizes role-playing, with an expanded character builder and improved weapons offering more tactical options. Casting spells and using weapon skills consumes FP, which can be restored with the Ashen Estus Flask or by resting at a bonfire. You can tailor your playstyle with two types of Estus Flasks: one for health and one for FP. All combat types have trade-offs and can be enhanced through leveling, smithing, consumables, rings, and new equipment.

Bonfires are central to the gameplay, serving as checkpoints throughout the world. Resting at a bonfire heals you, refills Estus Flasks, and allows leveling up with acquired souls, attuning magic, and repairing or upgrading equipment. However, resting also respawns all enemies, except bosses, mini-bosses, and defeated NPCs. Upon death, you respawn at the last bonfire rested at. Dying causes you to drop all collected souls at the death location, which are lost permanently if not recovered before dying again.

The humanity system has been simplified and revolves around Embers. Using an Ember puts your character in the Embered state, granting +30% max HP, the ability to summon allies, and vulnerability to invasions. Without an Ember, your character is hollowed, with normal HP, no summoning, and reduced invasion risk. Dying removes the Ember effect, requiring another Ember to regain the benefits.

The game offers strong replayability. After finishing, you can start New Game+, where enemies and bosses have increased health and damage. This continues up to NG+7, after which scaling stops, though you can continue playing on NG+.

Dark Souls III features online multiplayer for both co-op and PvP. In co-op play, players place a summon sign that becomes visible to others using a matching item; interacting with it brings the summoner into their world until the area boss is defeated, the host dies, or the summoned player dies. In PvP, summon signs can be used to challenge others to duels, or players can use special items to invade other worlds.

Dark Souls III offers a wide variety of enemies and bosses, each with distinct attack patterns that require careful observation and strategy to dodge and counter. Every enemy has unique moves, gear, and specific weaknesses and resistances, keeping combat engaging and varied.

Bosses are the true test of the Dark Souls III experience. Bosses are powerful, challenging foes. Each boss is unique and has varying attacks, weaknesses, and strategies to defeat it. The bosses are well-designed, featuring the best fights in the series.

Set in the kingdom of Lothric. Exploration is a core mechanic. You are encouraged to proceed cautiously, learn from mistakes, and discover alternative paths. The world is mostly hub-based, with the Firelink Shrine acting as the main hub connecting distinct areas. While some shortcuts and loops exist within individual zones, the overall world is more linear, with separate areas branching from the hub rather than forming a tightly interwoven map. Some areas do cleverly overlap vertically or through subtle shortcuts, giving a partial sense of connectivity, but it’s not as intricate as Lordran.

The game has fewer maps than Dark Souls II, but they are larger, detailed, and varied. Areas are well-designed, with meaningful rooms and little wasted space, encouraging exploration.

This game feels a bit more dark and gritty in its surroundings than its predecessors, but at the same time very beautiful. The visual design of Dark Souls III’s world is striking and atmospheric, emphasizing decay, grandeur, and contrast. Each area tells a story through its environment: crumbling castles, charred forests, and ruined cities reflect the kingdom’s collapse, while towering cathedrals, glowing lava pits, and misty cliffs create dramatic, memorable vistas.

Every enemy has a distinctive set of sounds—grunts, roars, weapon swings—that communicate their size, aggression, and attack patterns. Audio cues are essential for combat; a giant’s stomp, a dragon’s growl, or a sorcerer charging a spell signals danger and timing windows.

The story in Dark Souls III is good but can be confusing and hard to follow. You need to pay close attention to every NPC and exhaust all dialogue to understand the narrative and side quests. Like other FromSoftware games, figuring out what to do and when can be tricky without an online guide. Progressing too far without completing certain steps can permanently forfeit a quest, and there’s no journal or quest log to track your objectives. This lack of guidance can be frustrating, and it’s one of the few aspects of the Soulsborne formula I dislike.

The second thing I dislike is the co-op system. You can summon a player to help defeat a boss, but afterward they return to their world. To continue together, you must complete each area and boss twice—once in your world and once in your friend’s. It would be better if world progress, like defeated bosses and collected items, were shared and saved.

The third thing I dislike—actually, hate—is PvP. You can’t turn it off, so other players can invade your world unexpectedly. It’s often laggy and unbalanced, and in my opinion, PvP has never worked. The only way to avoid it is to play offline, which is difficult if you want to use co-op.

The few negatives—like side quests and co-op limitations—are far outweighed by the game’s many strengths. Dark Souls III is a masterpiece, especially when played solo, where completing it feels like a far greater achievement than relying on co-op assistance.

Dark Souls III is a challenging game that punishes mistakes—but in a fair way. Success depends on learning enemy and boss patterns and improving your skills. Deaths are your fault, not the game’s, requiring practice and perseverance. The combat is flawless. Death is always your fault, not the game’s, rewarding practice and skill improvement. With a huge variety of weapons, spells, and builds, the game offers incredible replayability and depth in combat.

If you enjoy FromSoftware and the Dark Souls series, don’t hesitate to play this one—it’s the best. I was completely blown away the first time I played it. I just played through the game again with a friend, marking my 29th completion (30 if you count his world too), if I count all the playthroughs, both Xbox and PC. It is without a doubt a favorite game.

Steam should offer a "Neutral" rating—because I have really mixed feelings about Dark Souls 3.

My first real Soulsborne experience was Elden Ring + DLC, and I love it. 300+ hours, currently on NG+3, did all dungeons, bosses, got all legendary items, and even the rarest Spirit Ashes (such as Dung Eater, Nephali Jolan, Jolan & Anna), Sorceris and Incantations. I've explored everything, read all item descriptions, and uncovered all secrets. After finishing everything, I wanted more, so I gave Dark Souls 3 a shot.

Maybe that’s the issue: I can't help but compare DS3 to Elden Ring. If I had started with Dark Souls 3, I’m not sure I would have even bought Elden Ring...

Positives:
✔ Mistakes are punished instantly—classic Souls gameplay
✔ Mostly challenging and interesting boss fights
✔ Well-placed bonfires
✔ Fast travel available
✔ Central hub with NPCs
✔ No item carry limits
✔ Exploration is rewarding
✔ No hand-holding—discovering things on your own enhances immersion

Neutral:
➖ Leveling up and upgrading flasks can only be done at the hub
➖ No mount, but levels are smaller and manageable on foot
➖ Item durability exists, but weapons auto-repair at bonfires, making blacksmith repairs pointless
➖ Even for 2016, the graphics were just average; DS3 hasn’t aged well, but gameplay matters more to me

Negatives:
✖ No map, no compass, and bland environments make navigation frustrating
✖ No marker for lost souls—retrieving them is often pointless
✖ Terrible PC optimization—frequent stuttering despite high-end hardware (i9 13900, RTX 4090, 64GB DDR5)
✖ No jump button—rolling over gaps instead feels ridiculous and immersion-breaking
✖ No duck possible - limited option to sneak around
✖ Getting stuck on small objects can lead to cheap deaths
✖ Many areas feel cramped and lack the sense of scale and exploration Elden Ring offers
✖ The world feels much smaller—after just 16 hours, I’ve seen already more than half of all areas, while my first Elden Ring playthrough took 150+ hours


Final Verdict:
Dark Souls 3 is not a bad game, but it feels vastly inferior to Elden Ring. I’m glad I started with Elden Ring first—otherwise, I might have skipped it entirely. I'll finish my playthrough, but I won’t come anywhere close to 300+ hours like I did in Elden Ring.

Would I recommend it? Only if you really want more Soulsborne after Elden Ring—just keep your expectations in check.

Posted: February 5
This game undid months of HRT in just 5 hours.
Jokes aside, I have a special place in my heart for DS3. Call me IGN, but I like that this game is accessible while still having its fair share of pain-inducing areas, including at least 4 poison swamps ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥-
The story, world-building, and assets all feel like genuine works of art (maybe because this game had artists working on it). I found myself genuinely attached to some of the NPCs, especially Seigward, Greirat, and the Fire Keeper. My player character, Velrin, is also making his way to my own game (with some design changes to avoid IP infringement).
I beat the game for the first time very recently and enjoyed it through and through. The music feels grand, tense, and inspiring, the NPC dialogue is especially good and conveys actual character development, the boss fights are, for the most part, super memorable and do a good job of forcing adaptability, and the areas are so. damn. pretty.
One thing I didn't like is the fact that the Deacons of the Deep felt really easy, for instance. Maybe since I was using a Dex build, but I feel like they could use a little more variety.
I also wish some of the quest lines were a little more forgiving, since if you miss them, you have to either start a new save or beat the game and do the quest line on New Game+.
Dark Souls 3 is a bit expensive, but if you can snatch it on sale like I did, it's worth every cent.
Also, From Software, please give us Bloodborne.

# Short Review

Very fun experience. If you have enjoyed playing DS1/DS2 then you are likely to enjoy playing this as well. Game is not very well optimized for my system, and in-fact plays better on Linux w/Proton than on Windows. Bosses are much better designed than in DS1/DS2; there are no throwaway bosses. The game looks and sounds great—but I have found myself annoyed at just how LOUD everything is.

I would highly recommend buying this game.

# System Information

CPU: AMD Ryzen 5 2600
GPU: nVidia TU116 [GeForce GTX 1660]
DSK: WD SN550 NVMe SSD
MEM: 1x Crucial Technology 16GB Module
MON: 1080p 180Hz IPS Monitor

# README

All spoilers are marked by spoiler tags.

All bosses were beaten solo.

Multiplayer functionality was intentionally disabled due to security concerns.

This review covers only the base game.

Nobody is going to read any of this and I'm too burned out to give a high-quality review.

# The Good

Every boss is well-designed and enjoyable to play with.

Combat is fluid and fast-paced, there is very little tedium, there is almost no suffering for the sake of suffering, but genuinely difficult and fun challenges.

NPC interactions are well-designed and interesting.

Fantastic aesthetic, lots of areas and enemies reminded me of Bloodborne.

Really good OST for bosses, audio quality is much better than DS2/DS1.

Great exploration, you actually want to explore each level thoroughly.

And so much more...

# The Bad

DS2 Spoiler:

Camera automatically adjusts and it can be a real nuisance.

Bosses are not difficult enough. Rolling is extremely forgiving after playing DS2.

Armour provides marginal benefits and slows you down considerably, heavy armour demands you level up vitality, which is less important than vigour/endurance/x.

Unlike DS2, shields are much less useful. Because dodging is so forgiving, you just don't need them. I never used a shield once, why bother?

Many weapons and yet not once did I feel like I needed to use any of them except for FUGS.
Why bother? No enemy made me adapt, there was NEED to switch weapon.

The game can get very loud with too many sounds playing at the same time. This might be very irritating for some.

Stuttering on Windows with my particular system, extremely minor stuttering on Linux using Proton. Others have reported the same thing (bug?), but it seems we are in the minority. It's especially noticeable if you go to open the inventory.

# Bosses - Ranked by difficulty

My Favorite Boss-Rush Game

If you are familiar with From Software's souls games, you may know Sekiro has best boss battles this series has to offer (Idk If you even consider Sekiro a souls game.). Why am I talking about Sekiro right now? You see, Sekiro was my first souls game and after playing it, other games like Elden Ring, Demon Souls and Dark Souls Remastered, I felt like something was missing from these games. Dark Souls 3 made me realize it was the unforgetable boss battles what I have been looking for. Of course I am not saying ER or DS don't have any good bosses but Dark Souls 3 is another level If we are talking about boss fights. We will come to this topic later but first, let's dive into other details.

Gameplay and Combat

Since there are hundres of souls games I assume you have played at least one of them. If you didn't, don't worry because combat is so simple. You have light/heavy attacks and you can use dodge roll to avoid attacks. That basically sums it up. Although it may sound simple, you will be addicted to it. There tons of fun builds you can mess around with, timing your attacks and roll times, finding enemies' weak spots etc. These are what makes it addictive. All weapons in the game has unique skills but unfortunately, execution is not as idea sounds. There is high chance that you will find yourself using weapons because you liked the weapon, not its skill. Eventually you will stop using weapon arts. I am glad FS actually fixed this problem in Elden Ring. Combat is faster than previous Dark Souls games. If you have played any Soulsborne game after Dark Souls 2 you will get used to it quickly.


It is a known fact that in terms of level design, Dark Souls 3 is very inferior to first game. While I agree with this, I don't have any problems with linear levels. Nothing comes to my mind when I think about a single bad designed level in DS3. Levels may not be connected to each other which kills the immersion of world. However, after playing hours and hours, it becomes to tiring to explore areas and do backtracking in first game. Dark Souls 3 is more comfortable in that regard.

As I said earlier boss fights are where this game shines. Most of fights aren't perfect, especially during early game. I almost quit this game because I found them so easy and boring (I am not flexing by the way.). Actually I can't remember a single boss that took more than 15 minutes to beat. But in later parts, fights became more engaging. Still easy, yes but yet so fun. Especially in DLCs, I don't want to spoil anything.

Answer is yes BUT wait for sale. Easily one of the best action-rpg on market.

---

Referat: Dark Souls 3 – Der beste Simulator für gescheiterte Lebensentscheidungen

1. Einleitung:
Stellen Sie sich vor, Sie bezahlen 60 Euro, um wahlweise ein Joypad, Ihre geistige Gesundheit oder gleich Ihre gesamte Lebensfreude zu verlieren. Willkommen in *Dark Souls 3*, dem Spiel, das Sie lehrt, dass Hoffnung nur ein weiterer Bosskampf entfernt ist – und dass dieser Boss Flügel hat, Feuer spuckt und Ihre Träume frisst.

---

2. Die Story (soweit irgendjemand sie versteht)
Die Handlung von *Dark Souls 3* lässt sich am besten mit einem verwirrenden Theaterstück beschreiben, in dem der Regisseur zu viel LSD genommen hat. Sie sind der "Auserwählte" – was bedeutet, dass Sie der arme Trottel sind, der die glühenden Aschefürsten finden und davon überzeugen muss, sich selbst zu verbrennen. Keine Sorge, die meisten dieser Fürsten sind entweder wahnsinnig, aggressiv oder beides.

Ach ja, und Lore? Die versteckt sich hinter Item-Beschreibungen, die kryptischer sind als das Liebesleben von Batman.

---

3. Die Bosse – Therapie für Masochisten
Dark Souls 3 hat Bosse, die Sie gleichzeitig zum Weinen und zum Lachen bringen werden. Eine kleine Auswahl:

- Vordt vom Nordwindtal: Ein riesiger Eisbär mit Aggressionsproblemen. Wenn er nicht gerade Ihre Träume zertrampelt, verbringt er vermutlich seine Freizeit als Kühlschrank.
- Abyss Watchers: Gothic-Wikinger, die offenbar den besten Fechtunterricht von Legolas persönlich erhalten haben. Und sobald Sie denken, Sie hätten gewonnen – Überraschung! Die haben einen zweiten Lebensbalken.
- Nameless King: Oder wie ihn seine Freunde nennen: "Der Typ, der dafür sorgt, dass Controller an Wänden landen."

---

4. Multiplayer: "Freund oder Feind?"
Multiplayer in *Dark Souls 3* ist wie Dating-Apps: Man ruft verzweifelt nach Hilfe und bekommt stattdessen einen Typen, der in rotem Glanz aufleuchtet und Sie mit einer Gabel erstechen will. Oder – wenn Sie Glück haben – eine nette Sonnenbrille, die Ihnen hilft und dabei konstant "Praise the Sun" schreit.

---

5. Fazit:
Warum spielen wir also Dark Souls 3? Weil wir Menschen sind und Menschen scheinbar ein Faible für sinnlose Selbstgeißelung haben. Oder vielleicht, weil nichts im Leben so befriedigend ist wie das Gefühl, wenn man nach 47 Versuchen endlich den Boss besiegt – nur um dann in der nächsten Zone von einer mickrigen Ratte getötet zu werden.

Also ja, Dark Souls 3 ist kein Spiel. Es ist eine existenzielle Krise in digitaler Form. Und wir lieben es.

Overview
I love Dark Souls 3. Just to preface, up to this point, I had played Elden Ring (+DLC), Bloodborne (+DLC), Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, and a multitude of other hard/souls-like games, such as beating P5 in Hollow Knight. Out of all of those games listed, this was by far the easiest experience for me. The only game that can come close in difficulty for me would be Bloodborne, which I found pretty easy. I just want to point that out before reading my review, since the generally easy difficulty may have skewed my review (I don't generally like challenge).

Look down at the specific sections of my review if you care enough, but here's my overview. This game is, for the most part, a masterpiece. You could absolutely argue that this game has some of the tightest gameplay, best bosses, and best soundtrack of any game ever. I think that, depending on the day, I could absolutely give this game my 'Best (not necessarily favorite) Bosses Award of All Time'. The other contenders in my mind are Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice, Hollow Knight, and Elden Ring, but Dark Souls 3 really does have an all-star boss lineup. The world and specific level design is also very very good. It's also arguably up there with some of the best level design of all time. There is not a lot to complain about with this game. The reason why it isn't higher up on my list/does more for me is just because it didn't do anything THAT good. I got exactly what I came for in this game. I came in expecting fantastic bosses, a killer soundtrack, fun gameplay, great exploration in a well-designed world, and I did get all that. I just wasn't blown away particularly often, as I have been in some of my favorite games of all time. This truly is one of my top 10 favorite games of all time, and I will tell anyone that it is a fantastic experience and one of my favorites, but it can't achieve goat status like a couple other of my favorite games have.

FromSoftware is now officially my favorite developer, after playing those games I mentioned above. They truly craft the best, most interesting world with the best bosses of all time, some of the best combat, and an absolutely fire lore to underline the entirety of the story from the back. Dark Souls 3 really does feel like a perfectly curated, tight adventure, more than any of the other games. All of the world feels pretty cohesive, the bosses are not too complex, making them fun and readable, and the levels are expansive but not too vast to feasibly explore with nice pacing between bosses. This was also easily the easiest FromSoftware game for me yet. I beat every single boss, optional and main, as well as getting almost every item and all covenants except one on one playthrough (DLC Included) in 31 hours. I recognize that this is significantly lower than the average, meaning that I probably had a much easier time than most people. And I'm honestly not a person that gets a whole lot out of difficulty in gaming. It sorta just annoys me. Sure it feels great when you beat the boss. Can't deny that. But I'm just tryna chill. I don't want to stress out over a fictional boss that much please. That's part of what made this game so enjoyable for me. It felt like a sort of summer breeze that I could kinda just dawdle my way through and kill bosses when I cam upon them, rather than making each boss feel like a massive undertaking.

There wasn't a single boss in the main game that I took more than 5 tries on, and I think the hardest has to be Nameless King. I beat Soul of Cinder first try (which felt a little anti-climactic, to be honest), and didn't die more than once to a single boss in the first half of the game. This is not a slight, just sharing my experience of relative ease with the game. My most attempts was 7 (on Darkeater Midir) in the DLC, but I'll talk more about that in my DLC reviews. I probably actually died more doing dumb things exploring the areas and ESPECIALLY to fall damage out in the more open levels. I spent a lot of time running back to ledges where my souls were sitting atop them, after watching myself fling my body into the void. It made me very happy to find shortcuts.

To sort of corral my thoughts, let me try to put it into a single sentence as a final 'review':

*Providing its ambitious players with a tight, grand adventure lined with one of the best boss casts, soundtracks, and level designs of all time, 'Dark Souls 3' cements itself as a fantastically fun video game and only enhances the Ethos of FromSoftware and their legendary catalogue.*

**If you happen to care, check out my whole review on backloggd: @whyfinn

After being afraid of anything Souls-like or Souls-related whatsoever for many years, having only ever heard how "brutal" and "difficult" the games were, I finally got into Souls-like games through Jedi: Fallen Order, of all things. About a third of the way through, I suddenly realized I recognized the design and "punishing" gameplay mechanics of a Souls-like game, and when I had finished that, I eventually started playing Elden Ring, loved it, and at that point a new passion had been born.

This is now the third From Software game and Souls/Souls-like game I've completed: Elden Ring, Dark Souls 1, and Dark Souls 3, and I am still absolutely in love with the mechanics, the design, the story-telling and the world lore. There's just something magical about it all that makes it difficult to put down, and impossible to forget. There's a perfect balance to the trial and error, with the game not telling you how to progress, but allowing you to practice and learn for yourself at your own pace. You only have to play smart to make the game trivial, and nothing stands in your way at all if you want to over-level and live out your own power fantasy.

So much to say that yes, I emphatically recommend this game, and all FromSoft Souls games and Souls-likes, to anyone that wants to have the feeling that *they* beat the game, and not just that the game ended because it was over. This is what "sense of pride and accomplishment" really feels like, and with not a micro-transaction nor battlepass in sight.

Just take a deep breath, make a plan for the next step you want to accomplish, and don't get greedy. That's how you beat this game.

With over 3,000 hours on console and a significant amount of time on PC, I can confidently say that Dark Souls 3 is an outstanding game. Most performance issues can be resolved since no PC port is flawless. The problems mentioned in negative reviews are often fixable with the right adjustments.

Addressing FPS Drops & Frame Stuttering

Some players report experiencing framerate drops and stuttering, but it's not a widespread problem. Many, including myself, enjoy Dark Souls 3 at a steady 60 frames per second without major issues. If you do encounter performance dips, here are some solutions:

1. Disable V-Sync Manually: While the game lacks an in-game option for V-Sync, you can disable it through your GPU settings:

For NVIDIA users:

Open NVIDIA Control Panel > Manage 3D Settings > Program Settings > Add Dark Souls 3.

Set V-Sync to Off and apply the changes.

For AMD users:

Open Radeon Software > Graphics > Select Dark Souls 3.

Turn off Wait for Vertical Refresh (V-Sync) and apply the changes.

2. Enable Borderless Windowed Mode: Although Dark Souls 3 doesn’t support this feature natively, you can achieve it using third-party tools like Borderless Gaming or Special K:

Download Borderless Gaming (available on GitHub or Steam).

Run the software and add Dark Souls 3 to the list of applications to enable borderless mode.

This can help minimize stuttering and enhance frame pacing.

3. Fixing Sudden FPS Drops in Certain Areas (Like Stairs & Detailed Locations):

Roll Back GPU Drivers – If the issue has arisen recently, consider installing an older GPU driver. New drivers can sometimes create compatibility issues with older games.

Disable background applications: Programs like Discord, MSI Afterburner, or overlays—such as GeForce Experience and Steam Overlay—can lead to stuttering. Try closing them to see if performance improves.

Set Windows Power Plan to High Performance:

Navigate to Control Panel > Power Options > High Performance to ensure your CPU and GPU are operating at their full potential.

Lower Graphics Settings:

While high-end PCs typically handle Dark Souls 3 without any problems, adjusting settings such as Shadows, Lighting, and Effects can enhance performance in crowded areas.

Dual-Monitor Issues

There’s no significant evidence that Dark Souls 3 has issues with dual-monitor setups, but if you’re facing difficulties:

Switch the game to Fullscreen Mode to prevent display conflicts.

Ensure your primary monitor is correctly configured in Windows display settings.

If you notice performance drops, try disconnecting the second monitor temporarily to see if it helps.

Why I Still Recommend Dark Souls 3 on PC

Even with some occasional technical issues, Dark Souls 3 is truly a masterpiece. The atmosphere, combat mechanics, and level design rank it among the best in the series. While FPS problems can be annoying, they’re seldom game-breaking and can usually be resolved with the right tweaks. With proper settings, you can have a smooth and rewarding experience on PC—one that, in my view, often outshines console gameplay.

For those willing to invest a bit of time in optimizing their setup, Dark Souls 3 remains one of the finest action RPGs ever created. I’ve dedicated thousands of hours to it, and I wouldn’t trade that experience for anything.

First off, let’s get this straight: I don’t think Dark Souls 3 is the best Dark Souls game. I’m coming at this from a DS1 -> DS3 perspective (never finished DS2, but I’m familiar with it).

Unlike DS2, which made me wanna wax my testicles, DS3 is actually fun. It’s more approachable, especially for newer players, with a smoother difficulty curve. The early game is well-balanced, with simple but effective level design, manageable enemies, and a natural sense of progression. The best part is undoubtedly the combat. It’s faster, smoother, and more responsive, with faster rolling and more I-frames. It feels closer to Elden Ring than DS1 or DS2, though panic-rolling is way too forgiving in my opinion. The fact that I can continuously get away unscathed just by spamming roll says a lot about the game's overall difficulty. (that kinda thing would not fly in Elden Ring thankfully)

There’s also a bigger variety of weapons, spells, and armor, and pyromancies feel way more balanced now that they use FP instead of limited casts. That said, you usually just end up commiting to a single build per playthrough, leaving most weapons unused. Miyazaki's will says we all have to use the ultra greatswords after all.

Unfortunately, DS3 stumbles in a few key areas. The level design? Pretty bad. It lacks DS1’s interconnected world and ends up feeling awfully linear, forcing you from bonfire to bonfire. And while DS2 had its own issues, at least it had variety. DS3’s environments mostly boil down to castles, swamps, and more crumbling fortresses, all painted with a boring green/brown/greyish color-palette. Irithyll being the only exception. Irithyll looks amazing, but that just makes the rest of the game feel even more forgettable. And even then, the pontiff knights are so obnoxious to fight against.

Another weird design choice: why do I have to warp back to Firelink every time I want to level up? Sitting through two loading screens just to increase my Strength by one point is just completely pointless. Thankfully, Elden Ring fixed this with Melina.

As for the bosses? Honestly, a bit underwhelming. They look cool, but most aren’t that difficult. The best (and more challenging) fights are found in the DLCs. Many of the main-game bosses feel frustrating or gimmicky (for the wrong reasons) rather than fun. Instead of designing more creative encounters, FromSoft just went with the "more is better" approach. This means longer combos, excessive AoEs, and relentless attack chains that drain stamina and leave barely any left to fight back with. Some fights feel like skill tests, but others feel like endurance matches against bosses who just want to show off their best Beyblade impression *cough cough dancer*. nevertheless, the faster combat makes it more than manageable. It's about patience and learning the boss patterns, after all.

So, should you play it?
Yeah, of course. It’s not FromSoft’s best, but it’s still an essential Dark Souls experience, and miles ahead of DS2 in everything but level design.

After doing some research I discovered a lot of other people had the same issue with this game in particular (as in, DS1 didn't bother them but this one did) and it turns out the way the camera follows the player in this game is fundamentally different from the first one.

In Dark Souls 1, the camera is always fixated directly on the player and won't move from that spot, so the player is always the central focus of the camera. If you roll, the camera stays right there with you and follows the player without any delay. In DS3 however, the camera doesn't stick to the player like it does in DS1, it just kind of trails behind the player. So, if you roll to the side for example, your character quickly darts to the side of the screen and then it takes a second for the camera to start moving and catch up with your character (see GIF linked below). This causes the camera to just fishtail behind your character the whole time, even when just turning to see around the room you are in which is extremely nauseating, especially if you are more sensitive to this kind of thing. There are mods you can install that will fix the camera somewhat but if you do then FromSoft will ban you for cheating unless you never go online which, in a Dark Souls game, takes away a lot of the fun.

I really tried, but I just can't do it it's extremely bothersome and I'm not going to go out of my way to try and fix FromSoft's problem and risk a ban. If they aren't interested in fixing their game (and are actively punishing people that do) then I am not interested in playing. I cannot in good faith recommend a game with such a glaring issue as this.

A Dark and Beautiful World
The world of Dark Souls 3 is a crumbling masterpiece in itself. Lothric is a land teetering on the brink of apocalypse, where the weight of history is felt in every shattered ruin and desolate castle. The interconnected level design, a hallmark of the series, is at its best here—each area seamlessly blending into the next, encouraging exploration and rewarding those who pay attention to the world’s secrets. The gothic architecture, melancholic landscapes, and haunting enemy designs create an unforgettable, oppressive atmosphere.

Combat: Fast, Fluid, and Brutal
While Dark Souls 3 retains the methodical combat of its predecessors, it refines the system with faster movement, smoother animations, and a better balance of aggressive and defensive playstyles. The introduction of Weapon Arts adds depth to combat, giving players new abilities that make each weapon feel distinct and rewarding to master. The boss fights are among the best in the series, with encounters like Pontiff Sulyvahn, The Nameless King, and Sister Friede delivering some of the most exhilarating battles in action RPG history.

A Story Told Through the Ashes
Like all Souls games, Dark Souls 3 tells its story through cryptic dialogue, environmental storytelling, and item descriptions. The themes of fading embers, cyclical decay, and the struggle between linking the fire and letting it fade are more pronounced than ever. Every NPC has a tragic tale, and every boss fight feels like the conclusion of a long-forgotten legend. The game doesn't force its lore upon the player but invites them to piece together its fragmented history, making every discovery feel earned.

A Fitting Conclusion to the Series
As the final entry in the Dark Souls trilogy, Dark Souls 3 serves as both a love letter to longtime fans and a powerful closing chapter to one of gaming’s most influential franchises. References to past games are woven naturally into the world, creating a sense of nostalgia while still standing strong as its own distinct story.

Final Verdict: A True Masterpiece
Few games manage to be as mechanically satisfying, visually striking, and emotionally resonant as Dark Souls 3. It refines everything that made the series great, offering a challenging yet rewarding experience that lingers long after the credits roll. Whether you're a seasoned Soulsborne veteran or a newcomer ready to test your limits, Dark Souls 3 is a must-play—an enduring testament to FromSoftware’s genius and one of the greatest action RPGs ever made.

Score: 10/10

Posted: January 30
My personal favorite Souls-like installment by FromSoftware. They kept more to their roots based on the mechanics and designs in this game when compared to Dark Souls 2 (you can see numerous references and connections to Dark Souls 1 in this third installment, which is why I recommend starting with Dark Souls 1 first if it's your first time checking out the trilogy). Combat is faster paced than the previous installments, which proves very challenging, but through repetition and practice, you will begin to see through any boss you are facing. Level design is visually stunning, but not necessarily as interconnected or intricate as Dark Souls 1, and is especially more linear (can be preferable, as some people don't want to find themselves lost in an unknown area, and definitely ensures that the player is in the right place at the right time). The two currently available DLC's are worthy of purchase (preferably on sale), and do seem to increase the difficulty by a little bit, so I would wait to enter them until after you've beaten the base game or hold a strong grasp on the combat.
Do not be afraid to look up guides or watch videos that can help educate you on your journey. Much of the community will speak against this, recommending that you delve into the game blindly. Although this is technically the 'intended gameplay loop', I understand how one may find it exceedingly frustrating when they reach a hefty roadblock, so try that boss while using everything at your disposal, and after plenty of your own attempts, educate yourself on tips and tricks. Don't cheese the experience, that's just plain boring, instead, learn the experience, by any means possible, and arrive victorious.
