Dark Souls 3: The Greatest Piece of Art Ever Created in Gaming
In the vast and ever-evolving world of video games, very few titles achieve true artistic mastery. Dark Souls 3 is not just a game—it is an interactive masterpiece, a testament to the power of gaming as a form of high art. Hidetaka Miyazaki and FromSoftware have crafted an experience that transcends entertainment, weaving together deep philosophical storytelling, breathtaking visual design, and masterful gameplay mechanics into a singular work that stands at the pinnacle of gaming history.

Aesthetic Brilliance and Environmental Storytelling
From the moment you step into Lothric, Dark Souls 3 envelops you in a world of decay, melancholy, and haunting beauty. The landscapes are not mere backdrops; they are living, breathing pieces of art, telling a story of a dying world clinging to its last embers of existence.

The ashen ruins of Lothric Castle, standing as a crumbling monument to a kingdom long past its prime.
The deep, abyssal horrors of the Untended Graves, showing a world where the cycle has already failed.
The ethereal glow of Irithyll, a dreamlike, gothic city that feels like it belongs in a lost painting of the divine.
Every corridor, ruined fortress, and fog-laden valley serves a purpose, conveying its history through visual storytelling. There are no exposition dumps—just silence, decay, and the weight of time pressing against you.

Combat as Poetry in Motion
The combat in Dark Souls 3 is perfection itself, a ballet of steel and fire, where every swing, dodge, and parry feels like a carefully choreographed dance. Combat is deliberate, methodical, and deeply rewarding, blending the best mechanics of its predecessors with the fast-paced fluidity of Bloodborne.

Every weapon has weight, every spell has consequence, and every encounter is a test of skill rather than an arbitrary stat-check.
Boss fights are legendary, from the tragic duel against The Abyss Watchers, to the godlike grandeur of The Nameless King, and the sheer emotional devastation of Slave Knight Gael.
Each battle is not just a fight but a narrative in motion, where every enemy, every struggle, and every victory tells a story.
This combat system, refined to near-perfection, is what makes Dark Souls 3 feel like a living, breathing work of art where the player is both an artist and the canvas.

Themes of Despair, Cycles, and Human Struggle
What sets Dark Souls 3 apart from other games is its ability to engage in philosophical storytelling without ever resorting to exposition-heavy narratives. The game is a meditation on the inevitability of decline, the weight of duty, and the endless struggle against fate.

The world is caught in a dying cycle, and you, the player, must decide whether to prolong its suffering or let it fade.
NPCs have tragic, existential arcs, reflecting the human experience of hopelessness, perseverance, and the search for meaning.
Dark Souls 3 does not give easy answers—it forces the player to interpret its world, to think deeply, and to come to terms with the sheer futility and beauty of existence.
Like a painting by Zdzisław Beksiński, or a poem from Dante's Inferno, Dark Souls 3 captures the beauty of ruin, the majesty of despair, and the art of struggle.

A Score That Elevates the Soul
If Dark Souls 3 is a masterpiece, then its soundtrack is the soul of that masterpiece. Composed by Yuka Kitamura and Motoi Sakuraba, the music perfectly complements the epic scale, emotional weight, and tragic beauty of the game.

The sweeping choirs in "Soul of Cinder" evoke the weight of every hero who came before you.
The somber melancholy of "Darkeater Midir" reminds you of the burden of an eternal existence.
The elegant despair in "Abyss Watchers" mirrors the honor-bound tragedy of its warriors.
This is not background music—it is a symphony of sorrow, hope, and inevitability, tying every encounter and every moment together in an auditory tapestry of brilliance.

The Ultimate Test of Human Will
What makes Dark Souls 3 truly special is its relationship with the player. It is not an easy game. It will test you, break you down, and force you to rise again. It is a trial by fire, an allegory for perseverance, a reflection of life itself.

Every death teaches you something. Every boss fight is a lesson in patience, adaptability, and skill. Every moment of frustration is matched by an overwhelming sense of accomplishment. The game does not care if you succeed—it demands that you earn it. And when you do, the feeling is nothing short of transcendental.

Conclusion: The Definitive Masterpiece of Gaming
Dark Souls 3 is more than a video game—it is a work of art, an experience that transcends the medium, a philosophical journey, and a testament to human perseverance. It combines stunning visual design, unparalleled gameplay, masterful storytelling, and breathtaking music to create something truly timeless.

In an industry filled with disposable entertainment, Dark Souls 3 stands as a monument to the artistic potential of video games. It is challenging, beautiful, heartbreaking, and rewarding—a masterpiece that will be studied, revered, and played for generations to come.

🔱 Final Verdict: 10/10 – The Greatest Piece of Art Ever Created in Gaming. 🔱

In my opinion, this game is awful. I've tried as much as I could manage, but for the life of me I can't imagine how anyone can actually like this slop. I especially can't fathom how people enjoy this game yet look down upon the original Dark Souls as well as Demons Souls (PS3), which were far better games with way more intelligent design and creative vision as opposed to just giving the player the middle finger the whole time like this game does.

For one, pretty much everything in the game is unoriginal material recycled from previous games, mainly DS1. Second, the enemies are extremely hyper aggressive and floaty no matter what, which is annoying, unimmersive, and kills variety in encounters, especially given the quantity of enemies and how spongy a lot of them are. Speaking of killing variety, you basically have to play the game lightly armored and with fast weapons, because using any other playstyle at all is completely unviable for a multitude of reasons. All armor is made of paper and offers no stagger protection, so have fun with with getting constantly stunlocked to death by all the heavily overtuned and highly aggressive enemies' each and every attack through your thick plate armor (yet various enemies, armor at all or not, are practically immune to staggering at all). They even had stagger protection from heavy armor completely programmed into the game and yet chose to flip it off. Shields are worthless because your guard gets broken almost instantly. Don't even BOTHER with magic, you hardly get any "FP" to cast with and your spells will hit like a wet noodle anyways, even if you pump your magic stats and convert all of your precious HP heals into FP heals. I've had enemies in one section stick giant swords like 12 feet out of several solid wall to kill me, even though your own weapons rebound off surfaces all too easily. The "lore" is about as interesting a pile of dirt, and the "story" is ironically about how it's better let something die than keep gradually decaying (which would be a great message if the game itself weren't an example of the very thing it's condemning). The color palette is also completely washed out, making the game terribly unappealing to look at. Have I mentioned the hitboxes? I swear the hitboxes are a total joke, especially with the obnoxious grab attacks that don't even touch you yet mysteriously teleport you into them anyway for an instant death.

I get the impression that they were so concerned about making the game "hard" that they forgot to make it "fun." My advice? Either play Dark Souls: Remastered or the PS3 version of Demons Souls. Dark Souls: Remastered has some changes to lighting, effects, equipment textures, and matchmaking that I disagree with, but it's still Dark Souls and is way better than this dumpster fire. The PS5 remake of DeS is atrociously put together, fixing and improving absolutely nothing while simultaneously ruining the game's art style, music, and overall atmosphere. If they ever want another remake, I sincerely hope that they never consider giving BluePoint the job again.

As a masterpiece of Souls games,in terms of game play, it's on the higher end of the overall difficulty scale, each enemy and boss has a unique attack pattern.

At the same time, the game's maps are well-designed, hiding a large number of shortcuts and hidden areas, a clever blend of exploration and puzzle elements.

The game builds a grand and dark worldview where the world is facing a crisis where the fire will be extinguished, and the player's choices will determine the fate of the world. This exploration of the meaning of life, destiny and the world adds a strong philosophical color to the game.

Audio-visual performance, the game screen is rich in detail, building, monster modeling fine, excellent light and shadow effects, will be the atmosphere of the best. The sound effects and soundtrack are also very good, the attack sound effects are realistic, the boss battle music is exciting, and the exploration music is mysterious and lonely, which perfectly fits the game scene.

The more time passes by and the more I play this videogame, the more I begin to feel like it's not all that good, and I've reached the point where, personally, it is one of my least favorite in the trilogy.

It's not that it isn't polished-- if anything it's really good. Its aesthetic is brilliant, the combat system, albeit too quick for my liking, is not bad at all and the story is magnificent, especially if we include the additions of the two DLCs. It's not a bad game if you've not invested a long time in the Souls series. However... I have.

You see, the first issue I think lies within its linearity. I don't mind it much, but at the same time I do. This is one of the least replayable games I've ever played, even more so than Elden Ring. No other playthrough, aside from builds, really changes how you go about things, and so it is very tough for me to come back to this. This wasn't an issue at all in neither Dark Souls 1 or 2, especially the latter. Did the previous 2 have jank that the community seems to be so obsessed with mentioning? Yes, but replayability is what makes Dark Souls stand out in my opinion, amongst other things.

Earlier I mentioned that the combat system is way too quick. Whether you like it or not is subjective, I personally did not. I really enjoyed the slow and methodical approach of the previous two games, and while you can definitely do that in Dark Souls 3, it's not encouraged. Why? Because rolling is the better option. It takes way less stamina than guarding, if you fast roll the iframes are insane and not to mention you can even reposition yourself. The same happened with previous games, don't get me wrong, but never to this absurd extent.

Another flaw is the fact that its enemy placement is absurd. Some areas you're better off just running forward and ignoring everyone, while others are so demanding in their enemies that you basically have to lock in just to progress. It gets even worse in Anor Londo with Gank City, but that's not an issue of the game itself, rather its massive PvP community.

Suffice to say it's not a game I'd recommend unless you've played the other souls games. Its also not one I'll probably be revisiting anytime soon, which saddens me because I want to replay it one day in the future. I've been able to do that with other Souls games just fine, mind you. But hey, this whole rambling might as well be completely wrong. Go play the game yourself and form your own opinion.

I would only recommend this game to fans of the series. This is the most obtuse of the three. It curtailed build variety and instead focuses on perfect dodge and parry skills. If that isn't the way you prefer to play these games, too bad. If you want to solo this game, you have no choice but to no-life each boss you get stuck on -learn every single telegraph and perfect your dodge rolls.

The inherent difference between DSIII and the first two is that this game is unforgiving. In the prior two, it was typically only bosses who could attack faster than the player. In DSIII most enemies are faster than the player. Enemies are more aggressive, way less downtime. All animations are sped up. The atmosphere is oppressive, suffocating and dreary.

I don't actually like this game, it is extremely frustrating. That said, I have played all the way through to the end of the Ringed City DLC. I am a Souls fan and I had to power through to the end. Ignore my playtime, it is not accurate.

For me, Archdragon Peak is the highlight of this game.

People who say this is the easiest one, what are you smoking? I cannot figure out how anyone could be so deluded.

From Software stretches the mechanics of the Dark Souls franchise past its limits with this one. As was the intention.

The art design in this game is unparalleled. The animations, effects, etc. are very impressive.

It's just not for me.

Where the prior two Souls games felt fair (most of the time) this game has a meanness to it that is really off-putting. It wants to be vicious and cruel.

If you find the castle sections to be the most boring levels in this series, then I have bad news for you. Most of the base game is set in decrepit war torn castles which you will revisit time and time again. This is easily the most linear of the trilogy.

Where the prior two were good for the psyche, III feels like an excruciating trip through torment and not in a good way.

I love the dual handed boss weapon animations, you can see how they took the concept even further in Elden Ring.

TLDR; There are great moments, but they are smothered by what is overall a miserable slog, book-ended with repetitive castle sections.

This game was absolute balls, super awesome.

Due to the linearity of the game, you don't get much from exploration outside of items. It's already less present in Dark Souls II, but you don't get to find secret passages or shortcuts that can net you a closer bonfire, that could even be in another area, making for a shorter runback. You just advance, find a lift or something of the sort that leads you to a previous bonfire of the area over and over until you get to the boss of the area.

Going through the areas aren't a problems though, quite the contrary. It's only exploration that I find is lacking. The game still does a wonderful job when it comes to world building and having pretty good common enemies.

It also wraps itself up pretty quickly. My playthrough was much shorter than DS1 and DS2.
I absolutely loved my playthrough.

I had been wanting to try Dark Souls, or a soulslike game, for a long time. I've always been one to play games at a lower difficulty, focusing on story or the feeling of great power from slashing down enemies with ease. But lately, the grind has been calling to me - the satisfaction of working hard, for a long time, and reaching my goal. When I had the opportunity for a free copy of DS3 I didn't hesitate - what was there to lose?

For a month, DS3 was my respite. After long days at work, during leisurely days off, talking to friends or alone, I've spent time and effort to learn, adapt and improve. It's been a great ride, from my first frustrated swings at Gundyr to finally taking that last swing at the Soul of Cinder. Great memories, shouts of joy, frustrated screaming and a deep, true happiness have been my main companions. As I completed the game, it felt like the end of something great, something beautiful.

75 hours. Just over a month. What a journey.

I think Dark Souls 3 has some of the best boss fights in Fromsoft's entire catalog. Some of the areas in this game are also shockingly beautiful and evocative, up there with the prettiest zones of Sekiro and Elden Ring. However, I thought many of its enemy encounters were too reliant on unmanagably large groups swarming you, and the level design was disappointingly safe.

Overall, this was a great time. I'd recommend playing Dark Souls 3 if you haven't. It's solid as both an entry point into the Souls series and as a synthesis of the ideas from DS1, Bloodborne, and DS2. And as the mechnical/design basis for Fromsoft's later games, it's also fun to examine as a checkpoint in time.

Although I really didn't think Dark Souls 1 could be topped, DS3 gives it a run for its money. For the most part it aims to pick out all the best aspects of Ds1, Ds2, and even Demon Souls in what feels like an attempt to make the ultimate souls game. Of course, all these games are very unique from one another, so its very hard for me to call it objectively the best. The art direction is stunning, and I was consistently amazed at how much detail it had considering the scale of it all. Instead of being totally interconnected like 1 or having the weird hard linear paths of 2, it sits at a fairly comfortable middle ground. The combat feels great and the weapon skills are an incredible addition that adds a nice new layer of depth. It is MUCH faster than DS1/2, but I don't see that as a positive or negative, merely different. I ultimately felt the story and ending wasn't as satisfying as DS1, but I also just got the default ending, so maybe the secret one is better.

All that said its fantastic and I recommend it, but unlike some I really wouldn't recommend it as your first souls game. DS3 isn't really much easier than DS1, just more linear. Plus your playthrough of DS3 will have a lot more weight having played 1, and 1 is amazing, so play that first!!!

Dark Souls III is not just a game—it’s a divine test of courage, skill, and determination, hand-delivered by the gods of gaming themselves. FromSoftware has crafted a masterpiece that transcends entertainment, delivering an experience that will break you down, lift you up, and leave you in awe.

The world of Dark Souls III is a hauntingly beautiful symphony of decay and grandeur. Every area, from the smoldering ruins of the Kiln of the First Flame to the eerie majesty of Anor Londo, is a work of art. The interconnected level design is genius, rewarding exploration with breathtaking vistas and chilling shortcuts that will make your heart race.

Combat is perfection. Every roll, parry, and strike is a calculated dance of life and death. The variety of weapons, spells, and playstyles ensures you can craft a character that’s uniquely yours. Want to be a nimble dual-wielder or a pyromancer hurling fireballs? Go for it. Or maybe you’d rather wield a greatsword the size of a small house—because why not? The possibilities are endless.

The bosses are nothing short of legendary. Each encounter is an unforgettable showdown of epic proportions, with jaw-dropping designs and soundtracks that make you feel like you’re fighting for your very soul. They’re punishing but fair, testing your skills and resolve in the most exhilarating ways.

And the lore. Oh, the lore. It’s a dark and tragic tapestry, woven with the ashes of a dying world. Piecing together its secrets is as rewarding as conquering its hardest bosses. The subtle storytelling invites you to dig deeper, uncovering layers of meaning that make the world feel alive—even as it crumbles around you.

This game doesn’t hold your hand, but it offers something far better: the triumph of overcoming insurmountable odds. If you’ve ever doubted yourself, Dark Souls III will remind you of your strength. It’s a game that challenges you, respects you, and ultimately rewards you with a sense of achievement that no other game can match.

Rating: 11/10 – Praise the Sun, and prepare to cry tears of joy.

Dark Souls III – A Masterpiece
FromSoftware’s Dark Souls III is a powerful closing chapter in one of gaming’s most iconic trilogies. Released in 2016, it refines the punishing yet rewarding formula the series is known for, combining oppressive atmosphere, elegant combat mechanics, and haunting world design into a near-perfect action RPG.
Atmosphere & World Design

Dark Souls III presents a crumbling world on the brink of collapse. Lothric and its surrounding lands are drenched in despair and decay, but they’re also stunningly beautiful in a bleak, gothic way. The interconnectedness of the areas, a signature of the series, returns with clever shortcuts and breathtaking vistas that encourage exploration.

Every location—from the frozen Catacombs of Carthus to the eerie Consumed King’s Garden—feels handcrafted and purposeful. There’s an overwhelming sense of history embedded in each ruin and relic, and storytelling through the environment remains one of the game’s strongest traits.
Gameplay & Combat

Combat is faster and more responsive than in previous Souls games, drawing influence from Bloodborne while retaining the methodical nature of Dark Souls. Timing, stamina management, and precision remain crucial, but the game encourages more aggressive playstyles than its predecessors.

Weapon arts—unique abilities tied to specific weapons—add depth and variety, allowing players to craft builds that suit their combat preferences. Magic, pyromancy, and miracles have all been rebalanced for greater viability, making spellcasters more competitive with melee builds.
Difficulty & Design Philosophy

Yes, Dark Souls III is hard, but not unfair. Its challenge lies in learning from failure and growing more adept with each attempt. Boss fights are fierce and often visually stunning, with standout encounters like the Abyss Watchers, Pontiff Sulyvahn, and the final battle against the Soul of Cinder delivering unforgettable moments.

The game respects the player’s intelligence and patience. There’s little hand-holding; discovery and mastery are hard-earned, which makes triumph all the more satisfying.
Multiplayer & Replayability

Multiplayer remains an integral, chaotic part of the experience. Summoning allies for co-op or facing invaders in tense PvP duels adds unpredictability and fun. The covenants system, while a bit convoluted, gives players additional goals and incentives for repeated playthroughs.
Final Verdict

Dark Souls III is not just a game; it’s a gauntlet of endurance, curiosity, and triumph. For veterans, it’s a fitting, emotionally resonant farewell to the series. For newcomers, it’s a demanding but immensely rewarding entry point. Few games challenge and reward players with such elegance and conviction.

Rating: 9.5/10
Dark Souls III isn’t just a great game—it’s a defining moment in modern game design.

This is my least favorite of the main Dark Souls trilogy, but it's still quite good. The 100% grind is quite painful (I got the rings from a friend, or my playtime would be significantly longer) and there are some animation bugs that were never fixed. This also represents the beginning of bosses faking out the player, or what Elden Ring players get upset about with irregular swing timings and unrealistic movements etc. That never really bothered me, but it's notable. The Ashes of War (or weapon abilities) were also solidified here. Previously they were limited to specific weapons, but now most weapons have one. The PvP is also cancer. People are much more polite in the first two games, but in 3 anything goes and people are usually quite rude. The large number of summons also leads to fight club phenomena. Hacking also used to be an atrocious issue with save injection and other issues. I did a little PvP since the servers came back online to get the darkmoon miracle and didn't notice those issues again, so maybe they've been fixed. Might be worth looking into though.

All this being said, this is still a good game, and I would say it has a higher degree of polish or consistent quality compared to the previous two titles. It just personally doesn't really grab me the same way. I'm not really sure why that is. It's definitely worth playing, but I would play the other games over this one.

Posted: January 13
The video game industry is vast and varied, with countless genres, styles, and experiences available for players worldwide. However, among the immense catalog of titles, Dark Souls 3 stands out as a transcendent experience that deserves to be titled the best game ever made. From its intricate world-building and atmospheric storytelling to its challenging combat and deep lore, Dark Souls 3 combines these elements in a way that captivates players and immerses them into its unforgiving world. The game's design, philosophy, and enduring impact have made it a definitive masterpiece, one that continually influences and inspires the gaming landscape.

Challenging, Rewarding Combat

One of the most iconic features of the Dark Souls series, and specifically Dark Souls 3, is its combat. The game is renowned for its difficulty, which requires players to master both the timing and the stamina management of their character. Every enemy encounter is a test of precision, patience, and skill. The combat is not about button-mashing or spamming attacks; it’s about understanding the rhythm of battle, reading enemy movements, and responding accordingly. The feeling of accomplishment that comes from defeating a tough boss or overcoming a difficult area is unparalleled. Unlike most games, Dark Souls 3 does not hold the player's hand. Every victory feels earned, and this sense of accomplishment is one of the game's strongest draws.

The variety of weapons and playstyles available in Dark Souls 3 further enriches its combat. Players can choose between heavy, slow weapons or fast, dexterous ones, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. Magic, pyromancy, and faith-based abilities also add layers of strategy to the gameplay. This flexibility allows players to tailor their approach to combat, creating a highly personalized experience that rewards experimentation and mastery.

World-Building and Environmental Storytelling

Another aspect that makes Dark Souls 3 so remarkable is its world-building. The game's setting, Lothric, is a desolate, decaying world where the flame that sustains life is flickering out. From the moment players step into the game, they are met with an overwhelming sense of atmosphere and tension. The architecture, the enemies, and the environments all tell a story of a once-great kingdom now on the brink of collapse.

What sets Dark Souls 3 apart from many other games is the way it conveys its lore. The game doesn’t provide exposition through long cutscenes or dialogues. Instead, it leaves pieces of the story scattered throughout the world for players to discover themselves. The locations are interconnected, and clues to the past can be found in the architecture, item descriptions, and the lore of the characters you encounter. This method of environmental storytelling is not only immersive, but it also encourages players to pay attention to their surroundings and piece together the narrative at their own pace.

The interconnected world of Dark Souls 3 is a work of art in its own right. The areas are meticulously designed, with hidden paths, secret rooms, and shortcuts that reward curiosity. Lothric is a world that feels alive, yet decayed, and the haunting beauty of its landscapes adds to the melancholy tone of the game. From the fiery ruins of the high walls to the eerie cathedral of the deep, each location tells its own tragic story, inviting players to explore every nook and cranny.

Philosophical Depth and Themes

The thematic richness of Dark Souls 3 is another factor that elevates it above other games. The central theme of the game revolves around the cyclical nature of life and death. The fire that sustains life is fading, and the world is slowly decaying, yet the characters in the game are determined to preserve it. This tension between preservation and decay runs throughout the entire narrative and manifests in the actions and motivations of the game's key figures.

The story of Dark Souls 3 revolves around the concept of "linking the fire," a ritual that keeps the flame alive and prolongs the world's existence. However, this ritual also brings suffering, as those who sacrifice themselves to the fire are condemned to an eternity of burning. In this way, Dark Souls 3 explores existential questions about the nature of sacrifice, the meaning of life, and the inevitability of death. The game doesn’t offer easy answers but instead leaves these questions for the player to contemplate as they progress.

This philosophical depth is what gives Dark Souls 3 its emotional resonance. It’s a game that challenges the player’s understanding of life and death and forces them to grapple with difficult moral decisions. The characters you meet along the way, whether allies or enemies, are all dealing with their own struggles and dilemmas. The game presents a world where hope is scarce, but it’s still possible to find meaning through perseverance and determination.

Enduring Impact on Gaming

Since its release in 2016, Dark Souls 3 has had a profound influence on the gaming industry. Its combination of difficult gameplay, intricate world-building, and philosophical themes has inspired countless developers and players. Many games have adopted elements from the Dark Souls series, such as challenging combat, interconnected worlds, and non-linear storytelling. The term "Souls-like" has become a genre unto itself, and Dark Souls 3 remains the gold standard by which all other games in this category are judged.

Furthermore, the game’s community continues to thrive, with players discussing strategies, uncovering hidden secrets, and sharing experiences. The game's online components, including invasions and co-op play, encourage player interaction in a way that few other games can match. The sense of camaraderie that emerges from helping others overcome difficult challenges is one of the many reasons Dark Souls 3 remains beloved by its fanbase.

Conclusion

In the world of video games, few titles achieve the level of excellence that Dark Souls 3 has attained. Its combination of challenging gameplay, deep lore, atmospheric world-building, and philosophical themes make it an experience unlike any other. The sense of accomplishment in overcoming its challenges, the joy of discovering its secrets, and the emotional weight of its narrative all contribute to making Dark Souls 3 the best game ever. It’s a game that demands respect, rewards perseverance, and leaves a lasting impression on everyone who steps into its dark and unforgiving world.

Posted: January 13
Dark Souls III is everything on the outside, that is claims to be on the inside.

Dark Souls III is a bleak, grey, melancholy conclusion to a trilogy that defined an era. To me, Dark Souls III is the weakest entry in the trilogy but one that serves its purpose perfectly. Without spoilers, Dark Souls III constantly paints a picture of a world run raw by its repeating nature. Characters, Items, and Locations from games that (lore-wise) took place thousands of years ago, reappear with no mention of how they have arrived, they share only one detail amongst them. All of these things are tired, and caught in a neverending cycle. This feeling is pushed onto the player through all of the recycled material and overall bleak and grey atmosphere the game provides.

Dark Souls III is as much a game as it is a commentary about the series as a whole, as great as it has been, the formula has become stale, and it's time to stop it now and move on to something new. If you have played Dark Souls I and Dark Souls II, Dark Souls III is a must play in order to conclude your adventure.

Dark Souls 3 – A Masterclass in Challenge, Storytelling, and Immersion

Dark Souls 3, developed by FromSoftware, is a crowning achievement in both design and storytelling. As a continuation of the series, it combines the best elements of its predecessors while refining mechanics and delivering a deeply immersive experience that challenges players in both skill and patience. If you're ready to dive into one of the most rewarding, atmospheric, and challenging games ever made, then Dark Souls 3 should be at the top of your list.

Story & Characters:
The narrative in Dark Souls 3 is, as with previous titles, deliberately fragmented and steeped in lore. The story revolves around the "Linking of the Fire," a recurring theme throughout the series. Players take on the role of the Ashen One, a chosen being destined to either rekindle the fire or allow the age of dark to begin. This overarching plot is simple on the surface but reveals layers of complexity through the game's environment, NPCs, and subtle clues.

The characters are some of the most memorable in the series. From the enigmatic Fire Keeper, who tends to your bonfires and offers cryptic guidance, to the tragic Siegward of Catarina, each character is rich with personality, history, and purpose. NPC dialogue is often minimal but laden with meaning, pushing players to piece together their motivations and destinies through exploration.

Exploration & World Design:
Exploration is at the heart of Dark Souls 3, and it rewards patience and careful observation. The interconnected world is masterfully designed, with areas that loop back on themselves, creating a sense of unity and discovery. From the decrepit Cathedral of the Deep to the eerie ruins of Lothric, each location feels unique and filled with secrets. Every corner hides items, hidden passages, or lore that further deepens the player's understanding of the world.

One of the game's greatest achievements is how it handles verticality and secrets. Areas that seem unreachable often become accessible later, after uncovering shortcuts or abilities. The layout of the world invites players to explore every nook and cranny, as every item, every door, and every hidden path is part of a greater puzzle. The rewards for exploration are tangible—from powerful weapons to lore-drenched artifacts.

Mechanics & Difficulty:
Dark Souls 3's core gameplay revolves around tight, deliberate combat that rewards careful timing and patience. The combat system is highly flexible, offering a wide array of weapons, armor, and spells that allow for various playstyles. Whether you prefer to engage with a giant sword, a fast dual-wielding build, or a sorcery-focused mage, the game offers tools for almost any playstyle. The magic system is deep and varied, with multiple schools of sorcery, pyromancy, and miracles to experiment with.

At its core, combat is about rhythm and patience. Every fight feels like a duel, whether you're battling a lowly enemy or a massive boss. Learning enemy attack patterns, timing your dodges and parries, and understanding the weight of your gear are essential. The game's difficulty is unrelenting but fair. Each death teaches something valuable—whether it’s about the terrain, the enemies, or your own limits.

Playstyles & Patience:
One of the most notable features of Dark Souls 3 is the range of viable playstyles. While many games constrain you to specific character builds, Dark Souls 3 offers the freedom to craft abilities and equipment to suit your preferences. Whether you focus on dexterity and agility, heavy tanking, or long-range magic, every playthrough feels distinct.

The game’s difficulty, often seen as a hallmark of the series, forces you to slow down and think critically. Learning enemy behaviors, taking note of environmental cues, and planning ahead are key to success. Death is not just a setback but a lesson, reinforcing the need for patience and adaptability. Through its challenges, Dark Souls 3 teaches perseverance and rewards determination with immense satisfaction.

Conclusion:
Dark Souls 3 is not just a game; it’s an experience. It’s a test of your skill, patience, and resilience. The characters, story, and world are meticulously crafted, and the combat is rewarding and unforgiving. Through its difficulty, the game teaches that progress comes not through brute force but understanding and adaptability. If you’re ready to embrace its challenges, Dark Souls 3 offers one of the most satisfying experiences in modern gaming.

Midir? Oh, yeah, I turned him into a pincushion with arrows. Thought I won, but then he reminded me he’s basically Bayle on steroids. Plus, the camera decided to team up with Midir, just to make sure I had no chance of surviving. After 500 deaths and tears, I reach Gael.
Gael. One of my favorite boss fights in DS3, right up there with Nameless King and Soul of Cinder. I died more than a thousand times, but honestly, I had more fun getting wrecked by him than playing with Halflight.

A Triumphant Finale!
Dark Souls 3 is everything a finale to the Souls series should be: grand, haunting, and packed with unforgettable moments.
It’s a love letter to fans of the series, a culmination of its best elements, and a fitting end to the dark, enigmatic world of Lordran and its many fallen kingdoms.
Right from the start, Dark Souls 3 impresses with its pace and polish.
The combat is faster and more fluid than ever, seamlessly blending the deliberate precision of the original Dark Souls with the agility of Bloodborne!
Every swing of your weapon, roll, and spell cast feels tight and responsive, making battles as exhilarating as they are challenging.
The world design is breathtaking, offering a mix of new areas and nostalgic callbacks.
Lothric is a sprawling, interconnected realm filled with gothic spires, cursed swamps, and decaying ruins.
From the terrifying descent into the Abyssal Cathedral to the melancholy beauty of the snow-covered Irithyll, every location is dripping with atmosphere and storytelling.
Boss battles are, as expected, a major highlight.
Each encounter is crafted with care, offering unique challenges that test your skill and patience.
From the ethereal grace of the Dancer of the Boreal Valley to the sheer ferocity of the Nameless King, the bosses are as memorable as they are brutal.
And for those seeking an even greater challenge, the DLCs deliver some of the most intense fights in the series.
What sets Dark Souls 3 apart is its sense of finality.
The game is rich with lore and nods to the previous entries, tying up threads and deepening the series’ overarching themes of cycles, decay, and the struggle to break free from fate.
It’s a story that resonates deeply, especially for longtime fans who’ve followed the series from the beginning.
Visually, the game is stunning, with detailed environments, intricate character designs, and a rich, oppressive atmosphere that perfectly complements the game’s tone.
While it may not reinvent the formula, Dark Souls 3 refines it to near perfection.
It’s a game that rewards exploration, persistence, and mastery, offering a journey that feels as epic as it does personal.
For newcomers, it’s a challenging but accessible entry point into the series.
For veterans, it’s a bittersweet farewell to one of gaming’s most iconic franchises!
Dark Souls 3 is a testament to the enduring brilliance of the series, a dark and beautiful masterpiece that reminds us why we fell in love with these games in the first place.

After having played Demon's Souls, Dark Souls, Dark Souls II SotFS and Elden Ring, I can safely say that this game is definitely my favourite so far. The gameplay is a massive step-up in quality compared to the 2 previous Dark Souls games, feeling more fluid and satisfying. The combat options feel more diverse with the new dual wield weapons (I think this method is better than the one in DS2 and ER) allowing for some neat combos and a whole new style of combat. The bosses are really fun and they're really memorable (Wolnir, Yhorm and Curse-Rotted Greatwood are an exception but they ain't really bad they're just less fun compared to the rest), showing amazing bosses like Soul of Cinder, Slave Knight Gael, Pontiff Sulyvahn and many others. The level design is more straightforward (very much welcome after the confusing design of DS2) but I would like it a bit more if it took DS1's approach (still good level design tho). The music, as always is incredible. Yuka Kitamura and Motoi Sakuraba never fail to provide an amazing listening experience.

All and all, I would say this game is a solid 9.5/10. I'll definitely revisit it many more times whenever I'm looking to have some good fun.

I have played this game an unhealthy amount of times and every play-through, I feel like something is missing and I finally think I figured it out. This game has a lot of fast moving, highly agile enemies that attack in large mobs. Imagine having a high-powered pistol in your off hand to quickly break the poise of your enemies when timed right, allowing you to then attack them stunned for large chunk of damage. A very visceral attack in its nature because it damages the enemies in their internal organs. The gun could use special bullets made from silver or mercury to give them a magical quality. But instead of calling them mercury bullets, because that’s lame, we could name them quicksilver bullets because it sounds way cooler. Then, because you are one-handing your main weapon, you could have it switch on the fly to a larger weapon with a new move-set. Think of a large dagger that flips out to a spear or scythe... or a big cleaver that flips out like a saw? This would be a great way to add combos to your fighting style, switching between light and heavy attacks in the folded version of the weapon and then switching to an entirely new move-set when it coverts. It may be kind of tricky at first to get used to, but I think it would make combat flow really nicely. I know, you are asking, “But what about shields or armor?” Well we all know it’s all about the fashion in this game so you would have armor that is more about moving fast than tanking damage. And shields, well shields are nice, but not if they engender passivity. Oh! And then you could have all kinds of few enemies that are not just your typical Knights and dragons to fight. What about having cosmic horror monsters? Tanking the best ideas from H.P. Lovecraft and Robert E. Howard’s Solomon Kane books and making a whole Nightmare world of new monsters! I know some of you probably think this is just a silly idea, but I think this could be a great addition to this game. And I bet if FromSoft made a game with these features would be a MONEY PRINTING MACHINE on ALL PLATFORMS and even game of the year or Greatest Hits down the road! Maybe I’m crazy for thinking this.

Dark Souls 3: A Masterpiece of Challenge and Atmosphere
Dark Souls 3 is a true gem in the gaming world, delivering a perfect blend of punishing difficulty, stunning world design, and deep lore. FromSoftware’s signature dark fantasy style reaches new heights in this entry, making it an unforgettable journey for fans of the series and newcomers alike.

Gameplay and Combat
The core of Dark Souls 3 is its tight, methodical combat. Every enemy encounter feels like a carefully choreographed dance where timing, strategy, and patience are key. The bosses are the crown jewels of the experience—intense, memorable, and beautifully designed. While challenging, each victory feels hard-earned and immensely rewarding, driving you to push forward despite the odds.

World and Exploration
The world of Dark Souls 3 is a hauntingly beautiful labyrinth. From the desolate ruins of Lothric Castle to the eerie swamps and fiery depths of Smouldering Lake, every area is dripping with atmosphere. The interconnected level design encourages exploration, rewarding curiosity with hidden treasures, shortcuts, and lore-rich details. It’s a world that feels alive, even in its decay.

Lore and Storytelling
As with all Souls games, the lore is deep and mysterious, woven into item descriptions, cryptic NPC dialogue, and environmental storytelling. Unraveling the story is a rewarding puzzle in itself, offering layers of depth to those who seek it. The melancholic tone and themes of despair, sacrifice, and rebirth add an emotional weight to the journey.

Replayability and Community
Dark Souls 3 offers incredible replay value, with multiple character builds, branching paths, and endings. Its online components—co-op, invasions, and messages—add an extra layer of unpredictability and fun. The community remains passionate, keeping the game alive with discussions, mods, and fan creations.

Verdict
Dark Souls 3 is a masterclass in game design, combining challenging gameplay, breathtaking environments, and rich storytelling into a seamless package. It’s a game that demands perseverance but rewards you with a sense of accomplishment few other games can match.

Rating: 10/10
A legendary action RPG that stands as one of the finest examples of FromSoftware’s brilliance. Prepare to die—and love every second of it

So I finished all the invasions needed for 100% achievements in the Covenants Without Farming in 2025. Decided to share a small report.
This was my first time playing, and it was hell a lot of fun! Really exciting!
The main point is, I'm kind of a below-average player: I occasionally avoid potential backstabs when somebody is fishing, can't really land any myself (except for some Weapon Art punishes), and haven't mastered parrying well in PvP.
The extreme part is that I was playing at SL 142. Since by the time I reached SL 137, I realized that for DS3, unlike Elden Ring, the meta is around SL 120. And at that point, I still needed to consume 5 dark sigils to achieve the unique ending. So I probably spent a lot more time waiting for an invasion then needed.
Some Notes About Time Required in Covenants: I managed to complete all covenants except Darkmoons and Watchdogs during my last two playthroughs, invading all the time in all the areas I visited. I regret I didn't start invading from the very first playthrough at low levels. So after I finished base NG++ it took me an extra ~15 hours to complete the Darkmoons and Watchdogs. Watchdogs of Farron was probably the most intense and interesting to me.
I hope this review will be somehow helping and encouraging to other players who are considering going online over farming.

Posted: January 5
What a journey.

I linked the Fire in Lordran, thinking it would solve anything.

I sought a different solution with Aldia in Drangleic, and whatever came of that, nobody knows.

I grew disillusioned by the linking of the First Flame, seeing the ruin it wrought Lothric and its merging lands. Old royalties perishing, legacies being made into nothing.

I gave my Fire Keeper eyes, seeing betrayal and putting an age that should've been long past put to rest. But before that, I followed the slave knight to the ends of the world. I gave the white-haired girl the immortal pigment of the Dark Soul, and putting the hope of the world in her painting.

In that final fight, everything came together. I felt like, in the final battle, Gwyn himself came back to stop me. Sensing betrayal of the fire, he shoved aside all the powerful Lords of Cinder so he could make himself manifest one last time. He realized that this was the one Ash that would put an end to his reign, and in horror he sprouted. To me, it felt like this was his desperate attempt to preserve his selfish Age of Flame that caused all the chaos. And even in his final moments, he would try to stop it. To keep the power from falling out of his grasp. No matter the end, his worst fears came to be. In the end, a world was made with the Dark Soul. One he could never hope to stop. One that would have no need for relinking. One that would last forever. Made in the very land of exile he created. One that would outlive him, and all who followed suit.

I guess what I'm trying to say, is that this series really clicked. All three entries are necessary for maximum enjoyment of this magical journey. The 'freedom of interpretation' story made it deeply personal, and I wish I could experience it for the first time again.

So savour your adventure, and don't you dare go hollow.

Posted: January 5
So, the third Souls game from FromSoft I've played, after Elden Ring & Dark Souls 1...

And? Well, it fel alot like revisiting Dark Souls 1, but everything's new and improved. There are merchants just like in DS1, but this time you can actually sell stuff to them, bonefires are placed more conveniently than in DS1, many bosses feels lke improved versions of past bosses in DS1 or in some cases prototypes of bosses we meet in Elden Ring - with some interesting switch-arounds; Gundyr, the first boss we encounter, is for example more like Margit than the Asylum demon, while Vordt is indeed more like the Asylum demon... But otherwise, many feels like encounters with new and improved versions of bosses, such as Midir of Kalamet or the Soul of Cinder of Gwyn - and perhaps Gael of Artorias, though we meet him last instead of first, making him yet another switch-around...

Anyway, it's what you'd expect from a Souls game - tough enemies both big and small, lots of deaths (many of those from some stupid mistake or another), rage/frustration over loss of souls, gorgeous views, intriguing lore, lots of attractive loot/treasure, interesting encounters with NPCs both hostile and not, plenty of exploration... All that's needed to keep the players interested as they stubbornly trudge along.

Now, while I've interacted more with the multiplayer part of the game here than in any other FromSoft Game thus far - and while I found it interesting enough, I'm still not entirely fond of how it works. I mean, if you want the stuff associated with the covenants, you pretty much HAVE to do multiplayer, since the only way to otherwise get the stuff you have to turn in to the covenants is by farming, but since they're very rare drops, it'll likely take longer to do than doing multiplayer... But doing multiplayer can turn out to be just as troublesome for a number of reasons. For one thing, if you're not very good at multiplayer, it'll be a while before you get the stuff you need. Secondly, there may be a lack of players and whatnot plus there's the level restrictions as well, so...

To paint an image, I spent a lot of time playing as a sunbro but getting the covenant items took more time than I feel should be necessary not just because it's a rare drop, but also because, while I did offer my services in coop for bosses it sometimes took a long time before I was summoned for the fight in question, if I was summoned at all. This surely makes it all more troublesome, because in addition to the restictions on coop, a lot of people in these souls games take pride in being able to finish a boss by themselves, or with the aid of only NPC-summons, which are more in-game/in-story than a summoned player could ever be.

I must say I'm also unhappy about the absence of any achievements for the DLCs. At least give us achievements for Friede, Midir and Gael, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥!

Speaking of the DLCs, I feel that in some ways, I can't really praise The Ringed City as much as I want to, in part because it's a little too, shall we say, gimmicky at times, meaning the existance of some enemies such as the constantly respawning skeletons at the Dreg Heap, the Angels and the Judicators with their summons and whatnot. In addition, the enemies along the path of the Ringed City are strong, tough or have annoying abilities (thinking specifically of the Hollow Clerics for the last one; they can target the player from much further away than I feel should be the case, while also remaining far or altogether out of sight from the player, making progressing through the Ringed City a slow annoying slog at best, which I feel shouldn't be the case...

In summary: if you have already played some souls game or another, you should feel right at home in the game and if not, it's a good place to start your learning curve - if beating Gundyr doesn't strike you as too daunting a task, as such...

Aside from the extremely unforgiving concequences of your actions I cannot see another problem with this game. It's beautiful. Stop reading the reviews and just buy it. If this is your first soulslike expect to die A LOT to the first two bosses. After them you are basically good to go since you're used to the controls. And remember, DO NOT GIVE UP. YOU WILL BEAT THE BOSS EVENTUALLY. And if you really cant beat it. Just pick up the sellsword twinblades from farron keep. Anyways I believe there is nothing more for me to discuss so I hope you enjoy this game as much as I did.
